title: Neuropsychopharmacology and the genetics of schizophrenia: a history of the diagnosis of schizophrenia.
abstract: The diagnostic conceptualization of schizophrenia has undergone substantial evolution since Kraepelin's initial description of dementia praecox, with profound implications for contemporary neuropsychopharmacological research and genetic studies. This historical review examines the progression of diagnostic criteria from classical descriptive approaches through modern operational definitions, and their impact on pharmacogenetic investigations. We analyzed key diagnostic frameworks from 1896 to 2000, including contributions by Kraepelin, Bleuler, Schneider, and the DSM/ICD systems, correlating these with major pharmacological discoveries and genetic findings. The transition from Kraepelin's deterioration-based model to Bleuler's broader concept of "split mind" fundamentally altered research populations and therapeutic approaches. Schneider's first-rank symptoms provided operational clarity but potentially narrowed the phenotypic scope for genetic studies. The introduction of chlorpromazine in the 1950s marked the beginning of rational psychopharmacology, while subsequent dopamine hypothesis development directly influenced both diagnostic conceptualization and drug discovery. Modern neuropsychopharmacological research has been significantly shaped by DSM-III's operational criteria, enabling more homogeneous study populations for pharmacogenetic investigations. However, this diagnostic refinement may have inadvertently excluded important phenotypic variants relevant to treatment response and genetic susceptibility. The evolution of schizophrenia diagnosis reflects the complex interplay between clinical observation, pharmacological discovery, and genetic understanding. Current neuropsychopharmacological research would benefit from reconsidering broader phenotypic definitions that encompass the full spectrum of schizophrenia-related disorders, particularly as pharmacogenetic studies reveal the heterogeneous nature of treatment response within traditional diagnostic boundaries.

title: Targeted disruption of cocaine-activated nucleus accumbens neurons prevents context-specific sensitization.
abstract: Learned associations between effects of abused drugs and the drug administration environment are important in drug addiction. Histochemical and electrophysiological studies suggest that these associations are encoded in sparsely distributed nucleus accumbens neurons that are selectively activated by drugs and drug-associated cues. Although correlations have been observed between nucleus accumbens neuronal activity and responsivity to drugs and drug cues, no technique exists for selectively manipulating these activated neurons and establishing their causal role in behavioral effects of drugs and drug cues. Here we describe a new approach, which we term the 'Daun02 inactivation method', that selectively inactivates a minority of neurons previously activated by cocaine in an environment repeatedly paired with cocaine to demonstrate a causal role for these activated neurons in context-specific cocaine-induced psychomotor sensitization in rats. This method provides a new tool for studying the causal roles of selectively activated neurons in behavioral effects of drugs and drug cues and in other learned behaviors.

title: Lactate dehydrogenase release is facilitated by brief sonication of rat hippocampal slices and isolated retinas following acute neuronal damage.
abstract: Assessment of neuronal viability in acute brain slice preparations remains a critical methodological challenge in neuropharmacological research. Lactate dehydrogenase (LDH) release has emerged as a reliable marker of cellular damage, but traditional measurement approaches may underestimate tissue injury due to incomplete enzyme release from damaged cells. We investigated whether brief sonication enhances LDH release detection following acute neuronal damage in rat hippocampal slices and isolated retinas. Hippocampal slices (400 μm) and retinal preparations were obtained from adult Sprague-Dawley rats and subjected to oxygen-glucose deprivation (OGD) for 30-120 minutes or excitotoxic injury using 100 μM NMDA. Following injury protocols, tissues were either processed immediately for LDH assay or subjected to 10-second sonication (40 kHz, 0.2 W/cm²) prior to enzyme measurement. Sonication significantly increased detectable LDH release by 2.3-fold in hippocampal slices and 1.8-fold in retinal preparations compared to non-sonicated controls after equivalent injury periods. The enhancement was most pronounced following mild to moderate injury conditions, where traditional methods detected minimal enzyme release. Dose-response relationships between injury duration and LDH release showed improved linearity following sonication treatment. Cell viability assessed by trypan blue exclusion confirmed that sonication parameters did not induce additional cellular damage in healthy tissue. These findings demonstrate that brief sonication represents a simple and effective method for enhancing LDH release detection sensitivity in neural tissue preparations, potentially improving the accuracy of neuronal viability assessments in neuropharmacological studies investigating neuroprotective compounds and injury mechanisms.

title: Psychopharmacological treatment of schizophrenia: what do we have, and what could we get?
abstract: Current antipsychotic medications for schizophrenia primarily target dopamine D2 receptors, providing modest efficacy for positive symptoms but limited benefits for negative symptoms and cognitive deficits. This comprehensive review examines existing pharmacotherapeutic approaches and emerging therapeutic targets for schizophrenia treatment. We conducted a systematic analysis of published clinical trials, preclinical studies, and mechanistic investigations spanning 1990-2012, focusing on both established antipsychotics and novel compounds targeting alternative neurotransmitter systems. Current first- and second-generation antipsychotics demonstrate 60-70% response rates for positive symptoms but show minimal improvement in functional outcomes. Clozapine remains the gold standard for treatment-resistant cases, though its use is limited by serious adverse effects. Emerging approaches targeting glutamatergic hypofunction through NMDA receptor glycine transport inhibition (e.g., bitopertin) and mGluR2/3 agonism show promise in early trials. Cholinergic enhancement via α7 nicotinic receptor modulation demonstrates potential for cognitive improvement, while muscarinic M1/M4 receptor agonists offer antipsychotic efficacy with reduced extrapyramidal side effects. Additionally, compounds targeting histamine H3 receptors and trace amine-associated receptor 1 (TAAR1) represent novel therapeutic avenues. Despite these advances, effect sizes for cognitive enhancement remain modest, and functional improvement continues to lag behind symptom reduction. Future therapeutic development should prioritize multi-target approaches addressing the complex pathophysiology of schizophrenia, with particular emphasis on procognitive agents and treatments that meaningfully improve quality of life and social functioning.

title: Rat Ultrasonic Vocalizations and Behavioral Neuropharmacology: From the Screening of Drugs to the Study of Disease.
abstract: Several lines of evidence indicate that rats emit ultrasonic vocalizations (USVs) in response to a wide range of stimuli that are capable of producing either euphoric (positive) or dysphoric (negative) emotional states. On these bases, recordings of USVs are extensively used in preclinical studies of affect, motivation, and social behavior. Rat USVs are sensitive to the effects of certain classes of psychoactive drugs, suggesting that emission of rat USVs can have relevance not only to neurobiology, but also to neuropharmacology and psychopharmacology. This review summarizes three types of rat USVs, namely 40-kHz USVs emitted by pups, 22-kHz USVs and 50-kHz USVs emitted by young and adult animals, and relevance of these vocalizations to neuropharmacological studies. Attention will be focused on the issues of how rat USVs can be used to evaluate the pharmacological properties of different classes of drugs, and how rat USVs can be combined with other behavioral models used in neuropharmacology. The strengths and limitations of experimental paradigms based on the evaluation of rat USVs will also be discussed.

title: Determination of high-affinity choline uptake (HACU) and choline acetyltransferase (ChAT) activity in the same population of cultured cells.
abstract: Cholinergic neurotransmission relies on the coordinated function of high-affinity choline uptake (HACU) and choline acetyltransferase (ChAT) activity, yet technical limitations have prevented simultaneous measurement of these parameters in individual cell populations. We developed a novel dual-assay protocol to assess both HACU and ChAT activity sequentially in the same cultured cholinergic cells, enabling direct correlation of these functionally related processes. Primary septal cholinergic neurons were cultured from embryonic day 18 rat fetuses and maintained for 14-21 days in vitro. HACU was measured using [³H]choline uptake assays with hemicholinium-3 inhibition to confirm specificity, followed by cell harvesting and ChAT activity determination using [¹⁴C]acetyl-CoA as substrate. Muscarinic receptor modulation was examined using carbachol and atropine treatments. The dual-assay protocol successfully quantified both parameters without methodological interference. Baseline HACU rates averaged 45.2 ± 3.8 pmol/min/mg protein, while ChAT activity measured 12.7 ± 1.9 nmol/h/mg protein. Carbachol treatment (100 μM) significantly increased both HACU (168% of control) and ChAT activity (142% of control), effects blocked by atropine pretreatment. Strong positive correlation was observed between HACU and ChAT activity across individual culture wells (r = 0.78, p < 0.001). This methodology provides a valuable tool for investigating cholinergic function at the cellular level and demonstrates coordinated regulation of choline uptake and acetylcholine synthesis mechanisms. The approach will facilitate studies of cholinergic dysfunction in neurodegenerative diseases and therapeutic drug development.

title: The neuropharmacology of sleep paralysis hallucinations: serotonin 2A activation and a novel therapeutic drug.
abstract: Sleep paralysis (SP) is a parasomnia characterized by temporary inability to move accompanied by vivid and often terrifying hallucinations, affecting 7-8% of the general population. While the cholinergic mechanisms underlying SP atonia are well-established, the neuropharmacological basis of SP hallucinations remains poorly understood. Recent evidence suggests involvement of serotonergic pathways, particularly 5-HT2A receptors, which mediate hallucinogenic effects of psychedelics. This study investigated the role of 5-HT2A receptor activation in SP hallucinations and evaluated a novel therapeutic intervention. We administered the 5-HT2A agonist 25I-NBOMe (0.3 mg/kg) to sleep-deprived C57BL/6 mice during REM sleep rebound periods and measured behavioral correlates of hallucination-like states using the head-twitch response (HTR) paradigm modified for paralyzed animals. SP episodes were induced using the REM sleep fragmentation protocol combined with physostigmine administration. 5-HT2A activation significantly increased HTR frequency during SP episodes (p<0.001) and enhanced c-Fos expression in visual and auditory cortices. The selective 5-HT2A antagonist MDL 11939 (2 mg/kg) reduced both HTR responses and hallucination-associated cortical activity by 68% compared to vehicle controls. Chronic MDL 11939 treatment over 14 days decreased SP episode frequency from 4.2±0.8 to 1.6±0.4 episodes per week in a clinical pilot study of 24 recurrent SP patients. These findings demonstrate that 5-HT2A receptor hyperactivation contributes to SP hallucinations and suggest that selective 5-HT2A antagonists may represent a promising therapeutic approach for managing this distressing parasomnia.

title: Measurement of kinetically resolved vesicular dopamine uptake and efflux using rotating disk electrode voltammetry.
abstract: The measurement of vesicular dopamine dynamics has been limited by the temporal resolution of existing techniques, hampering our understanding of presynaptic mechanisms underlying dopaminergic neurotransmission. We developed a novel approach using rotating disk electrode voltammetry (RDEV) to achieve kinetically resolved measurements of vesicular dopamine uptake and efflux in isolated synaptic vesicle preparations from rat striatum. Carbon fiber microelectrodes were incorporated into a rotating disk configuration and operated at 2000 rpm to enhance mass transport, enabling detection of rapid concentration changes with sub-second temporal resolution. Vesicular preparations were incubated with varying dopamine concentrations (1-100 μM) in the presence and absence of reserpine (10 μM) or bafilomycin A1 (100 nM) to pharmacologically characterize vesicular monoamine transporter 2 (VMAT2) activity. RDEV measurements revealed biphasic dopamine uptake kinetics with a rapid initial phase (t1/2 = 2.3 ± 0.4 s) followed by a slower equilibration phase (t1/2 = 18.7 ± 3.2 s), consistent with VMAT2-mediated transport. Reserpine completely abolished uptake, while bafilomycin A1 reduced uptake by 89 ± 7%. Efflux measurements using weak bases (NH4Cl, methylamine) demonstrated rapid dopamine release with kinetics matching the collapse of vesicular pH gradients. The technique achieved detection limits of 50 nM dopamine with signal-to-noise ratios exceeding 20:1. This RDEV approach provides unprecedented temporal resolution for studying vesicular dopamine handling and offers a valuable tool for investigating presynaptic dysfunction in neurological disorders.

title: A survey of predoctoral dental basic pharmacology education.
abstract: A survey of fifty-one of the fifty-three dental schools in the continental United States provided information about pharmacology curriculum content and time allocation. Most dental schools offer a traditional didactic course in basic pharmacology, with about 50 percent of the medical school-based (MSB) and 75 percent of the dental school-based (DSB) programs providing additional pharmacology material in other basic and clinical courses. The four combined medical-dental (M-D) student courses have more hours of instruction, clinical conferences and reviews, and number of instructors than either the dental school- or medical school-based courses. DSB and MSB pharmacology courses were similar in most regards, with a relatively low compliance with the current curriculum guidelines. The DSB and MSB courses provided more time for neuropharmacology and less time for agents related to various organ systems than the M-D courses. There is considerable potential to improve pharmacology instruction by expanding the inclusion of pharmacology in other courses, increasing the number of clinical conferences and discussions, and offering problem-based-learning sessions.

title: Future neuroprotective strategies.
abstract: Accumulating impairment in patients suffering from multiple sclerosis (MS) is the result of increasing axonal damage and neurodegeneration. Current therapeutic strategies only target the inflammatory side of this disease. Convincing effects of all established treatment options in chronic progressive disease stages are lacking. In recent years much progress has been made unraveling the molecular mechanisms involved in neuronal and axonal loss in this neuroinflammatory disease. Understanding the mechanisms of neurodegeneration in MS and its animal models is essential to select therapeutic targets which are most likely to reduce long-term disabilities in patients. In this article we review the current knowledge of possible strategies for future neuroprotective therapies for MS patients and point out which treatment options are currently being evaluated in clinical trials.

title: Cannabinoids: reward, dependence, and underlying neurochemical mechanisms--a review of recent preclinical data.
abstract: Starting with the discovery of an endogenous brain cannabinoid system with specific receptors and endogenous ligands, research in the cannabinoid field has accelerated dramatically over the last 15 years. Cannabis is the most used illicit psychotropic substance in the world but only recently have reliable preclinical models become available for investigating the rewarding and dependence-producing actions of its psychoactive constituent, delta9-tetrahydrocannabinol (THC). The goal of this review is to examine the various animal models currently available that are being used to facilitate our understanding of the rewarding and dependence-producing actions of cannabinoids, which are central to their abuse liability, and of the neurochemical mechanisms that may underlie these actions of cannabinoids. Recent demonstrations that strong and persistent intravenous self-administration behavior can be obtained in squirrel monkeys using a range of THC doses that are in agreement with the total intake and the single doses of THC normally self-administered by humans smoking marijuana cigarettes provides a reliable and direct tool for assessing the reinforcing effects of THC that are central to its abuse liability. In addition, recent demonstrations of persistent intravenous self-administration of synthetic cannabinoid CB1 receptor agonists by rats and mice and the development of genetically modified mice lacking specific cannabinoid receptors provide convenient rodent models for exploring underlying neurochemical mechanisms. Repeated demonstrations in rats that THC and synthetic CB1 agonists can induce conditioned place preferences or aversions, depending on details of dose and spacing, can reduce the threshold for intracranial self-stimulation behavior under certain conditions, and can serve as effective discriminative stimuli for operant behavior provide less direct, but more rapidly established, measures for investigating the rewarding effects of cannabinoids. Finally, there have been numerous recent reports of major functional interactions between endogenous cannabinoid, opioid, and dopaminergic neurotransmitter systems in areas such as analgesia, physical dependence and tolerance development, and drug reinforcement or reward. This provides an opportunity to search for drugs with the beneficial therapeutic effects of currently available cannabinoids or opioids but without undesirable adverse effects such as abuse liability.

title: Psychopharmacological profile of the alkaloid psychollatine as a 5HT2A/C serotonin modulator.
abstract: The naturally occurring indole alkaloid psychollatine, isolated from the Amazonian plant Psychotria psychollatrensis, has been traditionally used by indigenous communities for its psychoactive properties. Recent ethnobotanical surveys have identified this compound as a potential modulator of serotonergic neurotransmission, yet its specific receptor binding profile and behavioral effects remain uncharacterized. We investigated the in vitro binding affinity and functional activity of psychollatine at human 5-HT2A and 5-HT2C receptors expressed in HEK-293 cells, along with its behavioral effects in rodent models. Radioligand binding assays revealed that psychollatine exhibits high affinity for 5-HT2A receptors (Ki = 12.3 ± 2.1 nM) and moderate affinity for 5-HT2C receptors (Ki = 89.4 ± 8.7 nM), with minimal binding to other serotonin receptor subtypes. Functional calcium mobilization assays demonstrated that psychollatine acts as a partial agonist at 5-HT2A receptors (EC50 = 24.8 nM, Emax = 67% of serotonin) and as a weak partial agonist at 5-HT2C receptors (EC50 = 156 nM, Emax = 34% of serotonin). In vivo studies showed that psychollatine (0.5-2.0 mg/kg, i.p.) dose-dependently induced head-twitch responses in mice, an effect completely blocked by the selective 5-HT2A antagonist MDL 100907. The compound also modulated prepulse inhibition and locomotor activity in a manner consistent with 5-HT2A/2C activation. These findings establish psychollatine as a novel naturally occurring 5-HT2A/2C receptor modulator with potential applications in understanding serotonergic neurotransmission and developing new therapeutic agents.

title: Automated analysis of antidepressants' effect in the forced swim test.
abstract: The forced swim test (FST) is a widely used behavioral paradigm for screening antidepressant compounds, traditionally relying on manual scoring of immobility time by trained observers. However, this approach is labor-intensive, subject to inter-rater variability, and may introduce bias in drug efficacy assessments. We developed and validated an automated video-tracking system for quantifying behavioral parameters in the FST using adult male Sprague-Dawley rats (n=96). Animals were treated with vehicle, imipramine (10, 20, 30 mg/kg), fluoxetine (5, 10, 20 mg/kg), or bupropion (10, 20, 40 mg/kg) 1 hour prior to testing. Our system utilized high-resolution digital cameras and custom software algorithms to detect pixel changes corresponding to swimming, climbing, and immobility behaviors during the 6-minute test session. Manual scoring by two independent observers served as the validation standard. The automated system demonstrated excellent correlation with manual scoring for immobility time (r=0.94, p<0.001) and significantly reduced analysis time from 45 minutes to 3 minutes per session. All tested antidepressants showed dose-dependent reductions in immobility time, with bupropion and imipramine additionally increasing climbing behavior, while fluoxetine predominantly enhanced swimming activity. Inter-session reliability was superior for automated analysis (ICC=0.97) compared to manual scoring (ICC=0.83). This automated approach provides a standardized, objective, and efficient method for FST analysis, reducing variability and improving throughput in antidepressant screening protocols while maintaining sensitivity to drug-specific behavioral profiles.

title: [Therapeutic drug monitoring in neuropsychopharmacology : Summary of the consensus guidelines 2017 of the TDM task force of the AGNP].
abstract: Therapeutic drug monitoring (TDM) is the quantification and interpretation of drug concentrations in blood serum or plasma to optimize pharmacological therapy. TDM is an instrument with which the high interindividual variability of pharmacokinetics of patients can be identified and therefore enables a personalized pharmacotherapy. In September 2017 the TDM task force of the Working Group for Neuropsychopharmacology and Pharmacopsychiatry (AGNP) published an update of the consensus guidelines on TDM published in 2011. This article summarizes the essential statements for the clinical practice in psychiatry and neurology.

title: Post-traumatic stress disorder: the neurobiological impact of psychological trauma.
abstract: The classic fight-or-flight response to perceived threat is a reflexive nervous phenomenon thai has obvious survival advantages in evolutionary terms. However, the systems that organize the constellation of reflexive survival behaviors following exposure to perceived threat can under some circumstances become dysregulated in the process. Chronic dysregulation of these systems can lead to functional impairment in certain individuals who become "psychologically traumatized" and suffer from post-traumatic stress disorder (PTSD), A body of data accumulated over several decades has demonstrated neurobiological abnormalities in PTSD patients. Some of these findings offer insight into the pathophysiology of PTSD as well as the biological vulnerability of certain populations to develop PTSD, Several pathological features found in PTSD patients overlap with features found in patients with traumatic brain injury paralleling the shared signs and symptoms of these clinical syndromes. La clâsica respuesta de ataque o huida ante la perceptión de una amenaza es un fenómeno nervioso reflejo que, obviamente en términos evolutivos, tiene ventajas para la supervivencia. Sin embargo, los sistemas que organizan la constelación de conductas reflejas de supervivencia que siguen a la exposición a la amenaza percibida en algunas circunstancias pueden constituirse en procesos mal regulados. La mala regulatión crónica de estos sistemas puede llevar a un deterioro funcional en ciertos individuos quienes pueden convertirse en “traumatizados psicológicamente” y presentar un trastorno por estrés postraumático (TEPT), Una gran cantidad de informatión acumulada en varias décadas ha demostrado alteraciones neurobiológicas en los patientes con TEPT, Algunos de estos hallazgos permiten adentrarse en la fisiopatologia asi como en la vulnerabilidad biológica de ciertas poblaciones que van a desarrollar un TEPT Algunas caracteristicas patológicas encontradas en patientes con TEPT se sobreponen con caracteristicas de patientes con daño cerebral traumático, estableciendo un paralelo de signos y sintomas compartidos entre estos sindromes clinicos. La réponse classique de lutte ou de fuite à une menace perçue est un phénomène nerveux réflexe dont les avantages pour la survie sont évidents en termes d'évolution. Cependant, les systèmes organisés en constellation de comportements réflexes de survie après exposition à une menace perçue peuvent se déréguler dans certaines circonstances. Une dysregulation chronique de ces systèmes peut entraîner un déficit fonctionnel chez certains sujets qui deviennent « psychologiquement traumatisés » ef souffrent de l'état de stress posi-traumatique (ESPT), Des données recueillies pendant des dizaines d'années montrent des anomalies neurobiologiques chez les patients souffrant d'ESPT, ce qui permet de mieux comprendre la physiopathologie de l'ESPT ainsi que la vulnérabilité biologique de certaines populations à développer un ESPT, Certaines caractéristiques pathologiques de l'ESPT se superposent à celles trouvées chez des patients atteints de lésion cérébrale traumatique, en parallèle avec les signes et les symptômes partagés par ces deux syndromes.

title: Dopamine production in Enterococcus faecium: A microbial endocrinology-based mechanism for the selection of probiotics based on neurochemical-producing potential.
abstract: The mechanisms by which probiotics may influence host physiology are still incompletely understood. Microbial endocrinology, a field representing the union of microbiology, endocrinology and neurobiology, has theorized that microorganisms have the capacity to serve as neurochemical delivery vehicles [1]. According to microbial endocrinology, neurochemicals can serve as a common language between host and bacterium, enabling bidirectional communication. We report herein the first demonstration that Enterococcus sp. has the capacity to produce dopamine in a gastrointestinal-like environment when supplied with the dopamine precursor L-3,4 dihydroxyphenylalanine (L-dopa). The results presented herein provide a means to select probiotics based on neurochemical-producing potential and suggest the possibility that probiotics containing E. faecium may serve to influence the host through dopaminergic pathways.

title: Effects of dopamine D2/D3 receptor antagonism on human planning and spatial working memory.
abstract: Dopaminergic signaling in the prefrontal cortex and striatum plays a crucial role in executive functions, yet the specific contribution of D2/D3 receptors to higher-order cognitive processes remains poorly understood. This study investigated the effects of selective D2/D3 receptor antagonism on planning abilities and spatial working memory in healthy volunteers. We conducted a randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled crossover study with 24 healthy adults (mean age 24.3±3.1 years, 12 female). Participants received either sulpiride (400mg) or placebo and completed the Tower of London task to assess planning performance and a spatial n-back task (1-back, 2-back, 3-back) to evaluate working memory capacity. Cognitive testing occurred 2-3 hours post-administration during peak plasma concentrations. Compared to placebo, sulpiride significantly impaired planning performance, increasing both solution time (p=0.012) and number of moves required (p=0.031) on complex Tower of London problems. Spatial working memory showed a differential pattern, with sulpiride selectively impairing 3-back performance accuracy (p=0.018) while leaving 1-back and 2-back performance intact. Reaction times were prolonged across all working memory conditions under sulpiride (p=0.007). These findings demonstrate that D2/D3 receptors are critical for optimal planning and high-demand spatial working memory in humans. The selective impairment of complex cognitive operations suggests that D2/D3 antagonism disrupts prefrontal-striatal circuits specifically when cognitive load is high, providing insight into the neurochemical mechanisms underlying executive dysfunction in psychiatric disorders characterized by altered dopaminergic transmission.

title: "Novel Psychopharmacology for Depressive Disorders".
abstract: Depression affects over 300 million individuals worldwide, yet current antidepressant therapies demonstrate limited efficacy and significant latency to therapeutic response. Traditional monoaminergic approaches fail in approximately 30-40% of patients, necessitating exploration of novel pharmacological targets. This study investigated the therapeutic potential of selective modulation of AMPA receptor positive allosteric modulators (AMPAkines) combined with low-dose ketamine in treatment-resistant depression models. Male Sprague-Dawley rats (n=48) were subjected to chronic unpredictable mild stress (CUMS) for 6 weeks to induce depression-like behaviors. Animals were randomized into four groups: vehicle control, ketamine alone (2.5 mg/kg), AMPAkine CX717 alone (10 mg/kg), and combination therapy. Behavioral assessments included forced swim test, sucrose preference test, and elevated plus maze. Neurochemical analyses measured hippocampal BDNF, synaptic plasticity markers, and glutamate receptor expression via Western blot and RT-PCR. Combination therapy significantly reduced immobility time in forced swim test (p<0.001) and restored sucrose preference (p<0.01) compared to monotherapies. Hippocampal BDNF expression increased 2.3-fold with combination treatment, while GluA1 and PSD-95 protein levels were significantly elevated. Synergistic effects were observed in long-term potentiation enhancement and dendritic spine density restoration. The combination demonstrated rapid-acting antidepressant properties with sustained effects lasting 14 days post-treatment. These findings suggest that AMPAkine-ketamine combination therapy represents a promising novel approach for treatment-resistant depression, potentially offering superior efficacy through complementary glutamatergic mechanisms promoting synaptic plasticity and neuroplasticity.
journal: Advances in experimental medicine and biology

title: Ketamine modulates hippocampal neurochemistry and functional connectivity: a combined magnetic resonance spectroscopy and resting-state fMRI study in healthy volunteers.
abstract: A growing body of evidence suggests glutamate excess in schizophrenia and that N-methyl-d-aspartate receptor (NMDAR) hypofunction on γ-aminobutyric acid (GABA) interneurons disinhibiting pyramidal cells may be relevant to this hyperglutamatergic state. To better understand how NMDAR hypofunction affects the brain, we used magnetic resonance spectroscopy and resting-state functional magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) to study the effects of ketamine on hippocampal neurometabolite levels and functional connectivity in 15 healthy human subjects. We observed a ketamine-induced increase in hippocampal Glx (glutamate+glutamine; F=3.76; P=0.04), a decrease in fronto-temporal (t=4.92, PFDR<0.05, kE=2198, x=-30, y=52, z=14) and temporo-parietal functional connectivity (t=5.07, PFDR<0.05, kE=6094, x=-28, y=-36, z=-2), and a possible link between connectivity changes and elevated Glx. Our data empirically support that hippocampal glutamatergic elevation and resting-state network alterations may arise from NMDAR hypofunction and establish a proof of principle whereby experimental modelling of a disorder can help mechanistically integrate distinct neuroimaging abnormalities in schizophrenia.

title: Translational neuropharmacology and the appropriate and effective use of animal models.
abstract: This issue of the British Journal of Pharmacology is dedicated to reviews of the major animal models used in neuropharmacology to examine drugs for both neurological and psychiatric conditions. Almost all major conditions are reviewed. In general, regulatory authorities require evidence for the efficacy of novel compounds in appropriate animal models. However, the failure of many compounds in clinical trials following clear demonstration of efficacy in animal models has called into question both the value of the models and the discovery process in general. These matters are expertly reviewed in this issue and proposals for better models outlined. In this editorial, we further suggest that more attention be made to incorporate pharmacokinetic knowledge into the studies (quantitative pharmacology). We also suggest that more attention be made to ensure that full methodological details are published and recommend that journals should be more amenable to publishing negative data. Finally, we propose that new approaches must be used in drug discovery so that preclinical studies become more reflective of the clinical situation, and studies using animal models mimic the anticipated design of studies to be performed in humans, as closely as possible.

title: Psychopharmacology of tic disorders in children and adolescents.
abstract: Tic disorders, including Tourette syndrome, affect 0.5-3% of children and adolescents, causing significant functional impairment and psychosocial distress. Current pharmacological interventions demonstrate variable efficacy and tolerability profiles, necessitating evidence-based treatment guidelines. This comprehensive review synthesizes data from randomized controlled trials, open-label studies, and case series published between 1990-2010 examining psychopharmacological treatments for pediatric tic disorders. We systematically analyzed 47 studies encompassing 2,847 patients aged 6-18 years with chronic tic disorders or Tourette syndrome. Traditional antipsychotics (haloperidol, pimozide) demonstrated robust tic reduction (40-60% improvement) but showed high rates of extrapyramidal side effects (28-45%) and cognitive dulling. Atypical antipsychotics, particularly risperidone and aripiprazole, exhibited comparable efficacy (35-55% tic reduction) with improved tolerability profiles, though metabolic side effects remained concerning. Alpha-2 agonists (clonidine, guanfacine) provided moderate tic improvement (25-40%) with favorable side effect profiles, particularly beneficial for patients with comorbid ADHD. Emerging treatments including topiramate and botulinum toxin injections showed promise in preliminary studies but require further investigation. Treatment selection should consider tic severity, functional impairment, comorbid conditions, and individual risk-benefit profiles. Non-pharmacological interventions including behavioral therapy warrant consideration as first-line treatments for mild-moderate tics. Future research should focus on personalized treatment approaches, long-term safety data, and novel therapeutic targets to optimize outcomes while minimizing adverse effects in this vulnerable population.

title: Endocannabinoids and the neurochemistry of gluttony.
abstract: The endocannabinoid system has emerged as a critical mediator of feeding behavior and energy homeostasis, yet its role in pathological overeating remains poorly understood. We investigated the neurochemical mechanisms underlying cannabinoid-induced hyperphagia in the hypothalamic feeding circuits of adult male Wistar rats (n=48). Animals received bilateral microinjections of the CB1 receptor agonist WIN55,212-2 (0.1-1.0 μg) or vehicle into the paraventricular nucleus (PVN) or lateral hypothalamus (LH) during scheduled feeding periods. Food intake, meal microstructure, and tissue levels of anandamide (AEA) and 2-arachidonoylglycerol (2-AG) were measured using HPLC-MS/MS. Immunohistochemistry and in situ hybridization examined neuropeptide Y (NPY), agouti-related peptide (AgRP), and proopiomelanocortin (POMC) expression. WIN55,212-2 administration in the LH, but not PVN, significantly increased 24-hour food intake (vehicle: 24.3±1.8g vs. high dose: 41.7±3.2g, p<0.001) and meal frequency without affecting meal size. This hyperphagic response was blocked by the CB1 antagonist rimonabant. Hypothalamic AEA levels were elevated 3-fold in food-deprived animals compared to ad libitum fed controls (p<0.01), while 2-AG remained unchanged. CB1 activation upregulated NPY and AgRP mRNA expression in the arcuate nucleus by 180% and 220% respectively, while suppressing POMC expression by 60% (all p<0.05). These findings demonstrate that endocannabinoids promote excessive food intake through CB1-mediated disinhibition of orexigenic pathways in the lateral hypothalamus, suggesting therapeutic targets for treating pathological overeating disorders.

title: Clinical practice with anti-dementia drugs: A revised (third) consensus statement from the British Association for Psychopharmacology.
abstract: The British Association for Psychopharmacology convened an expert panel to update evidence-based recommendations for anti-dementia drug prescribing, following significant developments in clinical practice since the previous 2011 consensus. A multidisciplinary group of clinicians and researchers systematically reviewed recent literature on cholinesterase inhibitors (donepezil, rivastigmine, galantamine) and memantine, alongside emerging evidence on combination therapies and long-term treatment strategies. The panel evaluated randomized controlled trials, real-world effectiveness studies, and safety data published between 2011-2017, with particular attention to patient selection criteria, treatment optimization, and discontinuation practices. Updated recommendations emphasize earlier initiation of cholinesterase inhibitors in mild-to-moderate Alzheimer's disease, with donepezil remaining first-line treatment due to superior tolerability and adherence. Memantine should be considered as add-on therapy in moderate disease or as monotherapy when cholinesterase inhibitors are contraindicated. New evidence supports continuing treatment into severe dementia stages, challenging previous recommendations for discontinuation. The panel identified improved cognitive and functional outcomes with optimized dosing regimens and highlighted the importance of regular monitoring for therapeutic response and adverse effects. Combination therapy with cholinesterase inhibitors plus memantine showed modest additional benefits in moderate-to-severe disease. Despite limited disease-modifying effects, current anti-dementia drugs provide clinically meaningful symptomatic benefits when appropriately prescribed. These revised guidelines emphasize individualized treatment approaches, incorporating patient preferences and comorbidities, while acknowledging the continued need for novel therapeutic approaches targeting underlying disease mechanisms in dementia.

title: 1-Methyl-1,2,3,4-tetrahydroisoquinoline antagonizes a rise in brain dopamine metabolism, glutamate release in frontal cortex and locomotor hyperactivity produced by MK-801 but not the disruptions of prepulse inhibition, and impairment of working memory in rat.
abstract: N-methyl-D-aspartate (NMDA) receptor hypofunction has been implicated in the pathophysiology of schizophrenia, with NMDA receptor antagonists like MK-801 producing behavioral and neurochemical alterations resembling aspects of this disorder. 1-Methyl-1,2,3,4-tetrahydroisoquinoline (1MeTIQ), an endogenous compound with neuroprotective properties, has shown potential modulatory effects on dopaminergic neurotransmission. This study investigated whether 1MeTIQ could attenuate MK-801-induced neurochemical and behavioral changes in male Wistar rats. Animals received pretreatment with 1MeTIQ (25 or 50 mg/kg, i.p.) 30 minutes before MK-801 administration (0.3 mg/kg, i.p.). Dopamine metabolism was assessed by measuring DOPAC and HVA levels in striatum and frontal cortex using HPLC. Glutamate release in frontal cortex was monitored by in vivo microdialysis. Behavioral assessments included locomotor activity, prepulse inhibition (PPI) of acoustic startle, and spatial working memory using the Y-maze test. MK-801 significantly increased locomotor activity, dopamine turnover, and cortical glutamate efflux while disrupting PPI and impairing working memory performance. Pretreatment with 1MeTIQ dose-dependently prevented MK-801-induced hyperlocomotion, normalized dopamine metabolism, and blocked excessive glutamate release in frontal cortex. However, 1MeTIQ failed to reverse MK-801-induced PPI deficits or working memory impairment. These findings suggest that 1MeTIQ selectively modulates certain aspects of NMDA receptor hypofunction, particularly those involving dopaminergic hyperactivity and glutamatergic dysfunction, while cognitive and sensorimotor gating abnormalities remain unaffected. The partial protective profile of 1MeTIQ indicates distinct neurobiological mechanisms underlying different schizophrenia-like phenotypes induced by NMDA receptor

title: The footfault test as a screening tool in the 6-hydroxydopamine rat model of Parkinson's disease.
abstract: The 6-hydroxydopamine (6-OHDA) rat model is widely used to study Parkinson's disease (PD), but current behavioral assessments often lack sensitivity to detect mild-to-moderate dopaminergic deficits. The footfault test, which measures limb placement errors during grid walking, may provide a more sensitive screening tool for motor impairments in this model. We evaluated the footfault test as a behavioral screening method in unilaterally 6-OHDA-lesioned rats and compared its sensitivity to established tests including cylinder test and amphetamine-induced rotations. Adult male Sprague-Dawley rats (n=24) received unilateral 6-OHDA injections into the medial forebrain bundle or sham surgery. Behavioral testing was performed at 2, 4, and 6 weeks post-lesion using the footfault test (errors per 50 steps on wire grid), cylinder test (forelimb asymmetry), and amphetamine-induced rotations. Tyrosine hydroxylase immunohistochemistry confirmed lesion extent in the substantia nigra and striatum. The footfault test detected significant contralateral forelimb deficits as early as 2 weeks post-lesion in 6-OHDA rats compared to controls (p<0.01), with deficits correlating strongly with striatal dopamine depletion (r=0.78, p<0.001). Footfault sensitivity exceeded that of the cylinder test, detecting impairments in rats with 40-60% striatal dopamine loss, while cylinder test required >70% depletion. Amphetamine rotations showed comparable sensitivity but greater variability. The footfault test provides a simple, sensitive, and quantitative behavioral screening tool for detecting motor deficits in the 6-OHDA rat model, particularly useful for assessing mild-to-moderate dopaminergic lesions that may be missed by conventional tests.

title: Mercury, selenium and neurochemical biomarkers in different brain regions of migrating common loons from Lake Erie, Canada.
abstract: Common loons (Gavia immer) can be exposed to relatively high levels of dietary methylmercury (MeHg) through fish consumption, and several studies have documented MeHg-associated health effects in this species. To further study the neurological risks of MeHg accumulation, migrating loons dying of Type E botulism were collected opportunistically from the Lake Erie shore at Long Point (Ontario, Canada) and relationships between total mercury (THg), selenium (Se), and selected neurochemical receptors and brain enzymes were investigated. THg concentrations were 1-78 μg/g in liver; and 0.3-4 μg/g in the brain (all concentrations reported on a dry weight basis). A significant (p < 0.05) positive correlation was found between THg in liver and THg in 3 subregions of the brain (cerebral cortex: r = 0.433; cerebellum: r = 0.293; brain stem: r = 0.405). THg varied significantly among different brain regions, with the cortex having the highest concentrations. Se levels in the cortex and cerebellum were 1-29 and 1-10 μg/g, respectively, with no significant differences between regions. Se was not measured in brain stem due to insufficient tissue mass. There were molar excesses of Se over mercury (Hg) in both cortex and cerebellum at all Hg concentrations, and a significant positive relationship between THg and the Hg:Se molar ratio (cortex: r = 0.63; cerebellum: r = 0.47). No significant associations were observed between brain THg and the N-methyl-D-aspartic acid (NMDA) receptor concentration, nor between THg and muscarinic cholinergic (mACh) receptor concentration; however, brain THg levels were lower than in previous studies that reported significant Hg-associated changes in neuroreceptor densities. Together with previous studies, the current findings add to our understanding of Hg distribution in the brain of common loons, and the associations between Hg and sub-lethal neurochemical changes in fish-eating wildlife.

title: Methylmercury egg injections: part 2--pathology, neurochemistry, and behavior in the avian embryo and hatchling.
abstract: Methylmercury (MeHg) is a toxic metal that has been frequently linked to neurochemical alterations, brain lesions, neurobehavioral changes, and reproductive impairments in wild and captive birds. Much less is known about the effects of MeHg on the developing avian brain and resulting effects on hatchling behavior. The objective of this work was to use air cell injection studies to investigate the effect of in ovo MeHg exposure on brain pathology and four neurochemical biomarkers (N-methyl-d-aspartate (NMDA) receptor, γ-aminobutyric acid (GABA) receptor, glutamine synthetase (GS) and glutamic acid decarboxylase (GAD)) that have previously been studied in wild birds, and on hatchling righting response, balance, and startle response. In a series of six studies, we exposed white leghorn chicken and Japanese quail embryos to methylmercury chloride (MeHgCl) (range: 0-6.4μg/g egg) via egg injection on embryonic day (ED) 0 and measured receptor levels and enzyme activity at different stages of embryonic (days 11, 14, and 19 in chicken; day 15 in quail) and hatchling (day 1 and day 7) development, and in whole brain or discrete brain regions (cerebrum, cerebellum, optic lobe). We assessed neurobehaviors on post hatch (PH) days 1 and 7. Despite accumulating relatively high levels of Hg in the brain, embryos and hatchlings did not consistently display neurochemical changes consistent with those seen in wild birds and laboratory mammals. Hatchlings also did not demonstrate behavioral alterations. Pathology did not indicate a difference in occurrence and types of lesions between control and dosed birds. These findings suggest that in ovo MeHg exposure alone may not be responsible for neurological impacts in bird. This work draws attention to factors, such as age and species, that may influence responses to MeHg in birds.

title: Pharmacological regulation of gene expression.
abstract: The pharmacological regulation of gene expression represents a critical mechanism underlying therapeutic drug action and adverse effects in the central nervous system. While traditional approaches have focused on receptor binding and enzymatic inhibition, emerging evidence suggests that many psychoactive compounds exert their effects through direct modulation of transcriptional machinery. We investigated the capacity of classical neuropharmacological agents to regulate gene expression in primary cortical neurons and assessed the temporal dynamics of these responses. Primary rat cortical neurons were treated with clinically relevant concentrations of fluoxetine (10 μM), haloperidol (1 μM), diazepam (5 μM), or vehicle control for 6, 12, and 24 hours. Gene expression profiles were analyzed using quantitative RT-PCR arrays targeting 84 genes involved in synaptic plasticity, neurotransmitter synthesis, and cellular stress responses. Chromatin immunoprecipitation assays examined drug-induced changes in histone modifications at target gene promoters. Fluoxetine treatment resulted in significant upregulation of BDNF (2.3-fold, p<0.01) and CREB1 (1.8-fold, p<0.05) within 6 hours, accompanied by increased H3K4me3 marks at their promoter regions. Haloperidol induced delayed but sustained changes in dopamine receptor gene expression and immediate early genes. Diazepam primarily affected GABAergic signaling genes with minimal transcriptional effects. Time-course analysis revealed distinct temporal patterns for each compound class. These findings demonstrate that neuropharmacological agents possess intrinsic capacity to reshape neuronal gene expression programs through epigenetic mechanisms, providing novel insights into their therapeutic mechanisms and suggesting new targets for drug development in psychiatric and neurological disorders.

title: Cholinergic effects on fear conditioning II: nicotinic and muscarinic modulations of atropine-induced disruption of the degraded contingency effect.
abstract: In a companion study (Carnicella et al., 2005), we showed that the muscarinic antagonist atropine, when administered after extensive training during both conditioning and testing, affected neither cued nor contextual fear memories when both of them did not compete for the control of the overt behaviour. In contrast, atropine altered the degraded contingency effect (DCE), that is, the processes by which contextual fear memory competes with the cued one for the control of the conditioned response. Atropine-induced disruption of the DCE was fully reversed by the administration of the anticholinesterase inhibitor physostigmine, which suggests a direct cholinergic implication. The present series of experiments was conducted in order to define more precisely the involvement of the cholinergic system in such an effect. Oxotremorine (0.0, 0.0075, 0.015, or 0.03 mg/kg), pilocarpine (0.0, 0.3, 1, or 3 mg/kg), xanomeline (0.0, 2.5, 5.0, 10.0 or 20.0 mg/kg) and nicotine (0.0, 0.1, 0.2, or 0.4 mg/kg) were tested for reversal of the atropine-induced alteration of the DCE. Oxotremorine and pilocarpine did not reverse the atropine-induced alteration of the DCE. In contrast, xanomeline and nicotine reversed the effect of atropine on the DCE. The present series of experiments reveals complex pharmacological interactions within the cholinergic system when cued and contextual fear memories interact. Results are discussed in this connection and with regard to the relation between the properties of cholinergic agonists and their therapeutic values.

title: The future of pharmacotherapy for paediatric anxiety.
abstract: Anxiety disorders represent one of the most prevalent psychiatric conditions in children and adolescents, affecting approximately 10-20% of the pediatric population. Current pharmacological interventions for pediatric anxiety remain limited, with most treatments extrapolated from adult studies despite significant developmental differences in neurotransmitter systems and drug metabolism. This review examines emerging pharmacotherapeutic approaches and evaluates their potential clinical utility in pediatric anxiety management. A comprehensive literature search was conducted using MEDLINE and PsycINFO databases (1990-2002) focusing on novel anxiolytic agents, pediatric-specific clinical trials, and developmental pharmacokinetic studies. Particular attention was given to selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors (SSRIs), novel serotonergic compounds, and targeted neurotransmitter modulators. Current evidence suggests that while traditional SSRIs show promise in pediatric populations, concerns regarding suicidality and long-term developmental effects necessitate careful risk-benefit analysis. Emerging agents targeting specific anxiety circuits, including selective 5-HT1A receptor agonists and GABA-A receptor modulators with improved safety profiles, demonstrate encouraging preliminary results. Novel approaches incorporating pharmacogenomic principles may enable personalized dosing strategies accounting for developmental differences in cytochrome P450 enzyme activity. The future of pediatric anxiety pharmacotherapy lies in developing age-appropriate formulations, conducting rigorous pediatric clinical trials, and establishing evidence-based treatment algorithms. Integration of pharmacological interventions with cognitive-behavioral approaches remains essential. Regulatory initiatives encouraging pediatric drug development and improved understanding of neurodevelopmental processes will be crucial for advancing safe and effective anxiety treatments in children and adolescents.

title: 5-HT in the enteric nervous system: gut function and neuropharmacology.
abstract: In recent times, the perception of functional gastrointestinal disorders such as irritable bowel syndrome (IBS) has shifted fundamentally. Such disorders are now thought of as serious diseases characterized by perturbations in the neuronal regulation of gastrointestinal function. The concept of visceral hypersensitivity, the characterization of neuronal networks in the 'brain-gut axis' and the identification of several novel 5-HT-mediated mechanisms have contributed to this shift. Here, we review how some of the more promising of these new mechanisms (e.g. those involving 5-HT transporters and the 5-HT(2B), 5-HT(7) and putative 5-HT(1p) receptors) might lead to a range of second-generation therapies that could revolutionize the treatment of functional gastrointestinal disorders, particularly IBS.

title: [O. V. Palladin--patriarch of neurochemical science (on the 120th birthday of academician Oleksandr Volodymyrovych Palladin)].
abstract: The paper is dedicated to Academician Oleksandr Volodymyrovych Palladin (1895-1972), a founder of neurochemical school in Ukraine. The problem of biochemistry of the nervous system has been developed in Ukraine since the 20's of the 20th century. Theoretical principles and prospects of development of the basic trends in neurochemistry were stated for the first time in the paper by O. V. Palladin--the first work in biochemistry of the nervous system in Ukraine--"Biochemistry of the Brain and Psychochemistry", published in the journal "Nauka v Ukraine" ("Science in Ukraine") in 1922. Researches in neurochemistry have been carried out systematically since 1925 at the Ukrainian Biochemical Institute founded and headed by O. V. Palladin (O. V. Palladin Institute of Biochemistry, now). During the following period of half a century O.V. Palladin and his disciples made original works in the field of chemical topography of the nervous system, functional neurochemistry, in particular, in the field of protein and nitrous, lipid, nucleic metabolism, transmembrane transport of ions and metabolites, etc. Results of long-term investigations were reported at numerous international physiological and biochemical congresses, All-Union and Ukrainian biochemical conferences, seminars and have acquired the world fame. O. V. Palladin's great deserts in development of biochemistry of the nervous system were recognized and he was proclaimed a "patriarch" of the world neurochemistry at the first congress of the International Neurochemical Society in Strassburg (1967) and awarded a Gold Medal--one of the four delivered at the Second Congress of this Society in Milan (1969).

title: Looking at neurotransmitters in the microscope.
abstract: The visualization of neurotransmitter distribution and dynamics within neural circuits has remained a fundamental challenge in neurobiology, with traditional electrophysiological and biochemical approaches providing limited spatial resolution. Recent advances in fluorescence microscopy and genetically-encoded biosensors have opened new avenues for direct observation of neurotransmitter release and uptake at synaptic terminals. Here we review current methodologies for microscopic detection of major neurotransmitters including glutamate, GABA, dopamine, and acetylcholine in live brain tissue. We examined studies utilizing FM dyes for vesicle tracking, pH-sensitive indicators for exocytosis detection, and newly developed FRET-based sensors that provide real-time measurements of extracellular neurotransmitter concentrations with submicron spatial resolution. Two-photon microscopy has enabled imaging depths of up to 300 μm in acute brain slices, while confocal approaches have achieved temporal resolution below 100 milliseconds for synaptic events. Quantitative analysis reveals that these optical methods can detect neurotransmitter changes in the nanomolar to micromolar range, comparable to physiologically relevant concentrations. Correlation with patch-clamp recordings demonstrates high fidelity between optical signals and synaptic currents (r > 0.85). These microscopic approaches have revealed previously unappreciated heterogeneity in neurotransmitter release probability across individual synapses and have identified novel patterns of extrasynaptic spillover. The integration of optical neurotransmitter detection with cellular resolution represents a paradigm shift toward understanding chemical transmission in intact neural networks, providing unprecedented insights into synaptic plasticity mechanisms and neurotransmitter dysfunction in neurological disorders.

title: An Update on the Neurochemistry of Essential Tremor.
abstract: Essential tremor (ET) is the most prevalent movement disorder, affecting approximately 1% of the global population, yet its underlying neurochemical mechanisms remain incompletely understood. This review synthesizes current evidence regarding neurotransmitter systems implicated in ET pathophysiology, with particular focus on recent advances in neurochemical research from 2015-2020. We conducted a comprehensive literature review of preclinical and clinical studies examining neurotransmitter dysfunction in ET, including pharmacological, neuroimaging, and post-mortem analyses. Emerging evidence suggests that ET involves complex interactions between multiple neurotransmitter systems beyond the traditionally implicated GABAergic pathways. Recent studies demonstrate altered cerebellar GABA transmission, with reduced GABA-A receptor density in Purkinje cells and decreased GAD67 expression in ET patients. Additionally, dopaminergic dysfunction has been increasingly recognized, with PET imaging revealing reduced striatal dopamine transporter binding and altered D2 receptor availability in ET subjects compared to controls. Noradrenergic system abnormalities, including elevated plasma norepinephrine levels and altered α2-adrenoreceptor sensitivity, further contribute to tremor generation. Serotonergic involvement is supported by the therapeutic efficacy of certain SSRIs and altered 5-HT receptor binding in tremor-related brain regions. Glutamatergic hyperactivity in cerebellar-thalamic circuits has also been implicated through imaging and pharmacological studies. These findings reveal ET as a multisystem neurochemical disorder involving dysregulated inhibitory and excitatory neurotransmission across cerebellar-thalamic-cortical networks. Understanding these complex neurochemical interactions provides new therapeutic targets and may explain the heterogeneous clinical presentation and variable treatment responses observed in ET patients.

title: Optimized [1-(13)C]glucose infusion protocol for 13C magnetic resonance spectroscopy at 3T of human brain glucose metabolism under euglycemic and hypoglycemic conditions.
abstract: The effect of insulin-induced hypoglycemia on cerebral glucose metabolism is largely unknown. (13)C MRS is a unique tool to study cerebral glucose metabolism, but the concurrent requirement for [1-(13)C]glucose administration limits its use under hypoglycemic conditions. To facilitate (13)C MRS data analysis we designed separate [1-(13)C]glucose infusion protocols for hyperinsulinemic euglycemic and hypoglycemic clamps in such a way that plasma isotopic enrichment of glucose was stable and comparable under both glycemic conditions. (13)C MR spectra were acquired with optimized (13)C MRS measurement techniques to obtain high quality (13)C MR spectra with these protocols.
authors: van de Ven, Kim C C

title: Clinical neurochemistry: developments in dementia research based on brain bank material.
abstract: Brain tissue obtained at autopsy continues to provide unique opportunities in current dementia research. Not only is tissue analysis still essential for diagnosis, but investigation of neurochemical pathology, at a level of resolution beyond current in vivo imaging, continues to provide new insights into the involvement of neurotransmitter signalling systems. These are relevant to therapy which, with respect to symptoms such as cognitive impairment, psychosis and depression, is currently targeted to specific transmitter (cholinergic, dopaminergic and serotonergic) systems. This paper focuses on dopaminergic, cholinergic and histaminergic parameters in Alzheimer's disease (AD), Dementia with Lewy bodies (DLB) and Parkinson's disease (PD). In the normal striatum the dopamine transporter and D2 receptor exhibit distinct rostral-caudal distributions and D2 binding is affected by genetic polymorphism at the Taq 1A locus. The transporter is reduced in both DLB and PD but not AD, correlating with severity of extrapyramidal dysfunction, and receptor abnormalities are apparent in DLB patients responding adversely to neuroleptics. Striatal nicotine receptors are lost in all 3 disorders, further reduced as a result of neuroleptic medication, and elevated as a result of tobacco use. In the thalamus there are selective reductions in presynaptic cholinergic activity in DLB in the reticular nucleus which relate to symptoms of hallucinations and fluctuating consciousness prevalent in this disorder. In the hippocampus coupling of muscarinic M1 receptors, relevant to response to cholinergic therapy, is impaired in areas most affected by beta-amyloid plaques and intact in less affected areas. Analysis of histamine H2 receptors indicates that, despite presynaptic histamine abnormalities in AD, receptor numbers are normal. Such clinically and therapeutically relevant observations on human brain neurochemistry provide a basis for improving therapeutic strategies and prospects of diagnostic in vivo chemical imaging.

title: Alotamide A, a novel neuropharmacological agent from the marine cyanobacterium Lyngbya bouillonii.
abstract: Marine cyanobacteria have emerged as prolific sources of structurally diverse bioactive compounds with significant therapeutic potential. During our ongoing investigation of secondary metabolites from tropical marine cyanobacteria, we isolated a novel alkaloid, alotamide A, from cultured Lyngbya bouillonii collected from Hawaiian waters. The planar structure of alotamide A was elucidated through extensive spectroscopic analysis including 1D and 2D NMR, HRMS, and IR spectroscopy, revealing a unique indole-containing framework with an unprecedented cyclic peptide moiety. The absolute configuration was determined by advanced Mosher's method and confirmed by total synthesis. Alotamide A exhibited potent and selective antagonistic activity at NMDA receptors in primary rat cortical neurons with an IC₅₀ of 2.3 μM, demonstrating 15-fold selectivity over AMPA and kainate receptors. Electrophysiological patch-clamp studies revealed that alotamide A functions as a non-competitive NMDA receptor antagonist, likely binding to the channel pore region. In vivo studies using the mouse forced swim test showed significant antidepressant-like effects at 10 mg/kg (i.p.), comparable to ketamine treatment. Importantly, alotamide A displayed minimal psychotomimetic side effects and improved safety profile compared to conventional NMDA antagonists. Molecular docking studies suggested binding interactions with the GluN2B subunit, consistent with observed subunit selectivity. These findings establish alotamide A as a promising lead compound for developing novel antidepressant therapeutics targeting the glutamatergic system, while expanding the chemical diversity of marine-derived NMDA receptor modulators.

title: Bibliometric analysis of biomedical publications on SSRI during 1980-2000.
abstract: Selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors (SSRIs) have emerged as first-line pharmacotherapy for depression and anxiety disorders since the introduction of fluoxetine in the late 1980s. However, a comprehensive analysis of the scientific literature documenting SSRI research development has not been conducted. This bibliometric study examined patterns of biomedical publications on SSRIs from 1980 to 2000 to characterize research trends and identify key areas of investigation. We searched MEDLINE and PsycINFO databases using standardized search terms for fluoxetine, sertraline, paroxetine, fluvoxamine, and citalopram. Publications were categorized by year, journal, study type, therapeutic indication, and research focus. A total of 4,267 SSRI-related publications were identified, with exponential growth from 12 papers in 1980 to 587 in 2000. The greatest publication volume occurred during 1995-1998, coinciding with widespread clinical adoption. Major depression represented 42% of therapeutic focus, followed by obsessive-compulsive disorder (18%) and panic disorder (14%). Clinical trials comprised 35% of publications, while basic pharmacology and mechanism studies accounted for 28%. The Journal of Clinical Psychiatry, Archives of General Psychiatry, and Biological Psychiatry published the highest number of SSRI studies. International collaboration increased significantly after 1992, with European contributions rising from 15% to 38% by 2000. This analysis demonstrates the rapid evolution of SSRI research parallel to clinical implementation, with sustained focus on efficacy, safety, and mechanism of action studies that established the foundation for evidence-based psychopharmacology.

title: Pharmacotherapy in children and adolescents with pervasive developmental disorders.
abstract: Pervasive developmental disorders (PDDs), including autistic disorder, Asperger's disorder, and pervasive developmental disorder not otherwise specified, affect approximately 0.2-0.6% of children and present significant challenges in behavioral management and therapeutic intervention. While no medications are specifically approved for core symptoms of PDDs, various psychotropic agents have shown promise in managing associated behavioral disturbances. This comprehensive review examines the current literature on pharmacological interventions for children and adolescents with PDDs from 1990 to 1999, analyzing efficacy and safety profiles of commonly prescribed medications. Systematic analysis of 47 published studies, including 12 controlled trials and 35 open-label studies, encompassing 892 patients aged 2-18 years with diagnosed PDDs was conducted. Primary outcome measures included reduction in stereotypic behaviors, aggression, self-injurious behavior, hyperactivity, and social withdrawal. Results demonstrate that selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors, particularly fluoxetine and sertraline, showed moderate efficacy in reducing repetitive behaviors and improving social interaction in 60-70% of patients. Neuroleptics, including haloperidol and risperidone, were effective for managing severe aggression and self-injury, with risperidone showing superior tolerability profiles. Stimulant medications provided modest improvements in attention and hyperactivity in select patients without autism. Anticonvulsants showed limited benefit except in patients with comorbid seizure disorders. Adverse effects were generally consistent with known medication profiles, though children with PDDs appeared more sensitive to extrapyramidal symptoms. These findings suggest that targeted pharmacotherapy can be a valuable adjunct to behavioral interventions in managing specific symptom clusters in pediatric PDD populations.
journal: Journal of child and adolescent psychiatric nursing : official publication of the Association of Child and Adolescent Psychiatric Nurses, Inc

title: The psychopharmacology of autism spectrum disorder and Rett syndrome.
abstract: Autism spectrum disorder (ASD) appears in early childhood and is characterized by persistent deficits in communication and social interaction, as well as restricted interests, repetitive behaviors, and unusual sensory issues. ASD can be idiopathic or syndromic, in the latter case representing one of the many manifestations of a genetic disorder, such as Rett syndrome. Psychopharmacology cannot directly ameliorate core autistic symptoms, but rather aims at treating comorbid disorders, such as ADHD, sleep disturbances, psychomotor agitation and aggressiveness, seizures, and anxiety. Until the 1990s, it was mainly based on typical neuroleptics and tricyclic antidepressants, whereas second-generation antipsychotics later became first-line drugs for these same indications. In general, selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors have not proven effective in ASD patients. Psychostimulants are frequently prescribed, but display modest efficacy and enhanced potential for side effects and noncompliance. Targeted patients benefit from mood stabilizers and antiepileptic drugs. Experimental drug treatments for ASD include oxytocin and vasopressin, bumetanide, sulforaphane, nutritional supplements, memantine, and d-cycloserine. Work performed on syndromic forms, represents an important source of information for experimental therapies of ASD and knowledge of the unique mechanisms underlying autism in each individual patient may in the future pave the path to personalized drug treatments.

title: Contemporary approaches to pharmacotherapy in Tourette's syndrome and obsessive-compulsive disorder.
abstract: Tourette's syndrome (TS) and obsessive-compulsive disorder (OCD) represent complex neuropsychiatric conditions that frequently co-occur in pediatric populations, presenting significant challenges for clinicians and families. Traditional pharmacological approaches have relied primarily on haloperidol and pimozide for tic suppression, while clomipramine has been the mainstay for OCD treatment. This comprehensive review examines emerging pharmacotherapeutic strategies and evaluates their efficacy and safety profiles in children and adolescents. A systematic analysis of clinical trials published between 1990-1996 was conducted, focusing on novel therapeutic agents including risperidone, olanzapine, selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors (SSRIs), and alpha-2 adrenergic agonists. Data from 24 controlled studies involving 1,847 pediatric patients were analyzed for treatment response rates, adverse effects, and long-term outcomes. Results indicate that risperidone demonstrates comparable efficacy to haloperidol for tic reduction (68% vs 71% response rates) with significantly fewer extrapyramidal side effects. Fluoxetine and sertraline showed superior tolerability profiles compared to clomipramine while maintaining therapeutic efficacy for OCD symptoms (62-78% improvement on Y-BOCS scores). Clonidine proved particularly beneficial for patients with comorbid attention-deficit hyperactivity disorder. These findings suggest that newer pharmacological agents offer promising alternatives to traditional treatments, potentially improving treatment adherence and quality of life in pediatric patients. However, long-term safety data remain limited, necessitating continued research and careful monitoring protocols for optimal therapeutic outcomes.
journal: Journal of child and adolescent psychiatric nursing : official publication of the Association of Child and Adolescent Psychiatric Nurses, Inc

title: [Therapeutic drug monitoring in neuropsychopharmacology : Summary of the consensus guidelines 2017 of the TDM task force of the AGNP].
abstract: Therapeutic drug monitoring (TDM) has gained increasing importance in neuropsychopharmacology to optimize treatment outcomes and minimize adverse effects. The AGNP TDM task force developed updated consensus guidelines in 2017 to provide evidence-based recommendations for clinical practice. This summary reviews the key recommendations and their implementation in psychiatric treatment settings. The guidelines were developed through systematic literature review and expert consensus involving pharmacologists, psychiatrists, and laboratory specialists across German-speaking countries. Recommendations were categorized into four levels based on available evidence and clinical utility: strongly recommended, recommended, useful, and potentially useful. The 2017 guidelines provide specific recommendations for 60 psychotropic medications, including antidepressants, antipsychotics, mood stabilizers, and anxiolytics. TDM is strongly recommended for tricyclic antidepressants, several atypical antipsychotics including clozapine and olanzapine, lithium, and anticonvulsant mood stabilizers. For newer antidepressants such as SSRIs and SNRIs, TDM is recommended in specific clinical situations including treatment-resistant cases, suspected non-adherence, drug interactions, and vulnerable populations. The guidelines emphasize proper timing of blood sampling, consideration of active metabolites, and interpretation within clinical context. Implementation of these evidence-based TDM recommendations can significantly improve therapeutic outcomes in psychiatric patients by enabling personalized dosing strategies, enhancing medication adherence monitoring, and reducing the risk of adverse drug reactions. Regular application of TDM principles represents a crucial step toward precision medicine in neuropsychopharmacology.

title: Pharmacotherapy of mental illness--a historical analysis.
abstract: The history of pharmacotherapy of mental illness can be divided into three periods. Introduction of morphine, potassium bromide, chloral hydrate, hyoscine, paraldehyde, etc., during the second half of the 19th century (first period), led to the replacement of physical restraint by pharmacological means in behavior control. Introduction of nicotinic acid, penicillin, thiamine, etc., during the first half of the 20th century (second period), led to significant changes in the diagnostic distribution of psychiatric patients; psychoses due to cerebral pellagra, and dementia due to syphilitic general paralysis virtually disappeared from psychiatric hospitals, and the prevalence of dysmnesias markedly decreased. Treatment with therapeutically effective drugs of mania, schizophrenia, depression, bipolar disorder, generalized anxiety disorder, panic disorder, obsessive compulsive disorder, Alzheimer's disease, etc., during the second half of the 20th century (third period), brought to attention the heterogeneity of the populations within the diagnostic categories of schizophrenia and depression. Introduction of the first set of psychotropics and the spectrophotofluorimeter during the 1950s triggered the development of neuropsychopharmacology. Introduction of genetic technology for the separation of receptor subtypes in the 1980s opened the path for the "tailoring" of psychotropic drugs by the dawn of the 21st century, to receptor affinities.

title: Neurochemistry of the afferents to the rat cochlear root nucleus: possible synaptic modulation of the acoustic startle.
abstract: Afferents to the primary startle circuit are essential for the elicitation and modulation of the acoustic startle reflex (ASR). In the rat, cochlear root neurons (CRNs) comprise the first component of the acoustic startle circuit and play a crucial role in mediating the ASR. Nevertheless, the neurochemical pattern of their afferents remains unclear. To determine the distribution of excitatory and inhibitory inputs, we used confocal microscopy to analyze the immunostaining for vesicular glutamate and GABA transporter proteins (VGLUT1 and VGAT) on retrogradely labeled CRNs. We also used reverse transcription-polymerase chain reaction (RT-PCR) and immunohistochemistry to detect and localize specific neurotransmitter receptor subunits in the cochlear root. Our results show differential distributions of VGLUT1- and VGAT-immunoreactive endings around cell bodies and dendrites. The RT-PCR data showed a positive band for several ionotropic glutamate receptor subunits, M1-M5 muscarinic receptor subtypes, the glycine receptor alpha1 subunit (GlyRalpha1), GABAA, GABAB, and subunits of alpha2 and beta-noradrenergic receptors. By immunohistochemistry, we confirmed that CRN cell bodies exhibit positive immunoreaction for the glutamate receptor (GluR) 3 and NR1 GluR subunits. Cell bodies and dendrites were also positive for M2 and M4, and GlyRalpha1. Other subunits, such as GluR1 and GluR4 of the AMPA GluRs, were observed in glial cells neighboring unlabeled CRN cell bodies. We further confirmed the existence of noradrenergic afferents onto CRNs from the locus coeruleus by combining tyrosine hydroxylase immunohistochemistry and tract-tracing experiments. Our results provide valuable information toward understanding how CRNs might integrate excitatory and inhibitory inputs, and hence how they could elicit and modulate the ASR.

title: Mercury, selenium and neurochemical biomarkers in different brain regions of migrating common loons from Lake Erie, Canada.
abstract: Mercury (Hg) contamination in aquatic ecosystems poses significant neurological risks to piscivorous birds, while selenium (Se) may provide neuroprotective effects through antagonistic interactions. Common loons (Gavia immer) are particularly vulnerable due to their high trophic position and reliance on fish consumption. This study investigated regional brain distribution of Hg and Se, and associated neurochemical alterations in migrating loons from Lake Erie. Brain tissues from 24 adult loons collected during fall migration (2009-2010) were analyzed for total Hg and Se concentrations in cerebrum, cerebellum, and brainstem using cold vapor atomic absorption spectrophotometry and hydride generation atomic absorption spectrophotometry, respectively. Acetylcholinesterase (AChE) activity, dopamine, serotonin, and their metabolites were quantified using spectrophotometric and HPLC methods. Mercury concentrations ranged from 0.89-4.2 μg/g wet weight, with highest levels in cerebellum, followed by cerebrum and brainstem. Selenium concentrations (0.34-1.1 μg/g wet weight) showed similar regional patterns and were positively correlated with Hg (r=0.72, p<0.001). Significant decreases in AChE activity were observed in cerebrum (23% reduction) and cerebellum (31% reduction) relative to control values. Dopamine concentrations were reduced by 18% in cerebrum, while serotonin metabolite ratios indicated altered neurotransmitter turnover. Se:Hg molar ratios below 1.0 in 67% of individuals suggested inadequate Se protection.
