These findings demonstrate region-specific neurotoxic effects of Hg exposure in loons, with potential implications for motor function and behavior during critical migration periods.

title: Spontaneous and induced mouse mutations with cerebellar dysfunctions: behavior and neurochemistry.
abstract: Grid2(Lc) (Lurcher), Grid2(ho) (hot-foot), Rora(sg) (staggerer), nr (nervous), Agtpbp1(pcd) (Purkinje cell degeneration), Reln(rl) (reeler), and Girk2(Wv) (Weaver) are spontaneous mutations with cerebellar atrophy, ataxia, and deficits in motor coordination tasks requiring balance and equilibrium. In addition to these signs, the Dst(dt) (dystonia musculorum) spinocerebellar mutant displays dystonic postures and crawling. More recently, transgenic models with human spinocerebellar ataxia mutations and alterations in calcium homeostasis have been shown to exhibit cerebellar anomalies and motor coordination deficits. We describe neurochemical characteristics of these mutants with respect to regional brain metabolism as well as amino acid and biogenic amine concentrations, uptake sites, and receptors.

title: Identifying novel protein-protein interactions using co-immunoprecipitation and mass spectroscopy.
abstract: Protein-protein interactions (PPIs) are fundamental to neuronal signaling cascades and synaptic plasticity, yet many critical interactions remain uncharacterized. Traditional biochemical approaches have limited sensitivity for detecting transient or low-abundance protein complexes in neural tissues. We developed an optimized protocol combining co-immunoprecipitation (co-IP) with liquid chromatography-tandem mass spectrometry (LC-MS/MS) to systematically identify novel PPIs in mouse brain tissue. Adult C57BL/6 mice were sacrificed and cortical, hippocampal, and striatal regions were rapidly dissected and homogenized under native conditions. Co-IP was performed using antibodies against known synaptic scaffolding proteins including PSD-95, NMDAR1, and mGluR5. Immunoprecipitated complexes were resolved by SDS-PAGE, subjected to in-gel trypsin digestion, and analyzed by high-resolution LC-MS/MS using a linear ion trap mass spectrometer. Database searches identified peptides with >95% confidence scores. We identified 127 high-confidence protein interactions, including 43 novel PPIs not previously reported in neuronal tissues. Notable discoveries included interactions between mGluR5 and several previously uncharacterized membrane-associated proteins, and a novel binding partner for PSD-95 with predicted kinase activity. Validation by reciprocal co-IP confirmed 89% of identified interactions. Brain region-specific analysis revealed distinct interaction profiles, suggesting specialized protein complex assembly across neural circuits. This optimized co-IP/MS protocol provides a robust platform for discovering functionally relevant protein interactions in neural tissues and may facilitate identification of new therapeutic targets for neurological disorders.

title: Dextromethorphan-quinidine-responsive pseudobulbar affect (PBA): psychopharmacological model for wide-ranging disorders of emotional expression?
abstract: Pseudobulbar affect (PBA) is a neurological condition characterized by involuntary, exaggerated emotional outbursts that are incongruent with underlying mood states, commonly occurring in patients with amyotrophic lateral sclerosis, multiple sclerosis, traumatic brain injury, and stroke. The recent FDA approval of dextromethorphan-quinidine (DM-Q) for PBA treatment provides an opportunity to examine broader mechanisms of emotional dysregulation. We conducted a systematic review of clinical trial data and neuroimaging studies to evaluate whether the therapeutic mechanisms underlying DM-Q efficacy in PBA may inform treatment approaches for other disorders of emotional expression. Analysis of randomized controlled trials demonstrated that DM-Q significantly reduced PBA episode frequency and severity across multiple neurological populations through modulation of glutamatergic and serotonergic neurotransmission. Neuroimaging studies revealed that PBA involves dysfunction in cortico-limbic circuits regulating emotional expression, particularly connections between prefrontal cortex, anterior cingulate, and brainstem nuclei. These circuits overlap substantially with those implicated in emotional lability associated with bipolar disorder, borderline personality disorder, and treatment-resistant depression. The dual-mechanism action of DM-Q, combining NMDA receptor antagonism via dextromethorphan with enhanced bioavailability through quinidine-mediated CYP2D6 inhibition, represents a novel pharmacological approach that may have broader applications. Our findings suggest that PBA serves as a valuable model for understanding pathological emotional expression and that DM-Q's mechanisms may inform development of treatments for related conditions characterized by emotional dysregulation.

title: Biomarkers for major depression and its delineation from neurodegenerative disorders.
abstract: Major depressive disorders (MDD) are among the most debilitating diseases worldwide and occur with a high prevalence in elderly individuals. Neurodegenerative diseases (in particular Alzheimer's disease, AD) do also show a strong age-dependent increase in incidence and prevalence among the elderly population. A high number of geriatric patients with MDD show cognitive deficits and a very high proportion of AD patients present co-morbid MDD, which poses difficult diagnostic and prognostic questions. Especially in prodromal and in very early stages of AD, it is almost impossible to differentiate between pure MDD and MDD with underlying AD. Here, we give a comprehensive review of the literature on the current state of candidate biomarkers for MDD ("positive MDD markers") and briefly refer to established and validated diagnostic AD biomarkers in order to rule out underlying AD pathophysiology in elderly MDD subjects with cognitive impairments ("negative MDD biomarkers"). In summary, to date there is no evidence for positive diagnostic MDD biomarkers and the only way to delineate MDD from AD is to use "negative MDD" biomarkers. Because of this highly unsatisfactory current state of MDD biomarker research, we propose a research strategy targeting to detect and validate positive MDD biomarkers, which is based on a complex (genetic, molecular and neurophysiological) biological model that incorporates current state of the art knowledge on the pathobiology of MDD. This model delineates common pathways and the intersection between AD and MDD. Applying these concepts to MDD gives hope that positive MDD biomarkers can be successfully identified in the near future.

title: Nystagmus.
abstract: Acquired nystagmus causes excessive motion of images on the retina, which often reduces visual acuity and produces oscillopsia (illusory motion of the seen world). The pathogenesis of several forms of acquired nystagmus is now better understood and can be related to abnormalities of mechanisms that normally hold gaze steady. Basic research on the pharmacology of the neural pathways responsible for gaze holding has demonstrated an important role for gamma aminobutyric acid and led to new drug treatments for acquired nystagmus and its visual consequences.

title: Psychiatric safety of ketamine in psychopharmacology research.
abstract:
**Background:** Ketamine, a non-competitive NMDA receptor antagonist, has emerged as a valuable research tool in psychopharmacology for modeling psychotic symptoms and investigating glutamatergic dysfunction in psychiatric disorders. However, concerns regarding its psychiatric safety profile in research settings have limited its broader application. This study systematically evaluated the acute and subacute psychiatric effects of ketamine administration in healthy volunteers and psychiatric populations participating in research protocols.

**Methods:** We conducted a retrospective analysis of 847 ketamine administrations across 12 controlled studies (2003-2006) involving healthy controls (n=312) and patients with schizophrenia (n=89) or mood disorders (n=76). Subanesthetic doses ranged from 0.1-0.5 mg/kg intravenously. Psychiatric symptoms were assessed using the Brief Psychiatric Rating Scale (BPRS) and Clinician Administered Dissociative States Scale (CADSS) at baseline, peak effect (40 minutes), and follow-up timepoints (24 hours, 1 week).

**Results:** Ketamine produced dose-dependent increases in BPRS positive symptoms (peak increase: 8.3±2.1 points) and CADSS scores (21.4±5.7) that returned to baseline within 2-4 hours. No persistent psychotic symptoms occurred beyond 24 hours in any participant. Psychiatric patients showed similar symptom trajectories but required closer monitoring. Three participants (0.35%) experienced significant anxiety requiring early termination, and one healthy volunteer developed transient panic symptoms. No hospitalizations or serious adverse psychiatric events occurred.

**Conclusions:** These findings support the psychiatric safety of subanesthetic ketamine in controlled research environments with appropriate screening and monitoring protocols. The predictable, time-limited nature of ketamine's psychiatric effects enables its continued use as a valuable neuropharmacological probe while maintaining acceptable risk-benefit ratios for research participants.

title: Incident psychopharmacological treatment and psychiatric hospital contact in individuals with newly developed type 2 diabetes - a register-based cohort study.
abstract: To investigate the association between newly developed type 2 diabetes (T2D) and incident psychopharmacological treatment and psychiatric hospital contact. Via Danish registers, we identified all 56 640 individuals from the Central and Northern Denmark Regions with newly developed T2D (defined by the first HbA1c measurement ≥6.5%) in 2000-2016 as well as 315 694 age- and sex-matched controls (without T2D). Those having received psychopharmacological treatment or having had a psychiatric hospital contact in the 5 years prior to the onset of T2D were not included. For this cohort, we first assessed the 2-year incidence of psychopharmacological treatment and psychiatric hospital contact. Secondly, via Cox regression, we compared the incidence of psychopharmacological treatment/psychiatric hospital contact among individuals with T2D to propensity score-matched controls - taking a wide range of potential confounders into account. Finally, via Cox proportional hazards regression, we assessed which baseline (T2D onset) characteristics were associated with subsequent psychopharmacological treatment and psychiatric hospital contact. A total of 8.3% of the individuals with T2D initiated psychopharmacological treatment compared to 4.6% of the age- and sex-matched controls. Individuals with T2D were at increased risk of initiating psychopharmacological treatment compared to the propensity score-matched controls (HR = 1.51, 95% CI = 1.43-1.59), whereas their risk of psychiatric hospital contact was not increased to the same extent (HR = 1.14, 95% CI = 0.98-1.32). Older age, somatic comorbidity, and being divorced/widowed were associated with both psychopharmacological treatment and psychiatric hospital contact following T2D. Individuals with T2D are at elevated risk of requiring psychopharmacological treatment.

title: Drugs used to treat Parkinson's disease, present status and future directions.
abstract: Parkinson's disease (PD) affects approximately 1% of individuals over 65 years of age and represents one of the most prevalent neurodegenerative disorders worldwide. Current pharmacological interventions primarily target the dopaminergic deficit characteristic of PD, yet significant limitations remain in achieving optimal symptom control while minimizing adverse effects. This comprehensive review examines the current therapeutic armamentarium for PD management and evaluates emerging treatment paradigms. We systematically analyzed clinical efficacy data from randomized controlled trials published between 1990-2008 for established PD medications, including levodopa/carbidopa, dopamine agonists, monoamine oxidase-B inhibitors, and catechol-O-methyltransferase inhibitors. Additionally, we reviewed preclinical and early clinical data for novel therapeutic approaches targeting non-dopaminergic systems and neuroprotective strategies. Levodopa remains the gold standard for symptomatic treatment, providing superior motor benefit compared to dopamine agonists, though long-term use is associated with motor fluctuations and dyskinesias in 50-80% of patients within 5-10 years. Dopamine agonists demonstrate efficacy in early PD with reduced dyskinesia risk but increased psychiatric adverse effects. Emerging therapies show promise, including adenosine A2A receptor antagonists, glutamate modulators, and compounds targeting α-synuclein aggregation. Novel drug delivery systems, such as continuous dopaminergic stimulation via pumps and patches, may address motor fluctuations. Future PD therapeutics will likely emphasize combination approaches targeting multiple pathophysiological mechanisms, including neuroinflammation, mitochondrial dysfunction, and protein aggregation, potentially offering disease modification beyond current symptomatic treatments.

title: Modelling ischaemia in vitro: effects of temperature and glucose concentration on dopamine release evoked by oxygen and glucose depletion in a mouse brain slice.
abstract: Current pharmacological interventions for acute stroke are largely ineffective or confounded by adverse effects, emphasising the need to develop new pharmacological treatments for neuroprotection. We have developed a robust in vitro model previously used in rats to assess dopamine release in mouse caudate nucleus brain slices, measured by fast cyclic voltammetry, during oxygen and glucose deprivation (OGD) as a model for cerebral ischaemia: this model will allow the study of transgenic mouse strains. During the pre-OGD equilibration period we found that a temperature of 33°C, with solution containing 10 mM glucose provided the optimum baseline conditions from which reliable OGD-induced changes in dopamine efflux could be measured, without being susceptible to spontaneous release events. During OGD we found no significant difference in any of the parameters measured between perfusion with glucose-free solution, and perfusion with solution containing 2 mM glucose. We therefore suggest, in agreement with previous work, that using 2 mM glucose during OGD is appropriate, and using these conditions we were able to reliably produce OGD-evoked dopamine release.

title: Changes in striatal electroencephalography and neurochemistry induced by kainic acid seizures are modified by dopamine receptor antagonists.
abstract: The excitotoxic effects of kainic acid (KA) involve complex interactions between glutamatergic and dopaminergic systems, particularly in the striatum where these neurotransmitter pathways extensively converge. This study investigated whether dopamine receptor antagonists could modulate KA-induced electrophysiological and neurochemical alterations in the rat striatum. Adult male Wistar rats (n=8 per group) received intraperitoneal injections of KA (10 mg/kg) alone or in combination with the D1 receptor antagonist SCH23390 (0.5 mg/kg) or the D2 receptor antagonist haloperidol (1.0 mg/kg). Striatal electroencephalographic activity was continuously monitored for 6 hours post-injection, and neurochemical analyses were performed using high-performance liquid chromatography to measure dopamine, DOPAC, HVA, and GABA concentrations at 24 hours. KA administration produced characteristic epileptiform discharges in the striatum within 30 minutes, accompanied by significant depletion of striatal dopamine (-67%, p<0.001) and GABA (-43%, p<0.01) levels. Pretreatment with SCH23390 significantly attenuated the duration and amplitude of KA-induced seizure activity and partially protected against dopamine depletion (-31%, p<0.05 vs KA alone). Haloperidol pretreatment showed less pronounced protective effects on both electrophysiological and neurochemical parameters. These findings suggest that D1 receptor-mediated mechanisms contribute to KA-induced striatal excitotoxicity, and that selective D1 antagonism may offer neuroprotection against glutamate-mediated seizure damage in this brain region.

title: Dementia: the neurochemical basis of putative transmitter orientated therapy.
abstract: The neurotransmitter deficits of dementias, including Alzheimer's dementia, Lewy body dementia and Parkinson's disease are discussed in relation to cognitive and behavioural impairments together with neuropathological changes and available data on the status of receptor transmembrane signalling. Potential therapeutic strategies for dementia are outlined based on the following systems: excitatory amino acids, gamma-amino butyric acid, acetylcholine (muscarinic and nicotinic), noradrenaline, serotonin and peptides. These include the attenuation of transmitter deficits by agonists and agents inhibiting transmitter breakdown and support for surviving neurons by suppression of inhibitory inputs, trophic factors and neural implantation.

title: Vibration enhancement of slide-mounted immunofluorescence staining.
abstract: Immunofluorescence techniques allow the determination of protein and small molecule distribution within tissues and individual cells. There have been important, innovative modifications of these techniques since their introduction to the biosciences including the use of a mounting medium that prevents photo-bleaching, non-ionic detergents to permeabilize membranes, multiple immunofluorescence labeling and antigen recovery techniques for optimizing ligand-target interactions. While methods have been optimized for ligand-target accessibility in free-floating sections, little innovation has occurred to improve antibody access and epitope recognition in immunohistochemistry on slide-mounted sections or cell culture. During our studies of brain signaling pathways, we sought to improve the immunofluorescence signal to noise ratio in these specimens. We present here a minor modification of immunofluorescence procedures that significantly increases antibody access to epitopes within tissue and improves staining quality while significantly shortening incubation time. Antibody-epitope interactions are dependent on access and affinity. Our technique is based upon application of a vibration source during antibody incubation which increases epitope access, shortens incubation time and thereby minimizes background immunofluorescence. Data are presented on benefits evident with several antibodies raised against proteins and peptides localized in various subcellular compartments. Analysis of the quality of labeling was performed to show that signal intensity is enhanced and background intensity is often diminished when incubations are performed under gentle vibration. This, together with the significant saving of time, should make this procedure applicable to a wide range of neurobiological questions.

title: How human electrophysiology informs psychopharmacology: from bottom-up driven processing to top-down control.
abstract: This review surveys human event-related brain potential (ERP) and event-related magnetic field (ERF) approaches to psychopharmacology and psychopathology, and the way in which they complement behavioral studies and other neuroimaging modalities. The major paradigms involving ERP/ERF are P50 suppression, loudness-dependent auditory evoked potential (LDAEP), mismatch negativity (MMN), P300, mental chronometry, inhibitory control, and conflict processing (eg, error-related negativity (ERN)). Together these paradigms cover a range of more bottom-up driven to more top-down controlled processes. A number of relationships between the major neurotransmitter systems and electrocortical mechanisms are highlighted. These include the role of dopamine in conflict processing, and perceptual processing vs motor preparation; the role of serotonin in P50 suppression, LDAEP, and MMN; glutamate/NMDA and MMN; and the role of acetylcholine in P300 generation and memory-related processes. A preliminary taxonomy for these relationships is provided, which should be helpful in attuning possible new treatments or new applications of existing treatments to various disorders.
journal: Neuropsychopharmacology : official publication of the American College of Neuropsychopharmacology

title: The challenges of neuroethics.
abstract: This paper presents different conceptions of neuroethics, focusing both on applications of neuroscience aiming to provide access to other people's minds and on the theoretical study of the neural bases of moral judgement and decision making. It is argued that these studies have not yet seriously undermined the traditional view of humans as moral free agents, nor have they accomplished the radical naturalisation of ethics that is sometimes promised or predicted. A few implications for normative ethics and moral epistemology are also discussed.

title: [The role of positron emission tomography in neuropharmacology in the living human brain and drug development].
abstract: Neuroimaging is a powerful and innovative tool for studying the pathology of psychiatric and neurological diseases and, more recently, for studying the drugs used in their treatment. Technological advances in imaging have made it possible to noninvasively extract information from the human brain regarding a drug's mechanism and site of action. Until now, our understanding of human brain pharmacology has depended primarily on indirect assessments or models derived from animal studies. However, the advent of multiple techniques for human brain imaging allows researchers to focus directly on human pharmacology and brain function. In this review article, our PET studies on the histaminergic neuron system were presented as an example. We have developed and used the PET techniques for 10 years in order to examine the H1 receptors in the living human brain. This review outlines available PET techniques and examines how these various methods have already been applied to the drug development process and neuropharmacology in the living human brain.
journal: Nihon yakurigaku zasshi. Folia pharmacologica Japonica

title: Serotonin receptors of type 6 (5-HT6): what can we expect from them?
abstract: Serotonin 5-HT6 receptors represent one of the most recently discovered members of the serotonin receptor family and have emerged as promising therapeutic targets for cognitive disorders and neuropsychiatric conditions. Despite their restricted distribution primarily in the central nervous system, particularly in brain regions associated with learning and memory such as the hippocampus, cortex, and striatum, their precise physiological roles remain incompletely understood. This review synthesizes current knowledge regarding 5-HT6 receptor pharmacology, distribution, and therapeutic potential based on preclinical and early clinical findings through 2011. 5-HT6 receptors are positively coupled to adenylyl cyclase and exhibit high constitutive activity, distinguishing them from other serotonin receptor subtypes. Accumulating evidence suggests these receptors modulate cholinergic, GABAergic, and glutamatergic neurotransmission, positioning them as important regulators of cognitive function. Selective 5-HT6 receptor antagonists, including SB-271046, SB-399885, and Ro 04-6790, have demonstrated procognitive effects in various animal models of learning and memory, including novel object recognition, Morris water maze, and attentional set-shifting tasks. Additionally, these compounds show promise in models of Alzheimer's disease, schizophrenia, and obesity. Early clinical trials with 5-HT6 antagonists have yielded mixed but encouraging results in cognitive enhancement and weight reduction. The unique pharmacological profile and therapeutic potential of 5-HT6 receptors suggest they may offer novel approaches for treating cognitive impairment associated with aging, dementia, and psychiatric disorders, warranting continued investigation and development of selective modulators.

title: High temporal resolution for in vivo monitoring of neurotransmitters in awake epileptic rats using brain microdialysis and capillary electrophoresis with laser-induced fluorescence detection.
abstract: A method for high temporal resolution monitoring of five neurotransmitters, dopamine (DA), noradrenaline (NA), gamma-aminobutyric acid (GABA), glutamate (Glu), l-aspartate (L-Asp), in freely-moving rats using microdialysis and capillary electrophoresis with laser-induced fluorescence detection (CE-LIFD) was developed. An on-line device, including microdialysis and derivatization with naphthalene-2,3-dicarboxaldehyde, mixes the dialysate with derivatization reagents directly in the collection tube, i.e. with no reactor. Thereafter, collected derivatized samples are analyzed off-line with an automated CE system coupled to a LIFD using a 442 nm excitation. The sampling time was limited by the minimal volume required for the analysis by the automated CE system used: neurotransmitters could be determined in 667 nl dialysates (940 nl after derivatization), i.e. in samples collected every 20 s with a flow rate of 2 microl/min. The detection limits at the dialysis probe were 3 x 10(-9), 1 x 10(-9), 1.9 x 10(-8), 4.2 x 10(-7), 2.1 x 10(-7) mol/l for DA, NA, GABA, Glu and L-Asp, respectively. The protocol was validated using in vitro/in vivo tests and the performances--repeatability, linearity, characteristics of the probes--were determined. Finally, the high temporal resolution allowed the simultaneous monitoring of these neurotransmitters in rats with genetic absence epilepsy and revealed, for the first time, increases in GABA concentrations concomitantly with the seizures, detected when our new microdialysis method was combined to electroencephalographic recordings.

title: [The history of CINP between 1958 and 2000].
abstract: The Collegium Internationale Neuro-Psychopharmacologicum (CINP) was established in 1958 as the first international organization dedicated to advancing the scientific understanding of neuropsychopharmacology. This historical review examines the evolution and contributions of CINP during its formative decades from 1958 to 2000, analyzing its role in shaping modern psychopharmacology research and clinical practice. We conducted a comprehensive analysis of CINP archival materials, including congress proceedings, membership records, scientific publications, and correspondence from founding members. Historical documents were obtained from the CINP archives and supplemented with interviews of key figures who participated in the organization's early development. Between 1958 and 2000, CINP organized 22 biennial world congresses across four continents, facilitating international collaboration among 3,847 researchers from 67 countries. The organization played a pivotal role in establishing standardized methodologies for psychopharmacological research, promoting the development of rating scales for psychiatric disorders, and fostering the emergence of biological psychiatry as a distinct discipline. Notable achievements included the creation of task forces on depression, schizophrenia, and anxiety disorders, which produced influential consensus statements that guided clinical practice. CINP also contributed significantly to the training of young investigators through fellowship programs and mentorship initiatives. The period from 1958 to 2000 represents a foundational era for CINP, during which the organization established itself as the premier international forum for neuropsychopharmacology research. CINP's contributions during this period were instrumental in transforming psychopharmacology from an emerging field into a mature scientific discipline with global impact.
journal: Nihon shinkei seishin yakurigaku zasshi = Japanese journal of psychopharmacology

title: European College of Neuropsychopharmacology (ECNP) - 23rd Congress.
abstract: The 23rd Congress of the European College of Neuropsychopharmacology (ECNP) convened in Amsterdam, Netherlands, from August 28 to September 1, 2010, bringing together leading researchers in neuropsychopharmacology to present cutting-edge findings in psychiatric drug development and neuroscience. This comprehensive report summarizes key presentations and symposia focusing on novel therapeutic targets and investigational compounds for major psychiatric disorders. The congress featured 142 oral presentations, 756 poster presentations, and 28 satellite symposiums covering diverse areas including depression, schizophrenia, bipolar disorder, anxiety disorders, and addiction medicine. Notable highlights included presentations on next-generation antidepressants targeting the glutamatergic system, including mGluR2/3 agonists and NMDA receptor modulators showing promise in treatment-resistant depression. Significant attention was devoted to novel antipsychotic agents with improved metabolic profiles, particularly partial dopamine D2 receptor agonists and dual-acting compounds modulating both dopaminergic and serotonergic pathways. Innovative approaches in addiction medicine featured presentations on phosphodiesterase inhibitors and GABA-B receptor agonists for alcohol and cocaine dependence. Emerging biomarker strategies for personalized psychiatric medicine were extensively discussed, emphasizing pharmacogenomic approaches and neuroimaging-guided treatment selection. The congress highlighted the industry's shift toward more sophisticated mechanistic approaches in psychiatric drug development, moving beyond traditional monoaminergic targets. These advances represent significant progress in addressing the substantial unmet medical needs in psychiatry, with several investigational compounds expected to enter Phase III trials within the next two years, potentially transforming treatment paradigms for millions of patients worldwide.

title: A review of monoaminergic neuropsychopharmacology in zebrafish.
abstract: The zebrafish (Danio rerio) has emerged as a powerful vertebrate model for investigating monoaminergic neurotransmitter systems and their pharmacological modulation. This review synthesizes current knowledge of dopaminergic, serotonergic, and noradrenergic pathways in zebrafish and their relevance to neuropsychiatric drug discovery. We conducted a comprehensive analysis of published literature from 2000-2010 examining monoamine neurotransmitter distribution, receptor pharmacology, and behavioral responses to psychoactive compounds in zebrafish. Immunohistochemical and in situ hybridization studies reveal that zebrafish possess conserved monoaminergic nuclei analogous to mammalian systems, including dopaminergic clusters in the posterior tuberculum and ventral diencephalon, serotonergic raphe-like neurons, and noradrenergic locus coeruleus homologs. Pharmacological characterization demonstrates that zebrafish monoamine receptors exhibit similar ligand binding profiles and G-protein coupling patterns to their mammalian counterparts. Behavioral assays measuring locomotion, anxiety-like responses, and social behaviors show dose-dependent effects of classical psychotropic drugs including SSRIs, dopamine receptor agonists/antagonists, and norepinephrine reuptake inhibitors that parallel mammalian responses. Importantly, genetic mutants affecting monoamine synthesis and metabolism display behavioral phenotypes consistent with human neuropsychiatric conditions. These findings establish zebrafish as a valuable translational model bridging molecular mechanisms and behavioral outcomes in monoaminergic neuropsychopharmacology. The conservation of monoamine systems across vertebrates, combined with zebrafish experimental advantages including genetic tractability, optical transparency, and high-throughput screening capabilities, positions this model organism as an important tool for understanding monoamine function and developing novel therapeutics for psychiatric disorders.

title: In-vivo brain dialysis of neurotransmitters.
abstract: Recent advances in microdialysis technology have provided unprecedented opportunities to monitor neurotransmitter release in the living brain with high temporal resolution. This technique involves the stereotaxic implantation of a dialysis probe with a semipermeable membrane into specific brain regions, allowing continuous sampling of extracellular fluid. We evaluated the efficacy of in-vivo microdialysis for measuring dopamine, norepinephrine, serotonin, and acetylcholine release in the striatum, hippocampus, and prefrontal cortex of freely moving rats. Dialysis probes (2-4 mm membrane length, 20,000 Da molecular weight cutoff) were perfused with artificial cerebrospinal fluid at 1-2 μL/min, and samples were collected at 20-minute intervals for high-performance liquid chromatography analysis. Basal concentrations of dopamine (5-15 nM), norepinephrine (2-8 nM), and serotonin (1-5 nM) were successfully detected, representing approximately 10-20% of actual extracellular levels due to probe recovery limitations. Pharmacological validation experiments demonstrated that amphetamine (2 mg/kg, i.p.) increased striatal dopamine release by 400-600%, while haloperidol (0.5 mg/kg, i.p.) produced a delayed elevation consistent with autoreceptor blockade. Local perfusion of tetrodotoxin abolished neurotransmitter release, confirming the neuronal origin of detected compounds. These findings establish microdialysis as a powerful tool for real-time neurochemical monitoring, offering significant advantages over traditional post-mortem analyses. The technique promises to advance our understanding of neurotransmitter dynamics in behavioral, pharmacological, and pathophysiological studies.

title: I Believe I Can Fly!: Use of Drosophila as a Model Organism in Neuropsychopharmacology Research.
abstract: The fruit fly Drosophila melanogaster has emerged as a powerful model organism for neuropsychopharmacological research, offering unique advantages including genetic tractability, conserved neurotransmitter systems, and behavioral paradigms relevant to human psychiatric disorders. This review synthesizes current applications of Drosophila in drug discovery and mechanism elucidation across major neuropsychiatric conditions. We conducted a comprehensive literature analysis of Drosophila-based studies published between 2010-2016, focusing on models of addiction, depression, anxiety, schizophrenia, and cognitive disorders. Our analysis reveals that Drosophila exhibits conserved responses to major classes of psychoactive compounds, including dopaminergic, serotonergic, and GABAergic modulators. Notably, fly models have successfully recapitulated key features of human drug addiction, including sensitization, tolerance, and withdrawal behaviors following exposure to cocaine, alcohol, and nicotine. Sleep and circadian rhythm studies in flies have provided crucial insights into the neurobiological basis of mood disorders and identified novel therapeutic targets. Furthermore, Drosophila models of neurodevelopmental disorders have facilitated the discovery of small molecule compounds that rescue behavioral and synaptic deficits. High-throughput screening approaches using fly models have identified several lead compounds currently advancing through mammalian preclinical testing. These findings demonstrate that Drosophila serves as an invaluable bridge between molecular mechanisms and mammalian studies in neuropsychopharmacology. The genetic precision, cost-effectiveness, and translational relevance of fly models position Drosophila as an essential component of the modern drug discovery pipeline for neuropsychiatric therapeutics.
journal: Neuropsychopharmacology : official publication of the American College of Neuropsychopharmacology

title: Gene regulation and genetics in neurochemistry, past to future.
abstract: Ask any neuroscientist to name the most profound discoveries in the field in the past 60 years, and at or near the top of the list will be a phenomenon or technique related to genes and their expression. Indeed, our understanding of genetics and gene regulation has ushered in whole new systems of knowledge and new empirical approaches, many of which could not have even been imagined prior to the molecular biology boon of recent decades. Neurochemistry, in the classic sense, intersects with these concepts in the manifestation of neuropeptides, obviously dependent upon the central dogma (the established rules by which DNA sequence is eventually converted into protein primary structure) not only for their conformation but also for their levels and locales of expression. But, expanding these considerations to non-peptide neurotransmitters illustrates how gene regulatory events impact neurochemistry in a much broader sense, extending beyond the neurochemicals that translate electrical signals into chemical ones in the synapse, to also include every aspect of neural development, structure, function, and pathology. From the beginning, the mutability - yet relative stability - of genes and their expression patterns were recognized as potential substrates for some of the most intriguing phenomena in neurobiology - those instances of plasticity required for learning and memory. Near-heretical speculation was offered in the idea that perhaps the very sequence of the genome was altered to encode memories. A fascinating component of the intervening progress includes evidence that the central dogma is not nearly as rigid and consistent as we once thought. And this mutability extends to the potential to manipulate that code for both experimental and clinical purposes. Astonishing progress has been made in the molecular biology of neurochemistry during the 60 years since this journal debuted. Many of the gains in conceptual understanding have been driven by methodological progress, from automated high-throughput sequencing instruments to recombinant-DNA vectors that can convey color-coded genetic modifications in the chromosomes of live adult animals. This review covers the highlights of these advances, both theoretical and technological, along with a brief window into the promising science ahead. This article is part of the 60th Anniversary special issue.

title: Optofluidic control of rodent learning using cloaked caged glutamate.
abstract: Glutamate is the major excitatory neurotransmitter in the brain, and photochemical release of glutamate (or uncaging) is a chemical technique widely used by biologists to interrogate its physiology. A basic prerequisite of these optical probes is bio-inertness before photolysis. However, all caged glutamates are known to have strong antagonism toward receptors of γ-aminobutyric acid, the major inhibitory transmitter. We have developed a caged glutamate probe that is inert toward these receptors at concentrations that are effective for photolysis with violet light. Pharmacological tests in vitro revealed that attachment of a fifth-generation (G5) dendrimer (i.e., cloaking) to the widely used 4-methoxy-7-nitro-indolinyl(MNI)-Glu probe prevented such off-target effects while not changing the photochemical properties of MNI-Glu significantly. G5-MNI-Glu was used with optofluidic delivery to stimulate dopamine neurons of the ventral tegmental area of freely moving mice in a conditioned place-preference protocol so as to mediate Pavlovian conditioning.
journal: Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences of the United States of America

title: Therapeutic potential of vanilloid receptor TRPV1 agonists and antagonists as analgesics: Recent advances and setbacks.
abstract: The vanilloid receptor TRPV1 is a homotetrameric, non-selective cation channel abundantly expressed in the nociceptors (c-fibers). TRPV1 is considered as a highly validated pain target because, i) its agonists such as capsaicin cause desensitization of TRPV1 channels that relieves pain behaviors in preclinical species, and ii) its antagonists relieve pain behaviors in rodent models of inflammation, osteoarthritis, and cancer. Hence, both agonists and antagonists of TRPV1 are being evaluated as potential analgesics in clinical trials. Clinical trial results of TRPV1 agonists such as resiniferatoxin in interstitial cystitis, NGX 4010 in post-herpetic neuralgia, and 4975 (Adlea) in osteoarthritis, bunionectomy, and Morton's neuroma have been reported. Similarly, clinical trial results of TRPV1 antagonists such as SB-705498 and AMG 517 have also been published recently. Overall, some molecules (e.g., capsaicin) demonstrated potential analgesia in certain conditions (postsurgical pain, postherpetic neuralgia, pain in diabetic neuropathy, osteoarthritis, bunionectomy, and Morton's neuroma), whereas others fell out of the clinic due to on-target liabilities or failed to demonstrate efficacy. This review summarizes recent advances and setbacks of TRPV1 agonists and antagonists in the clinic and predicts future directions.

title: Diagnosis and management of chronic daily headache.
abstract: Chronic daily headache (CDH) affects approximately 4-5% of the general population and represents a significant neurological burden with substantial impact on quality of life and healthcare utilization. The classification encompasses transformed migraine, chronic tension-type headache, new daily persistent headache, and hemicrania continua, each requiring distinct therapeutic approaches. This comprehensive review analyzes current diagnostic criteria and evidence-based management strategies for CDH syndromes. We systematically reviewed published literature from 1990-2009, focusing on randomized controlled trials and large observational studies examining diagnostic methodologies and therapeutic interventions. Clinical assessment should emphasize headache frequency, medication overuse patterns, and associated neurological symptoms to differentiate CDH subtypes. Neuroimaging is indicated when red flag symptoms are present or in cases of new-onset daily headache. Medication overuse headache, present in 30-50% of CDH patients, requires structured detoxification protocols. First-line preventive treatments include topiramate (50-100mg daily) and amitriptyline (25-75mg daily), with response rates of 40-60% in controlled trials. Botulinum toxin A shows emerging efficacy for chronic migraine, with significant headache reduction in 70% of patients. Behavioral interventions, including cognitive-behavioral therapy and biofeedback, provide additive benefits when combined with pharmacotherapy. Early recognition and appropriate management of CDH can substantially reduce headache frequency and improve functional outcomes. A multidisciplinary approach incorporating pharmacological and non-pharmacological strategies, with emphasis on medication overuse prevention, represents the optimal management paradigm for these challenging headache disorders.

title: Towards a clinical methodology for neuropsychopharmacological research.
abstract: Neuropsychopharmacological research faces significant methodological challenges that limit the translation of preclinical findings to clinical applications. Current approaches often lack standardized protocols for assessment of cognitive and behavioral endpoints, leading to inconsistent results across studies. We propose a comprehensive clinical methodology framework designed to enhance reliability and validity in neuropsychopharmacological investigations. Our approach integrates standardized neurocognitive test batteries, validated rating scales, and objective biomarkers including electroencephalography and neuroimaging parameters. We evaluated this methodology in a pilot study involving 45 healthy volunteers and 38 patients with major depressive disorder, examining the effects of selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors on cognitive function over 8 weeks. The proposed battery demonstrated excellent test-retest reliability (ICC = 0.84-0.92) and sensitivity to drug-induced changes in attention, working memory, and executive function. Significant correlations were observed between subjective mood ratings and objective cognitive measures (r = 0.67, p < 0.001). EEG theta/beta ratios showed predictive value for treatment response (AUC = 0.78). Inter-rater reliability for clinical assessments exceeded 0.90 across all measures. The methodology successfully detected subtle cognitive improvements that emerged after 4 weeks of treatment, preceding clinical mood improvements by approximately 2 weeks. This standardized approach provides a robust framework for future neuropsychopharmacological studies, potentially accelerating drug development and improving clinical outcomes through more precise measurement of therapeutic effects.
journal: Neuropsychopharmacologia Hungarica : a Magyar Pszichofarmakologiai Egyesulet lapja = official journal of the Hungarian Association of Psychopharmacology

title: Are You Ready for Change?
abstract: Under the direction of the Journal's new Psychopharmacology Section Editor, a different approach will be taken: psychotropic drugs will be classified based on their neurobiological mechanism of action in the brain. This approach focuses on how the drug acts on brain cells and neurotransmitters involved in the specific symptoms presented by patients. The current article introduces the approach and will help clinicians think about medications as they relate to the neurobiological functions they are trying to correct. [Journal of Psychosocial Nursing and Mental Health Services, 56(2), 11-13.].
journal: Journal of psychosocial nursing and mental health services

title: Neuropharmacological profile of bifeprunox: merits and limitations in comparison with other third-generation antipsychotics.
abstract: Schizophrenia is characterized by a range of positive and negative symptoms, and cognitive deficits. While positive symptoms respond to current antipsychotic agents, negative symptoms and cognitive deficits are often resistant to pharmacopea. Thus research is now focused on developing third-generation antipsychotics that combine antagonism or partial agonism at dopamine D(2)-like receptors with agonism at serotonin 5-HT(1A) receptors. Such an association is anticipated to provide therapeutic benefits against a broader range of schizophrenia symptoms. Bifeprunox is one such third-generation antipsychotic agent which acts as a partial agonist at D(2)-like receptors and is an efficacious agonist at 5-HT(1A) receptors, with little interaction at 5HT(2A/2C), muscarinic or histaminergic H(1) receptors. This review summarizes the pharmacological profiles of the current antipsychotic agents and describes the rationale behind the development of third-generation antipsychotics. It also evaluates current data concerning bifeprunox in comparison with currently available antipsychotics, as well as those that are still under clinical development.
journal: Current opinion in investigational drugs (London, England : 2000)

title: The Texas Children's Medication Algorithm Project: revision of the algorithm for pharmacotherapy of attention-deficit/hyperactivity disorder.
abstract: **Background:** The Texas Children's Medication Algorithm Project (TCMAP) was developed to provide evidence-based pharmacotherapy guidelines for attention-deficit/hyperactivity disorder (ADHD) in children and adolescents. Since the original algorithm's publication in 2001, new medications have been approved and additional efficacy and safety data have emerged, necessitating algorithmic revision. **Objective:** To update the TCMAP ADHD algorithm incorporating recent clinical trial data and expert consensus recommendations. **Method:** A multidisciplinary panel of child psychiatrists, developmental pediatricians, and clinical researchers reviewed controlled trials of ADHD medications published between 2001-2006, focusing on stimulants, atomoxetine, and alpha-2 agonists. The panel utilized a modified Delphi consensus process to integrate new evidence with clinical expertise. Safety profiles, tolerability data, and real-world effectiveness studies were systematically evaluated. **Results:** The revised algorithm maintains stimulants as first-line treatment but incorporates atomoxetine as an alternative first-line option for patients with comorbid tic disorders, substance abuse concerns, or stimulant intolerance. Extended-release formulations are prioritized to improve adherence. The algorithm includes specific recommendations for preschool children, emphasizing lower starting doses and more frequent monitoring. Alpha-2 agonists are positioned as adjunctive therapy for patients with partial response to stimulants. Updated safety monitoring guidelines address cardiovascular screening and growth parameters. **Conclusions:** The revised TCMAP ADHD algorithm reflects current evidence-based practice, providing clinicians with updated guidance for medication selection, dosing strategies, and safety monitoring. Implementation of these guidelines may improve treatment outcomes and reduce practice variability in pediatric ADHD pharmacotherapy.
journal: Journal of the American Academy of Child and Adolescent Psychiatry

title: Linear quantitation of Abeta aggregation using Thioflavin T: reduction in fibril formation by colostrinin.
abstract: Thioflavin T (ThT) fluorescence is a commonly used method to monitor Abeta protein fibril formation. This method is particularly attractive since ThT fluoresces only when bound to fibrils, the reaction is completed within 1min and ThT does not interfere with aggregation of Abeta fibrils. One of the drawbacks of this method is the lack of a strict quantitative relationship between ThT fluorescence and fibril content. It was observed that, when the same gram molecular weight of Abeta (1-40) is dissolved into varying amounts of base then placed into a constant volume of aqueous buffer, a non-linear fluorescent response is obtained. By maintaining a strict relationship between Abeta content and the volume of base, this anomalous result can be alleviated and a linear dose response curve is obtained at much lower Abeta concentrations than is typically observed. In addition, differences in Abeta batch to batch preparations are alleviated. It was previously reported that colostrinin (CLN), a proline-rich peptide derived from colostrum, reduces fibril content and protects neuroblastoma cells against Abeta peptide-induced toxicity. The newly developed ThT fluorescence protocol was used to quantify Abeta fibril content after treatment with CLN. We also demonstrate that CLN, can solubilize Abeta fibrils in a dose and time-dependent fashion.

title: What have positron emission tomography and 'Zippy' told us about the neuropharmacology of drug addiction?
abstract: Translational molecular imaging with positron emission tomography (PET) and allied technologies offer unrivalled applications in the discovery of biomarkers and aetiological mechanisms relevant to human disease. Foremost among clinical PET findings during the past two decades of addiction research is the seminal discovery of reduced dopamine D(2/3) receptor expression in the striatum of drug addicts, which could indicate a predisposing factor and/or compensatory reaction to the chronic abuse of stimulant drugs. In parallel, recent years have witnessed significant improvements in the performance of small animal tomographs (microPET) and a refinement of animal models of addiction based on clinically relevant diagnostic criteria. This review surveys the utility of PET in the elucidation of neuropharmacological mechanisms underlying drug addiction. It considers the consequences of chronic drug exposure on regional brain metabolism and neurotransmitter function and identifies those areas where further research is needed, especially concerning the implementation of PET tracers targeting neurotransmitter systems other than dopamine, which increasingly have been implicated in the pathophysiology of drug addiction. In addition, this review considers the causal effects of behavioural traits such as impulsivity and novelty/sensation-seeking on the emergence of compulsive drug-taking. Previous research indicates that spontaneously high-impulsive rats--as exemplified by 'Zippy'--are pre-disposed to escalate intravenous cocaine self-administration, and subsequently to develop compulsive drug taking tendencies that endure despite concurrent adverse consequences of such behaviour, just as in human addiction. The discovery using microPET of pre-existing differences in dopamine D(2/3) receptor expression in the striatum of high-impulsive rats suggests a neural endophenotype that may likewise pre-dispose to stimulant addiction in humans.

title: Tobacco smoking and nicotine neuropsychopharmacology: some future research directions.
abstract: Tobacco smoking remains one of the most prevalent forms of drug dependence worldwide, yet fundamental questions regarding nicotine's neuropsychopharmacological mechanisms continue to limit therapeutic interventions. While nicotinic acetylcholine receptors have been established as primary targets for nicotine's reinforcing effects, the complex interactions between receptor subtypes, regional brain distribution, and behavioral outcomes require systematic investigation. This review synthesizes current knowledge of nicotine neuropharmacology and proposes critical research directions for the next decade. Key areas requiring investigation include: (1) characterization of neuronal nicotinic receptor subunit diversity and their differential roles in dependence processes, (2) examination of nicotine's effects on dopaminergic, GABAergic, and glutamatergic neurotransmission in reward circuits, (3) development of animal models that better recapitulate human smoking patterns and individual vulnerability factors, and (4) identification of genetic polymorphisms influencing nicotine metabolism and receptor function. Emerging molecular techniques, including receptor subtype-selective ligands, transgenic mouse models, and in vivo microdialysis, offer unprecedented opportunities to dissect nicotine's neurobiological effects. Additionally, neuroimaging approaches in human subjects may bridge findings from animal studies to clinical populations. Future research should prioritize understanding how chronic nicotine exposure produces long-lasting neuroadaptations that maintain tobacco dependence. Such investigations will be essential for developing more effective pharmacotherapies and identifying individuals at highest risk for tobacco addiction, ultimately reducing the substantial public health burden of smoking-related diseases.
journal: Neuropsychopharmacology : official publication of the American College of Neuropsychopharmacology

title: Cocaine increases stimulated dopamine release more in periadolescent than adult rats.
abstract: Adolescence represents a critical period of brain development characterized by ongoing maturation of dopaminergic systems. Previous studies have demonstrated age-related differences in cocaine's behavioral effects, but the neurochemical basis for these differences remains poorly understood. The present study examined whether cocaine differentially affects dopamine release in the nucleus accumbens of periadolescent (postnatal day 35) versus adult (postnatal day 70) male Sprague-Dawley rats. Using in vivo microdialysis, we measured extracellular dopamine concentrations following acute cocaine administration (10 mg/kg, i.p.) in freely moving animals. Baseline dopamine levels were significantly higher in periadolescent compared to adult rats (2.8 ± 0.3 vs 1.9 ± 0.2 nM, p<0.05). Cocaine administration produced robust increases in extracellular dopamine in both age groups; however, the magnitude of increase was significantly greater in periadolescent animals. Peak dopamine levels reached 847% ± 76% of baseline in periadolescent rats compared to 542% ± 48% in adults (p<0.01). The enhanced dopaminergic response in periadolescent animals persisted for approximately 2 hours longer than in adults. Western blot analysis revealed significantly lower dopamine transporter (DAT) protein expression in periadolescent nucleus accumbens tissue compared to adults, which may partially explain the age-related differences in cocaine sensitivity. These findings suggest that the developing adolescent brain exhibits heightened vulnerability to cocaine's neurochemical effects, potentially contributing to increased addiction liability during this developmental period. Understanding these age-related differences may inform prevention strategies and treatment approaches for adolescent substance abuse.

title: Affect recognition deficits in schizophrenia: neural substrates and psychopharmacological implications.
abstract: Schizophrenia patients demonstrate consistent deficits in facial affect recognition that significantly impact social functioning and quality of life. While these impairments have been well-documented behaviorally, the underlying neural mechanisms and potential therapeutic targets remain poorly understood. This review synthesizes current neuroimaging and psychopharmacological evidence to elucidate the neurobiological basis of affect recognition deficits in schizophrenia and evaluate treatment implications. We conducted a comprehensive literature review of neuroimaging studies examining affect recognition in schizophrenia patients compared to healthy controls, and analyzed clinical trials investigating pharmacological interventions targeting these deficits. Converging neuroimaging evidence demonstrates abnormal activation patterns in the amygdala, fusiform face area, and prefrontal cortical regions during affect recognition tasks in schizophrenia patients. Structural abnormalities in these regions correlate with behavioral deficits, suggesting a neuroanatomical substrate. While typical antipsychotics show limited efficacy for improving affect recognition, emerging evidence suggests that atypical antipsychotics, particularly those with significant serotonergic activity, may provide modest benefits. Adjunctive treatments targeting cholinergic, GABAergic, and glutamatergic systems show preliminary promise in small-scale studies. However, effect sizes remain modest and clinical significance unclear. Affect recognition deficits in schizophrenia involve disrupted limbic-cortical circuitry, with the amygdala and prefrontal regions as key substrates. Current pharmacological approaches provide limited therapeutic benefit, highlighting the need for novel treatment strategies targeting specific neurotransmitter systems underlying social cognitive dysfunction.

title: Drugs used to treat Parkinson's disease, present status and future directions.
abstract: Parkinson's disease (PD) is the second most common neurodegenerative disorder, after Alzheimer's disease. In PD, motor symptoms result from the degeneration and loss of pigmented dopaminergic neurons of the substantia nigra pars compacta of the basal ganglia. Other neuronal fields and neurotransmitter systems are also involved, including non-adrenergic, serotonergic and cholinergic neurons. Since the early 1960s the treatment of PD has been based on the pharmacologic replacement of dopamine accomplished with the precursor of dopamine, 3, 4-dihydroxy-L-phenylalanine (L-dopa). The addition of carbidopa, an inhibitor of the decarboxylase represented a tremendous improvement in therapy and is still a mainstay of the treatment of PD. Dopamine agonists may also be used, as well as inhibitors of monoamine oxidase-B or catechol-O-methyltransferase. Other medications include anticholinergics and amantadine. These therapies are only symptomatic and none halt or lessen dopaminergic neuron degeneration and the progression of the disease. This has prompted the search for novel and alternative pharmacological targets and neuroprotective therapies. In this context, there are data to suggest a benefit from glial cell line-derived neurotrophic factor, neuroimmumophilin ligands, minocycline, Coenzyme Q10, creatine, reduced glutathione, adenosine A2A receptor antagonists as well as glutamate release inhibitors. Restorative techniques to compensate for cell loss include tissue transplantation and gene transfer therapy. Due to the paucity of data regarding non-pharmacological approaches such as diet therapy or antioxidant therapy, these await more studies. There are also few studies on medicinal plants. Other areas of increasing importance would thus include the investigation of active constituents of plants and phytomedicines with a view to the discovery of new compounds. Finally, stem cell therapy may offer the promise of restoring functionality.

title: 7th ISN special neurochemistry conference 'Synaptic function and dysfunction in brain diseases'.
abstract: This article is part of a mini review series: "Synaptic Function and Dysfunction in Brain Diseases".

title: Neuroimmune Pharmacology, 2nd Edition - A Perspective.
abstract: It has been almost nine years since the 1st edition of Neuroimmune Pharmacology was published on May 23rd, 2008. The 2nd edition of Neuroimmune Pharmacology by Ikezu and Gendelman (Editors) with Przedborski, Masliah and Cosentino (Associate Editors) manages to fulfill two separate missions: to provide comprehensive, but highly topical access to a rapidly evolving field and to serve as a standalone reference for scientists and clinicians in need of guidance regarding questions pertinent to neuroimmune pharmacology. Doing a PubMed search on the terms neuroimmune and pharmacology yields 1090 publications, with a first publication by Dougherty and Dafny, published in the Journal of Neuroscience Research in 1988, entitled "Neuroimmune intercommunication, central opioids, and the immune response to bacterial endotoxin." Since 2000, there have been 979 publications using these search terms, with 137 published since the beginning of 2016. The obvious conclusion to be drawn is that this is a burgeoning field that represents the cusp between our understanding of relationships between the immune and nervous systems and how we might treat disease with pharmacologic approaches when normal homeostatic mechanisms go awry.
journal: Journal of neuroimmune pharmacology : the official journal of the Society on NeuroImmune Pharmacology

title: Grafted dopamine neurons: Morphology, neurochemistry, and electrophysiology.
abstract: Grafting of dopamine-rich tissue to counteract the symptoms in Parkinson's disease became a promising tool for future treatment. This article discusses how to improve the functional outcome with respect to graft outgrowth and functions of dopamine release and electrophysiological responses to graft implantation in the host brain striatal target. It has been documented that a subpopulation of the dopamine neurons innervates the host brain in a target-specific manner, while some of the grafted dopamine neurons never project to the host striatum. Neurochemical studies have demonstrated that the graft-induced outgrowth synthesize, store, metabolize and release dopamine and possibly other neurotransmitters such as 5-HT. Furthermore, the released dopamine affects the dopamine-depleted brain in areas that are larger than the graft-derived nerve fibers reach. While stem cells will most likely be the future source of cells to be used in grafting, it is important to find the guiding cues for how to reinnervate the dopamine-depleted striatum in a proper way with respect to the dopamine subpopulations of A9 and A10 to efficiently treat the motor abnormalities seen in Parkinson's disease.

title: From the search for a molecular code of memory to the role of neurotransmitters: a historical perspective.
abstract: The molecular basis of memory formation has been a central pursuit in neuroscience for over five decades, evolving from early theories of protein synthesis to our current understanding of neurotransmitter-mediated synaptic plasticity. This historical review examines the paradigmatic shifts in memory research from 1950 to 2004, analyzing key experimental findings and theoretical frameworks that shaped the field. We conducted a comprehensive literature analysis of landmark studies, tracing the progression from Hyden's early RNA hypothesis through Kandel's work on Aplysia to contemporary investigations of NMDA receptor-dependent long-term potentiation. The search for a discrete "molecular code" of memory initially focused on stable protein modifications and RNA templates as information storage mechanisms. However, accumulating evidence revealed that memory consolidation involves dynamic, network-based processes mediated by neurotransmitter systems rather than static molecular engrams. Critical discoveries include the identification of CREB as a transcriptional switch for memory formation, the role of protein kinase A in synaptic strengthening, and the requirement for de novo protein synthesis during memory consolidation. The field has transitioned from reductionist models seeking single molecular determinants to systems-level approaches emphasizing neurotransmitter-receptor interactions, particularly glutamatergic and cholinergic signaling cascades. These findings demonstrate that memory formation emerges from coordinated neurotransmitter-mediated changes in synaptic efficacy rather than discrete molecular codes, fundamentally reshaping our understanding of neural plasticity mechanisms and providing new therapeutic targets for memory-related disorders.

title: Principles of motivation revealed by the diverse functions of neuropharmacological and neuroanatomical substrates underlying feeding behavior.
abstract: The neural mechanisms underlying feeding behavior represent a complex integration of homeostatic, hedonic, and cognitive processes that provide fundamental insights into motivational systems. While hypothalamic circuits have long been recognized as primary regulators of energy balance, emerging evidence demonstrates that feeding behavior engages distributed neural networks involving dopaminergic reward pathways, limbic structures, and prefrontal regulatory regions. This review synthesizes current understanding of the neuropharmacological and neuroanatomical substrates controlling food intake, with particular emphasis on how these systems reveal broader principles of motivated behavior. We examine the roles of key neurotransmitter systems, including dopamine, serotonin, GABA, and neuropeptides such as orexin and NPY, in mediating different aspects of feeding motivation. The hypothalamic arcuate nucleus emerges as a critical integration hub, receiving peripheral metabolic signals and coordinating responses through projections to brainstem and forebrain regions. Dopaminergic circuits in the ventral tegmental area and nucleus accumbens process food reward and incentive salience, while prefrontal cortical areas exert top-down control over feeding decisions. Dysregulation of these interconnected systems contributes to obesity and eating disorders, highlighting their clinical relevance. The multifaceted nature of feeding control illuminates general principles governing motivated behavior, including the integration of homeostatic need states with reward processing, the role of learning and memory in shaping behavioral responses, and the hierarchical organization of neural control systems. Understanding these principles advances both basic neuroscience and therapeutic approaches to metabolic disorders.

title: [Regulatory molecular mechanisms of the neurochemical processes. History and the present time].
abstract: The understanding of regulatory molecular mechanisms governing neurochemical processes has evolved dramatically over the past century, from early observations of synaptic transmission to contemporary insights into complex signaling cascades. This review examines the historical development and current state of knowledge regarding molecular regulation in neurotransmission, focusing on key discoveries that have shaped modern neuropharmacology. We analyzed seminal publications from 1900-2003, tracing the progression from Dale's principle through the identification of neurotransmitter receptors, second messenger systems, and gene expression regulation. Historical analysis reveals three major evolutionary phases: the classical period (1900-1950) characterized by neurotransmitter identification, the receptor era (1950-1980) marked by pharmacological classification of binding sites, and the molecular biology revolution (1980-present) enabling detailed characterization of signaling mechanisms. Contemporary research demonstrates that neurochemical regulation involves intricate networks of G-protein coupled receptors, ion channels, protein kinases, and transcription factors operating across multiple temporal scales. Key regulatory mechanisms include presynaptic autoreceptor feedback, postsynaptic receptor desensitization, synaptic plasticity through CREB-mediated gene expression, and glial-neuronal interactions. Recent advances in molecular biology techniques have revealed previously unknown complexity in neurotransmitter systems, including receptor heteromerization, epigenetic modifications, and non-coding RNA regulation. Understanding these multilevel regulatory mechanisms provides crucial insights for developing targeted therapeutic interventions in neurological and psychiatric disorders, representing a paradigm shift from traditional pharmacological approaches toward precision neuromedicine based on molecular pathophysiology.

title: Self-powered electrochemical systems as neurochemical sensors: toward self-triggered in vivo analysis of brain chemistry.
abstract: Real-time in vivo analysis of neurochemical dynamics has great physiological and pathological implications for a full understanding of the brain. Self-powered electrochemical systems (SPESs) built on galvanic cell configurations bear the advantages of easy miniaturization for implantation and no interference to electric activities of neurons over traditional externally-powered electrochemical sensors for self-triggered in vivo analysis. However, this is still a new concept for in vivo neurochemical sensing with few implanted examples reported so far. This tutorial review summarizes the development of SPESs toward implantable applications from both principal and practical perspectives, ultimately aimed at providing a guide map to the future design of neurochemical sensors for in vivo analysis of brain chemistry.

title: Succi nervorum: a brief history of neurochemistry.
abstract: The nature of intracellular communication and integration in the central nervous system remained a source of controversy long after it had been accepted that the brain is intrinsically involved in the reception of external and internal sensory impressions, in the control of both voluntary and involuntary physiological functions, and in the processes associated with consciousness and psychic function in humans. The role of the specific chemistry of the brain in these functions was specifically addressed only in the 20th century, although chemical examination of brain tissue can be traced at least as far back as 1719 to Hensing's Cerebri examen chemicum. Throughout the 1940s and 1950s evidence accumulated from a variety of laboratories that certain chemical substances, such as acetylcholine, noradrenaline and histamine, might be involved in central nervous system neurotransmission, but conclusive evidence for such communication was difficult to obtain. Commencing with Carlsson's 1957 paper on the anti-reserpine effects of DOPA and culminating in the successful amelioration of parkinsonian akinesia by Birkmayer and Hornykiewicz via administration of L-DOPA in 1961, followed by the identification of specific nervous tracts which utilized dopamine as a transmitter, chemical neurotransmission in the brain was ultimately demonstrated through a combination of pharmacological, physiological and clinical research. Neurochemistry had thereby graduated from a branch of general physiology to being centrally involved in models of central nervous system function.

title: Neuroglialpharmacology: white matter pathophysiologies and psychiatric treatments.
abstract: Psychotropic treatments such as second generation or atypical antipsychotics are efficacious in a wide spectrum of psychiatric disorders ranging from schizophrenia to depression, bipolar disorder, and autism. These treatments are associated with peripheral metabolic derangements that are often also present in drug-naive patients. Furthermore, altering lipid composition/levels (with omega 3 fatty acids) and ameliorating oxidative toxicities may treat/prevent disease. The above observations are reexamined from the perspective of a myelin-centered model of the human brain. The model proposes that the human brain's extensive myelination required higher metabolic resources that caused evolutionary adaptations resulting in our quadratic (inverted U) myelination trajectory that peaks in the sixth decade of life. It further proposes that optimal brain function depends on exquisite action potential synchronization that myelin makes possible and that myelin's exceptional vulnerability to subtle metabolic/oxidative abnormalities may promote both developmental and degenerative diseases. Available data are integrated herein to suggest that widely used psychotropic treatments have under-appreciated CNS metabolic and neurotransmitter effects on myelination, its plasticity, and repair that may substantially contribute to their mechanisms of action.

title: The use of biomarkers in psychiatric research: how serotonin transporter occupancy explains the dose-response curves of SSRIs.
abstract: Selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors (SSRIs) demonstrate considerable interindividual variability in therapeutic response and optimal dosing, with clinical dose-response relationships often appearing nonlinear or plateau-shaped. While therapeutic drug monitoring has shown limited utility for SSRIs due to poor correlation between plasma concentrations and clinical outcomes, neuroimaging biomarkers may provide superior insights into central nervous system target engagement. This study investigated whether serotonin transporter (SERT) occupancy measurements could explain the pharmacodynamic basis of SSRI dose-response curves and inform personalized dosing strategies. We conducted a systematic review of positron emission tomography (PET) studies measuring SERT occupancy across different SSRI doses, combined with meta-analysis of clinical efficacy data from randomized controlled trials. PET studies using [11C]DASB tracer were identified through PubMed and cross-referenced with corresponding clinical trials reporting Hamilton Depression Rating Scale scores. Twenty-three PET studies encompassing 312 subjects and 41 clinical trials with 8,847 patients were analyzed. SERT occupancy demonstrated a hyperbolic relationship with SSRI dose, reaching 50-80% occupancy at standard therapeutic doses across different agents. Clinical response curves paralleled occupancy data, with maximal efficacy occurring at 70-85% SERT occupancy. Higher occupancy levels (>85%) were associated with increased adverse effects without additional therapeutic benefit. These findings suggest that SERT occupancy represents a valuable biomarker for optimizing SSRI dosing, potentially explaining the plateau effect observed in clinical practice. Implementation of occupancy-guided dosing strategies may improve treatment outcomes while minimizing adverse effects, supporting the integration of neuroimaging biomarkers into psychiatric clinical practice.

title: Academic psychiatry and the pharmaceutical industry.
abstract: The relationship between academic psychiatry and the pharmaceutical industry has become increasingly complex, raising concerns about potential conflicts of interest that may influence research integrity and clinical practice. This study examined the extent and nature of financial relationships between academic psychiatrists and pharmaceutical companies, and their potential impact on research outcomes and clinical recommendations. We conducted a comprehensive survey of 847 academic psychiatrists at 25 major medical institutions across the United States, supplemented by analysis of pharmaceutical company disclosure reports and published research from 2003-2005. Additionally, we performed a systematic review of 312 randomized controlled trials in major psychiatry journals to assess correlations between industry funding and reported outcomes. Our findings revealed that 78% of academic psychiatrists reported some form of financial relationship with pharmaceutical companies, including consulting fees (45%), research grants (52%), speakers' bureau participation (31%), and equity holdings (12%). Industry-funded studies were significantly more likely to report favorable outcomes for the sponsor's drug compared to independently funded research (OR = 3.4, 95% CI: 2.1-5.6, p < 0.001). Furthermore, 23% of department chairs reported that industry relationships influenced faculty hiring and promotion decisions. These results demonstrate widespread financial entanglements between academic psychiatry and pharmaceutical companies that may compromise scientific objectivity and patient care. Academic medical centers must develop more stringent conflict-of-interest policies and transparency requirements to preserve the integrity of psychiatric research and maintain public trust in the profession.

title: Pitfalls in presenting and interpreting clinical trial data.
abstract: Clinical trials in psychopharmacology have become increasingly complex, yet significant methodological and interpretive issues continue to compromise the validity of reported findings. This review examines common pitfalls encountered in the presentation and analysis of clinical trial data in neuropharmacological research. We analyzed 127 published clinical trials of psychotropic medications from 1990-1994, focusing on study design, statistical methodology, and data interpretation practices. Major deficiencies identified include inadequate sample size calculations (found in 43% of studies), inappropriate statistical analyses such as multiple comparisons without correction (38%), selective reporting of favorable outcomes (31%), and misinterpretation of statistical significance versus clinical significance (29%). Post-hoc analyses were frequently presented without appropriate caveats in 47% of trials. Dropout rates exceeding 30% were inadequately addressed in 52% of studies, with last-observation-carried-forward methods applied inappropriately. Placebo response rates varied dramatically (15-65%) across similar conditions, suggesting heterogeneous patient populations or study conduct issues. Adverse event reporting was inconsistent, with systematic collection methods described in only 34% of trials. Industry-sponsored studies showed significantly higher rates of positive primary outcomes compared to investigator-initiated trials (78% vs. 52%, p<0.01). These findings highlight critical areas requiring attention to enhance the reliability and clinical utility of psychopharmacological research. Standardized reporting guidelines, appropriate statistical consultation, and enhanced peer review processes are essential to address these systematic weaknesses in clinical trial methodology and interpretation.
