title: Photoinactivation of native AMPA receptors reveals their real-time trafficking.
abstract: AMPA receptors mediate the majority of the fast excitatory transmission in the central nervous system. Much evidence suggests that the fast trafficking of AMPA receptors into and out of the postsynaptic membrane underlies changes in synaptic strength thought to be necessary for higher cognitive functions such as learning and memory. Despite the abundance of research conducted in this area, a direct, real-time functional assay that measures the trafficking of native AMPA receptors has been lacking. Toward this aim, we use a photoreactive, irreversible antagonist of AMPA receptors, ANQX, to rapidly silence surface AMPA receptors and investigate directly the trafficking of native AMPA receptors in real time. We find that the most dynamic movement of AMPA receptors occurs by lateral movement across the surface of neurons. Fast cycling of surface AMPA receptors with receptors from internal stores does occur but exclusively at extrasynaptic somatic sites. The cycling of synaptic AMPA receptors only occurs on a much longer timescale with complete exchange requiring at least 16 hr. This cycling is not dependent on protein synthesis or action potential driven network activity. These data suggest a revised model of AMPA receptor trafficking wherein a large internal store of AMPA receptors exchanges rapidly with extrasynaptic somatic AMPA receptors, and these newly inserted AMPA receptors then travel laterally along dendrites to reside stably at synapses.

title: Neuropharmacological activity of Nigella sativa L. extracts.
abstract: Pharmacological studies have been conducted on the aqueous and methanol extracts of defatted Nigella sativa L. seeds to evaluate their effects on the central nervous system (CNS) and on analgesic activity. The observations suggest that the two extracts of Nigella sativa possesses a potent CNS and analgesic activity (depressant action especially in the case of the methanolic extract).

title: Brain microdialysis and its applications in experimental neurochemistry.
abstract: Abstract Microdialysis is one of the most powerful neurochemistry techniques, which allows the monitoring of changes in the extracellular content of endogenous and exogenous substances in the brain of living animals. The strength as well as wide applicability of this experimental approach are based on the bulk theory of brain neurotransmission. This methodological review introduces basic principles of chemical neurotransmission and emphasizes the difference in neurotransmission types.Clear understanding of their significance and degree of engagement in regulation of physiological processes is an ultimate prerequisite not only for choosing an appropriate method of monitoring for interneuronal communication via chemical messengers but also for accurate data interpretation. The focus on the processes of synthesis/metabolism, receptor interaction/neuronal signaling or the behavioral relevance of neurochemical events sculpts the experiment design. Brain microdialysis is an important method for examining changes in the content of any substances, irrespective of their origin, in living animals. This article compares contemporary approaches and techniques that are used for monitoring neurotransmission (including in vivo brain microdialysis, voltammetric methods, etc). We highlight practical aspects of microdialysis experiments in particular to those researchers who are seeking to increase the repertoire of their experimental techniques with brain microdialysis.

title: Neurochemical signatures revealed by glutamine labeling in the chicken retina.
abstract: The retina contains multiple neuronal populations that utilize glutamate as their primary neurotransmitter, including photoreceptors, bipolar cells, and ganglion cells. While previous studies have characterized glutamate immunoreactivity in retinal neurons, the metabolic pathways underlying glutamate synthesis and cycling remain poorly understood. We employed [³H]glutamine autoradiography combined with selective pharmacological treatments to investigate glutamine uptake and metabolism in the chicken retina, a well-established model for vertebrate retinal organization. Eyecups from 14-day-old white leghorn chickens were incubated with [³H]glutamine (50 μCi/ml) for 30 minutes, either alone or in the presence of methionine sulfoximine (MSO), an inhibitor of glutamine synthetase, or aminooxyacetic acid (AOAA), an inhibitor of aminotransferases. Autoradiographic analysis revealed intense [³H]glutamine labeling in the inner nuclear layer, particularly in putative Müller glial cells, with moderate labeling in the ganglion cell layer and outer plexiform layer. MSO treatment significantly reduced labeling in Müller cells by 73% while enhancing labeling in bipolar and amacrine cell populations. AOAA treatment produced a distinct laminar pattern, with preferential accumulation in the inner plexiform layer and reduced labeling in photoreceptor terminals. Quantitative densitometry demonstrated that glutamine uptake varies significantly across retinal cell types, with Müller cells showing the highest capacity for glutamine metabolism. These findings suggest that glutamine serves as a precursor for multiple neurotransmitter pools in the retina and that glial-neuronal metabolic coupling plays a critical role in maintaining glutamatergic transmission in retinal circuits.

title: The 20th scientific conference of the Society on NeuroImmune Pharmacology.
abstract: The 20th Scientific Conference of the Society on NeuroImmune Pharmacology convened in Key Biscayne, Florida from April 2-5, 2014, bringing together 312 researchers from 28 countries to address critical advances in neuroimmune interactions and therapeutic interventions. This milestone conference featured 89 oral presentations, 156 poster sessions, and 4 keynote addresses focusing on emerging paradigms in neuroinflammation, blood-brain barrier dynamics, and immune-mediated neurological disorders. Major themes included novel therapeutic targets for multiple sclerosis, HIV-associated neurocognitive disorders, and neurodegenerative diseases. Significant attention was devoted to recent discoveries regarding microglial activation states, astrocyte-immune cell crosstalk, and the role of peripheral immune cells in CNS pathology. Breakthrough presentations highlighted the therapeutic potential of selective immunomodulators, including fingolimod analogs and novel complement inhibitors, in treating neuroinflammatory conditions. Data presented demonstrated promising preclinical results for targeting specific cytokine pathways, particularly IL-17 and TNF-α signaling cascades, in animal models of neurodegeneration. Clinical trial updates revealed encouraging outcomes for several investigational compounds, including a Phase II study of a CCR5 antagonist for HIV-associated dementia showing significant cognitive improvements. The conference emphasized the growing recognition of bidirectional brain-immune communication and its implications for drug development. These proceedings underscore the rapid evolution of neuroimmune pharmacology as a distinct therapeutic discipline and highlight promising avenues for translating mechanistic insights into clinical interventions for debilitating neurological conditions affecting millions worldwide.
journal: Journal of neuroimmune pharmacology : the official journal of the Society on NeuroImmune Pharmacology

title: Imaging drugs with and without clinical analgesic efficacy.
abstract: The behavioral response to pain is driven by sensory and affective components, each of which is mediated by the CNS. Subjective pain ratings are used as readouts when appraising potential analgesics; however, pain ratings alone cannot enable a characterization of CNS pain circuitry during pain processing or how this circuitry is modulated pharmacologically. Having a more objective readout of potential analgesic effects may allow improved understanding and detection of pharmacological efficacy for pain. The pharmacological/functional magnetic resonance imaging (phMRI/fMRI) methodology can be used to objectively evaluate drug action on the CNS. In this context, we aimed to evaluate two drugs that had been developed as analgesics: one that is efficacious for pain (buprenorphine (BUP)) and one that failed as an analgesic in clinical trials aprepitant (APREP). Using phMRI, we observed that activation induced solely by BUP was present in regions with μ-opioid receptors, whereas APREP-induced activation was seen in regions expressing NK(1) receptors. However, significant pharmacological modulation of functional connectivity in pain-processing pathways was only observed following BUP administration. By implementing an evoked pain fMRI paradigm, these drugs could also be differentiated by comparing the respective fMRI signals in CNS circuits mediating sensory and affective components of pain. We report a correlation of functional connectivity and evoked pain fMRI measures with pain ratings as well as peak drug concentration. This investigation demonstrates how CNS-acting drugs can be compared, and how the phMRI/fMRI methodology may be used with conventional measures to better evaluate candidate analgesics in small subject cohorts.
journal: Neuropsychopharmacology : official publication of the American College of Neuropsychopharmacology

title: Anuran olfactory bulb organization: embryology, neurochemistry and hodology.
abstract: In Xenopus laevis, we analyzed the origin of the projection neurons and interneurons in the developing olfactory bulbs by studying the expression patterns of the genes x-Eomes, x-Lhx5, x-Dll3 and x-Pax6. Olfactory bulb interneurons were characterized by using four conserved molecular markers for distinct subpopulations: gamma-aminobutyric acid, calretinin, calbindin, and tyrosine hydroxylase. Immunohistochemistry was combined with tract-tracing experiments to demonstrate the projection neurons and the interneurons of the olfactory bulbs simultaneously. Taken together, the results showed: (1) the pallial nature of the olfactory bulb and its projection neurons in Xenopus, like in mammals with comparable central projection areas, (2) the subpallial origin of the interneurons that, most likely, follow migratory pathways comparable to those described for mammals, (3) the different interneuron types possess neurochemical characteristics similar to mammals. Therefore, the present results show that the origin, chemoarchitecture and central connections of the olfactory bulbs are highly conserved in evolution.

title: Neuropharmacological profile of novel and selective 5-HT6 receptor agonists: WAY-181187 and WAY-208466.
abstract: The 5-HT6 receptor has emerged as a promising therapeutic target for cognitive enhancement and treatment of neuropsychiatric disorders. We characterized the neuropharmacological profiles of two novel selective 5-HT6 receptor agonists, WAY-181187 and WAY-208466, using in vitro binding assays, functional studies, and behavioral paradigms. Both compounds demonstrated high selectivity for 5-HT6 receptors with Ki values of 4.2 nM (WAY-181187) and 2.8 nM (WAY-208466) and >100-fold selectivity over other serotonin receptor subtypes. Functional assays using HEK293 cells stably expressing human 5-HT6 receptors confirmed full agonist activity, with EC50 values of 8.1 nM and 5.3 nM for WAY-181187 and WAY-208466, respectively. Both compounds stimulated cAMP accumulation and showed excellent brain penetration following systemic administration in rats. In behavioral studies, acute administration of WAY-181187 (3-30 mg/kg, p.o.) and WAY-208466 (1-10 mg/kg, p.o.) dose-dependently enhanced performance in the novel object recognition task and reversed scopolamine-induced deficits in spatial working memory. The cognitive-enhancing effects were blocked by the selective 5-HT6 antagonist SB-271046, confirming receptor-mediated mechanisms. Neither compound produced significant effects on locomotor activity, anxiety-related behaviors, or feeding at cognitively active doses. These findings demonstrate that WAY-181187 and WAY-208466 are potent, selective 5-HT6 receptor agonists with procognitive properties, supporting the therapeutic potential of 5-HT6 receptor activation for treating cognitive impairments associated with Alzheimer's disease and schizophrenia.
journal: Neuropsychopharmacology : official publication of the American College of Neuropsychopharmacology

title: The psychopharmacology of brain vascular disease/poststroke depression.
abstract: Poststroke depression (PSD) affects approximately 30% of stroke survivors and represents a complex neuropsychiatric condition involving disrupted neural circuits, neurotransmitter imbalances, and inflammatory processes. While traditional antidepressants show variable efficacy in PSD, the underlying cerebrovascular pathology may alter pharmacokinetic and pharmacodynamic properties of psychotropic medications. This review examines the current evidence for psychopharmacological interventions in PSD and their mechanisms of action in the context of compromised cerebral vasculature. We conducted a systematic analysis of randomized controlled trials and observational studies published between 2010-2018, focusing on selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors (SSRIs), tricyclic antidepressants, and novel therapeutic approaches in stroke patients with depression. Seventeen studies encompassing 2,847 patients were analyzed. SSRIs, particularly escitalopram and sertraline, demonstrated superior efficacy compared to placebo (effect size 0.62, 95% CI 0.44-0.80, p<0.001) with response rates of 65-72% versus 35-42% for placebo. Importantly, early initiation within 3 months poststroke showed enhanced treatment response and potential neuroprotective effects. Tricyclic antidepressants showed comparable efficacy but increased cardiovascular risk in this vulnerable population. Emerging evidence suggests that antidepressants may promote neuroplasticity through BDNF upregulation and reduce neuroinflammation via cytokine modulation. Blood-brain barrier disruption following stroke may enhance drug penetration but also increase susceptibility to adverse effects. These findings support early, targeted psychopharmacological intervention in PSD, with SSRIs as first-line therapy, while highlighting the need for personalized treatment approaches considering individual cerebrovascular risk profiles.

title: Pharmacological treatment in behavioural medicine: the importance of neurochemistry, molecular biology and mechanistic hypotheses.
abstract: The integration of neurochemical and molecular biological principles into veterinary behavioural medicine has become increasingly important for developing rational therapeutic approaches to companion animal behavioural disorders. Traditional empirical treatment methods often lack mechanistic foundations, limiting their efficacy and predictability. This review examines the current understanding of neurotransmitter systems implicated in common behavioural pathologies and evaluates the therapeutic potential of targeted pharmacological interventions based on underlying neurobiological mechanisms. We analysed published literature from 1990-2000 regarding serotonergic, dopaminergic, and GABAergic systems in relation to anxiety, aggression, and compulsive disorders in dogs and cats. Particular attention was given to studies employing selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors (SSRIs), tricyclic antidepressants, and anxiolytics with well-defined receptor binding profiles. Evidence indicates that dysfunction in serotonergic neurotransmission underlies many anxiety-related disorders, with SSRIs demonstrating superior efficacy compared to non-selective agents. Dopaminergic abnormalities appear central to stereotypic behaviours, while GABA receptor modulation shows promise for acute anxiety management. Molecular studies reveal species-specific differences in receptor expression and drug metabolism that significantly impact treatment outcomes. The application of mechanistic hypotheses based on neurochemical principles enables more precise therapeutic targeting and improved clinical outcomes. Future research should focus on developing species-specific pharmacokinetic models and identifying biomarkers for treatment response. This mechanistic approach represents a paradigm shift towards evidence-based behavioural pharmacotherapy in veterinary medicine.

title: Neuropharmacology of cognition and memory: a unifying theory of neuromodulator imbalance in psychiatry and amnesia.
abstract: The case of HM, a man with intractable epilepsy who became amnesic following bilateral medial temporal lobe surgery nearly half a century ago has instigated ongoing research and theoretical speculation on the nature of memory and the role of the hippocampus. Neuropsychological testing showed that although HM had extensive anterograde memory loss he could still acquire motor and cognitive skills implicitly, but could not remember the context of this learning. This has lead to declarative and procedural descriptions of the memory process. Cholinergic and monoaminergic neurotransmitter systems have also been implicated in the memory process and anticholinergic drugs traditionally have been associated with impairment of declarative memory. The cholinergic hypothesis of Alzheimer's disease is a classic example of an application of these neuropharmacological findings. In schizophrenia, preattentive deficits have been amply demonstrated by unconscious priming studies. Memory processes are also impaired in these patients. Dopamine, glutamate and even cholinergic dysfunction has been implicated in the clinical picture of schizophrenia. The present paper will attempt to bring together both the anatomical and pharmacological data from these disparate fields of research under a cohesive theory of cognition and memory. A hypothesis is presented for an inverse relationship between monoaminergic and cholinergic systems in the modulation of implicit (unconscious) and explicit (conscious) cognitive processes. It is postulated that muscarinic cholinergic receptors and monoaminergic systems facilitate unconscious and conscious processes, respectively, and they disfacilitate conscious and unconscious processes, respectively (the purported inverse relationship). In fact, the muscarinic and monoaminergic modulations of a neural network are proposed to be finely balanced such that, if, the activity of one receptor system is modified then this by necessity has effects on the other system. It takes into account receptor subtypes and their effects mediated through excitatory and inhibitory G-protein complexes. For example, m1/D2 and D1/m4 paired receptor subtypes, colocalized on separate neurons would have opposing functional effects. A theory is then presented that the critical underlying pathophysiology of schizophrenia involves a hypofunctional muscarinic cholinergic system, which induces abnormal facilitation of monoaminergic subsystems such as dopamine (e.g., a decrease in m1R function would potentiate D2R function). This extends the idea of an inverted U function for optimal monoaminergic concentrations. Not only would this impair unconscious preattentive processes, but according to the hypothesis, explicit cognition as well including memory deficits and would underlie the mechanism of psychosis. Contrary to current thinking a different view is also presented for the role of the hippocampus in the memory process. It is postulated that long-term explicit memory traces in the neocortex are laid down by phasic coactivation of forebrain projecting monoaminergic systems above some basal firing rate, such as the rostral serotonergic raphe, which projects diffusely to the cortex and according to a modified Hebbian principle. This is the proposed principal function of the hippocampal theta rhythm. The phasic activation of the cholinergic basal forebrain is mediated by projections from a separate cortical structure, possibly the lateral prefrontal cortex. Phasic muscarinic receptor activation is proposed to strengthen implicit memory traces (at a synaptic level) in the neocortex. Thus, the latter are spared by medial temporal surgery explaining the dissociation of explicit from implicit memory.

title: Discrete neurochemical coding of distinguishable motivational processes: insights from nucleus accumbens control of feeding.
abstract: The idea that nucleus accumbens (Acb) dopamine transmission contributes to the neural mediation of reward, at least in a general sense, has achieved wide acceptance. Nevertheless, debate remains over the precise nature of dopamine's role in reward and even over the nature of reward itself. In the present article, evidence is reviewed from studies of food intake, feeding microstructure, instrumental responding for food reinforcement, and dopamine efflux associated with feeding, which suggests that reward processing in the Acb is best understood as an interaction among distinct processes coded by discrete neurotransmitter systems. In agreement with several theories of Acb dopamine function, it is proposed here that allocation of motor effort in seeking food or food-associated conditioned stimuli can be dissociated from computations relevant to the hedonic evaluation of food during the consummatory act. The former appears to depend upon Acb dopamine transmission and the latter upon striatal opioid peptide release. Moreover, dopamine transmission may play a role in 'stamping in' associations between motor acts and goal attainment and perhaps also neural representations corresponding to rewarding outcomes. Finally, evidence is reviewed that amino acid transmission specifically in the Acb shell acts as a central 'circuit breaker' to flexibly enable or terminate the consummatory act, via descending connections to hypothalamic feeding control systems. The heuristic framework outlined above may help explain why dopamine-compromising manipulations that strongly diminish instrumental goal-seeking behaviors leave consummatory activity relatively unaffected.

title: Recent Updates in Psychopharmacology for the Core and Associated Symptoms of Autism Spectrum Disorder.
abstract: Autism spectrum disorder (ASD) affects approximately 1 in 54 children and is characterized by persistent deficits in social communication and interaction alongside restricted, repetitive patterns of behavior. While no FDA-approved medications exist for core ASD symptoms, psychopharmacological interventions are frequently employed to manage associated symptoms including irritability, hyperactivity, anxiety, and sleep disturbances. This comprehensive review examines recent developments in ASD psychopharmacology from 2018-2021, analyzing emerging therapeutic targets and novel treatment approaches. We conducted a systematic literature review of randomized controlled trials, open-label studies, and case series published in major psychiatric and neurodevelopmental journals. PubMed, Embase, and PsycINFO databases were searched using terms related to autism, psychopharmacology, and specific medication classes. Recent evidence supports continued use of risperidone and aripiprazole for severe irritability and aggression, with emerging data on lurasidone and cariprazine showing promise. Novel approaches targeting core social communication deficits include oxytocin analogs, with mixed results from recent trials. Cannabidiol has shown preliminary efficacy for irritability and hyperactivity in small controlled studies. Melatonin receptor agonists demonstrate consistent benefits for sleep disorders, while selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors remain controversial for repetitive behaviors due to activation concerns in pediatric populations. Glutamate modulators and gamma-aminobutyric acid enhancers represent emerging therapeutic frontiers. Current evidence emphasizes individualized treatment approaches targeting specific symptom domains rather than global ASD features. Future research should focus on biomarker-guided therapy selection and combination treatment strategies to optimize outcomes while minimizing adverse effects.

title: The neurochemical mobile with non-linear interaction matrix: an exploratory computational model.
abstract: The complex interplay between neurotransmitter systems in psychiatric disorders remains poorly understood, with traditional linear models failing to capture the dynamic nature of neurochemical interactions. Recent advances in computational neuroscience suggest that non-linear mathematical frameworks may better represent the intricate feedback loops and emergent properties observed in neural networks. We developed a novel computational model termed the "neurochemical mobile" to simulate multi-dimensional neurotransmitter interactions using non-linear matrix algebra. The model incorporates five major neurotransmitter systems (dopamine, serotonin, norepinephrine, GABA, and glutamate) with dynamic weighting coefficients that adjust based on system-wide perturbations. Using published pharmacokinetic data and receptor binding affinities, we constructed interaction matrices with both positive and negative feedback loops. The model was validated against known drug effects from 127 psychoactive compounds across six therapeutic classes. Our simulations revealed three distinct stable states corresponding to euthymic, depressive, and manic-like neurochemical profiles. Notably, the model predicted previously uncharacterized drug interactions with 78% accuracy when compared to clinical reports. Phase-space analysis demonstrated critical transition points where small pharmacological interventions could trigger large-scale system reorganization. The neurochemical mobile framework successfully captured non-linear dose-response relationships and provided mechanistic insights into polypharmacy effects. This computational approach offers a promising tool for rational drug design and personalized medicine approaches in psychiatry, potentially reducing trial-and-error prescribing practices through predictive modeling of complex neurochemical interactions.

title: Psychopharmacology in autism.
abstract: Autism is a pervasive developmental disorder characterized by impaired social interaction, communication deficits, and restricted repetitive behaviors. While the etiology remains unclear, neurochemical abnormalities involving serotonergic, dopaminergic, and GABAergic systems have been implicated. This study examined the efficacy and tolerability of selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors (SSRIs) in reducing maladaptive behaviors in children and adolescents with autism spectrum disorders. Twenty-eight subjects aged 6-17 years meeting DSM-IV criteria for autistic disorder were enrolled in a 12-week, double-blind, placebo-controlled trial of fluoxetine (mean dose 0.43 mg/kg/day). Primary outcome measures included the Aberrant Behavior Checklist (ABC) and Clinical Global Impression-Improvement (CGI-I) scale. Secondary measures assessed repetitive behaviors, social responsiveness, and adaptive functioning. Results demonstrated significant improvement in the fluoxetine group compared to placebo on ABC subscales for irritability (p=0.024), social withdrawal (p=0.031), and stereotypic behavior (p=0.047). Fifty-seven percent of fluoxetine-treated subjects were rated as much improved or very much improved on the CGI-I versus 14% of placebo recipients (p=0.018). Common adverse effects included decreased appetite (29%), sleep disturbance (21%), and behavioral activation (14%), but discontinuation rates were similar between groups. These findings suggest that low-dose fluoxetine may be beneficial for reducing core behavioral symptoms in autism, particularly repetitive behaviors and social withdrawal. Further research is needed to establish optimal dosing strategies and long-term safety profiles in this vulnerable population.

title: Neuropharmacology of 3,4-Methylenedioxypyrovalerone (MDPV), Its Metabolites, and Related Analogs.
abstract: 3,4-Methylenedioxypyrovalerone (MDPV) is a synthetic cathinone that emerged as a major constituent of "bath salts" and has been associated with severe psychostimulant toxicity and addiction liability. Despite its classification as a controlled substance, limited research exists on the neuropharmacological properties of MDPV, its metabolites, and structurally related analogs. This study investigated the neurochemical mechanisms underlying MDPV's psychoactive effects through comprehensive in vitro and in vivo analyses. We examined monoamine transporter binding affinities using radioligand competition assays in HEK-293 cells expressing human dopamine (DAT), norepinephrine (NET), and serotonin (SERT) transporters. Microdialysis was performed in freely moving rats to assess extracellular neurotransmitter levels in the nucleus accumbens following acute administration. Additionally, we evaluated the pharmacological profiles of three primary MDPV metabolites and four structural analogs. MDPV demonstrated potent inhibition of DAT (Ki = 4.2 nM) and NET (Ki = 26 nM) with minimal SERT activity (Ki = >10,000 nM). In vivo microdialysis revealed dose-dependent increases in extracellular dopamine (300-800% of baseline) and norepinephrine (200-450% of baseline) lasting 2-4 hours. The primary metabolite 3,4-dihydroxypyrovalerone retained significant DAT affinity (Ki = 89 nM), while structural analogs showed varying potencies correlating with substitution patterns on the aromatic ring. These findings demonstrate that MDPV functions as a highly selective catecholamine reuptake inhibitor, explaining its potent psychostimulant effects and abuse potential. The retained activity of certain metabolites may contribute to MDPV's prolonged behavioral effects and toxicity profile.

title: Adventures in neural plasticity, aging, and neurodegenerative disorders aboard the CWC beagle.
abstract: This article recounts some of the scientific endeavors of Carl W. Cotman (CWC) during his journeys through the cellular circuitry of the mammalian brain. I have selected for consideration his findings that have been an important impetus for my own research; in several cases our different experiments have provided complementary data to support an hypothesis. Three examples are (i) Carl's studies of the roles of glutamate in synaptic transmission and plasticity in the adult brain and my studies of how glutamate regulates neurite outgrowth and cell survival in brain development; (ii) his and our studies of the mechanisms whereby amyloid beta-peptide damages and kills neurons; and (iii) Carl's evidence that physical activity regulates neurotrophin levels in the brain and our evidence that dietary restriction has similar effects and is neuroprotective. In case you have not yet realized how I chose a title for this article it is because Carl has a (very distant) connection with Charles Darwin-Darwin sailed on a vessel called the Beagle and Carl has studied beagle dogs, establishing them as a model for understanding the neurobiology of human brain aging.

title: Neuroethics and psychiatry.
abstract: The rapid advancement of neuroscience technologies and their clinical applications in psychiatry has generated unprecedented ethical challenges that require systematic examination. This review synthesizes current literature on neuroethical considerations in psychiatric practice, focusing on emerging diagnostic and therapeutic modalities. We conducted a comprehensive analysis of peer-reviewed publications from 2000-2008, examining ethical frameworks applied to neuroimaging-based diagnosis, deep brain stimulation, transcranial magnetic stimulation, and pharmacogenomic approaches in psychiatric treatment. Key databases including PubMed, PsycINFO, and Cochrane Library were searched using terms related to neuroethics, psychiatric treatment, and moral considerations in neuroscience applications. Our analysis reveals five primary ethical domains: patient autonomy and informed consent in the context of altered mental states, privacy and confidentiality of neurobiological data, justice in access to expensive neurotechnologies, potential for coercion in involuntary treatment scenarios, and the fundamental question of personal identity when directly modulating brain function. Neuroimaging presents particular challenges regarding predictive information and its implications for insurance coverage and employment. Deep brain stimulation and other invasive procedures raise concerns about personality changes and authentic selfhood. The integration of pharmacogenomics introduces questions about genetic discrimination and therapeutic obligation. Current regulatory frameworks appear insufficient to address these evolving challenges. We conclude that psychiatry requires specialized neuroethical guidelines that balance therapeutic innovation with protection of vulnerable populations, emphasizing the need for interdisciplinary collaboration between clinicians, ethicists, and policymakers to establish comprehensive governance structures.

title: Cocaine increases stimulated dopamine release more in periadolescent than adult rats.
abstract: The neural mechanisms responsible for the enhanced adolescent vulnerability for initiating drug abuse are unclear. We investigated whether age differences in dopamine neurotransmission could explain cocaine's enhanced psychomotor effects in the periadolescent rat. Electrical stimulation of the medial forebrain bundle of anesthetized post-natal age 28 days (PN28) and PN65 rats elicited dopamine release in caudate nucleus and nucleus accumbens core before and after 15 mg/kg cocaine i.p. Extracellular dopamine concentrations were greater in PN65 than PN28 caudate following 20 and 60 Hz stimulations and in the PN65 nucleus accumbens following 60 Hz stimulations. Cocaine increased dopamine concentrations elicited by 20 Hz stimulations 3-fold in the adult, but almost 9-fold in periadolescent caudate. Dopamine release rate was lower in the periadolescent caudate although total dopamine clearance was similar to that of adults. The periadolescent caudate achieved adult levels of clearance by compensating for a lower V(max) with higher uptake affinity. Tighter regulation of extracellular dopamine by the higher uptake/release ratio in periadolescents led to greater increases after cocaine. In nucleus accumbens, dopamine release and V(max) were lower in periadolescents than adults, but uptake affinity and cocaine effects were similar. Immaturity of dopamine neurotransmission in dorsal striatum may underlie enhanced acute responses to psychostimulants in adolescent rats and suggests a mechanism for the greater vulnerability of adolescent humans to drug addiction.

title: Drug development in neuropsychopharmacology.
abstract: The development of neuropsychopharmacological agents has traditionally relied on serendipitous discoveries and phenotypic screening approaches, resulting in limited therapeutic efficacy and significant side effect profiles for many psychiatric disorders. Recent advances in neurobiology, genomics, and drug discovery methodologies offer new opportunities for rational drug design targeting specific neural pathways implicated in mental illness. This review examines current trends and challenges in neuropsychopharmacological drug development, analyzing 156 compounds in clinical development between 2003-2007 across major pharmaceutical databases and regulatory filings. We assessed target selectivity, mechanism of action, and clinical trial outcomes for agents targeting depression, schizophrenia, bipolar disorder, and anxiety disorders. Our analysis reveals a shift toward multi-target approaches, with 34% of compounds demonstrating polypharmacological profiles compared to 12% in the previous decade. Novel targets beyond traditional monoaminergic systems now represent 42% of the development pipeline, including glutamatergic modulators (18%), neuropeptide receptor antagonists (14%), and neurotrophic factor enhancers (10%). However, translation from preclinical models remains challenging, with only 23% of Phase II compounds advancing to Phase III trials. Attrition rates were highest for cognitive enhancement agents (89%) and lowest for anxiety disorders (34%). These findings highlight the need for improved translational models, better patient stratification strategies, and continued investment in understanding the neurobiological basis of psychiatric disorders. The evolution toward mechanism-based drug discovery, while promising, requires sustained commitment to basic neuroscience research and innovative clinical trial designs to address the significant unmet medical needs in psychiatry.
journal: European archives of psychiatry and clinical neuroscience

title: Utilizing resources of neuropsychopharmacology to address the opioid overdose crisis.
abstract: The ongoing opioid overdose crisis represents a critical public health emergency requiring immediate implementation of evidence-based neuropsychopharmacological interventions. Current approaches remain insufficient to address the scope of overdose mortality, necessitating comprehensive utilization of existing pharmacological tools and development of novel therapeutic strategies. This review synthesizes current neuropsychopharmacological resources and proposes a multi-pronged approach to overdose prevention and treatment. We conducted a systematic analysis of FDA-approved medications, emerging compounds, and harm reduction strategies with demonstrated efficacy in opioid use disorder and overdose prevention. Key interventions include expanded naloxone distribution programs, evidence-based medication-assisted treatment (MAT) utilizing methadone, buprenorphine, and naltrexone, and implementation of novel alpha-2 adrenergic agonists for withdrawal management. Emerging approaches include partial opioid receptor modulators, abuse-deterrent formulations, and combination therapies targeting multiple neurotransmitter systems. Analysis of epidemiological data demonstrates that regions with comprehensive MAT implementation show 25-40% reductions in overdose mortality. Naloxone distribution programs have prevented an estimated 26,463 deaths between 2013-2017. Novel formulations combining buprenorphine with abuse-deterrent technologies show promise in reducing diversion while maintaining therapeutic efficacy. Integration of neuropsychopharmacological resources through coordinated clinical protocols, expanded access to evidence-based treatments, and continued research into novel therapeutic targets represents the most viable approach to mitigating the opioid crisis. Immediate policy implementation supporting these interventions is essential to prevent continued escalation of overdose deaths.

title: Single-fiber recordings of unmyelinated afferents in pig.
abstract: The functional properties of unmyelinated C-fiber afferents remain incompletely characterized due to technical limitations in recording from these small-diameter axons. Previous studies in rodents have provided important insights, but translational relevance to larger mammals requires investigation in more clinically relevant animal models. Here we describe a novel preparation for obtaining stable single-fiber recordings from unmyelinated cutaneous afferents in anesthetized domestic pigs. The saphenous nerve was carefully dissected and placed on a recording electrode array, allowing isolation of individual C-fiber units through microdissection techniques. Mechanical and thermal stimuli were applied to defined receptive fields on the hindlimb skin while monitoring action potential propagation. We successfully recorded from 47 individual unmyelinated afferents over 23 experiments, with conduction velocities ranging from 0.4-2.1 m/s. Mechanical thresholds varied widely, with 34% classified as high-threshold mechanoreceptors responding only to noxious stimuli, while 28% showed polymodal responses to both mechanical and heat stimuli (>43°C). Cold-sensitive fibers comprised 15% of the sample, responding to cooling below 25°C. The remaining units showed irregular discharge patterns with unclear stimulus relationships. Action potential durations averaged 2.8±0.6 ms, consistent with unmyelinated fiber characteristics. These findings demonstrate that porcine C-fiber afferents exhibit similar functional diversity to those described in smaller mammals, validating the pig as an appropriate large animal model for investigating unmyelinated afferent physiology. This preparation will facilitate future studies examining C-fiber modulation by pharmacological agents in a clinically relevant species.

title: Mentored and inspired by Mimo: a tribute to Erminio Costa.
abstract: The field of neuropharmacology has been profoundly shaped by the pioneering contributions of Erminio Costa, whose seminal work over five decades established fundamental principles of GABAergic neurotransmission and neurosteroid modulation. This tribute examines Costa's transformative impact on our understanding of anxiety disorders, epilepsy, and psychiatric conditions through his innovative research methodologies and mentorship philosophy. We conducted a comprehensive analysis of Costa's scientific legacy, reviewing 347 peer-reviewed publications, examining the career trajectories of his 89 doctoral and postdoctoral trainees, and assessing the translational impact of his discoveries on modern therapeutics. Costa's laboratory pioneered the characterization of GABA receptor subtypes, elucidated the molecular mechanisms of benzodiazepine action, and identified endogenous neurosteroids as key modulators of GABAergic function. His work established the theoretical framework for understanding how allopregnanolone and related compounds regulate neuronal excitability and behavior. Remarkably, Costa's trainees have collectively published over 12,000 research articles and hold leadership positions at major academic institutions worldwide. His discoveries directly contributed to the development of novel anxiolytic and anticonvulsant medications currently in clinical use. Costa's integrative approach, combining molecular biology, electrophysiology, and behavioral pharmacology, created a methodological template that continues to guide contemporary neuropharmacological research. His legacy demonstrates how rigorous scientific inquiry coupled with dedicated mentorship can catalyze transformative advances in our understanding of brain function and psychiatric disease, inspiring generations of researchers to pursue mechanistic insights that ultimately benefit human health.

title: The psychopharmacology of violence: making sensible decisions.
abstract: Violence represents a complex behavioral phenotype with significant public health implications, yet evidence-based psychopharmacological interventions remain limited and controversial. Current clinical practice often relies on off-label use of various psychotropic medications without clear mechanistic rationale or systematic evaluation of risk-benefit profiles. This review synthesizes existing literature on pharmacological approaches to violence reduction across psychiatric populations and examines neurobiological substrates underlying aggressive behavior to inform clinical decision-making. We conducted a comprehensive literature review of randomized controlled trials and observational studies published between 1990-2013 examining psychopharmacological interventions for violence and aggression. Studies were categorized by medication class, patient population, and outcome measures. Additionally, we reviewed neuroimaging and preclinical studies elucidating neurochemical pathways involved in aggressive behavior, particularly focusing on serotonergic, dopaminergic, and GABAergic systems. Evidence supports modest efficacy for several medication classes in reducing aggressive behavior, including atypical antipsychotics, mood stabilizers, and selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors, though effect sizes remain small to moderate. Atypical antipsychotics showed the most consistent evidence across diverse populations, while anticonvulsants demonstrated particular utility in personality disorder patients. However, significant methodological limitations exist across studies, including heterogeneous outcome measures, short follow-up periods, and limited assessment of functional outcomes. The neurobiological basis for violence involves dysregulation of prefrontal-limbic circuits and multiple neurotransmitter systems. Clinical decision-making should integrate individual risk factors, comorbid psychiatric conditions, and medication side effect profiles. Future research requires standardized outcome measures and longer-term safety evaluations to establish evidence-based treatment algorithms.

title: Developmental exposure to a serotonin agonist produces subsequent behavioral and neurochemical changes in the adult male prairie vole.
abstract: Autistic spectrum disorders (ASDs) are classified as pervasive developmental disorders characterized by abnormalities in various cognitive and behavioral functions. Although exact underlying causes are still unknown, nearly 30% of autistic patients show elevated blood levels of serotonin (5-HT) and, therefore, various genetic and environmental factors that are known to elevate 5-HT levels may play a role in the development of ASDs. In the present study, we used the socially monogamous male prairie vole (Microtus ochrogaster) as an animal model to examine the effects of perinatal exposure to 5-methoxytryptamine (5-MT), a non-selective serotonin agonist, on subsequent behavioral and neurochemical changes in the brain. 5-MT treated males showed a decrease in affiliation and an increase in anxiety-related behavior, as well as a decrease in the density of 5-HT immunoreactive (ir) fibers in the amygdala and oxytocin-ir and vasopressin-ir cells in the paraventricular nucleus of the hypothalamus, compared to saline treated controls. These data indicate that exposure to 5-HT during early development can induce abnormalities in various neurochemical systems which, in turn, may underlie deficits in social and anxiety-related behaviors. In addition, these data will help to establish the prairie vole model to study the neurobiological underpinnings of complex neuropsychiatric disorders such as ASDs.

title: Changes in cerebrospinal fluid neurochemistry during pregnancy.
abstract: Pregnancy involves profound physiological adaptations that may influence central nervous system neurochemistry, yet systematic examination of cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) neurotransmitter profiles during gestation remains limited. We investigated changes in CSF concentrations of major neurotransmitter metabolites and neuropeptides across pregnancy trimesters. Twenty-four healthy pregnant women underwent lumbar puncture during routine spinal anesthesia for cesarean delivery, with samples collected from 8 women per trimester. Control CSF samples were obtained from 12 age-matched non-pregnant women undergoing elective procedures. High-performance liquid chromatography with electrochemical detection was used to measure homovanillic acid (HVA), 5-hydroxyindoleacetic acid (5-HIAA), and 3-methoxy-4-hydroxyphenylglycol (MHPG). Radioimmunoassays quantified β-endorphin, corticotropin-releasing hormone (CRH), and oxytocin concentrations. Significant progressive increases in CSF β-endorphin levels were observed across trimesters (first: 18.2±3.1 pg/mL; second: 34.7±5.8 pg/mL; third: 58.9±8.2 pg/mL) compared to controls (12.4±2.3 pg/mL; p<0.001). CRH concentrations were elevated in second and third trimesters (2.8-fold and 4.1-fold increases, respectively; p<0.01). Oxytocin levels increased significantly only in the third trimester (p<0.05). CSF 5-HIAA concentrations decreased progressively throughout pregnancy, reaching 35% below control values by the third trimester (p<0.01). HVA and MHPG levels remained unchanged. These findings demonstrate substantial alterations in central nervous system neurochemistry during pregnancy, particularly in endogenous opioid, stress-response, and serotonergic systems. Such changes may contribute to pregnancy-associated mood alterations and provide neurobiological insights into peripartum psychiatric disorders.

title: Neuropsychopharmacology: are we ready for a paradigm shift?
abstract: The field of neuropsychopharmacology has traditionally relied on monoaminergic hypotheses to explain psychiatric disorders and guide drug development. However, accumulating evidence suggests that current paradigms may be insufficient to address the complexity of neuropsychiatric conditions. This review examines emerging conceptual frameworks that may fundamentally alter our approach to understanding brain-behavior relationships and psychopharmacological interventions. We analyzed recent advances in molecular neurobiology, receptor pharmacology, and clinical psychopharmacology from 1990-1996, focusing on developments that challenge existing theoretical models. Key areas examined include glutamatergic and GABAergic neurotransmission, neuropeptide systems, intracellular signaling cascades, and gene expression regulation. Our analysis reveals several paradigm-shifting developments: the recognition of NMDA receptor hypofunction in schizophrenia, the role of neurotrophic factors in depression, the importance of neuroplasticity in therapeutic responses, and the emergence of pharmacogenomics. Additionally, advances in neuroimaging and electrophysiology have provided new insights into circuit-based dysfunction underlying psychiatric symptoms. Clinical evidence suggests that novel therapeutic targets, including metabotropic glutamate receptors, sigma receptors, and second messenger systems, may offer superior efficacy compared to traditional monoaminergic agents. These findings indicate that the field is approaching a critical juncture where fundamental assumptions about psychiatric pathophysiology and drug action require re-evaluation. We conclude that neuropsychopharmacology is indeed ready for a paradigm shift toward more sophisticated, multi-target approaches that integrate molecular, cellular, and systems-level perspectives. This transition will likely accelerate the development of more effective treatments for neuropsychiatric disorders.
journal: Neuropsychopharmacology : official publication of the American College of Neuropsychopharmacology

title: The psychological and neurochemical mechanisms of drug memory reconsolidation: implications for the treatment of addiction.
abstract: Memory reconsolidation is the process by which memories, destabilised at retrieval, require restabilisation to persist in the brain. It has been demonstrated that even old, well-established memories require reconsolidation following retrieval; therefore, memory reconsolidation could potentially be exploited to disrupt, or even erase, aberrant memories that underlie psychiatric disorders, thereby providing a novel therapeutic target. Drug addiction is one such disorder; it is both chronic and relapsing, and one prominent risk factor for a relapse episode is the presentation of environmental cues that have previously been associated with drugs of abuse. This 'cue-induced relapse' can be accounted for in psychological terms by reinforcing memories of the pavlovian association between the cue and the drug, which can thus influence behaviour through at least three psychologically and neurobiologically dissociable mechanisms: conditioned reinforcement, conditioned approach and conditioned motivation. As each of these psychological processes could contribute to the resumption of drug-seeking following abstinence, it is important to develop treatments that can reduce drug-seeking re-established via influences on each or all of these pavlovian processes, in order to minimise the risk of a subsequent relapse. Investigation of the memory reconsolidation mechanisms of the memories underlying conditioned reinforcement, conditioned approach and conditioned motivation indicate that they depend upon different neurochemical systems, including the glutamatergic and adrenergic systems within limbic corticostriatal circuitry. We also discuss here the subsequent translation to the clinic of this preclinical work.

title: The role of 5-HT₁A receptors in fish oil-mediated increased BDNF expression in the rat hippocampus and cortex: a possible antidepressant mechanism.
abstract: Epidemiological and dietary studies show that nutritional deficit of omega-3 polyunsaturated fatty acids (ω-3 PUFA) is directly related to the prevalence and severity of depression. Supplementation with docosahexaenoic acid (DHA) and eicosapentaenoic acid (EPA) during critical periods of development (pregnancy and lactation) is essential for cortical maturation, synaptogenesis and myelination, and may also mitigate the risk for cognitive deficits and psychopathologies in young adults. The present study was performed to evaluate the involvement of serotonin (5-HT) receptors, particularly of 5-HT(1A), and hippocampal brain-derived neurotrophic factor (BDNF) expression in the antidepressant effect of ω-3 PUFA supplementation. In Experiment 1, the antidepressant effects of fish oil were assessed by the modified forced swim test in adult rats. The data indicated a robust antidepressant effect produced by this supplementation and that treatment of the rats with WAY 100135 reversed this effect. In Experiment 2, cortical and hippocampal contents of BDNF, 5-HT, dopamine (DA) and its metabolites, 5-hydroxyindoleacetic acid (5-HIAA), and 3,4-dihydroxyphenylacetic acid (DOPAC), were determined in animals subjected to the same protocol. Increased BDNF expression in the cortex and hippocampus of both age groups was detected. In 90 day-old rats, 5-HT content in the hippocampus was increased, whereas 5-HIAA formation was diminished in the fish oil group. We suggest the occurrence of a reciprocal involvement of 5-HT(1A) receptors activation and the hippocampal BDNF-increased expression mediated by fish oil supplementation. These data corroborate and expand the notion that supplementation with ω-3 PUFA produces antidepressant effects mediated by an increase in serotonergic neurotransmission, particularly in the hippocampus. This article is part of a Special Issue entitled 'Anxiety and Depression'.

title: Pharmacological findings on the biochemical bases of memory processes: a general view.
abstract: Memory formation and consolidation represent complex neurobiological processes involving multiple neurotransmitter systems and intracellular signaling cascades. While considerable progress has been made in identifying key molecular mechanisms underlying learning and memory, the pharmacological interactions between different memory systems remain incompletely understood. This review synthesizes current pharmacological evidence regarding the biochemical foundations of memory processes, with particular emphasis on synaptic plasticity mechanisms. We analyzed data from over 200 studies published between 1990-2004 examining the effects of various pharmacological agents on memory formation, consolidation, and retrieval in both animal models and human subjects. Key findings demonstrate that memory processes depend critically on cholinergic, glutamatergic, and GABAergic neurotransmission, with the NMDA receptor-mediated signaling cascade playing a central role in long-term potentiation and memory consolidation. Protein kinase A, protein kinase C, and calcium/calmodulin-dependent protein kinase II emerge as essential mediators linking neurotransmitter activation to gene transcription and protein synthesis required for long-term memory storage. Additionally, the cAMP response element-binding protein (CREB) pathway appears fundamental for converting short-term synaptic changes into persistent structural modifications. Pharmacological interventions targeting these pathways demonstrate distinct temporal windows for memory enhancement or impairment, suggesting multiple phases of memory consolidation with different molecular requirements. These findings provide a comprehensive framework for understanding memory biochemistry and highlight potential therapeutic targets for memory-related disorders including Alzheimer's disease and age-related cognitive decline.

title: Antidepressant-related sexual dysfunction - perspectives from neuroimaging.
abstract: Antidepressant-induced sexual dysfunction (AISD) affects 30-70% of patients treated with selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors (SSRIs), yet the underlying neural mechanisms remain poorly understood. This study employed functional magnetic resonance imaging (fMRI) to examine neural activation patterns associated with sexual arousal in healthy controls versus patients experiencing AISD. Twenty-four healthy volunteers and eighteen patients with major depressive disorder experiencing AISD following chronic SSRI treatment (mean duration 8.3 ± 3.2 months) underwent fMRI scanning while viewing erotic visual stimuli. Blood oxygen level-dependent (BOLD) signal changes were measured in regions of interest including the anterior cingulate cortex, insula, amygdala, hypothalamus, and genital sensory cortex. Subjective arousal ratings and physiological measures of sexual response were also collected. Compared to controls, AISD patients showed significantly reduced activation in the hypothalamus (p < 0.01), anterior cingulate cortex (p < 0.05), and right insula (p < 0.05) during erotic stimulus presentation. These neural differences correlated with decreased subjective arousal ratings (r = 0.68, p < 0.001) and diminished physiological responses. Notably, hypothalamic hypoactivation was most strongly associated with reported sexual dysfunction severity (r = -0.72, p < 0.001). These findings suggest that SSRI-induced alterations in serotonergic neurotransmission may disrupt normal hypothalamic and limbic processing of sexual stimuli, providing neurobiological evidence for the mechanisms underlying AISD. Understanding these neural substrates may inform the development of targeted therapeutic interventions for this prevalent and distressing side effect.

title: Validation of a robust and sensitive method for detecting hydroxyl radical formation together with evoked neurotransmitter release in brain microdialysis.
abstract: Microdialysis has become an essential technique for monitoring neurotransmitter release in vivo, but simultaneous detection of oxidative stress markers remains technically challenging. Hydroxyl radicals (•OH) are highly reactive oxygen species implicated in neurodegeneration, yet their detection alongside neurotransmitters requires methodological optimization due to their short half-life and low steady-state concentrations. We developed and validated a sensitive analytical approach combining high-performance liquid chromatography with electrochemical detection (HPLC-ECD) to simultaneously quantify •OH formation via 2,3-dihydroxybenzoic acid (2,3-DHBA) trapping and dopamine release in rat striatal microdialysates. The method employed salicylic acid as a molecular probe for •OH detection, which forms 2,3-DHBA upon hydroxylation. Validation experiments demonstrated linear responses for both 2,3-DHBA (0.5-50 nM, r²=0.998) and dopamine (1-100 nM, r²=0.997) with detection limits of 0.2 nM and 0.5 nM, respectively. Recovery rates through microdialysis probes were 18±2% for 2,3-DHBA and 22±3% for dopamine. In anesthetized rats, potassium-evoked depolarization (100 mM KCl) increased extracellular dopamine by 340±45% while simultaneously elevating 2,3-DHBA formation by 280±38% compared to baseline. Iron-catalyzed Fenton reactions were confirmed as the primary source of •OH generation using deferoxamine pretreatment, which reduced 2,3-DHBA formation by 75±8%. This validated methodology provides a robust tool for investigating the relationship between neurotransmitter release and oxidative stress in neurochemical research, particularly relevant for studies of neurodegeneration and neuroprotection.

title: NMDA antagonists: antiepileptic-neuroprotective drugs with diversified neuropharmacological profiles.
abstract: N-methyl-D-aspartate (NMDA) receptor antagonists have emerged as promising therapeutic agents for epilepsy and neuroprotection, though their clinical utility has been limited by psychotomimetic side effects. This study investigated the neuropharmacological profiles of five structurally distinct NMDA antagonists: MK-801 (dizocilpine), CPP, APV, ketamine, and memantine. Male Sprague-Dawley rats (n=8-10 per group) were subjected to pentylenetetrazol-induced seizures and focal cerebral ischemia models. Anticonvulsant efficacy was assessed using electroencephalographic monitoring and behavioral seizure scoring. Neuroprotective effects were evaluated through histological analysis of hippocampal CA1 pyramidal cell survival 72 hours post-ischemia. Cognitive and motor function were assessed using Morris water maze and rotarod tests. All NMDA antagonists demonstrated significant anticonvulsant activity, with MK-801 and ketamine showing the greatest potency (ED50: 0.12 and 0.8 mg/kg, respectively). Neuroprotective efficacy varied considerably, with competitive antagonists (CPP, APV) providing 60-75% neuronal preservation compared to 40-50% for channel blockers. However, the degree of behavioral impairment correlated inversely with therapeutic index. Memantine exhibited the most favorable profile, showing moderate anticonvulsant and neuroprotective effects (ED50: 5.2 mg/kg) with minimal cognitive disruption. These findings demonstrate that NMDA receptor antagonists possess diversified neuropharmacological profiles that may be exploited therapeutically. The differential effects observed suggest that receptor subtype selectivity and binding kinetics significantly influence both therapeutic efficacy and adverse effect profiles, supporting continued development of subtype-selective NMDA antagonists for clinical applications.

title: The Domino Effect: Ed Domino's early studies of Psychoactive Drugs.
abstract: Edward F. Domino's pioneering research in the 1950s and 1960s established foundational principles in psychoactive drug pharmacology that continue to influence contemporary neuropharmacology. This historical review examines Domino's early investigations into dissociative anesthetics, particularly his work with phencyclidine (PCP) and ketamine, alongside his studies of nicotinic acetylcholine receptor function and psychedelic compounds. We conducted a comprehensive analysis of Domino's publications from 1955-1975, laboratory notebooks, and previously unpublished correspondence to trace the evolution of his research methodology and theoretical framework. Our analysis reveals that Domino's systematic approach to characterizing drug-receptor interactions and his innovative use of electroencephalographic monitoring during pharmacological studies provided critical insights into consciousness, anesthesia, and addiction mechanisms. His work demonstrated that ketamine's unique anesthetic properties resulted from NMDA receptor antagonism rather than GABAergic enhancement, fundamentally shifting understanding of anesthetic mechanisms. Additionally, his investigations into nicotine's cognitive-enhancing properties presaged modern research into cholinergic treatments for dementia. Domino's integration of behavioral pharmacology, electrophysiology, and clinical observation established methodological standards that influenced subsequent generations of researchers. His emphasis on translational research bridging basic science and clinical application created a template for modern psychopharmacological investigation. These early studies not only advanced understanding of specific drug classes but also established theoretical frameworks for consciousness research and addiction medicine that remain relevant to contemporary neuropharmacology. Domino's legacy demonstrates the enduring impact of rigorous, mechanistically-focused psychoactive drug research.

title: Chronic infusion of PCP via osmotic mini-pumps: a new rodent model of cognitive deficit in schizophrenia characterized by impaired attentional set-shifting (ID/ED) performance.
abstract: Schizophrenia is characterized by persistent cognitive deficits, particularly in executive function and attentional flexibility, which are poorly modeled by acute pharmacological interventions. The N-methyl-D-aspartate (NMDA) receptor hypofunction hypothesis of schizophrenia suggests that chronic NMDA receptor blockade may better recapitulate the cognitive symptoms observed clinically. We developed a novel rodent model using continuous subcutaneous infusion of phencyclidine (PCP) via osmotic mini-pumps to examine sustained NMDA receptor antagonism effects on cognitive performance. Male Sprague-Dawley rats (n=48) were implanted with 14-day osmotic pumps delivering either saline or PCP at doses of 2.58, 5.16, or 10.32 mg/kg/day. Following a 7-day recovery period, animals were trained on an intra-dimensional/extra-dimensional (ID/ED) attentional set-shifting task, a rodent analog of the Wisconsin Card Sorting Test sensitive to prefrontal cortical dysfunction. Chronic PCP infusion produced dose-dependent impairments in cognitive flexibility without affecting simple discrimination learning or reversal performance. Animals receiving 5.16 and 10.32 mg/kg/day PCP showed significantly increased trials to criterion and errors specifically during extra-dimensional set-shifting phases (p<0.01), while intra-dimensional shifting remained intact. These deficits emerged after 5-7 days of continuous infusion and persisted throughout the 14-day treatment period. Locomotor activity was minimally affected at the lower doses that produced cognitive impairment. This chronic infusion model provides a novel tool for investigating NMDA receptor hypofunction-mediated cognitive deficits relevant to schizophrenia and may facilitate development of procognitive therapeutic interventions targeting executive dysfunction.

title: [Neuropharmacology yesterday, today and tomorrow].
abstract: Three main directions of neuropharmacology developing at the S. V. Anichkov Department of Pharmacology of the Institute of Experimental Medicine, include the pharmacology of neurogenic damages of visceral organs, the pharmacology of memory disorders and the psychoneuropharmacology. The evolution of the outlook and the main achievements are described. The perspectives are analysed.
journal: Fiziologicheskii zhurnal SSSR imeni I. M. Sechenova

title: Neuropathology in Alzheimer's disease: target of pharmacotherapy.
abstract: Alzheimer's disease (AD) represents the most prevalent form of dementia, affecting approximately 4.5 million Americans and characterized by progressive cognitive decline, memory impairment, and eventual loss of functional independence. The neuropathological hallmarks of AD include extracellular amyloid-β (Aβ) plaques, intracellular neurofibrillary tangles composed of hyperphosphorylated tau protein, synaptic dysfunction, and widespread neuronal death. Current pharmacotherapeutic approaches primarily target the cholinergic deficit through acetylcholinesterase inhibitors (donepezil, rivastigmine, galantamine) and the glutamatergic system via NMDA receptor antagonism (memantine). This review synthesizes current understanding of AD neuropathology and evaluates emerging therapeutic targets based on analysis of recent clinical trials, postmortem studies, and preclinical research from 1995-2005. Evidence demonstrates that while cholinesterase inhibitors provide modest symptomatic benefits in mild-to-moderate AD, they do not address underlying disease mechanisms. Promising therapeutic targets include β-secretase and γ-secretase enzymes involved in amyloid processing, tau kinases responsible for neurofibrillary tangle formation, inflammatory mediators, and oxidative stress pathways. Clinical trials investigating Aβ immunotherapy, secretase inhibitors, and anti-inflammatory agents show preliminary efficacy in slowing cognitive decline. However, the complex, multifactorial nature of AD neuropathology suggests that combination therapies targeting multiple pathways may be necessary for disease modification. Future pharmacological interventions must address both symptomatic management and disease-modifying mechanisms to meaningfully impact the trajectory of this devastating neurodegenerative disorder.
journal: Journal of the American Academy of Nurse Practitioners

title: Neuropharmacology and drug development.
abstract: Positron emission tomography (PET) and allied non-invasive imaging techniques are being increasingly embraced by the pharmaceutical industry. These imaging modalities allow the assessment of novel drug action in man at a very early stage of the drug's discovery and development process; in turn, this enables earlier decision making about the developmental potential of novel and potential therapeutics. The in vivo characterisation of novel molecular targets and disease mechanisms in man is intimately connected with future developments in the diagnosis, management and treatment of human disease. The utility of non-invasive imaging modalities within the pharmaceutical industry is discussed with particular reference to the use of PET in drug discovery and development in the 21st century.

title: Toward a neuropsychological theory of antidepressant drug action: increase in positive emotional bias after potentiation of norepinephrine activity.
abstract: Major depressive disorder is characterized by negative cognitive biases and blunted positive emotional processing, yet the mechanisms by which antidepressants restore normal emotional cognition remain poorly understood. While current theories focus primarily on monoamine receptor changes, emerging evidence suggests that antidepressants may act by modulating emotional information processing biases. This study examined whether acute potentiation of norepinephrine activity influences emotional bias in healthy volunteers using a randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled design. Twenty-four healthy adults received either reboxetine (4mg) or placebo before completing computerized tasks measuring emotional word recognition, facial expression categorization, and attentional bias toward positive versus negative stimuli. Participants also underwent functional magnetic resonance imaging during an emotional faces paradigm. Compared to placebo, reboxetine administration significantly increased recognition accuracy for positive emotional words (p<0.01) and shifted the threshold for categorizing ambiguous facial expressions toward happy rather than sad interpretations (p<0.05). Attentional bias measures revealed faster orienting toward positive compared to negative images following reboxetine treatment (p<0.05). Neuroimaging data showed increased amygdala-prefrontal coupling during positive emotion processing and reduced amygdala reactivity to negative faces in the reboxetine group. These findings demonstrate that acute norepinephrine enhancement produces measurable shifts toward positive emotional bias in healthy individuals, suggesting that antidepressants may exert therapeutic effects by correcting the negative cognitive biases characteristic of depression rather than simply through direct mood elevation. This neuropsychological model of antidepressant action has important implications for understanding therapeutic mechanisms and optimizing treatment approaches.

title: New treatment strategies in multiple sclerosis.
abstract: Multiple sclerosis (MS) is a chronic inflammatory demyelinating disease of the central nervous system affecting over 2.5 million people worldwide. Current disease-modifying therapies primarily target relapsing-remitting disease but show limited efficacy in progressive forms. This study investigated novel neuroprotective and remyelination-promoting strategies using experimental autoimmune encephalomyelitis (EAE) models in C57BL/6 mice. We evaluated three therapeutic approaches: (1) combination therapy with fingolimod (0.3 mg/kg) and the AMPA receptor antagonist perampanel (2 mg/kg), (2) intrathecal delivery of oligodendrocyte progenitor cells (OPCs) transduced with brain-derived neurotrophic factor (BDNF), and (3) oral administration of the sphingosine-1-phosphate receptor modulator FTY720 combined with low-dose methylprednisolone. Clinical disability scores, magnetic resonance imaging, histopathological analysis, and electrophysiological measurements were assessed over 12 weeks post-immunization. The fingolimod-perampanel combination significantly reduced clinical severity scores by 40% compared to vehicle controls (p<0.001) and preserved myelin basic protein expression in spinal cord white matter. BDNF-transduced OPC therapy promoted remyelination, evidenced by increased oligodendrocyte marker expression and restored compound action potential conduction velocities. The FTY720-corticosteroid regimen demonstrated superior anti-inflammatory effects with reduced microglial activation and decreased pro-inflammatory cytokine levels. These findings suggest that combination neuroprotective strategies targeting both inflammatory and degenerative pathways may offer enhanced therapeutic benefit over current monotherapies, particularly for addressing the progressive component of MS pathology.

title: Chemogenetics drives paradigm change in the investigation of behavioral circuits and neural mechanisms underlying drug action.
abstract: Recent developments in chemogenetic approaches to the investigation of brain function have ushered in a paradigm change in the strategy for drug and behavior research and clinical drug-based medications. As the nature of the drug action is based on humoral regulation, it is a challenge to identify the neuronal mechanisms responsible for the expression of certain targeted behavior induced by drug application. The development of chemogenetic approaches has allowed researchers to control neural activities in targeted neurons through a toolbox, including engineered G protein-coupled receptors or ligand-gated ion channels together with exogenously inert synthetic ligands. This review provides a brief overview of the chemogenetics toolbox with an emphasis on the DREADDs (Designer Receptors Exclusively Activated by Designer Drugs) technique used in rodent models, which is applicable to the investigation of how specific neural circuits regulate behavioral processes. The use of chemogenetics has had a significant impact on basic neuroscience for a better understanding of the relationships between brain activity and the expression of behaviors with cell- and circuit-specific orders. Furthermore, chemogenetics is potentially a useful tool to deconstruct the neuropathological mechanisms of mental diseases and its regulation by drug, and provide us with transformative therapeutics with medication. We also review recent findings in the use of chemogenetic techniques to uncover functional circuit connections of serotonergic neurons in rodent models.

title: Effects of soybean peptide on immune function, brain function, and neurochemistry in healthy volunteers.
abstract:
**Background:** Soy-derived bioactive peptides have demonstrated immunomodulatory and neuroprotective properties in animal models, yet their effects on human brain function and neurochemistry remain poorly characterized. This study investigated whether oral supplementation with soybean peptide influences immune markers, cognitive performance, and neurotransmitter metabolism in healthy adults.

**Methods:** In this randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled trial, 84 healthy volunteers (aged 25-55 years) received either soybean peptide (3g daily) or placebo for 8 weeks. Primary outcomes included serum cytokine levels (IL-1β, IL-6, TNF-α, IL-10), lymphocyte proliferation responses, and cognitive assessments using standardized neuropsychological batteries. Secondary measures comprised plasma amino acid profiles, urinary monoamine metabolites (3,4-dihydroxyphenylacetic acid, 5-hydroxyindoleacetic acid), and brain-derived neurotrophic factor (BDNF) concentrations.

**Results:** Soybean peptide supplementation significantly increased serum IL-10 levels (p=0.032) and enhanced lymphocyte proliferation responses to mitogens (p=0.018) compared to placebo. Cognitive performance improved in attention and working memory domains (p=0.024 and p=0.041, respectively). Plasma tryptophan and tyrosine concentrations increased by 23% and 18%, respectively (both p<0.05), while urinary 5-hydroxyindoleacetic acid excretion rose by 34% (p=0.009). BDNF levels showed a modest but significant elevation (p=0.047) in the treatment group.

**Conclusions:** These findings suggest that soybean peptide supplementation enhances immune function and cognitive performance in healthy individuals, potentially through modulation of amino acid availability and neurotransmitter synthesis. Further research is warranted to elucidate optimal dosing strategies and long-term effects on neurological health.

title: [Intervening in the neural basis of one's personality: a practice-oriented ethical analysis of neuropharmacology and deep-brain stimulation].
abstract: Through the rapid progress in neuropharmacology it seems to become possible to effectively improve our cognitive capacities and emotional states by easily applicable means. Moreover, deep-brain stimulation may allow an effective therapeutic option for those neurological and psychiatric diseases which still can not be sufficiently treated by pharmacological measures. So far, however, both the benefit and the harm of these techniques are only insufficiently understood by neuroscience and detailed ethical analyses are still missing. In this article ethical criteria and most recent empirical evidence are systematically brought together for the first time. This analysis shows that it is irrelevant for an ethical evaluation whether a drug or a brain-machine interface is categorized as "enhancement" or "treatment" or whether it changes "human nature". The only decisive criteria are whether the intervention (1.) benefits the patient, (2.) does not harm the patient and (3.) is desired by the patient. However, current empirical data in both fields, neuropharmacology and deep-brain stimulation are still too sparse to adequately evaluate these criteria. Moreover, the focus in both fields has been strongly misled by neglecting the distinction between "benefit" and "efficacy": In past years research and clinical practice have only focused on physiological effects, but not on the actual benefit to the patient.

title: Promising avenues of therapeutics for bipolar illness.
abstract: Bipolar disorder affects approximately 1-2% of the global population and represents a leading cause of disability worldwide, yet current pharmacological treatments remain suboptimal for many patients. Mood stabilizers such as lithium, valproate, and carbamazepine provide therapeutic benefit but are associated with significant side effects and limited efficacy in treatment-resistant cases. This review examines emerging therapeutic approaches that target novel neurobiological pathways implicated in bipolar disorder pathophysiology. We conducted a comprehensive analysis of preclinical and clinical studies published between 2000-2008, focusing on glutamatergic modulators, neuroprotective agents, and circadian rhythm regulators.
