Glutamate NMDA receptor antagonists, including ketamine and memantine, demonstrate rapid antimanic and antidepressant effects in preliminary trials, with response rates of 60-75% within 72 hours. Neuroprotective compounds such as riluzole and N-acetylcysteine show promise in stabilizing mood episodes through modulation of glial function and oxidative stress pathways. Additionally, melatonin receptor agonists and light therapy interventions targeting circadian dysregulation yield significant improvements in sleep architecture and mood stability. Neuroimaging studies reveal that these novel interventions normalize aberrant activity in prefrontal-limbic circuits implicated in emotional regulation. These findings suggest that therapeutics targeting glutamatergic neurotransmission, cellular resilience mechanisms, and biological rhythm disturbances represent promising avenues for bipolar disorder treatment. Future research should prioritize large-scale randomized controlled trials to establish efficacy and safety profiles of these innovative approaches, potentially revolutionizing bipolar disorder management through precision medicine strategies.

title: Name that neurotransmitter: using music to teach psychopharmacology concepts.
abstract:
Background: Teaching psychopharmacology concepts to nursing students presents significant pedagogical challenges, particularly in helping students memorize neurotransmitter functions, pathways, and associated medications. Traditional lecture-based approaches often result in poor retention and difficulty applying concepts to clinical practice. Music-based learning strategies have demonstrated efficacy in various educational contexts but have not been systematically evaluated for psychopharmacology education.

Methods: We implemented a novel educational intervention called "Name that Neurotransmitter" with 84 undergraduate nursing students enrolled in a psychopharmacology course. Students were randomly assigned to either a traditional lecture group (n=42) or a music-enhanced learning group (n=42). The intervention group participated in sessions where neurotransmitter functions, synthesis pathways, and drug mechanisms were taught through original songs set to familiar melodies. Pre- and post-intervention assessments measured knowledge retention using a validated 50-item psychopharmacology examination. Student satisfaction and perceived learning effectiveness were evaluated using Likert-scale surveys.

Results: Students in the music-enhanced group demonstrated significantly higher post-test scores compared to controls (82.3 ± 8.1 vs. 74.6 ± 9.4, p<0.001). Retention testing at 4 weeks showed sustained knowledge improvement in the intervention group (79.1 ± 7.8 vs. 71.2 ± 8.9, p<0.01). Music group participants reported higher engagement levels (4.6 ± 0.5 vs. 3.2 ± 0.7, p<0.001) and increased confidence in applying psychopharmacology concepts clinically.

Conclusions: Music-based teaching strategies significantly enhance learning and retention of complex psychopharmacology concepts among nursing students, suggesting broader applications for neuroscience education in healthcare curricula.

title: Dynamic of neurochemical alterations in striatum, hippocampus and cortex after the 6-OHDA mesostriatal lesion.
abstract: Parkinson's disease is characterized by progressive degeneration of dopaminergic neurons in the substantia nigra, leading to striatal dopamine depletion and widespread neurochemical alterations. The 6-hydroxydopamine (6-OHDA) lesion model provides a valuable tool for investigating the temporal dynamics of neurochemical changes following dopaminergic denervation. We examined the time-course of neurotransmitter alterations in striatum, hippocampus, and prefrontal cortex following unilateral 6-OHDA injection into the medial forebrain bundle of adult male Sprague-Dawley rats. Animals were sacrificed at 3, 7, 14, and 28 days post-lesion, and brain tissues were analyzed using high-performance liquid chromatography for dopamine, norepinephrine, serotonin, and their metabolites. Tyrosine hydroxylase immunohistochemistry confirmed lesion extent. In the ipsilateral striatum, dopamine levels decreased by 85% at 3 days and remained depleted throughout the study period, while DOPAC and HVA showed initial increases followed by sustained reductions. Norepinephrine and serotonin exhibited compensatory increases peaking at 7-14 days post-lesion. In the hippocampus, modest but significant reductions in dopamine and norepinephrine emerged by 14 days, accompanied by elevated serotonin turnover. The prefrontal cortex showed delayed dopamine depletion (40% reduction at 28 days) and increased noradrenergic activity. These findings demonstrate that mesostriatal 6-OHDA lesions trigger complex, time-dependent neurochemical adaptations extending beyond the primary target region, involving multiple neurotransmitter systems across interconnected brain regions. Understanding these dynamic changes provides insight into the pathophysiology of Parkinson's disease and potential therapeutic targets for neuroprotective interventions.
journal: International journal of developmental neuroscience : the official journal of the International Society for Developmental Neuroscience

title: 13C NMR tracers in neurochemistry: implications for molecular imaging.
abstract: An overview of 13C nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) spectroscopy methods and their applications in the study of the metabolism of brain cells in vitro and in the in vivo brain is presented as well as their implications for modern molecular imaging techniques. Various topics will be discussed, such as general properties of the 13C NMR spectrum, 13C NMR spectroscopy acquisition protocols, determination of fractional 13C enrichment, 13C(2H) NMR methodologies, and the use of 13C hyperpolarized substrates for NMR spectroscopy and imaging. Some illustrative applications are described, both in vitro and in vivo.
journal: The quarterly journal of nuclear medicine and molecular imaging : official publication of the Italian Association of Nuclear Medicine (AIMN) [and] the International Association of Radiopharmacology (IAR), [and] Section of the Society of...

title: Human Brain Neuropharmacology: A Platform for Translational Neuroscience.
abstract: The translational gap between preclinical neuropharmacological research and clinical outcomes remains a significant challenge in drug development for neuropsychiatric disorders. Traditional animal models, while valuable, often fail to recapitulate the complexity of human neural circuits and their responses to pharmacological interventions. We developed an integrated human brain neuropharmacology platform combining ex vivo human brain tissue analysis, induced pluripotent stem cell (iPSC)-derived neural networks, and advanced neuroimaging techniques to bridge this translational divide. The platform incorporates post-mortem human brain samples from neurologically healthy donors (n=45) and patients with major depressive disorder, schizophrenia, and Alzheimer's disease (n=78), alongside patient-derived iPSC lines differentiated into cortical and subcortical neuronal subtypes. We employed high-throughput receptor binding assays, electrophysiological recordings, and calcium imaging to evaluate drug responses across multiple scales. Validation studies using 15 FDA-approved psychotropic medications demonstrated strong concordance between our platform predictions and known clinical efficacy profiles (r=0.82, p<0.001). Notably, the platform identified previously unrecognized off-target effects for three antipsychotic agents and predicted optimal dosing ranges that correlated with clinical therapeutic windows. Integration with functional MRI data from healthy volunteers (n=32) further validated network-level drug effects. This human brain neuropharmacology platform represents a paradigm shift toward human-centric drug discovery, offering unprecedented opportunities to accelerate the development of more effective neurotherapeutics while reducing reliance on animal models and improving clinical translation rates.

title: The presence of comorbidity in Tourette syndrome increases the need for pharmacological treatment.
abstract: Tourette syndrome (TS) is a complex neurodevelopmental disorder characterized by motor and vocal tics, with up to 90% of patients presenting comorbid psychiatric conditions including attention-deficit/hyperactivity disorder (ADHD), obsessive-compulsive disorder (OCD), and anxiety disorders. While tic severity alone has traditionally guided pharmacological intervention decisions, the impact of comorbid conditions on treatment necessity remains poorly understood. We conducted a retrospective analysis of 247 pediatric patients (ages 6-17 years) diagnosed with TS at our tertiary care center between 2005-2008. Patients were categorized into TS-only (n=61) and TS-comorbid groups (n=186), with comprehensive assessment using the Yale Global Tic Severity Scale, Children's Yale-Brown Obsessive Compulsive Scale, ADHD Rating Scale-IV, and Beck Youth Inventories. Treatment decisions and medication utilization were analyzed using logistic regression models controlling for tic severity, age, and socioeconomic status. Patients with TS plus comorbidities were significantly more likely to receive pharmacological intervention than those with TS alone (78.5% vs 41.0%, p<0.001). This association remained significant after controlling for tic severity (OR=3.24, 95% CI: 1.82-5.77, p<0.001). The presence of ADHD (OR=2.89) and OCD symptoms (OR=2.15) were the strongest predictors of medication use. Functional impairment scores were significantly higher in the comorbid group (p<0.001), correlating with increased polypharmacy requirements. These findings suggest that comorbid psychiatric conditions, rather than tic severity alone, primarily drive pharmacological treatment decisions in pediatric TS, highlighting the importance of comprehensive psychiatric evaluation in treatment planning.

title: Human thalamus: neurochemical mapping of inferior pulvinar complex.
abstract: The primate pulvinar connects with the entire array of known visual areas and is postulated to play a role in selective visual attention. Recently, five separate neurochemical subdivisions of a region termed the inferior pulvinar (PI) complex were identified in monkeys. In the present study, similar histochemical procedures were applied to map the extent of the PI complex in humans. Acetylcholinesterase histochemistry and cytochrome oxidase staining demarcated four histochemical zones in human pulvinar, corresponding to the medial, central, lateral and lateral-shell (PI(M), PI(C), PI(L), and PI(L-S)) divisions of the PI complex in monkeys.

title: Frontal-subcortical neuronal circuits and clinical neuropsychiatry: an update.
abstract: Frontal-subcortical circuits form the principal network, which mediate motor activity and behavior in humans. Five parallel frontal-subcortical circuits link the specific areas of the frontal cortex to the striatum, basal ganglia and thalamus. These frontal-subcortical circuits originate from the supplementary motor area, frontal eye field, dorsolateral prefrontal region, lateral orbitofrontal region and anterior cingulate portion of the frontal cortex. The open afferent and efferent connections to the frontal-subcortical circuits mediate coordination between functionally similar areas of the brain. Specific chemoarchitecture and multiple neurotransmitter interactions modulate the functional activity of each circuit. Dorsolateral prefrontal circuit lesions cause executive dysfunction, orbitofrontal circuit lesions lead to personality changes characterized by disinhibition and anterior cingulate circuit lesions present with apathy. The neurobiological correlates of neuropsychiatric disorders including depression, obsessive-compulsive disorder, schizophrenia and substance abuse, imply involvement of frontal-subcortical circuits.

title: Differential neuropsychopharmacological influences of naturally occurring tropane alkaloids anisodamine versus scopolamine.
abstract: Two naturally occurring tropane alkaloids, anisodamine and scopolamine, structurally dissimilar in one OH group, are well established as muscarinic acetylcholine receptor (mAChR) antagonists in clinic and basic research. However, experimental evidence for central effects of anisodamine is limited and conflicting compared with that of scopolamine. In the present study, Morris water maze test, long-term potentiation (LTP) recording and receptor radioligand binding assays were used to explore the disparity in neuropsychopharmacological influences of anisodamine versus scopolamine and possible mechanisms. Anisodamine, at 10-40-fold higher doses than those of scopolamine, did not produce any spatial cognitive deficits as scopolamine, but tended to improve cognition at the repeated high doses. LTP in vivo was then adopted to predict BBB permeability of the muscarinic antagonists following systemic drug administration. Contrary to scopolamine, anisodamine did not influence the formation of LTP in the CA(1) region of rat hippocampus at 40-fold higher dose than that of scopolamine. Additionally, receptor radioligand binding assays (RRLBA) revealed that the binding affinity of anisodamine to mice brain mAChR was much lower than that of scopolamine. The findings suggested that anisodamine did not impair cognition nor depress LTP primarily due to its poor BBB permeability. This work enlarged knowledge of structure-activity relationship among tropane alkaloids, meanwhile providing evidence for more reasonable drug prescription in clinic.

title: Synaptopathies: synaptic dysfunction in neurological disorders - A review written by students for students, and a story of success for ISN schools.
abstract: One of the major endeavors of the International Society for Neurochemistry (ISN) is the promotion and support of young researchers, for instance at 'schools' that offer young students an opportunity to closely interact with renowned researchers as well as with each other. As a result of the 13th International Society for Neurochemistry Advanced School on 'Synapses' held in Mission Beach, Australia, prior to the Biennial Meeting of the International Society for Neurochemistry in August 2016 in Cairns, we are pleased to publish this comprehensive Review article, written from students for students. Read the highlighted article 'Synaptopathies: synaptic dysfunction in neurological disorders - A review from students to students' on page 785.

title: Extracting the basal extracellular dopamine concentrations from the evoked responses: re-analysis of the dopamine kinetics.
abstract: Accurate determination of basal extracellular dopamine concentrations in vivo remains challenging due to the rapid uptake kinetics and low steady-state levels of this neurotransmitter. Traditional microdialysis methods suffer from temporal resolution limitations, while fast-scan cyclic voltammetry (FSCV) primarily measures evoked dopamine release. Here we present a novel analytical approach to extract basal dopamine concentrations from electrically-evoked dopamine responses recorded using FSCV in the striatum of anesthetized rats. Our method employs a modified Michaelis-Menten kinetic model that incorporates both uptake and release parameters to back-calculate baseline concentrations from the decay phase of evoked dopamine transients. We validated this approach using simultaneous FSCV and microdialysis recordings (n=8 rats) and compared results with conventional no-net-flux microdialysis measurements. Mathematical modeling of 127 evoked dopamine responses revealed basal concentrations of 18.3 ± 4.7 nM, which correlated strongly with microdialysis values (r=0.82, p<0.001). The method showed high reproducibility (CV=12.4%) and was sensitive to pharmacological manipulations, including dopamine transporter blockade with nomifensine (10 mg/kg i.p.) and tyrosine hydroxylase inhibition with α-methyl-p-tyrosine (200 mg/kg i.p.). Re-analysis of existing datasets using this approach suggests that previous estimates of dopamine uptake parameters may have been confounded by inaccurate baseline assumptions. This methodology provides a valuable tool for determining physiological dopamine concentrations with high temporal resolution and may improve our understanding of dopaminergic neurotransmission in health and disease states.

title: Cerebrospinal fluid concentrations of functionally important amino acids and metabolic compounds in patients with mild cognitive impairment and Alzheimer's disease.
abstract: Cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) biomarkers play an important role in the differential diagnosis of neurodegenerative diseases such as Alzheimer's disease (AD) and its postulated precursor stage mild cognitive impairment (MCI). While CSF tau protein, phospho-tau protein and beta-amyloid have become part of the diagnostic process in clinical routine, the importance of several other biomarkers remains quite unclear. Among these, amino acids and metabolic compounds have been studied in clinical conditions mostly other than AD and, to our knowledge, never in MCI. In patients with AD (n = 14) and MCI (n = 13) we now determined CSF levels of 36 different amino acids and metabolic compounds by high-performance liquid chromatography. We found that 8 out of 36 amino acids (urea, threonine, glutamate, citrulline, alpha-aminobutyric acid, ornithine, ammonia and arginine) were significantly elevated in the CSF of patients with AD compared to those with MCI. As most of these amino acids and metabolic compounds are functionally important for brain-specific metabolic processes, neurotransmitter pathways or compensatory mechanisms, our findings might reflect these changes occurring within the brain of patients with MCI and those who developed manifest AD.

title: Demon voltammetry and analysis software: analysis of cocaine-induced alterations in dopamine signaling using multiple kinetic measures.
abstract: The fast sampling rates of fast scan cyclic voltammetry make it a favorable method for measuring changes in brain monoamine release and uptake kinetics in slice, anesthetized, and freely moving preparations. The most common analysis technique for evaluating changes in dopamine signaling uses well-established Michaelis-Menten kinetic methods that can accurately model dopamine release and uptake parameters across multiple experimental conditions. Nevertheless, over the years, many researchers have turned to other measures to estimate changes in dopamine release and uptake, yet to our knowledge no systematic comparison amongst these measures has been conducted. To address this lack of uniformity in kinetic analyses, we have created the Demon Voltammetry and Analysis software suite, which is freely available to academic and non-profit institutions. Here we present an explanation of the Demon Voltammetry acquisition and analysis features, and demonstrate its utility for acquiring voltammetric data under in vitro, in vivo anesthetized, and freely moving conditions. Additionally, the software was used to compare the sensitivity of multiple kinetic measures of release and uptake to cocaine-induced changes in electrically evoked dopamine efflux in nucleus accumbens core slices. Specifically, we examined and compared tau, full width at half height, half-life, T₂₀, T₈₀, slope, peak height, calibrated peak dopamine concentration, and area under the curve to the well-characterized Michaelis-Menten parameters, dopamine per pulse, maximal uptake rate, and apparent affinity. Based on observed results we recommend tau for measuring dopamine uptake and calibrated peak dopamine concentration for measuring dopamine release.

title: Neuropharmacology of the mesolimbic system and associated circuits on social hierarchies.
abstract: Social hierarchies are fundamental organizational structures across mammalian species, yet the underlying neuropharmacological mechanisms remain poorly understood. The mesolimbic dopaminergic system, critical for reward processing and motivation, has been implicated in dominance behaviors, but its specific role in hierarchy establishment and maintenance requires further investigation. Here, we examined the contribution of dopamine D1 and D2 receptors within the nucleus accumbens (NAc) and ventral tegmental area (VTA) to social rank determination in adult male C57BL/6J mice. Using a tube test paradigm to assess dominance relationships, we administered selective D1 (SCH23390) and D2 (sulpiride) receptor antagonists via bilateral microinjections prior to social encounters. Additionally, we employed optogenetic stimulation of VTA dopamine neurons during hierarchy formation trials. Behavioral analysis revealed that D1 receptor blockade in the NAc significantly reduced dominance scores (p<0.01), while D2 antagonism had minimal effects. VTA dopamine neuron stimulation enhanced dominant behaviors in subordinate animals (p<0.05). Immunohistochemical analysis showed increased c-Fos expression in NAc medium spiny neurons following successful dominance displays. Microdialysis measurements confirmed elevated extracellular dopamine levels in dominant animals during social interactions. These findings demonstrate that mesolimbic D1 receptor signaling is necessary for establishing social dominance, while VTA dopamine release is sufficient to promote hierarchical status. Our results provide novel insights into the neuropharmacological basis of social hierarchies and suggest potential therapeutic targets for disorders involving aberrant social behaviors.

title: [Neuropharmacology yesterday, today and tomorrow].
abstract: The development of neuropharmacology as a scientific discipline has undergone remarkable transformation since the pioneering investigations of Sechenov and Pavlov. Early neurophysiological studies focused primarily on anatomical localization and basic reflex mechanisms, while contemporary neuropharmacological research emphasizes molecular mechanisms of synaptic transmission and receptor pharmacology. This analytical review examines the evolution of neuropharmacological concepts and methodologies from classical neurophysiology to modern molecular approaches. Historical analysis was conducted through systematic examination of key publications spanning seven decades, from initial studies of autonomic pharmacology to recent investigations of neurotransmitter systems. Classical experiments established fundamental principles of chemical neurotransmission, beginning with acetylcholine at neuromuscular junctions and extending to central monoaminergic pathways. Contemporary research has identified numerous neurotransmitter systems including dopamine, serotonin, GABA, and neuropeptides, elucidating their roles in behavior, cognition, and neurological disorders. Methodological advances in radioligand binding, electrophysiology, and biochemical assays have enabled precise characterization of receptor subtypes and their pharmacological profiles. Current investigations focus on G-protein coupled receptors, ion channels, and intracellular signaling cascades. Future directions will likely emphasize molecular cloning approaches, computer-assisted drug design, and therapeutic applications for neurodegenerative diseases. The integration of classical neurophysiological principles with emerging molecular techniques represents the optimal strategy for advancing neuropharmacological knowledge in service of clinical medicine and our understanding of higher nervous system function.
journal: Fiziologicheskii zhurnal SSSR imeni I. M. Sechenova

title: Understanding schizoaffective disorder: from psychobiology to psychosocial functioning.
abstract: Psychobiologic evidence and psychosocial functioning in patients with schizoaffective disorder suggest that the disease may be a distinct disorder, a variant of schizophrenia or affective disorders, the comorbidity of schizophrenia and a mood disorder, or an intermediate disorder on a spectrum that ranges from schizophrenia to mood disorders. These data, although inconclusive, contribute to clinicians' understanding of the etiology of the disorder. Further research may lead to an increased understanding of the disorder, improved treatment, and, ultimately, better outcomes.

title: Amino acid neurochemistry of the vertebrate retina.
abstract: The vertebrate retina contains a diverse array of amino acid neurotransmitters and neuromodulators that mediate synaptic transmission between photoreceptors, bipolar cells, horizontal cells, amacrine cells, and ganglion cells. While glutamate and GABA have been extensively characterized as the primary excitatory and inhibitory neurotransmitters respectively, the functional roles of glycine, taurine, aspartate, and other amino acids remain incompletely understood. We employed high-performance liquid chromatography, immunocytochemistry, and electrophysiological recordings to comprehensively map amino acid distribution and function across retinal layers in cat, rabbit, and goldfish preparations. Glutamate immunoreactivity was predominantly localized to photoreceptor terminals and ON-bipolar cells, with tissue concentrations reaching 8-12 mM in outer plexiform layer. GABA was concentrated in horizontal cells and a subset of amacrine cells, showing species-specific distribution patterns. Glycine demonstrated robust immunostaining in narrow-field amacrine cells and exhibited co-localization with GABA in 23% of GABAergic neurons. Taurine, present at unexpectedly high concentrations (15-20 mM), showed uniform distribution across all retinal layers with particular enrichment in Müller cells. Electrophysiological analysis revealed that glycine mediates crossover inhibition in OFF-pathway ganglion cells, while taurine modulates calcium homeostasis and exhibits neuroprotective properties under ischemic conditions. These findings demonstrate that amino acid neurotransmission in the vertebrate retina involves complex, overlapping systems that extend beyond classical glutamatergic and GABAergic pathways, suggesting novel therapeutic targets for retinal degenerative diseases.
journal: Progress in retinal and eye research

title: The Domino Effect: Ed Domino's early studies of Psychoactive Drugs.
abstract: University of Michigan Pharmacology Professor Ed Domino is an expert in the field of neuropsychopharmacology. For over six decades, Dr. Domino has made many contributions to our understanding of psychoactive drugs, but is most well-known for his role in the development of ketamine anesthesia. This article covers the story behind this discovery, along with many other fascinating personal and professional anecdotes, all of which provide insight into the career of a remarkable scientist.

title: An integrative dynamic model of brain energy metabolism using in vivo neurochemical measurements.
abstract: Brain energy metabolism is critical for normal neuronal function, yet the dynamic relationships between glucose utilization, oxygen consumption, and neurotransmitter cycling remain poorly understood in vivo. We developed an integrative computational model that combines real-time neurochemical measurements with metabolic flux analysis to characterize brain energy dynamics during physiological states. Using microdialysis probes in awake, freely-moving rats (n=12), we simultaneously measured extracellular glucose, lactate, glutamate, and GABA concentrations in the hippocampus during rest, mild stress, and recovery periods. Oxygen tension was monitored using polarographic electrodes, while local field potentials were recorded to assess neural activity. These experimental data were incorporated into a multi-compartment mathematical model describing neuronal and glial metabolic interactions, including the astrocyte-neuron lactate shuttle and glutamate-glutamine cycle. The model successfully reproduced observed metabolic patterns, revealing that astrocytic glucose uptake increased 340% during neural activation while neuronal glucose utilization rose only 180%. Lactate production by astrocytes peaked within 2 minutes of stimulus onset, preceding maximal neuronal lactate consumption by 45 seconds. The model predicted that glutamate clearance accounted for 60-70% of total energy expenditure during high activity states. Sensitivity analysis identified astrocytic glucose transport and glutamate uptake kinetics as critical parameters governing metabolic responses. This integrative approach provides quantitative insights into brain bioenergetics and establishes a framework for investigating metabolic dysfunction in neurological disorders. The model's predictive capability may prove valuable for optimizing neuroprotective strategies and understanding energy-related pathophysiology.

title: Amino acid neurochemistry of the vertebrate retina.
abstract: The dominant neurochemicals involved in encoding sensory information are the amino acid neurotransmitters, glutamate, gamma-aminobutyrate (GABA) and glycine, which mediate fast point-to-point synaptic transmission in the retina and other parts of the central nervous system. The relative abundance of these neurochemicals and the existence of neuronal and glial uptake mechanisms as well as a plethora of receptors support the key role these neurochemicals play in shaping neural information. However, in addition to subserving neurotransmitter roles, amino acids subserve normal metabolic,cellular functions, may be precursors for other amino acids, and may also be associated with protein synthesis. Post-embedding immunocytochemistry of small molecules has allowed the characterization of multiple amino acid profiles within subpopulations of neurons in the vertebrate retina. The general theme emerging from these studies is that the retinal through pathway uses glutamate as its neurotransmitter, and the lateral elements, GABA and/or glycine. Co-localization studies using quantitative immunocytochemistry have shown that virtually all neuronal space can be accounted for by the three dominant amino acids. In addition, co-localization studies have demonstrated that there are no purely aspartate, glutamine, alanine. leucine or ornithine immunoreactive neurons and thus these amino acids are likely to act as metabolites and may sustain glutamate production through a multitude of enzymatic pathways. The mapping of multiple cellular metabolic profiles during development or in degenerating retinas has shown that amino acid neurochemistry is a sensitive marker for metabolic activity. In the degenerating retina, (RCS retina), neurochemical anomalies were evident early in development (from birth), even before photoreceptors mature at PND6-8 implying a generalized metabolic dysfunction. Identification of metabolic anomalies within subpopulation of neurons is now possible and can be used to investigate a multitude of retinal functions including amino acid metabolic and neurochemical changes secondary to external insult as well as to expand our understanding of the intricate interrelationship between neurons and glia.
journal: Progress in retinal and eye research

title: Psychopharmacology in fragile X syndrome--present and future.
abstract: Fragile X syndrome (FXS), caused by mutations in the FMR1 gene leading to absence of fragile X mental retardation protein (FMRP), represents the most common inherited cause of intellectual disability and autism spectrum behaviors. Current psychopharmacological interventions for FXS rely primarily on symptom-based treatments targeting hyperactivity, anxiety, aggression, and stereotypic behaviors, with limited understanding of underlying neurobiological mechanisms. This review synthesizes existing literature on psychopharmacological approaches in FXS and examines emerging therapeutic targets based on recent advances in FXS pathophysiology. A comprehensive literature search identified 47 studies examining pharmacological interventions in FXS populations, including stimulants, selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors, antipsychotics, and anticonvulsants. Traditional medications show modest efficacy for behavioral symptoms but do not address core cognitive deficits. Stimulants demonstrate effectiveness for attention and hyperactivity in 65-70% of individuals, while SSRIs show variable response for anxiety and repetitive behaviors. Antipsychotics may reduce aggression but carry significant metabolic risks. Emerging research on FMRP function reveals dysregulation of metabotropic glutamate receptor 5 (mGluR5) signaling and altered synaptic plasticity, suggesting novel therapeutic targets. Preclinical studies with mGluR5 antagonists, GABA receptor modulators, and protein synthesis inhibitors show promising results in FXS animal models, with several compounds entering clinical trials. Future psychopharmacological approaches in FXS should incorporate mechanism-based therapies targeting underlying synaptic dysfunction while maintaining symptom-focused treatments. Integration of genetic insights with clinical pharmacology may ultimately provide more effective, individualized therapeutic strategies for this complex neurodevelopmental disorder.
journal: Mental retardation and developmental disabilities research reviews

title: Trends in use of psychotropic medications among patients treated with cholinesterase inhibitors in Japan from 2002 to 2010.
abstract: The concurrent use of psychotropic medications with cholinesterase inhibitors (ChEIs) in dementia patients has raised concerns about potential drug interactions and adverse effects, yet prescribing patterns remain poorly characterized in Asian populations. This retrospective cohort study examined trends in psychotropic medication use among Japanese dementia patients receiving ChEI therapy between 2002 and 2010. We analyzed prescription data from the Japanese Medical Data Center database, identifying 18,456 patients aged ≥65 years who initiated ChEI treatment (donepezil, rivastigmine, or galantamine). Concurrent psychotropic medications were categorized as antipsychotics, antidepressants, anxiolytics, or hypnotics. Temporal trends were assessed using Cochran-Armitage tests, and logistic regression models identified factors associated with polypharmacy. Overall, 64.2% of patients received at least one psychotropic medication concurrently with ChEIs. Antipsychotic use increased significantly from 28.1% in 2002 to 41.7% in 2010 (p<0.001), while antidepressant use rose from 22.3% to 35.4% (p<0.001). Anxiolytic and hypnotic prescribing remained stable at approximately 45% and 38%, respectively. Atypical antipsychotics comprised 78% of antipsychotic prescriptions by 2010. Factors associated with increased psychotropic use included younger age, male gender, and residence in urban areas. The high prevalence and increasing trend of psychotropic co-prescribing with ChEIs in Japanese dementia patients highlights the need for careful monitoring of potential drug interactions and development of evidence-based prescribing guidelines for this vulnerable population.

title: Executive (dys)function after stroke: special considerations for behavioral pharmacology.
abstract: Stroke is a worldwide leading cause of death and long-term disability with concurrent secondary consequences that are largely comprised of mood dysfunction, as well as sensory, motor, and cognitive deficits. This review focuses on the cognitive deficits associated with stroke specific to executive dysfunction (including decision making, working memory, and cognitive flexibility) in humans, nonhuman primates, and additional animal models. Further, we review some of the cellular and molecular underpinnings of the individual components of executive dysfunction and their neuroanatomical substrates after stroke, with an emphasis on the changes that occur during biogenic monoamine neurotransmission. We concentrate primarily on changes in the catecholaminergic (dopaminergic and noradrenergic) and serotonergic systems at the levels of neurotransmitter synthesis, distribution, reuptake, and degradation. We also discuss potential secondary stroke-related behavioral deficits (specifically, poststroke depression as well as drug-abuse potential and addiction) and their relationship with stroke-induced deficits in executive function, an especially important consideration given that the average age of the human stroke population is decreasing. In the final sections, we address pharmacological considerations for the treatment of ischemia and the subsequent functional impairment, as well as current limitations in the field of stroke and executive function research.

title: Neurochemical substrates linked to impulsive and compulsive phenotypes in addiction: A preclinical perspective.
abstract: Addiction is characterized by a transition from impulsive drug-seeking to compulsive drug-taking behaviors, yet the neurochemical mechanisms underlying this phenotypic shift remain poorly understood. We examined dopaminergic, serotonergic, and GABAergic neurotransmitter systems in brain regions implicated in addiction using a well-validated rat self-administration model. Male Sprague-Dawley rats (n=48) underwent cocaine self-administration training followed by behavioral phenotyping using delay discounting and compulsivity tasks. Animals were classified as high-impulsive (HI) or low-impulsive (LI) based on delay discounting performance, and high-compulsive (HC) or low-compulsive (LC) based on punishment-resistant responding. Post-mortem neurochemical analysis was performed using high-performance liquid chromatography in the nucleus accumbens, prefrontal cortex, and dorsal striatum. HI animals showed significantly reduced dopamine transporter expression in the nucleus accumbens (p<0.01) and elevated dopamine D2 receptor binding in the prefrontal cortex compared to LI rats. HC animals exhibited decreased serotonin transporter density in the dorsal striatum (p<0.05) and reduced GABA concentrations in the prefrontal cortex relative to LC animals. Importantly, impulsive and compulsive phenotypes were associated with distinct neurochemical profiles, with minimal overlap between groups. Correlation analyses revealed that compulsivity scores negatively correlated with prefrontal GABA levels (r=-0.67, p<0.001), while impulsivity measures correlated with accumbal dopamine transporter expression (r=-0.58, p<0.01). These findings suggest that impulsive and compulsive addiction phenotypes are mediated by dissociable neurochemical substrates, providing potential targets for personalized therapeutic interventions in substance use disorders.

title: A pre-clinical study showing how dopaminergic drugs administered during pre-exposure can impair or facilitate latent inhibition.
abstract: Latent inhibition (LI), the reduced conditioning to a stimulus following non-reinforced pre-exposure, is disrupted in schizophrenia and serves as a valuable model for studying attentional and associative learning deficits. While dopaminergic dysfunction has been implicated in schizophrenia pathophysiology, the specific role of dopamine receptor subtypes in LI remains poorly understood. This study examined how selective dopaminergic manipulations during pre-exposure affect LI acquisition in rats. Male Sprague-Dawley rats (n=8-10 per group) received intraperitoneal injections of saline, the D2/D3 agonist quinpirole (0.5 mg/kg), the D1/D5 agonist SKF-38393 (10 mg/kg), the D2 antagonist haloperidol (0.1 mg/kg), or the D1 antagonist SCH-23390 (0.05 mg/kg) 30 minutes before each of five tone pre-exposure sessions. Following a two-day interval, all animals underwent tone-shock conditioning and extinction testing. Vehicle-treated pre-exposed animals demonstrated robust LI compared to non-pre-exposed controls (p<0.01). Quinpirole administration during pre-exposure completely abolished LI (p<0.001), while SKF-38393 had no significant effect. Conversely, haloperidol enhanced LI magnitude (p<0.05), whereas SCH-23390 produced no change. D2-like receptor stimulation during pre-exposure impaired subsequent LI expression, while D2 blockade facilitated it. These findings suggest that D2-mediated dopaminergic activity during stimulus pre-exposure critically modulates attentional processing mechanisms underlying LI, supporting the hypothesis that excessive dopaminergic transmission may contribute to attentional deficits observed in schizophrenia through disruption of normal stimulus filtering processes.

title: Proteomic identification of binding partners for the brain metabolite lanthionine ketimine (LK) and documentation of LK effects on microglia and motoneuron cell cultures.
abstract: Lanthionine ketimine (LK) represents a poorly understood class of thioethers present in mammalian CNS. Previous work has indicated high-affinity interaction of LK with synaptosomal membrane protein(s), but neither LK binding partners nor specific bioactivities have been reported. In this study, LK was chemically synthesized and used as an affinity agent to capture binding partners from mammalian brain lysate. Liquid chromatography with electrospray ionization-mass spectrometry of electrophoretically separated, LK-bound proteins identified polypeptides implicated in axon remodeling or vesicle trafficking and diseases including Alzheimer's disease and schizophrenia: collapsin response mediator protein-2/dihydropyrimidinase-like protein-2 (CRMP2/DRP2/DPYSL2), myelin basic protein, and syntaxin-binding protein-1 (STXBP1/Munc-18). Also identified was the recently discovered glutathione-binding protein lanthionine synthetase-like protein-1. Functional consequences of LK:CRMP2 interactions were probed through immunoprecipitation studies using brain lysate wherein LK was found to increase CRMP2 coprecipitation with its partner neurofibromin-1 but decreased CRMP2 coprecipitation with beta-tubulin. Functional studies of NSC-34 motor neuron-like cells indicated that a cell-permeable LK-ester, LKE, was nontoxic and protective against oxidative challenge with H(2)O(2). LKE-treated NSC-34 cells significantly increased neurite number and length in a serum concentration-dependent manner, consistent with a CRMP2 interaction. Finally, LKE antagonized the activation of EOC-20 microglia by inflammogens. The results are discussed with reference to possible biochemical origins, paracrine functions, neurological significance, and pharmacological potential of lanthionyl compounds.
journal: The Journal of neuroscience : the official journal of the Society for Neuroscience

title: Integral methods for automatic quantification of fast-scan-cyclic-voltammetry detected neurotransmitters.
abstract: Fast-scan cyclic voltammetry (FSCV) has emerged as the gold standard for real-time detection of neurotransmitter release in brain tissue, yet current quantification methods rely heavily on manual peak identification and baseline correction, introducing operator bias and limiting throughput. Existing automated approaches often fail to accurately detect transient dopamine signals in noisy biological recordings, particularly during complex behavioral paradigms. We developed a novel integral-based computational framework for automated FSCV signal quantification that leverages continuous wavelet transforms and machine learning algorithms to identify and quantify dopamine release events. Our method was validated using synthetic datasets with known signal characteristics and tested on ex vivo brain slice recordings from rat nucleus accumbens (n=8) and in vivo recordings from freely-moving mice during reward-seeking behavior (n=12). The integral-based approach demonstrated superior performance compared to traditional peak-detection methods, achieving 94.3% accuracy in synthetic data validation and significantly reducing false positive rates (2.1% vs 8.7%, p<0.001) in biological recordings. Quantification precision improved by 35% compared to manual analysis, with inter-rater reliability increasing from r=0.76 to r=0.94. Processing time was reduced from 45 minutes to 3 minutes per dataset. The algorithm successfully detected dopamine transients with amplitudes as low as 50 nM and temporal resolution of 100 ms, while maintaining robust performance across different electrode types and experimental conditions. This automated quantification framework represents a significant advancement in FSCV data analysis, enabling high-throughput screening applications and reducing analytical bias in neurochemical measurements.

title: Discovery of novel tetrahydro-pyrazolo [4,3-c] pyridines for the treatment of neuropathic pain: synthesis and neuropharmacology.
abstract: Neuropathic pain represents a significant therapeutic challenge with limited treatment options displaying both efficacy and tolerability. Voltage-gated sodium channels, particularly Nav1.7 and Nav1.8, have emerged as promising targets for neuropathic pain management due to their preferential expression in nociceptive neurons. In this study, we report the discovery and characterization of a novel series of tetrahydro-pyrazolo[4,3-c]pyridine derivatives as selective sodium channel modulators. A library of 47 compounds was synthesized through a convergent approach involving cyclization of appropriately substituted hydrazines with β-ketoesters, followed by subsequent reduction and functionalization. Lead optimization focused on substitutions at the N1 and C6 positions to enhance potency and selectivity. Electrophysiological evaluation using whole-cell patch-clamp techniques on recombinant Nav channels revealed that compound 23c exhibited potent inhibition of Nav1.7 (IC₅₀ = 180 nM) and Nav1.8 (IC₅₀ = 95 nM) with >50-fold selectivity over cardiac Nav1.5. Structure-activity relationships indicated that electron-withdrawing groups at the C6 position and lipophilic substituents at N1 were crucial for activity. In vivo studies using the chronic constriction injury model in rats demonstrated significant anti-allodynic effects for compound 23c (ED₅₀ = 12 mg/kg, p.o.) without motor impairment or cardiovascular effects at therapeutic doses. These results establish tetrahydro-pyrazolo[4,3-c]pyridines as a promising new chemotype for developing next-generation analgesics targeting peripheral sodium channels in neuropathic pain conditions.

title: Chelators for investigating zinc metalloneurochemistry.
abstract: The physiology and pathology of mobile zinc signaling has become an important topic in metalloneurochemistry. To study the action of mobile zinc effectively, specialized tools are required that probe the temporal and positional changes of zinc ions within live tissue and cells. In the present article we describe the design and implementation of selective zinc chelators as antagonists to interrogate the function of mobile zinc, with an emphasis on the pools of vesicular zinc in the terminals of hippocampal mossy fiber buttons.

title: Psychopharmacology of the endocannabinoids: far beyond anandamide.
abstract: The study of endocannabinoid pharmacology has proceeded from the discovery of Δ9-tetrahydrocannabinol, the main psychoactive compound in Cannabis sativa, to the identification of an endogenous endocannabinoid system that is essential for physiological modulation of neuronal functions. We have not yet achieved a complete understanding of the various roles of the endocannabinoids, but this is one of the fastest-growing fields in psychopharmacology. This review starts with a brief historical description of the discovery of the endocannabinoids and then focuses on recent pharmacological advances and recently discovered endocannabinoid mechanisms of action (e.g. functional selectivity, allosterism, and receptor trafficking). Finally, we will discuss the contention that the existence of evidence-based therapeutic applications for cannabinoids and the wide range of physiological functions affected by endocannabinoids suggests that the careful study of the endocannabinoid system may lead to the development of novel therapeutic drugs with higher societal acceptability and lower side effects profiles.

title: Photochemical and pharmacological evaluation of 7-nitroindolinyl-and 4-methoxy-7-nitroindolinyl-amino acids as novel, fast caged neurotransmitters.
abstract: Reagents capable of rapid and efficient release of neuroactive amino acids (L-glutamate, GABA and glycine) upon flash photolysis of thermally stable, inert precursors have been elusive. 7-Nitroindolinyl (NI)-caged and 4-methoxy-7-nitroindolinyl (MNI)-caged compounds that fulfil these criteria are evaluated here. These caged precursors are highly resistant to hydrolysis. Photolysis is fast (half time< or =0.26 ms) and the conversion achieved with a xenon flashlamp is about 15% for the NI-caged L-glutamate and about 35% for the MNI-caged L-glutamate. A procedure is described for calibration of photolysis in a microscope-based experimental apparatus. NI-caged L-glutamate itself showed no agonist or antagonist effects on AMPA and NMDA receptors in cultured neurones, and had no effect on climbing fibre activation of Purkinje neurones. A control compound with identical photochemistry that generated an inert phosphate upon photolysis was used to confirm that the intermediates and by-products of photolysis have no deleterious effects. MNI-caged L-glutamate is as stable and fast as NI-caged L-glutamate and similarly inert at glutamate receptors, but about 2.5 times more efficient. However, NI-caged GABA is an antagonist at GABA(A) receptors and NI-glycine an antagonist at glycine receptors. The results show the utility and limitations of these fast and stable caged neurotransmitters in the investigation of synaptic processes.

title: Regional neurochemical profiles in the human brain measured by ¹H MRS at 7 T using local B₁ shimming.
abstract: Increased sensitivity and chemical shift dispersion at ultra-high magnetic fields enable the precise quantification of an extended range of brain metabolites from (1)H MRS. However, all previous neurochemical profiling studies using single-voxel MRS at 7 T have been limited to data acquired from the occipital lobe with half-volume coils. The challenges of (1)H MRS of the human brain at 7 T include short T(2) and complex B(1) distribution that imposes limitations on the maximum achievable B(1) strength. In this study, the feasibility of acquiring and quantifying short-echo (TE =8 ms), single-voxel (1)H MR spectra from multiple brain regions was demonstrated by utilizing a 16-channel transceiver array coil with 16 independent transmit channels, allowing local transmit B(1) (B(1)(+)) shimming. Spectra were acquired from volumes of interest of 1-8 mL in brain regions that are of interest for various neurological disorders: frontal white matter, posterior cingulate, putamen, substantia nigra, pons and cerebellar vermis. Local B(1)(+) shimming substantially increased the transmit efficiency, especially in the peripheral and ventral brain regions. By optimizing a STEAM sequence for utilization with a 16-channel coil, artifact-free spectra were acquired with a small chemical shift displacement error (<5% /ppm/direction) from all regions. The high signal-to-noise ratio enabled the quantification of neurochemical profiles consisting of at least nine metabolites, including γ-aminobutyric acid, glutamate and glutathione, in all brain regions. Significant differences in neurochemical profiles were observed between brain regions. For example, γ-aminobutyric acid levels were highest in the substantia nigra, total creatine was highest in the cerebellar vermis and total choline was highest in the pons, consistent with the known biochemistry of these regions. These findings demonstrate that single-voxel (1)H MRS at ultra-high field can reliably detect region-specific neurochemical patterns in the human brain, and has the potential to objectively detect alterations in neurochemical profiles associated with neurological diseases.

title: Proton magnetic resonance spectroscopy detection of neurotransmitters in dorsomedial medulla correlate with spontaneous baroreceptor reflex function.
abstract: The dorsomedial medulla (DMM) contains critical cardiovascular regulatory neurons that modulate baroreceptor reflex sensitivity, but the neurochemical mechanisms underlying this control remain poorly understood. We hypothesized that GABA and glutamate concentrations in the DMM would correlate with spontaneous baroreceptor reflex function in conscious rats. Male Sprague-Dawley rats (n=24) were instrumented with arterial catheters for continuous blood pressure monitoring. Spontaneous baroreceptor sensitivity (BRS) was calculated using sequence analysis of systolic pressure and pulse interval over 2-hour recording periods. Animals were then anesthetized and subjected to localized proton magnetic resonance spectroscopy (¹H-MRS) using a 7.0 Tesla scanner with a 2×2×2 mm³ voxel positioned in the DMM. GABA and glutamate concentrations were quantified relative to creatine using LCModel analysis. BRS ranged from 0.8 to 3.2 ms/mmHg across animals. DMM GABA concentrations showed a strong positive correlation with BRS (r=0.71, P<0.001), while glutamate levels were inversely correlated with BRS (r=-0.58, P=0.003). The GABA/glutamate ratio demonstrated the strongest relationship with baroreceptor sensitivity (r=0.79, P<0.001). Multiple regression analysis revealed that DMM neurotransmitter balance accounted for 65% of the variance in spontaneous BRS. These findings provide the first direct neurochemical evidence linking GABAergic-glutamatergic balance in the DMM to baroreceptor reflex function in vivo, suggesting that altered neurotransmitter homeostasis in this region may contribute to impaired cardiovascular regulation in hypertensive states.

title: Emerging roles for novel antipsychotic medications in the treatment of schizophrenia.
abstract: Antipsychotic medications are the mainstay of treatment for schizophrenia. The recent advent of atypical antipsychotics has provided new clinical options and set higher expectations for the treatment of schizophrenia. It is not yet clear how each different drug will fit within the therapeutic armamentarium and this lack is most evident with considering patients with treatment refractory schizophrenia. On the other hand, the expectation of superior efficacy, more benign side effect profile and potential to impact the longitudinal course of schizophrenia provide a rationale for the use of novel antipsychotics as a first-line treatment of schizophrenia.
journal: The Psychiatric clinics of North America

title: Effect of ethanolic extract of Rubia peregrina L. (Rubiaceae) on monoamine-mediated behaviour.
abstract: Rubia peregrina L. (Rubiaceae), commonly known as wild madder, has been traditionally used in Mediterranean folk medicine for treating various neuropsychiatric disorders, yet its neuropharmacological properties remain largely unexplored. This study investigated the effects of ethanolic extract of R. peregrina aerial parts on monoamine-mediated behaviours in experimental models. The ethanolic extract was prepared using standardized procedures and subjected to preliminary phytochemical screening. Male Swiss albino mice (25-30 g) were administered R. peregrina extract (100, 200, and 400 mg/kg, p.o.) and evaluated using forced swimming test, tail suspension test, open field test, and elevated plus maze. Additionally, monoamine oxidase (MAO-A and MAO-B) inhibitory activity was assessed using fluorometric assays, while brain monoamine levels were determined by HPLC. Phytochemical analysis revealed the presence of anthraquinones, flavonoids, and phenolic compounds. R. peregrina extract (200 and 400 mg/kg) significantly reduced immobility time in both forced swimming test (p<0.01) and tail suspension test (p<0.05) compared to control, suggesting antidepressant-like activity. The extract increased locomotor activity and exploratory behaviour in open field test, while enhancing anxiolytic-like effects in elevated plus maze. Biochemical analysis demonstrated significant inhibition of MAO-A (IC₅₀ = 145.2 μg/ml) and moderate MAO-B inhibition (IC₅₀ = 298.7 μg/ml). Brain tissue analysis revealed increased serotonin, dopamine, and norepinephrine levels following extract treatment. These findings suggest that R. peregrina possesses significant psychoactive properties mediated through monoaminergic mechanisms, supporting its traditional use and warranting further investigation as a potential natural antidepressant.

title: Demonstration and use of nanoliter sampling of in vivo rat vitreous and vitreoretinal interface.
abstract: An understanding of the chemical microenvironments at different locations on the retina can provide unique insights into retinal neurochemistry and pathology. The anatomical shape and the small volumes available from a spatially restricted volume greatly complicate these types of measurements. The aim of this study was to demonstrate an in vivo sampling system to probe different regions of the rat retina. A low-flow push-pull perfusion probe was developed with concentric fused-silica capillaries. It was designed to fit through a 29-gauge needle for placement in the vitreous and at the vitreoretinal interface of the rat eye. Physiological saline was perfused and withdrawn through outer and inner capillaries, respectively, at flow rates between 10 and 50 nl/min. Samples of 500 nl were collected for amino acid analysis by capillary electrophoresis. Perfusion of a potent and selective inhibitor of the excitatory amino acid transporters was performed through the probe with the tip located 1-2 mm away from the optic nerve head. Ten amino acids were quantified from the perfusates of vitreous and the vitreoretinal interface. Sampling through time showed the use of this system to monitor retinal changes in these amino acids. The infusion of a transport protein antagonist shows a statistically significant increase in the glutamate concentration in collected samples when the probe tip is placed peripheral to but not over the optic nerve head. We demonstrate a new method for following neurochemical changes at the retina with spatial resolution. This in vivo method is widely applicable to the site-specific study of states of normal and dysfunctional retinal neurochemistry.

title: How to design PET experiments to study neurochemistry: application to alcoholism.
abstract: Positron emission tomography (PET) has emerged as a powerful tool for investigating neurochemical alterations in alcohol use disorders, yet methodological considerations critically impact data interpretation and reproducibility. This review synthesizes current best practices for designing PET neurochemistry studies in alcoholism research, addressing common pitfalls and optimization strategies. We examined 127 published PET studies investigating dopaminergic, GABAergic, and glutamatergic systems in alcohol-dependent subjects from 2000-2013, analyzing experimental design parameters including radioligand selection, scanning protocols, and subject characterization. Key design considerations include appropriate radioligand choice based on target receptor density and binding kinetics, standardized abstinence periods to minimize acute withdrawal effects, and careful matching of control subjects for age, sex, and smoking status. Quantification approaches significantly influenced effect sizes, with reference tissue methods showing greater sensitivity for detecting group differences in striatal dopamine D2/D3 receptor availability compared to arterial input function methods. Studies incorporating pharmacological challenges revealed enhanced detection of synaptic neurotransmitter changes, particularly for dopamine release paradigms. Sample size calculations indicated that detecting 15-20% group differences in receptor binding requires 12-15 subjects per group with 80% power. Standardized abstinence periods of 3-4 weeks optimized detection of trait-related neuroadaptations while minimizing acute withdrawal confounds. These methodological guidelines provide a framework for designing robust PET neurochemistry studies in alcoholism, facilitating more reliable characterization of addiction-related neurobiological alterations and improved translation to therapeutic interventions.
journal: The Yale journal of biology and medicine

title: Astra Award Lecture. The psychopharmacology of 5-HT3 receptors.
abstract: The 5-HT3 receptor represents a unique member of the serotonin receptor family, distinguished by its ligand-gated ion channel structure rather than the G-protein coupling characteristic of other 5-HT receptor subtypes. Recent advances in molecular pharmacology have enabled detailed characterization of this receptor's distribution, function, and therapeutic potential. This review synthesizes current knowledge of 5-HT3 receptor psychopharmacology based on radioligand binding studies, electrophysiological recordings, and behavioral pharmacology in rodent models. 5-HT3 receptors demonstrate highest density in limbic structures including the hippocampus and amygdala, with moderate expression in cortical areas and brainstem nuclei involved in emesis and autonomic control. Selective antagonists such as ondansetron and tropisetron exhibit nanomolar affinity and demonstrate anxiolytic-like effects in conflict paradigms, reduce ethanol consumption in voluntary drinking models, and block chemotherapy-induced emesis in ferret studies. Conversely, 5-HT3 receptor agonists including 2-methyl-5-HT produce anxiogenic responses and facilitate dopamine release in mesolimbic pathways. Electrophysiological studies reveal that 5-HT3 activation produces rapid membrane depolarization through sodium and calcium influx, particularly in GABAergic interneurons. These findings suggest 5-HT3 receptors modulate anxiety, addiction, and emetic responses through distinct neural circuits. The development of highly selective 5-HT3 antagonists represents a promising therapeutic approach for anxiety disorders, substance abuse, and chemotherapy-induced nausea, warranting continued investigation of this receptor system's clinical potential.

title: Consensus Guidelines for Therapeutic Drug Monitoring in Neuropsychopharmacology: Update 2017.
abstract: Therapeutic drug monitoring (TDM) has emerged as a critical tool for optimizing neuropsychopharmacological treatment, yet inconsistent implementation across clinical settings has highlighted the need for updated evidence-based guidelines. This consensus update reviews current literature and expert recommendations for TDM applications in psychiatric and neurological disorders through 2017. A multidisciplinary expert panel conducted systematic literature reviews of TDM studies published between 2012-2017, focusing on antidepressants, antipsychotics, mood stabilizers, and antiepileptic drugs used in neuropsychiatric conditions. Recommendations were developed using modified Delphi methodology incorporating pharmacokinetic data, clinical efficacy studies, and real-world effectiveness evidence. The panel identified strong evidence supporting routine TDM for lithium, several tricyclic antidepressants, and select antipsychotics including clozapine, olanzapine, and aripiprazole. Moderate evidence supported TDM for lamotrigine, valproic acid, and certain SSRIs in specific populations. New recommendations include expanded TDM indications for treatment-resistant depression, pediatric and geriatric populations, and patients with comorbid medical conditions affecting drug metabolism. Updated therapeutic reference ranges were established for 23 neuropsychiatric medications based on recent concentration-response studies. Cost-effectiveness analyses demonstrated favorable outcomes when TDM was implemented systematically rather than selectively. These updated guidelines provide clinicians with evidence-based recommendations for implementing TDM to improve treatment outcomes, minimize adverse effects, and reduce healthcare costs in neuropsychopharmacological practice. Regular TDM should be considered standard of care for high-risk populations and specific medication classes.

title: Photolysis of caged neurotransmitters: theory and procedures for light delivery.
abstract: Caged neurotransmitters have emerged as powerful tools for investigating synaptic transmission and neural circuit function with high spatial and temporal resolution. However, optimal application of these photolabile compounds requires precise understanding of photolysis kinetics and standardized protocols for light delivery systems. We present a comprehensive theoretical framework for caged neurotransmitter photolysis and describe validated procedures for implementing UV flash photolysis in acute brain slice preparations. Photolysis efficiency was characterized using caged glutamate (CNB-glutamate) and caged GABA (CNB-GABA) in hippocampal CA1 pyramidal neurons through whole-cell patch-clamp recordings. Flash lamp systems delivering 1-5 ms UV pulses (peak wavelength 365 nm) achieved 60-80% uncaging efficiency with spatial resolution of 10-20 μm when coupled to appropriate objective lenses. Theoretical modeling revealed that photolysis follows first-order kinetics with rate constants dependent on photon flux density, cage photolysis cross-section, and quantum yield. Practical considerations include cage compound concentration (50-500 μM), flash intensity calibration, and optical pathway optimization to minimize tissue photodamage while maximizing neurotransmitter release. Validation experiments demonstrated reliable induction of fast synaptic responses with rise times of 2-8 ms, comparable to natural synaptic transmission. These standardized protocols enable reproducible caged compound experiments across laboratories and provide essential technical foundations for advanced applications including synaptic plasticity studies, circuit mapping, and investigation of neurotransmitter receptor kinetics in native tissue environments.

title: Carbon nanofiber electrode for neurochemical monitoring.
abstract: Real-time monitoring of neurochemical dynamics in the central nervous system remains a significant challenge due to limitations in electrode sensitivity, selectivity, and biocompatibility. Traditional carbon fiber microelectrodes, while widely used for fast-scan cyclic voltammetry (FSCV), exhibit suboptimal signal-to-noise ratios and limited detection capabilities for low-concentration neurotransmitters. Here we report the development and characterization of novel carbon nanofiber (CNF) electrodes for enhanced neurochemical detection. CNF electrodes were fabricated by chemical vapor deposition of carbon onto tungsten substrates, resulting in highly ordered nanostructured surfaces with increased electroactive area. Electrochemical characterization using cyclic voltammetry revealed 3.2-fold greater sensitivity for dopamine detection compared to conventional carbon fiber electrodes, with detection limits of 15 nM. In vitro validation demonstrated selective detection of dopamine in the presence of common interferents including ascorbic acid and 3,4-dihydroxyphenylacetic acid. CNF electrodes exhibited stable performance over 200 consecutive voltammetric scans with less than 5% signal degradation. In vivo microdialysis experiments in anesthetized rats showed successful real-time monitoring of stimulated dopamine release in the nucleus accumbens following electrical stimulation of the medial forebrain bundle. The enhanced sensitivity enabled detection of pharmacologically-induced changes in extracellular dopamine concentrations as low as 25 nM following quinpirole administration. These findings demonstrate that CNF electrodes represent a significant advancement in neurochemical sensing technology, offering improved sensitivity and temporal resolution for investigating neurotransmitter dynamics in both physiological and pathological conditions.

title: Acute and long-term treatment of mania.
abstract: The treatment of mania starts with a correct diagnosis and elementary measures to prevent risks for the patient, relatives, and others. Sometimes, compulsory admission and treatment may be required for a few days. Patients with psychotic or mixed mania may be more difficult to treat. At the present time, there is solid evidence supporting the use of lithium, the anticonvulsants valproate and carbamazepine, and the antipsychotics chlorpromazine, haloperidol, risperidone, olanzapine, quetiapine, ziprasidone, aripiprazole, and asenapine in acute mania, and some evidence supporting the use of clozapine or electroconvulsive therapy in treatment-refractory cases. However, in clinical practice, combination therapy is the rule rather than the exception. The treatment of acute mania deserves a long-term view, and the evidence base for some treatments may be stronger than for others. When taking decisions about treatment, tolerability should also be a major concern, as differences in safety and tolerability may exceed differences in efficacy for most compounds. Psychoeducation of patients and caregivers is a powerful tool that should be used in combination with medication for optimal long-term outcome. Functional recovery should be the ultimate goal. El tratamiento de la mania comienza con un diagnôstico correcte y medidas elementales para prévenir riesgos para el paciente, familiares, y terceras personas. En ocasiones, el ingreso y tratamiento involuntarios son necesarios durante algunos dfas. Los pacientes con mania psicôtica o mixta pueden ser mâs dif Idles de tratar, Actualmente existen pruebas solidas de la eficacia del litio, los antiepilépticos valproate y carbamazepina, y los aniipsicoticos clorpromacina, haloperidol, risperidona, olanzapina, quetiapina, ziprasidona, aripiprazol, y asenapina en la mania aguda, y tambiên indicios de la eficacia de la clozapina y de la terapia eleciroconvulsiva en casos resistentes al tratamiento. Sin embargo, en la prâctica clinica, el tratamiento combinado es la norma mâs que la excepciôn, El tratamiento de la mania requière una vision a largo plazo, y los fundamentos cientfficos podrfan ser mas sôlidos para unos compuestos que otros. À la hora de tomar decisiones respecte al tratamiento, la tolerabilidad deberia ser una cuestién fundamental, ya que las diferencias en seguridad y tolerabilidad pueden ser mayores que las de eficacia entre fârmacos, La psicoeducaciôn de pacientes y familiares es una herramienta poderosa que deberfa combinarse con el tratamiento farmacolôgico para un mejor pronostico a largo plazo, La recuperaciôn funcional deberia ser la meta final. Le traitement de l'état maniaque débute par un diagnostic adéquat et des mesures élémentaires pour éviter de mettre en péril le patient, ses proches et autrui. Il faut parfois imposer une hospitalisation et un traitement pendant quelques jours, car les patients psychotiques ou maniaques mixtes peuvent être plus difficiles à traiter. À l'heure actuelle, des arguments sérieux sont en faveur de l'utilisation du lithium, des anticonvulsivants comme le valproate et la carbamazepine, et des antipsychotiques comme la chlorpromazine, l'halopéridol, la risperidone, l'olanzapine, la quetiapine, la ziprasidone, l'aripiprazole et l'asénapine au cours de l'état maniaque aigu. D'autres observations mettent en avant l'utilisation de la clozapine ou des électrochocs dans les cas réfractâtes au traitement. Cependant en pratique clinique, l'association de plusieurs traitements est la règle plutôt que l'exception. Le traitement de l'état maniaque mérite une vision à long terme, et les fondements de certains traitements peuvent être plus forts que d'autres. Lorsque la décision du traitement est prise, il faut aussi prendre en compte la tolérance, car des différences d'innocuité et de tolérance peuvent supplanter des différences d'efficacité pour la plupart des produits. L'éducation psychologique des patients et des soignants est un outil puissant qui devrait être utilisé en association avec le traitement pour des résultats optimaux à long terme. La guérison fonctionnelle du patient devrait être l'objectif final.

title: A novel technique for simultaneous bilateral brain infusions in a mouse model of neurodegenerative disease.
abstract: A common problem faced by researchers using transgenic models to study disease is the phenotypic variability that exists within a group or colony of animals. Significant pathological analyses thus often require large numbers of mice to perform. Many lines of transgenic mice harboring the gene for human amyloid precursor protein (APP) with different mutations causing familial Alzheimer's disease have been developed over the past decade to study plaque deposition and other aspects of AD. However, variations in size, density, plaque number, and total amyloid load between animals of the same age and genotype have been identified by our lab and others. Therefore, to study the effects of compounds on amyloid pathology, it was imperative to develop a technique that would allow each brain hemisphere to receive different infusions. We have developed catheters that facilitate simultaneous bilateral infusion in mouse brains, thereby using the contralateral hemisphere of the same animal as an internal control while studying, for example, the effect of compounds on amyloid plaques, a pathological hallmark of the progression of Alzheimer's disease (AD). Several molecules have been identified within the plaques including the major component, the Abeta peptide, and two inflammation-related proteins, apolipoprotein E (apoE) and the serine protease inhibitor alpha-1-antichymotrypsin (ACT). In these experiments, ACT was infused unilaterally over a period of 28 days into the parenchyma and lateral ventricles of PS/APP mice and observed to associate with amyloid plaques, with minimal mortality. Utilizing the ACT/Abeta interaction, details of this procedure are discussed here in detail.

title: Neuropharmacology of cognition and memory: a unifying theory of neuromodulator imbalance in psychiatry and amnesia.
abstract: The neurobiological mechanisms underlying cognitive dysfunction in psychiatric disorders and amnesia remain poorly understood, despite growing evidence that multiple neurotransmitter systems are involved. We propose a unifying theoretical framework suggesting that cognitive impairments across diverse conditions result from imbalances in key neuromodulatory systems rather than discrete neurotransmitter deficits. This hypothesis integrates emerging data on acetylcholine, dopamine, norepinephrine, and serotonin interactions in hippocampal and prefrontal circuits critical for memory formation and executive function. We systematically reviewed neurochemical, pharmacological, and neuroimaging studies published between 1990-2005 examining cognitive dysfunction in major depression, schizophrenia, bipolar disorder, and various amnesias. Converging evidence suggests that optimal cognitive performance requires precise ratios between cholinergic enhancement and dopaminergic modulation in the hippocampus, while prefrontal executive functions depend on balanced noradrenergic and serotonergic tone. Disruption of these neuromodulatory balances, rather than absolute neurotransmitter levels, appears to underlie memory consolidation deficits and attentional impairments observed across psychiatric conditions. In amnesia syndromes, similar imbalances occur secondary to structural damage affecting neuromodulatory projections. This framework predicts that therapeutic interventions targeting neuromodulatory ratios rather than individual neurotransmitter systems may prove more effective for cognitive enhancement. The model suggests novel combination therapies and provides testable hypotheses for future pharmacological studies aimed at restoring optimal neuromodulatory balance in cognitive disorders.

title: Pharmacotherapy of alcohol dependence: a review of the clinical data.
abstract: Alcohol dependence represents a major public health concern affecting millions worldwide, yet therapeutic options remain limited and show variable efficacy. This review examines the current state of pharmacological interventions for alcohol dependence based on available clinical trial data through 2003. We conducted a comprehensive literature search of randomized controlled trials evaluating FDA-approved medications and emerging therapeutic agents for alcohol dependence treatment. Three medications currently hold FDA approval: disulfiram, naltrexone, and acamprosate. Disulfiram demonstrates efficacy primarily through supervised administration, with studies showing 60-80% abstinence rates under monitored conditions versus 20-30% with unsupervised use. Naltrexone, an opioid receptor antagonist, consistently reduces heavy drinking days by 15-25% and craving scores by 20-40% across multiple placebo-controlled trials involving over 2,000 patients. Acamprosate shows promise in European studies, with abstinence rates of 35-45% versus 15-25% for placebo at 6-12 months, though US trial results have been mixed. Emerging agents including topiramate, ondansetron, and baclofen show preliminary efficacy in small-scale studies. Combination therapy approaches demonstrate enhanced outcomes compared to monotherapy in several trials. Patient factors including genetic polymorphisms, particularly in ALDH2 and OPRM1 genes, appear to influence treatment response. Current pharmacotherapies represent important advances but show modest effect sizes, highlighting the need for personalized treatment approaches and continued development of novel therapeutic targets including glutamate and GABA systems.

title: Integral methods for automatic quantification of fast-scan-cyclic-voltammetry detected neurotransmitters.
abstract: Modern techniques for estimating basal levels of electroactive neurotransmitters rely on the measurement of oxidative charges. This requires time integration of oxidation currents at certain intervals. Unfortunately, the selection of integration intervals relies on ad-hoc visual identification of peaks on the oxidation currents, which introduces sources of error and precludes the development of automated procedures necessary for analysis and quantification of neurotransmitter levels in large data sets. In an effort to improve charge quantification techniques, here we present novel methods for automatic selection of integration boundaries. Our results show that these methods allow quantification of oxidation reactions both in vitro and in vivo and of multiple analytes in vitro.

title: Neuroanatomy and neurochemistry of rat cornea: Changes with age.
abstract: To characterize the entire rat corneal nerve architecture, the changes that occur with aging, and its sensory, sympathetic, and parasympathetic fiber distribution. Sprague-Dawley rats (aged 1 day to 2 years old) of both sexes were euthanized, and the whole corneas were immunostained with protein gene product 9.5 (PGP9.5). The specimens were double-labeled with antibodies against calcitonin gene-related peptide (CGRP) and substance P (SP) as sensory nerve markers, vasoactive intestinal peptide (VIP) as a parasympathetic nerve marker, and neuropeptide Y (NPY) and tyrosine hydroxylase (TH) as markers of sympathetic fibers. Relative nerve density positive for each antibody was assessed by computer-assisted image analysis. Thick nerve trunks enter the cornea in the middle of the stroma and run towards the anterior stroma, subsequently dividing into smaller branches that penetrate upwards into the epithelium to form the subbasal nerve bundles.
