There was no significant difference in corneal innervation between sexes. CGRP and SP were the major sensory neuropeptides with 47.6% ± 3.5% and 34.9% ± 5.1%, respectively, of the total nerves. VIP was 18.4% ± 5.7%, and NPY and TH positive fibers took up 6.92% ± 2.66% and 2.92% ± 1.52%, respectively. Epithelial nerve density increased with age, reached full development at 5 weeks, and decreased at 120 weeks. This study provides a complete nerve architecture and content of components of sensory, parasympathetic, and sympathetic nerves in the rat cornea. The normal innervation pattern described here will provide an essential baseline for investigators who use the rat model for assessing corneal pathologies that involve nerve alterations.

title: [Use of the antisense oligonucleotide technique in neuropsychopharmacology research].
abstract: The antisense oligonucleotide technique represents a powerful molecular approach for investigating gene function in neuropsychopharmacological research by selectively inhibiting target mRNA translation. This study evaluated the efficacy and specificity of phosphorothioate-modified antisense oligodeoxynucleotides targeting dopamine D2 receptor mRNA in rat striatal neurons. Male Wistar rats (n=32) received bilateral intrastriatal injections of either D2 receptor antisense oligonucleotides (20 μg/2 μL), corresponding sense oligonucleotides, or vehicle control. Animals were assessed for locomotor activity, rotational behavior following apomorphine challenge (0.5 mg/kg s.c.), and D2 receptor binding density using [³H]spiperone autoradiography at 24, 48, and 72 hours post-injection. Northern blot analysis confirmed selective reduction of D2 receptor mRNA levels. Antisense treatment produced significant decreases in spontaneous locomotor activity (62% reduction at 48h, p<0.01) and attenuated apomorphine-induced rotation compared to controls. [³H]spiperone binding was reduced by 45-55% in the injected striatum at 48-72 hours, with maximal effect observed at 48 hours. Sense oligonucleotides and vehicle produced no significant behavioral or biochemical changes. No effects were observed on D1 receptor binding or tyrosine hydroxylase immunoreactivity, confirming target specificity. These findings demonstrate that antisense oligonucleotides provide a valuable tool for temporally controlled, reversible knockdown of specific neurotransmitter receptors, offering distinct advantages over traditional pharmacological approaches for dissecting molecular mechanisms underlying neuropsychiatric disorders and drug action in the central nervous system.
journal: Nihon shinkei seishin yakurigaku zasshi = Japanese journal of psychopharmacology

title: The Neuropharmacology of Cluster Headache and other Trigeminal Autonomic Cephalalgias.
abstract: Trigeminal autonomic cephalalgias (TACs), including cluster headache, paroxysmal hemicrania, and short unilateral neuralgiform headache attacks, represent a distinct group of primary headache disorders characterized by severe unilateral pain and ipsilateral autonomic symptoms. Despite significant advances in understanding their clinical features, the underlying neuropharmacological mechanisms remain incompletely elucidated. This review synthesizes current knowledge of the pathophysiology and therapeutic targets in TACs, with particular emphasis on cluster headache as the prototype disorder. The hypothalamic-trigeminal-autonomic network appears central to TAC pathogenesis, involving activation of trigeminal nociceptors, parasympathetic outflow via the sphenopalatine ganglion, and hypothalamic dysfunction. Key neurotransmitter systems implicated include serotonergic, GABAergic, and neuropeptidergic pathways, with calcitonin gene-related peptide (CGRP) emerging as a critical mediator. Neuroimaging studies have revealed hypothalamic activation during attacks and structural alterations in pain-processing regions. Current therapeutic approaches target multiple neuropharmacological pathways: acute treatments include high-flow oxygen and subcutaneous sumatriptan, while preventive therapies encompass verapamil, lithium, and topiramate. Novel therapeutic targets under investigation include CGRP receptor antagonists, neurostimulation techniques, and hypothalamic deep brain stimulation for refractory cases. Recent clinical trials with monoclonal antibodies targeting CGRP pathways show promising results. Understanding the complex neuropharmacology of TACs has led to more targeted therapeutic strategies, though significant gaps remain in our knowledge of disease mechanisms. Future research directions should focus on identifying biomarkers, developing personalized treatment approaches, and investigating novel therapeutic targets to improve outcomes for patients with these debilitating conditions.

title: Microdialysis in freely moving mice: determination of acetylcholine, serotonin and noradrenaline release in galanin transgenic mice.
abstract: The neuropeptide galanin has been implicated in the modulation of cholinergic, serotonergic, and noradrenergic neurotransmission, yet its precise effects on neurotransmitter release in vivo remain poorly characterized. We employed intracerebral microdialysis in freely moving mice to investigate the effects of galanin overexpression on basal and stimulated release of acetylcholine (ACh), serotonin (5-HT), and noradrenaline (NA) in the hippocampus and frontal cortex. Male transgenic mice overexpressing galanin under the platelet-derived growth factor-β promoter and wild-type littermates were implanted with CMA/7 microdialysis probes. Following a 24-hour recovery period, dialysate samples were collected at 20-minute intervals and analyzed using high-performance liquid chromatography with electrochemical detection for monoamines and radioenzymatic assay for ACh. Potassium-evoked neurotransmitter release was assessed by perfusion with 100 mM KCl for 20 minutes. Galanin transgenic mice exhibited significantly reduced basal ACh release in both hippocampus (-32%, p<0.01) and frontal cortex (-28%, p<0.05) compared to controls. Basal 5-HT release was decreased by 24% in the hippocampus (p<0.05) but unchanged in frontal cortex. NA release remained unaffected in both regions. Potassium-stimulated ACh release was markedly attenuated in transgenic mice (hippocampus: -45%, frontal cortex: -38%, both p<0.01), while 5-HT and NA responses were preserved. These findings demonstrate that chronic galanin overexpression selectively impairs cholinergic neurotransmission in vivo, providing insights into galanin's role in cognitive dysfunction and supporting its potential as a therapeutic target for cholinergic disorders.

title: Achieving synaptically relevant pulses of neurotransmitter using PDMS microfluidics.
abstract: Precise spatiotemporal control of neurotransmitter delivery remains a significant challenge in neuropharmacological research, limiting our ability to study synaptic transmission mechanisms. Traditional drug application methods, including bath perfusion and iontophoresis, lack the temporal resolution necessary to mimic physiological neurotransmitter release kinetics. Here, we describe a novel microfluidic approach using polydimethylsiloxane (PDMS) devices to generate synaptically relevant pulses of neurotransmitters with millisecond temporal precision and micrometer spatial resolution. We fabricated multi-channel PDMS microfluidic chips with integrated pneumatic valves capable of switching between control and neurotransmitter-containing solutions within 2-5 milliseconds. The system was validated using whole-cell patch-clamp recordings from cultured hippocampal neurons and acute brain slices. Glutamate pulses (1 mM, 10-100 ms duration) applied to individual dendritic spines generated AMPA receptor-mediated currents with rise times of 1.8 ± 0.3 ms and decay kinetics comparable to miniature excitatory postsynaptic currents. Similarly, GABA applications (10 mM, 5-50 ms) produced physiologically relevant inhibitory responses. The spatial confinement of neurotransmitter application was confirmed using fluorescent dextran tracers, demonstrating effective targeting within 5 μm of the delivery site. This microfluidic platform enables reproducible delivery of brief neurotransmitter pulses that closely approximate synaptic release, providing a valuable tool for investigating receptor kinetics, synaptic integration, and pharmacological mechanisms under physiologically relevant conditions. The technique offers significant advantages over existing methods for studying fast synaptic transmission and drug action at individual synapses.

title: Targeted disruption of cocaine-activated nucleus accumbens neurons prevents context-specific sensitization.
abstract: The nucleus accumbens (NAc) plays a critical role in drug addiction, yet the specific neuronal populations mediating different aspects of cocaine-induced plasticity remain poorly defined. Context-specific sensitization, in which repeated cocaine exposure in a particular environment leads to enhanced locomotor responses upon re-exposure to that context, represents a key model of addiction-related learning. Here we used activity-dependent genetic targeting to selectively ablate cocaine-activated NAc neurons and examine their contribution to sensitization. We employed transgenic mice expressing diphtheria toxin receptor under control of the immediate early gene c-fos promoter, allowing targeted elimination of neurons activated during initial cocaine exposure. Mice received cocaine (15 mg/kg, i.p.) or saline in distinct environmental contexts for five consecutive days, followed by diphtheria toxin administration to ablate c-fos-expressing NAc neurons. After a 10-day withdrawal period, animals were challenged with cocaine in both the drug-paired and unpaired contexts. Control mice exhibited robust context-specific sensitization, displaying significantly enhanced locomotor activity when cocaine was administered in the previously drug-paired environment compared to the unpaired context (2.8-fold vs 1.4-fold increase over saline baseline, p<0.001). In contrast, mice with targeted ablation of cocaine-activated NAc neurons showed equivalent, non-sensitized responses in both contexts, with locomotor activity comparable to drug-naive animals. Importantly, acute cocaine responses remained intact, indicating preserved basic drug sensitivity. These findings demonstrate that NAc neurons activated during initial cocaine exposure form a stable cellular engram essential for context-dependent sensitization, providing direct evidence for ensemble-specific encoding of addiction-related memories.

title: Treatment of hyperkinetic movement disorders.
abstract: Hyperkinetic movement disorders, including dystonia, chorea, and tremor, represent a heterogeneous group of neurological conditions with limited therapeutic options and significant functional impairment. Current pharmacological interventions often provide inadequate symptom control with substantial adverse effects. We conducted a randomised, double-blind, placebo-controlled trial to evaluate the efficacy and safety of riluzole, a glutamate antagonist with neuroprotective properties, in patients with treatment-refractory hyperkinetic movement disorders. Between January 2006 and September 2008, we enrolled 184 patients aged 18-75 years from 12 European centres. Participants were randomly assigned to receive either riluzole 100 mg daily (n=92) or matched placebo (n=92) for 16 weeks, with a 4-week washout period. The primary endpoint was change in the Unified Huntington's Disease Rating Scale motor subscore from baseline to week 16. Secondary endpoints included quality of life measures, functional disability scores, and safety parameters. Riluzole treatment resulted in a significant reduction in motor symptoms compared with placebo (mean difference -8·2 points, 95% CI -12·4 to -4·0; p=0·0002). Functional disability improved substantially in the riluzole group (p=0·018), with corresponding improvements in quality of life indices. Treatment was generally well tolerated, with mild gastrointestinal disturbances being the most common adverse events. These findings suggest that riluzole represents a promising therapeutic approach for hyperkinetic movement disorders, offering clinically meaningful symptom improvement with an acceptable safety profile. Further studies are warranted to establish optimal dosing regimens and long-term efficacy.

title: A new cross-correlation algorithm for the analysis of "in vitro" neuronal network activity aimed at pharmacological studies.
abstract: In vitro neuronal networks cultured on microelectrode arrays (MEAs) have emerged as powerful tools for investigating network dynamics and drug effects on neural activity. However, existing cross-correlation analysis methods often fail to capture the complex spatiotemporal patterns of synchronized activity characteristic of these networks, particularly when evaluating pharmacological interventions. Here we present a novel cross-correlation algorithm specifically designed to analyze spike train data from MEA recordings with enhanced sensitivity to network-wide synchronization events. Our algorithm incorporates adaptive time-windowing and weighted correlation coefficients that account for electrode-specific firing rates and inter-electrode distances. We validated this method using simulated spike trains with known correlation structures and compared its performance against conventional cross-correlation approaches using recordings from primary cortical cultures (DIV 14-21) treated with various concentrations of bicuculline (10-50 μM), a GABA_A receptor antagonist known to enhance network synchronization. The new algorithm demonstrated superior detection of correlation changes, showing 40% improved sensitivity compared to standard methods in identifying dose-dependent increases in network synchronization following bicuculline application. Additionally, the algorithm successfully distinguished between global network effects and localized clustering patterns that emerged during drug treatment. Computational efficiency was maintained with processing times comparable to existing methods. This cross-correlation algorithm provides researchers with an improved analytical tool for characterizing network-level effects of pharmacological compounds in MEA-based assays, potentially accelerating drug discovery efforts targeting neurological disorders involving altered network connectivity such as epilepsy and autism spectrum disorders.

title: Differential neuropsychopharmacological influences of naturally occurring tropane alkaloids anisodamine versus scopolamine.
abstract: Tropane alkaloids derived from Solanaceae plants have been extensively studied for their anticholinergic properties, yet differential neuropsychopharmacological effects among structurally similar compounds remain poorly characterized. This study compared the behavioral and neurochemical effects of anisodamine, a naturally occurring tropane alkaloid from Anisodus tanguticus, with scopolamine in adult male Sprague-Dawley rats. Animals received intraperitoneal injections of anisodamine (0.5, 1.0, 2.0 mg/kg), scopolamine (0.5, 1.0, 2.0 mg/kg), or saline vehicle. Behavioral assessments included locomotor activity monitoring, Morris water maze performance, and prepulse inhibition testing. Neurochemical analyses measured acetylcholine, dopamine, and serotonin levels in hippocampus, striatum, and prefrontal cortex using HPLC. Both compounds dose-dependently impaired spatial learning and memory consolidation, with scopolamine producing more severe deficits at equivalent doses. Anisodamine induced moderate hyperlocomotion at higher doses, whereas scopolamine caused initial hypoactivity followed by delayed hyperactivity. Prepulse inhibition was disrupted by both alkaloids, but anisodamine showed less pronounced effects. Neurochemically, scopolamine produced greater acetylcholine depletion in hippocampus and prefrontal cortex compared to anisodamine. Interestingly, anisodamine selectively increased striatal dopamine levels, an effect not observed with scopolamine. These findings suggest that despite structural similarity, anisodamine exhibits a distinct neuropsychopharmacological profile characterized by milder cognitive impairment and unique dopaminergic interactions, potentially offering therapeutic advantages over conventional anticholinergic agents in certain clinical applications.

title: Comparison of the effects of clozapine and 8-hydroxy-2-(di-n-propylamino)tetralin (8-OH-DPAT) on progressive ratio schedule performance: evidence against the involvement of 5-HT1A receptors in the behavioural effects of clozapine.
abstract:
**Background:** Clozapine, an atypical antipsychotic with unique clinical efficacy, exhibits complex pharmacological properties including interactions with multiple neurotransmitter systems. While its therapeutic effects are primarily attributed to dopaminergic and serotonergic mechanisms, the specific contribution of 5-HT1A receptor activity to clozapine's behavioral profile remains unclear. Progressive ratio (PR) schedules, which measure motivation and reward sensitivity, provide a valuable tool for examining the behavioral effects of psychoactive compounds.

**Methods:** Male Sprague-Dawley rats were trained on a PR schedule for food reinforcement until stable baseline performance was achieved. Separate groups received acute injections of clozapine (1.25, 2.5, 5.0 mg/kg, i.p.), the selective 5-HT1A receptor agonist 8-OH-DPAT (0.025, 0.05, 0.1, 0.2 mg/kg, s.c.), or vehicle. Breakpoint (highest ratio completed) and response rate were measured as primary outcome variables.

**Results:** Clozapine dose-dependently decreased breakpoint and response rate, with significant effects observed at 2.5 and 5.0 mg/kg. In contrast, 8-OH-DPAT produced a biphasic response pattern: low doses (0.025-0.05 mg/kg) slightly increased breakpoint, while higher doses (0.1-0.2 mg/kg) markedly decreased performance. The temporal pattern and magnitude of behavioral disruption differed substantially between compounds. Pretreatment with the 5-HT1A antagonist WAY-100635 blocked 8-OH-DPAT effects but did not alter clozapine's actions.

**Conclusions:** These findings demonstrate distinct behavioral profiles for clozapine and 8-OH-DPAT on PR performance, suggesting that 5-HT1A receptor activation does not significantly contribute to clozapine's motivational effects. The results challenge previous hypotheses regarding 5-HT1A involvement in atypical antipsychotic action and support alternative mechanisms underlying clozapine's unique pharmacological properties.

title: Application of MRS to mouse models of neurodegenerative illness.
abstract: The rapid development of transgenic mouse models of neurodegenerative diseases, in parallel with the rapidly expanding growth of MR techniques for assessing in vivo, non-invasive, neurochemistry, offers the potential to develop novel markers of disease progression and therapy. In this review we discuss the interpretation and utility of MRS for the study of these transgenic mouse and rodent models of neurodegenerative diseases such as Alzheimer's (AD), Huntington's (HD) and Parkinson's disease (PD). MRS studies can provide a wealth of information on various facets of in vivo neurochemistry, including neuronal health, gliosis, osmoregulation, energy metabolism, neuronal-glial cycling, and molecular synthesis rates. These data provide information on the etiology, natural history and therapy of these diseases. Mouse models enable longitudinal studies with useful time frames for evaluation of neuroprotection and therapeutic interventions using many of the potential MRS markers. In addition, the ability to manipulate the genome in these models allows better mechanistic understanding of the roles of the observable neurochemicals, such as N-acetylaspartate, in the brain. The argument is made that use of MRS, combined with correlative histology and other MRI techniques, will enable objective markers with which potential therapies can be followed in a quantitative fashion.

title: [Mechanism of drug dependency: answers to the questions of KATO].
abstract: The complicated mechanisms of drug dependency were discussed from the perspective of a reward, especially the perspective of psychic dependency of the brain. In Kato's article, the following questions were raised. (1) Improper usage of terminology in describing the objective pharmacological behavior and describing the subjective psychic experience, e.g. the term "obsessional comfort". (2) The enhanced effects of morphine and d-amphetamine in ICSS (intracranial self-stimulation) were not suitably regarded, as these might be the results of stimulus current running in the brain. The conclusion that "morphine and d-amphetamine are 2 kinds of addictive drugs due to the reinforcement effects on ICSS" appears to be putting the incidental before the fundamental because these are addictive drugs in clinical use. (3) It was unknown why the basic problem of drug dependency was limited to psychic stimulant (antihypnotic, etc.), and why only opiates were represented as an addictive drug. (4) The conclusion that the reinforcement effects of barbital and benzodiazepine were not observed in ICSS contradicted Kato's conclusion, in which the reinforcement effects were detected in an experiment involving self-administration. (5) He reiterated that the usage of terminology was confused in describing the psychic experience and pharmacological behavioral. In response to the comments of the editors, I would answer the questions as follows: It is well-known that the terminologies used in psychiatry are strict and prudent. This should also be the case in the description of pharmacological behaviors in animal models. The author admitted Kato's profound relevant experiences in drug dependency, and did not disapprove of his criticisms about the terminology used in the article to describe objective behaviors and observe subjective experience. That article was excerpted from one of my lectures, which to some extent allowed freedom in phrases and sentences. The following are my answers to Kato's questions.
journal: Seishin shinkeigaku zasshi = Psychiatria et neurologia Japonica

title: Obsessive-compulsive disorder and tic syndromes.
abstract: Obsessive-compulsive disorder (OCD) and tic syndromes, including Tourette's disorder, represent neuropsychiatric conditions with overlapping clinical features and proposed shared neurobiological substrates involving cortico-striato-thalamo-cortical circuits. While selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors (SSRIs) remain first-line treatment for OCD, their efficacy in tic disorders is limited, suggesting distinct pathophysiological mechanisms. We conducted a double-blind, placebo-controlled trial examining the differential effects of sertraline versus haloperidol in 84 patients with comorbid OCD and chronic tic disorder. Participants were randomized to receive sertraline (50-200 mg daily), haloperidol (1-6 mg daily), combination therapy, or placebo for 12 weeks. Primary outcome measures included the Yale-Brown Obsessive Compulsive Scale (Y-BOCS) and Yale Global Tic Severity Scale (YGTSS). Secondary measures assessed neuropsychological function and adverse effects. Sertraline significantly reduced obsessive-compulsive symptoms (mean Y-BOCS reduction: 8.4 points, p<0.001) but showed minimal impact on tic severity. Haloperidol effectively suppressed tics (mean YGTSS reduction: 12.7 points, p<0.001) with modest effects on compulsions. Combination therapy provided superior improvement in both symptom domains compared to monotherapy. Neuropsychological testing revealed preserved cognitive function with sertraline but attention deficits with haloperidol. These findings support the hypothesis that OCD and tic syndromes, while phenomenologically related, involve distinct neurotransmitter systems requiring targeted pharmacological approaches. Combination therapy may optimize treatment outcomes in patients with comorbid presentations while minimizing cognitive side effects associated with dopamine antagonists.
journal: The Medical clinics of North America

title: The neuropharmacological basis for the use of memantine in the treatment of Alzheimer's disease.
abstract: Memantine has been demonstrated to be safe and effective in the symptomatic treatment of Alzheimer's disease (AD). While the neurobiological basis for the therapeutic activity of memantine is not fully understood, the drug is not a cholinesterase inhibitor and, therefore, acts differently from current AD therapies. Memantine can interact with a variety of ligand-gated ion channels. However, NMDA receptors appear to be a key target of memantine at therapeutic concentrations. Memantine is an uncompetitive (channel blocking) NMDA receptor antagonist. Like other NMDA receptor antagonists, memantine at high concentrations can inhibit mechanisms of synaptic plasticity that are believed to underlie learning and memory. However, at lower, clinically relevant concentrations memantine can under some circumstances promote synaptic plasticity and preserve or enhance memory in animal models of AD. In addition, memantine can protect against the excitotoxic destruction of cholinergic neurons. Blockade of NMDA receptors by memantine could theoretically confer disease-modifying activity in AD by inhibiting the "weak" NMDA receptor-dependent excitotoxicity that has been hypothesized to play a role in the progressive neuronal loss that underlies the evolving dementia. Moreover, recent in vitro studies suggest that memantine abrogates beta-amyloid (Abeta) toxicity and possibly inhibits Abeta production. Considerable attention has focused on the investigation of theories to explain the better tolerability of memantine over other NMDA receptor antagonists, particularly those that act by a similar channel blocking mechanism such as dissociative anesthetic-like agents (phencyclidine, ketamine, MK-801). A variety of channel-level factors could be relevant, including fast channel-blocking kinetics and strong voltage-dependence (allowing rapid relief of block during synaptic activity), as well as reduced trapping (permitting egress from closed channels). These factors may allow memantine to block channel activity induced by low, tonic levels of glutamate--an action that might contribute to symptomatic improvement and could theoretically protect against weak excitotoxicity--while sparing synaptic responses required for normal behavioral functioning, cognition and memory.

title: Human brain aminopeptidase A: biochemical properties and distribution in brain nuclei.
abstract: Aminopeptidase A (APA) generated brain angiotensin III, one of the main effector peptides of the brain renin angiotensin system, exerting a tonic stimulatory effect on the control of blood pressure in hypertensive rats. The distribution of APA in human brain has not been yet studied. We first biochemically characterized human brain APA (apparent molecular mass of 165 and 130 kDa) and we showed that the human enzyme exhibited similar enzymatic characteristics to recombinant mouse APA. Both enzymes had similar sensitivity to Ca(2+). Kinetic studies showed that the K(m) (190 mumol/L) of the human enzyme for the synthetic substrate-l-glutamyl-beta-naphthylamide was close from that of the mouse enzyme (256 mumol/L). Moreover, various classes of inhibitors including the specific and selective APA inhibitor, (S)-3-amino-4-mercapto-butyl sulfonic acid, had similar inhibitory potencies toward both enzymes. Using (S)-3-amino-4-mercapto-butyl sulfonic acid, we then specifically measured the activity of APA in 40 microdissected areas of the adult human brain. Significant heterogeneity was found in the activity of APA in the various analyzed regions. The highest activity was measured in the choroids plexus and the pineal gland. High activity was also detected in the dorsomedial medulla oblongata, in the septum, the prefrontal cortex, the olfactory bulb, the nucleus accumbens, and the hypothalamus, especially in the paraventricular and supraoptic nuclei. Immunostaining of human brain sections at the level of the medulla oblongata strengthened these data, showing for the first time a high density of immunoreactive neuronal cell bodies and fibers in the motor hypoglossal nucleus, the dorsal motor nucleus of the vagus, the nucleus of the solitary tract, the Roller nucleus, the ambiguus nucleus, the inferior olivary complex, and in the external cuneate nucleus. APA immunoreactivity was also visualized in vessels and capillaries in the dorsal motor nucleus of the vagus and the inferior olivary complex. The presence of APA in several human brain nuclei sensitive to angiotensins and involved in blood pressure regulation suggests that APA in humans is an integral component of the brain renin angiotensin system and strengthens the idea that APA inhibitors could be clinically tested as an additional therapy for the treatment of certain forms of hypertension.

title: Cholinergic effects on fear conditioning I: the degraded contingency effect is disrupted by atropine but reinstated by physostigmine.
abstract: Classical fear conditioning paradigms have demonstrated that degraded contingency between conditioned stimulus (CS) and unconditioned stimulus (US) presentations results in impaired associative learning, yet the neurochemical mechanisms underlying this phenomenon remain poorly understood. Given the established role of cholinergic neurotransmission in attention and associative learning, we investigated whether muscarinic cholinergic signaling modulates the degraded contingency effect in auditory fear conditioning. Male Sprague-Dawley rats (n=8-10 per group) received systemic injections of either atropine (50 mg/kg), physostigmine (0.25 mg/kg), or saline vehicle 30 minutes prior to fear conditioning sessions. Animals underwent either standard fear conditioning (CS-US contingency = 1.0) or degraded contingency training (CS-US contingency = 0.33) using tone-footshock pairings. Freezing behavior was assessed during CS presentations in both acquisition and extinction test sessions 24 hours later. Under vehicle conditions, degraded contingency training produced significantly reduced freezing responses compared to standard conditioning (p<0.001). Atropine administration eliminated this degraded contingency effect, with both training groups showing equivalently low freezing levels. Conversely, physostigmine treatment restored the degraded contingency effect, reinstating the behavioral distinction between standard and degraded training conditions. These findings suggest that muscarinic cholinergic activity is necessary for the normal processing of CS-US contingency information during fear learning. Cholinergic enhancement appears to sharpen contingency detection mechanisms, while cholinergic blockade disrupts the ability to discriminate between high and low contingency learning situations. These results have important implications for understanding attention-based learning disorders and cholinergic therapeutic interventions.

title: [Neurophysiological, neurochemical, autonomous and chronobiological basics of sleep medicine].
abstract: The review discusses various mechanisms of the rapidly growing problem of Sleep Medicine. The "Sleep-wakefulness" cycle is a continuum of different functional states and the diseases that these states might prompt to manifest themselves in various ways. In these cases, we must say that change produces the conditions of disease manifestation rather than the disease itself. The paper describes the dynamics of the autonomous parameters during sleep, emphasizes the role and importance of chronobiological aspects of the "sleep-wakefulness" cycle. The holographic principle of the operation I sleep cycle is described which persists even in the cerebral stroke. From the standpoint of neurochemistry, modern hypnotics and drugs of the nearest future can be divided into 2 groups: proS (pro sleep)--for sleeping, and antiW (anti-wakefulness)--vs. wakefulness.
journal: Rossiiskii fiziologicheskii zhurnal imeni I.M. Sechenova

title: On the prescribing analyst.
abstract: The integration of psychopharmacological interventions within psychoanalytic practice has generated considerable debate regarding therapeutic boundaries and analytic neutrality. This study examined the clinical outcomes and transference dynamics when psychoanalysts directly prescribe psychotropic medications to their analysands, compared to traditional referral models. We conducted a retrospective analysis of 127 patients in psychoanalysis over a 5-year period (2008-2013), comparing three groups: patients whose analysts prescribed medications directly (n=41), patients referred to psychiatrists for medication management (n=43), and medication-naive controls (n=43). Treatment outcomes were assessed using standardized measures including the Hamilton Depression Rating Scale, Global Assessment of Functioning, and detailed process notes analyzing transference manifestations. Patients with prescribing analysts demonstrated significantly greater treatment retention (89% vs. 67%, p<0.01) and showed equivalent symptomatic improvement compared to the referral group. However, distinct transference patterns emerged, including heightened idealization (43% vs. 18%) and increased somatic preoccupations during sessions (62% vs. 31%). Process analysis revealed that the dual role created unique opportunities for exploring the patient's relationship to dependency and authority, though it also generated more complex countertransference reactions in analysts. These findings suggest that the prescribing analyst model may enhance therapeutic alliance and retention while introducing novel transference configurations that require careful analytic attention. The integration of prescribing responsibilities appears clinically viable when accompanied by rigorous self-analysis and appropriate training in psychopharmacology.

title: S32504, a novel naphtoxazine agonist at dopamine D3/D2 receptors: I. Cellular, electrophysiological, and neurochemical profile in comparison with ropinirole.
abstract: Dopamine D3 receptors represent promising therapeutic targets for neurological disorders, yet selective pharmacological tools remain limited. This study characterized S32504, a novel naphtoxazine derivative, as a potential D3/D2 receptor agonist and compared its profile to the established antiparkinsonian agent ropinirole. Binding affinity was determined using radioligand displacement assays in CHO cells expressing human D2 and D3 receptors. Functional activity was assessed via cAMP inhibition assays and patch-clamp electrophysiology in primary rat striatal neurons. In vivo neurochemical effects were evaluated using microdialysis in freely moving rats. S32504 displayed high affinity for D3 receptors (Ki = 0.8 nM) with 15-fold selectivity over D2 receptors, compared to ropinirole which showed 3-fold D3 selectivity. In cAMP assays, S32504 acted as a full agonist at D3 receptors (EC50 = 2.1 nM) and partial agonist at D2 receptors. Electrophysiological recordings revealed that S32504 (0.1-10 μM) dose-dependently reduced neuronal firing rates and inhibited spontaneous excitatory postsynaptic currents, effects blocked by the D2/D3 antagonist sulpiride. Microdialysis studies demonstrated that S32504 (0.1-1.0 mg/kg s.c.) decreased extracellular dopamine levels in the nucleus accumbens more potently than ropinirole, consistent with preferential D3 autoreceptor activation. These findings establish S32504 as a novel D3-preferring agonist with distinct pharmacological properties compared to ropinirole, suggesting potential therapeutic applications in conditions associated with dopaminergic dysfunction where enhanced D3 receptor selectivity may provide clinical advantages.
journal: The Journal of pharmacology and experimental therapeutics

title: Anxiolytics antagonize yohimbine-induced central noradrenergic activity: a concomitant in vivo voltammetry-electrophysiology model of anxiety.
abstract: Anxiety disorders involve complex interactions between noradrenergic and GABAergic neurotransmitter systems, yet the real-time neurochemical mechanisms underlying anxiolytic drug action remain poorly characterized. We developed a novel dual-modality approach combining in vivo voltammetry with simultaneous electrophysiology to examine how clinically relevant anxiolytics modulate yohimbine-induced noradrenergic activation in the locus coeruleus (LC) and its projection targets. Male Sprague-Dawley rats (n=48) were implanted with carbon fiber electrodes for voltammetric detection of norepinephrine in the LC, hippocampus, and prefrontal cortex, alongside tungsten microelectrodes for single-unit recordings of LC neuronal activity. Yohimbine (2 mg/kg, i.p.) significantly increased extracellular norepinephrine levels by 340±45% in the LC, 280±38% in hippocampus, and 195±32% in prefrontal cortex, concurrent with increased LC firing rates (baseline: 1.2±0.3 Hz; post-yohimbine: 4.8±0.7 Hz). These neurochemical and electrophysiological changes correlated with elevated anxiety-like behaviors in the elevated plus maze. Pretreatment with diazepam (1 mg/kg) or buspirone (5 mg/kg) significantly attenuated yohimbine-induced norepinephrine release by 65-75% across all brain regions and normalized LC firing patterns. The temporal correlation between neurochemical and electrophysiological measurements (r=0.87, p<0.001) validated our methodological approach. These findings demonstrate that anxiolytics act, in part, by restraining excessive noradrenergic activity and provide a robust experimental model for real-time assessment of anxiety-related neurochemical processes. This dual-modality technique offers enhanced temporal and spatial resolution for investigating anxiolytic mechanisms and may facilitate development of novel anti-anxiety therapeutics.

title: Improved high-performance liquid chromatographic method for GABA and glutamate determination in regions of the rodent brain.
abstract: A C18 reversed-phase column and isocratic fluorescence HPLC method for the simultaneous detection of glutamate and gamma-aminobutyric acid (GABA) is described. In this article a fast and more efficient method for the extraction of these neurotransmitters in rat brain tissue is also presented. The supernatant was derivatized with o-phthalaldehyde (OPA) and analyzed by HPLC with fluorescence detection. Intraday reproducibility was 97.0% and 96.7% and interday reproducibility was 97.1% and 93.7% for GABA and glutamate, respectively. Recovery assays indicate that the accuracy of the method for GABA is 99.6+/-2.3% and for glutamate is 101.9+/-1.8%. In addition, the time consumed to run a sample is lower than that described by other authors. Mean elution time was 3.10 min and 8.22 min for glutamate and GABA, respectively. Thus, in a total runtime of less than 9 min both neurotransmitters were detected. Moreover, when compared to the current methods, the extraction solution used here allowed a high drawing out of the neurotransmitters, glutamate and GABA, from the hippocampus, thalamus and prefrontal cortex of the rat brain.

title: The footfault test as a screening tool in the 6-hydroxydopamine rat model of Parkinson's disease.
abstract: The administration of 6-hydroxydopamine (6-OHDA) into the nigrostriatal pathway is a rat model of Parkinson's disease (PD). The footfault test is a behavioural task in which rodents have their motor functions assessed. Here, we observed that unilaterally 6-OHDA-lesioned animals show a context-induced ipsilateral rotational behaviour when placed on the footfault apparatus for 3 min and this may be used as index to detect lesioned animals. Our results showed a sensitivity and specificity of 100% for lesions higher than 94% and 64%, respectively (ROC curve: AUC=0.988). A binary logistic regression model showed an expB=1.116 (95% CI, 1.007-1.236) and C=-9.081+/-4.554 (p=0.046) using the nigral tyrosine hidroxylase immunocontent as standard (each unit represents a 10%-lesion extension). Additionally, the footfault test was more sensitive than apomorphine challenging at 1mg/kg when these tests were carried out days apart and it was less sensitive than methylphenidate at 40 mg/kg (sign test, p<0.05). Therefore, the footfault test may be very useful in the PD animal model for screening animals since it is fast and simple and it does not require a drug to induce rotational activity.

title: PET Neurochemical Imaging Modes.
abstract: Positron emission tomography (PET) has emerged as a powerful tool for investigating neurochemical processes in vivo, offering unprecedented insights into neurotransmitter systems and their alterations in neurological and psychiatric disorders. This comprehensive review examines current PET neurochemical imaging approaches and their clinical applications. We analyzed literature from 2010-2015 encompassing major neurotransmitter systems including dopaminergic, serotonergic, cholinergic, and GABAergic pathways, evaluating radiotracer development, imaging protocols, and quantitative methodologies. Key dopaminergic tracers such as [11C]raclopride and [18F]DOPA continue to provide robust measures of receptor availability and presynaptic function, while newer compounds like [11C]PE2I offer improved specificity for dopamine transporter imaging. Serotonergic imaging has advanced significantly with [11C]DASB for serotonin transporter quantification and [18F]altanserin for 5-HT2A receptor studies. Cholinergic system evaluation benefits from [18F]fluorobenzovesamicol for vesicular acetylcholine transporter imaging, particularly valuable in dementia research. GABAergic imaging remains challenging, though [11C]flumazenil provides reliable benzodiazepine receptor measurements. Quantitative analysis methods have evolved from simple standardized uptake values to sophisticated kinetic modeling approaches using arterial input functions and reference tissue methods. Recent developments in partial volume correction and motion compensation have enhanced accuracy. PET neurochemical imaging continues to expand our understanding of brain chemistry in health and disease, with emerging tracers for glutamatergic, histaminergic, and cannabinoid systems promising further advances in translational neuroscience research and drug development.

title: CSF biomarkers for Alzheimer's disease: use in early diagnosis and evaluation of drug treatment.
abstract: Early diagnosis of Alzheimer's disease is important in initiating symptomatic treatment with acetylcholine esterase inhibitors, and will be of even greater significance if drugs with a potential to slow down the degenerative process, such as beta-secretase inhibitors and beta-amyloid vaccination, prove to have a clinical effect. During the last decade, research efforts have focused on developing cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) biomarkers for Alzheimer's disease. In this review, the background and principles for, and the diagnostic performance of, the CSF biomarkers total tau, phosphorylated tau and the 42-amino acid form of beta-amyloid, are reviewed. New candidate CSF biomarkers and new strategies, including multiparameter immunoassays and CSF proteomics techniques, in the search of additional CSF biomarkers are also reviewed. Finally, the rationale for the use of CSF biomarkers to identify and monitor the biochemical effect of new drug candidates is reviewed.

title: Subcellular probes for neurochemical recording from multiple brain sites.
abstract: Spatially resolved neurochemical measurements are essential for understanding complex brain signaling networks, yet current approaches are limited by probe dimensions and multiplexing capabilities. Traditional microelectrodes lack the spatial resolution to distinguish subcellular compartments, while existing microsensor arrays cannot simultaneously monitor neurochemicals across distant brain regions. Here we report the development of ultrasmall carbon fiber microelectrodes (2-5 μm diameter) integrated with multiplexed recording capabilities for real-time neurochemical detection from multiple brain sites. We fabricated 16-channel arrays using photolithographic patterning of carbon fiber bundles coupled to miniaturized potentiostat circuits. The devices were characterized using cyclic voltammetry and demonstrated selective detection of dopamine, serotonin, and adenosine with sub-second temporal resolution. In vivo validation was performed in anesthetized rats (n=8) with simultaneous recordings from striatum, nucleus accumbens, and prefrontal cortex during electrical stimulation and behavioral paradigms. Our microsensor arrays detected stimulus-evoked dopamine release with 50 nM sensitivity and spatial resolution sufficient to distinguish synaptic versus extrasynaptic signaling within individual brain nuclei. Cross-correlation analysis revealed coordinated neurochemical dynamics between brain regions that were previously undetectable with single-site measurements. The multiplexed recordings demonstrated region-specific differences in neurotransmitter kinetics and revealed novel patterns of inter-regional neurochemical communication. This technology enables unprecedented spatial and temporal mapping of brain neurochemistry, providing new insights into distributed neural network function and offering significant advantages for neuropharmacological research and therapeutic monitoring applications.

title: Recent advances in the neurobiology and neuropharmacology of Alzheimer's disease.
abstract: Alzheimer's disease (AD) is an age-related neurodegenerative disorder characterized by progressive deterioration of cognitive functions. The pathological hallmarks are extracellular deposits of amyloid plaques and intracellular neurofibrillary tangles of tau protein. The cognitive deficits seen are thought to be due to synaptic dysfunction and neurochemical deficiencies. Various neurochemical abnormalities have been observed during progressive ageing, and are linked to cognitive abnormalities as seen with the sporadic form of AD. Acetylcholinesterase inhibitors are one of the major therapeutic strategies used for the treatment of AD. During the last decade, various new therapeutic strategies have shown beneficial effects in preclinical studies and under clinical development for the treatment of AD. The present review is aimed at discussing the neurobiology of AD and association of neurochemical abnormalities associated with cognitive deterioration and new therapeutic strategies for the treatment of AD.

title: Past and future of neuropsychopharmacology.
abstract: The field of neuropsychopharmacology has undergone remarkable transformation since the serendipitous discovery of chlorpromazine's antipsychotic properties in the 1950s. This review examines the historical development of psychotropic medications and projects future directions based on emerging neuroscientific understanding. We analyzed the evolution of major drug classes including antipsychotics, antidepressants, anxiolytics, and mood stabilizers through comprehensive literature review spanning 1950-1989, evaluating therapeutic efficacy, mechanism elucidation, and adverse effect profiles. Additionally, we assessed contemporary research trends in receptor pharmacology, neurotransmitter systems, and molecular biology techniques. Historical analysis reveals three distinct phases: the empirical discovery period (1950s-1960s), characterized by phenothiazines and tricyclic antidepressants; the mechanistic understanding era (1970s-1980s), marked by dopamine hypothesis development and selective serotonin reuptake inhibitor introduction; and the emerging molecular phase (late 1980s), featuring receptor subtype characterization. Current limitations include incomplete understanding of psychiatric disease etiology, significant adverse effects, and variable therapeutic response. Future developments likely include: selective receptor subtype modulators based on advancing molecular pharmacology; neuropeptide-targeted therapeutics; drugs addressing specific neurotransmitter pathway dysfunctions; and personalized medicine approaches utilizing pharmacogenetic principles. The integration of modern neuroscience methodologies with traditional pharmacological approaches promises more effective, better-tolerated treatments. Success will depend upon continued collaboration between clinical psychiatry, basic neuroscience, and pharmaceutical research, potentially revolutionizing psychiatric therapeutics within the next two decades.

title: A history of the Collegium Internationale Neuro-Psychopharmacologicum (1957-2004).
abstract: Recognition that one of the essential prerequisites of successful neuro-psychopharmacological research is a continuous dialogue between clinicians and basic scientists led to the founding of the Collegium Internationale Neuro-Psychopharmacologicum (CINP) in Zurich on September 2, 1957. At the time, CINP was the only association in neuro-psychopharmacology. It was hoped that by organizing biennial congresses, it would facilitate the interaction among the many disciplines involved in the new field. It was also envisaged that discussion of findings in translational research would provide guidance for developing more selective and thereby more effective psychotropic drugs. These expectations were not fulfilled. In the years that followed, major developments in neuropharmacology without parallel development in the methodology of clinical investigations, created a widening gap between neuro- and psychopharmacology. Clinical interpretations of neuropharmacological findings filled in the missing information from translational research. In the 1980s, to facilitate the dissemination of these interpretations, CINP established Travel Awards for Young Investigators to assist them in attending the biennial meetings. In the 1990s, the college extended its activities by organizing other meetings, including president's workshops, regional meetings, educational seminars and other programs. By the end of the 20th century CINP was a legal entity that was registered in Switzerland with domicile in Zurich. It had also become a financially secure organization with sufficient funds to support administration and coordination. In the early years of the 21st century CINP established a central office and a congress-organizing group. To meet the needs of its steadily growing membership from Asia, Australia and the Latin Americas, CINP has been regionalized. In order to regain its relevance to all its members it is hoped that CINP will return to its roots.

title: Emerging roles for novel antipsychotic medications in the treatment of schizophrenia.
abstract: Second-generation antipsychotic medications have emerged as promising alternatives to conventional neuroleptics in the treatment of schizophrenia, offering potential advantages in efficacy and tolerability. This review examines the clinical evidence supporting the use of clozapine, risperidone, and olanzapine in schizophrenic patients, with particular attention to their effects on positive symptoms, negative symptoms, and extrapyramidal side effects. A comprehensive analysis of randomized controlled trials published between 1990 and 1997 was conducted, encompassing over 3,000 patients across multiple treatment centers. Clinical efficacy was assessed using the Positive and Negative Syndrome Scale (PANSS) and the Brief Psychiatric Rating Scale (BPRS), while extrapyramidal symptoms were evaluated using standardized rating instruments. Results demonstrate that novel antipsychotics produce superior improvements in negative symptoms compared to haloperidol and other typical agents, with effect sizes ranging from 0.4 to 0.8. Clozapine showed particular efficacy in treatment-resistant populations, with 30-40% of previously refractory patients achieving clinically meaningful improvement. Risperidone and olanzapine demonstrated comparable positive symptom control to conventional agents while producing significantly fewer extrapyramidal side effects at therapeutic doses. However, novel agents were associated with distinct adverse effect profiles, including weight gain, sedation, and potential metabolic disturbances. These findings suggest that second-generation antipsychotics represent a significant advancement in schizophrenia treatment, offering improved therapeutic outcomes and enhanced patient tolerability, though careful monitoring remains essential for optimal clinical management.
journal: The Psychiatric clinics of North America

title: Taurine Protects from Pentylenetetrazole-Induced Behavioral and Neurochemical Changes in Zebrafish.
abstract: Epilepsy is a common neurological disorder characterized by recurrent unprovoked seizures, which culminate in various neurobehavioral and neurochemical changes. Taurine (TAU) is an amino sulfonic acid which acts an endogenous inhibitory neuromodulator. Moreover, TAU displays intrinsic antioxidant activity, contributing to its beneficial actions in the CNS. Here, we evaluated whether TAU pretreatment protects from pentylenetetrazole (PTZ)-induced behavioral alterations and oxidative stress-related parameters in zebrafish brain tissue. Fish were pretreated with 42, 150, and 400 mg/L TAU (40 min) and further exposed to 10 mM PTZ (20 min) to analyze the seizure-like behaviors. As a positive control, another group was previously treated with 75 μM diazepam (DZP). Afterwards, biochemical experiments were performed. All TAU concentrations tested decreased seizure intensity in the first 150 s. Importantly, 150 mg/L TAU attenuated seizure-like behavioral scores, decreased seizure intensity, reduced the frequency of clonic-like seizures (score 4), and increased the latency to score 4. TAU (150 mg/L) also prevented oxidative stress in PTZ-challenged fish by decreasing lipid peroxidation and protein carbonylation and preventing changes on nonprotein thiol levels. No significant changes were observed in MTT assay and LDH activity. Differently than observed in DZP group, TAU did not affect the overall swimming activity of fish, suggesting different mechanisms of action. Collectively, we show that TAU attenuates PTZ-induced seizure-like behaviors and brain oxidative stress in zebrafish, suggesting the involvement of antioxidant mechanisms in neuroprotection.

title: Neurobiology and neuroimmunology of Tourette's syndrome: an update.
abstract: Tourette's syndrome (TS) is a complex neuropsychiatric disorder characterized by chronic motor and vocal tics, with emerging evidence implicating both neurodevelopmental abnormalities and immune dysfunction in its pathogenesis. Recent neurobiological investigations have expanded our understanding of the cortico-striato-thalamo-cortical (CSTC) circuits underlying tic generation, while neuroimmunological studies suggest autoimmune mechanisms may contribute to symptom onset and exacerbation in susceptible individuals. We conducted a comprehensive review of recent neuroimaging, post-mortem, and immunological studies published between 1995-2003, analyzing data from over 2,400 TS patients and 1,800 controls across multiple research centers. Structural and functional neuroimaging studies consistently demonstrate altered dopaminergic neurotransmission within the caudate nucleus and putamen, with increased D2 receptor binding density correlating with tic severity (r=0.67, p<0.001). Post-mortem analyses reveal decreased interneuron populations in the striatum, particularly parvalbumin-positive GABAergic cells, suggesting disinhibition of motor programs. Immunological investigations demonstrate elevated anti-neuronal antibodies in 34% of TS patients versus 8% of controls (p<0.0001), with molecular mimicry between Group A streptococcal antigens and basal ganglia proteins. Cytokine profiling reveals increased IL-12 and TNF-α levels during symptom exacerbations. These findings support a multifactorial model wherein genetic susceptibility, environmental triggers, and immune dysregulation converge to disrupt CSTC circuit function. The identification of specific neuroimmune biomarkers may facilitate early diagnosis and guide development of targeted immunomodulatory therapies for TS management.
journal: Cellular and molecular life sciences : CMLS

title: The psychopharmacology of Huntington disease.
abstract: Huntington disease (HD) is a hereditary neurodegenerative disorder caused by an expanded cytosine-adenine-guanine triplet repeat in the huntingtin gene. The current diagnosis is based on the presence of typical motor signs in combination with a positive gene test. The motor onset of the disease is usually between 30 and 50 years of age, and the disease then progresses over around 20 more years. Nonmotor symptoms and signs such as cognitive decline, metabolic dysfunction, sleep disturbances, as well as psychiatric symptoms are common and can occur many years before motor onset. Psychiatric symptoms include irritability, apathy, depression, anxiety, and OCD. Although there exist no disease-modifying treatment, available pharmacologic drugs often offer significant symptom relief and improve quality of life. Today, there are only two drugs that are approved by the US Food and Drug Association for the treatment of HD. These are the dopamine-depleting drugs tetrabenazine and deutetrabenazine that both target motor symptoms. The current status of best clinical practice for HD is based on expert opinions as well as evidence and/or experience of treating similar symptoms in other conditions. In this chapter, we provide an overview of the complex clinical manifestations of HD and the commonly used psychopharmacologic treatments.

title: Addiction: decreased reward sensitivity and increased expectation sensitivity conspire to overwhelm the brain's control circuit.
abstract: Based on brain imaging findings, we present a model according to which addiction emerges as an imbalance in the information processing and integration among various brain circuits and functions. The dysfunctions reflect (a) decreased sensitivity of reward circuits, (b) enhanced sensitivity of memory circuits to conditioned expectations to drugs and drug cues, stress reactivity, and (c) negative mood, and a weakened control circuit. Although initial experimentation with a drug of abuse is largely a voluntary behavior, continued drug use can eventually impair neuronal circuits in the brain that are involved in free will, turning drug use into an automatic compulsive behavior. The ability of addictive drugs to co-opt neurotransmitter signals between neurons (including dopamine, glutamate, and GABA) modifies the function of different neuronal circuits, which begin to falter at different stages of an addiction trajectory. Upon exposure to the drug, drug cues or stress this results in unrestrained hyperactivation of the motivation/drive circuit that results in the compulsive drug intake that characterizes addiction.
journal: BioEssays : news and reviews in molecular, cellular and developmental biology

title: Neuropharmacological profile of an atypical antipsychotic, NRA0562.
abstract: The therapeutic limitations of conventional antipsychotics and the need for improved atypical agents with reduced side effect profiles have prompted continued investigation of novel dopaminergic compounds. NRA0562, a benzisoxazole derivative, was evaluated for its neuropharmacological properties as a potential atypical antipsychotic agent. Radioligand binding studies were performed using rat brain membranes to determine receptor affinity profiles. Behavioral assays included apomorphine-induced climbing, haloperidol-induced catalepsy, and prepulse inhibition tests in male Sprague-Dawley rats. Extracellular dopamine and serotonin levels were measured using in vivo microdialysis in the nucleus accumbens and medial prefrontal cortex. NRA0562 demonstrated high affinity for dopamine D2 (Ki = 2.1 nM) and serotonin 5-HT2A receptors (Ki = 0.8 nM), with a 5-HT2A/D2 ratio of 0.38. Moderate affinity was observed for α1-adrenergic (Ki = 15.3 nM) and histamine H1 receptors (Ki = 21.7 nM). The compound effectively blocked apomorphine-induced climbing (ED50 = 0.24 mg/kg, p.o.) while producing minimal catalepsy at antipsychotic doses. NRA0562 significantly improved prepulse inhibition deficits induced by apomorphine (p < 0.01). Microdialysis studies revealed preferential increases in cortical dopamine release compared to striatal regions, with concurrent elevation of prefrontal serotonin levels. These findings suggest that NRA0562 exhibits a favorable neuropharmacological profile characteristic of atypical antipsychotics, with potential advantages in treating both positive and negative symptoms of schizophrenia while minimizing extrapyramidal side effects. Further preclinical and clinical evaluation is warranted.

title: Gaddum and LSD: the birth and growth of experimental and clinical neuropharmacology research on 5-HT in the UK.
abstract: The discovery of lysergic acid diethylamide (LSD) and John Henry Gaddum's pioneering work in the 1950s fundamentally transformed our understanding of serotonin (5-hydroxytryptamine, 5-HT) neuropharmacology in the United Kingdom. This historical review examines the development of 5-HT research from Gaddum's initial observations of LSD's antagonistic effects on smooth muscle preparations through to the establishment of modern clinical neuropharmacology programmes. We analysed primary literature, laboratory records, and correspondence from key British institutions including the National Institute for Medical Research, Edinburgh University, and the Medical Research Council between 1953-1980. Gaddum's seminal 1953 paper demonstrating LSD's ability to block 5-HT responses in isolated tissue preparations provided the first evidence linking indoleamine neurotransmission to psychoactive drug effects. This work catalysed subsequent investigations by Brodie, Woolley, and Shaw, leading to the identification of 5-HT in mammalian brain tissue and the development of the first selective serotonergic ligands. The establishment of the MRC Neuropsychopharmacology Unit in 1961 under Gaddum's influence created a framework for systematic clinical studies of 5-HT function in psychiatric disorders. By 1975, British researchers had characterised multiple 5-HT receptor subtypes, developed novel antidepressant compounds, and established serotonin's role in mood regulation. Gaddum's initial LSD studies represent a pivotal moment in neuropharmacology, demonstrating how serendipitous observations of psychedelic compounds can illuminate fundamental neurotransmitter mechanisms and drive therapeutic innovation in psychiatry and neurology.

title: Neuropharmacological profile of an atypical antipsychotic, NRA0562.
abstract: Schizophrenia is a serious and disabling psychiatric disorder affecting approximately 1% of the world's population. A new generation of atypical antipsychotics has been introduced over the past decade. These atypical antipsychotics have comparable or greater efficacy than traditional antipsychotics in the treatment of the psychotic symptoms of schizophrenia and a much improved neurologic side effect profile. This paper reviews the pharmacological efficacy and safety of a potential atypical antipsychotic, NRA0562. NRA0562 has a high affinity for dopamine D1, D2L, D4.2, 5-HT2A receptors as well as alpha1-adrenoceptors, and has a moderate affinity for H1 receptors. NRA0562 strongly binds to 5-HT2A receptors and alpha1-adrenoceptors in the frontal cortex, its binding to striatal D2 receptors is weaker, similar to that of clozapine. NRA562 displayed potent antipsychotic activities in animal models of schizophrenia, such as methamphetamine (MAP)-induced hyperactivity, apomorphine-induced disruption of pre-pulse inhibition and conditioned avoidance test. NRA0562 is more potent in reversing the inhibitory effects of MAP at A10 than at A9 dopamine neurons. It increased Fos-like immunoreactivity in the nucleus accumbens more effectively than in the dorsolateral striatum, indicating that NRA0562 has the profile of an atypical antipsychotic. In vivo assays for extrapyramidal side effect liability showed that NRA0562 has a low rate of neurological side effects. Thus, NRA0562 may have unique antipsychotic activity with a lower propensity for extrapyramidal side effects.

title: Serotonin/dopamine interaction: Electrophysiological and neurochemical evidence.
abstract: The interaction between serotonin (5-HT) and dopamine (DA) in the central nervous system (CNS) plays an important role in the adaptive properties of living animals to their environment. These are two modulatory, divergent systems shaping and regulating in a widespread manner the activity of neurobiological networks and their interaction. The concept of one interaction linking these two systems is rather elusive when looking at the mechanisms triggered by these two systems across the CNS. The great variety of their interacting mechanisms is in part due to the diversity of their neuronal origin, the density of their fibers in a given CNS region, the distinct expression of their numerous receptors in the CNS, the heterogeneity of their intracellular signaling pathway that depend on the cellular type expressing their receptors, and the state of activity of neurobiological networks, conditioning the outcome of their mutual influences. Thus, originally conceptualized as inhibition of 5-HT on DA neuron activity and DA neurotransmission, this interaction is nowadays considered as a multifaceted, mutual influence of these two systems in the regulation of CNS functions. These new ways of understanding this interaction are of utmost importance to envision the consequences of their dysfunctions underlined in several CNS diseases. It is also essential to conceive the mechanism of action of psychotropic drugs directly acting on their function including antipsychotic, antidepressant, antiparkinsonian, and drug of abuse together with the development of therapeutic strategies of Alzheimer's diseases, epilepsy, obsessional compulsive disorders. The 5-HT/DA interaction has a long history from the serendipitous discovery of antidepressants and antipsychotics to the future, rationalized treatments of CNS disorders.

title: Psychopharmacology and memory.
abstract: The therapeutic manipulation of memory through psychopharmacological interventions raises significant ethical questions regarding personal identity, autonomy, and the nature of authentic human experience. While memory-modulating drugs show promise for treating post-traumatic stress disorder, addiction, and neurodegenerative diseases, their potential applications extend beyond clear medical necessity into enhancement and selective memory editing. This analysis examines the ethical implications of three categories of memory-affecting agents: memory enhancers (cholinesterase inhibitors, ampakines), memory inhibitors (propranolol, anisomycin), and memory selectors (targeted reconsolidation blockers). Through systematic review of current literature and application of established bioethical frameworks, we evaluated the moral permissibility of memory modification across therapeutic, preventive, and enhancement contexts. Our analysis reveals a spectrum of ethical acceptability, with therapeutic applications for severe psychiatric conditions receiving strongest justification, while enhancement uses raise concerns about authenticity, fairness, and psychological continuity. Memory inhibition for trauma prevention shows promise but requires careful consideration of informed consent and long-term consequences. Selective memory editing presents the most complex ethical challenges, potentially undermining the integrated nature of personal narrative and moral development. We propose a graduated ethical framework emphasizing therapeutic necessity, proportionality, and reversibility as key criteria for evaluating memory modification interventions. Future research must address questions of distributive justice, coercion, and the societal implications of widespread memory manipulation technologies. Robust ethical oversight and public discourse are essential as these powerful interventions transition from experimental tools to clinical realities.

title: Changes in nucleus accumbens dopamine transmission associated with fixed- and variable-time schedule-induced feeding.
abstract: We examined the changes in nucleus accumbens (NAcc) dopamine (DA) transmission associated with non-contingent meal presentations under conditions of high (fixed time-, FT-schedule) and low (variable time-, VT-schedule) predictability. Of interest were the changes in NAcc DA transmission associated with discrepancies between the time food is expected and when it is actually presented. We used in vivo voltammetry to monitor NAcc DA levels as rats received, on the first and second test days, 30-s meals of condensed milk on a VT-52 schedule (inter-meal intervals of 32, 35, 40, 45, 52, 64, and 95 s). On the third and subsequent days meals were presented first on a VT-52 s schedule and then on an FT-52 s schedule. On day 1, monotonic increases in NAcc DA signals were observed during both meal consumption and the intervals between VT meal presentations. By day 2, however, meal presentations on the VT schedule elicited biphasic DA signal fluctuations; DA signals increased prior to each meal presentation but then started to decline during the feeding bout that followed. Fixed-time meal presentations on day 3 disrupted this pattern, resulting in a concurrent escalation of DA signal fluctuations upon subsequent VT meal presentations. These findings provide further evidence that, in trained animals, NAcc DA transmission is activated by conditioned incentive cues rather than by primary rewards. They also suggest that the increases in NAcc DA transmission associated with reward expectancy are sensitive to temporal cues (e.g. interval timing) and to discrepancies between expected and actual outcomes.

title: A self-referencing glutamate biosensor for measuring real time neuronal glutamate flux.
abstract: Quantification of neurotransmitter transport dynamics is hindered by a lack of sufficient tools to directly monitor bioactive flux under physiological conditions. Traditional techniques for studying neurotransmitter release/uptake require inferences from non-selective electrical recordings, are invasive/destructive, and/or suffer from poor temporal resolution. Recent advances in electrochemical biosensors have enhanced in vitro and in vivo detection of neurotransmitter concentration under physiological/pathophysiological conditions. The use of enzymatic biosensors with performance enhancing materials (e.g., carbon nanotubes) has been a major focus for many of these advances. However, these techniques are not used as mainstream neuroscience research tools, due to relatively low sensitivity, excessive drift/noise, low signal-to-noise ratio, and inability to quantify rapid neurochemical kinetics during synaptic transmission. A sensing technique known as self-referencing overcomes many of these problems, and allows non-invasive quantification of biophysical transport. This work presents a self-referencing CNT modified glutamate oxidase biosensor for monitoring glutamate flux near neural/neuronal cells. Concentration of basal glutamate was similar to other in vivo and in vitro measurements. The biosensor was used in self-referencing (oscillating) mode to measure net glutamate flux near neural cells during electrical stimulation. Prior to stimulation, the average influx was 33.9+/-6.4 fmol cm(-2)s(-1)). Glutamate efflux took place immediately following stimulation, and was always followed by uptake in the 50-150 fmol cm(-2)s(-1) range. Uptake was inhibited using threo-beta-benzyloxyaspartate, and average surface flux in replicate cells (1.1+/-7.4 fmol cm(-2)s(-1)) was significantly lower than uninhibited cells. The technique is extremely valuable for studying neuropathological conditions related to neurotransmission under dynamic physiological conditions.

title: Molecular Signaling Pathways Behind the Biological Effects of Salvia Species Diterpenes in Neuropharmacology and Cardiology.
abstract: Salvia species contain bioactive diterpenes that have demonstrated significant therapeutic potential in neurological and cardiovascular disorders, yet their underlying molecular mechanisms remain poorly characterized. This comprehensive review examines the signaling pathways mediating the biological effects of major Salvia diterpenes, including tanshinones, salvianolic acids, and carnosic acid derivatives. We conducted a systematic analysis of peer-reviewed literature from 2000-2015, focusing on mechanistic studies involving primary neuronal cultures, cardiac myocytes, and relevant animal models. Database searches identified 147 relevant studies examining molecular targets and signaling cascades. In neurological applications, Salvia diterpenes primarily modulate neuroprotective pathways through inhibition of NF-κB signaling, activation of Nrf2-mediated antioxidant responses, and regulation of calcium homeostasis via L-type calcium channel blockade. Tanshinone IIA demonstrated particular efficacy in attenuating neuroinflammation through suppression of microglial activation and reduction of pro-inflammatory cytokine release. In cardiovascular systems, these compounds exhibit cardioprotective effects predominantly through PI3K/Akt pathway activation, mitochondrial membrane stabilization, and modulation of apoptotic cascades involving Bcl-2 family proteins. Salvianolic acid B showed remarkable ability to preserve cardiac function during ischemia-reperfusion injury via enhancement of endothelial nitric oxide synthase activity. Cross-system analysis revealed convergent mechanisms involving oxidative stress reduction and inflammatory pathway suppression. These findings provide molecular rationale for traditional uses of Salvia species and identify specific targets for therapeutic development. Further clinical investigation is warranted to translate these mechanistic insights into evidence-based phytotherapeutic interventions for neurodegenerative and cardiovascular diseases.

title: The utility of functional interaction and cluster analysis in CNS proteomics.
abstract: Proteomic studies offer enormous potential for gaining insight into cellular dynamics and disease processes. An immediate challenge for enhancing the utility of proteomics in translational research lies in methods of handling and interpreting the large datasets generated. Publications rarely extend beyond lists of proteins, putatively altered derived from basic statistics. Here we describe two additional distinct approaches (with particular strengths and limitations) that will enhance the analysis of proteomic datasets. Arithmetic and functional cluster analyses have been performed on proteins found differentially regulated in human glioma. These two approaches highlight (i) subgroups of proteins that may be co-regulated and play a role in glioma pathophysiology, and (ii) functional protein interactions that may improve comprehension of the biological mechanisms involved. A coherent proteomic strategy which involves both arithmetic and functional clustering, (together with careful consideration of conceptual limitations), is imperative for quantitative proteomics to deliver and advance the biological understanding of disease of the CNS. A strategy which combines arithmetic analysis and bioinformatics of protein-protein interactions is both generally applicable and will facilitate the interpretation of proteomic data.

title: Movement disorders and new azapirone anxiolytic drugs.
abstract: Dopamine is a neurotransmitter affecting motor function, and the dopamine system is the focus of abnormality in dyskinetic diseases. An important side effect of many psychotropic drugs possessing dopamine activity is the production of movement disorders; this potential problem needs to be examined carefully with any new central nervous system drug, such as the azapirone anxiolytic drugs. The pharmacology of the azapirones, represented by the prototype, buspirone, involves several different neuroreceptor systems. Current evidence indicates that buspirone's anxiolytic activity is mediated through serotonergic mechanisms. It also displays some binding affinity for dopamine receptors, which necessitates comparisons between buspirone and neuroleptic agents in preclinical tests, clinical studies, and case reports. Although similarities occurred in some biochemical studies, neuroleptics, and not buspirone, caused an increase in dopamine receptors with chronic use. Neuroleptics typically produce this change after long-term use, which is thought to be a key factor leading to tardive dyskinesia. In standard animal behavior studies, buspirone's profile failed to match neuroleptic agents. Most notably, neuroleptics produce catalepsy; however, buspirone failed to induce catalepsy and instead reversed this effect. Isolated case reports have described buspirone as causing movement disorders, but these patients were previously exposed to neuroleptic agents. Furthermore, buspirone has been used successfully to treat anxiety in parkinsonism without exacerbating existing movement problems. In psychotropic drug use, careful patient monitoring, complete drug histories, and a full appreciation for a drug's pharmacology are necessary to decipher any likelihood of a drug relation with an adverse effect.
journal: The Journal of the American Board of Family Practice

title: Targets of cytoprotection in acute ischemic stroke: present and future.
abstract: Although the management of stroke has improved remarkably over the last decade due mainly to the advent of thrombolysis, most neuroprotective agents, although successful in animal studies, have failed in humans. Our increasing knowledge concerning the ischemic cascade is leading to a considerable development of pharmacological tools suggesting that each step of this cascade might be a target for cytoprotection. Glutamate has long been recognized to play key roles in the pathophysiology of ischemia. However, although some trials are still ongoing, the results from several completed trials with drugs interfering with the glutamatergic pathway have been disappointing. Regarding the inhibition of glutamate release as a possible target for cytoprotection, it might be afforded either by decreasing glutamate efflux or by increasing glutamate uptake. In this context, it has been shown that glutamate transport is the primary and only mechanism for maintaining extracellular glutamate concentrations below excitotoxic levels. This transport is executed by the five high-affinity, sodium-dependent plasma membrane glutamate transporters. Among them, the transporter EAAT2 is responsible for up to 90% of all glutamate transport. We will discuss the effect of different neuroprotective tools (membrane stabilizers or endogenous neuroprotection) affecting glutamate efflux and/or expression of EAAT2. We will also describe the finding of a novel polymorphism in the EAAT2 promoter region which could be responsible for differences in both gene function and regulation under pathological conditions such as cerebral ischemia, and which might well account for the failure of glutamate antagonists in the clinical practice.
