These results may possess important therapeutic implications in the management of patients at risk of ischemic events, since it has been demonstrated that those patients with progressing stroke have higher plasma concentrations of glutamate which remain elevated up to 24 h when compared to the levels in patients without neurological deterioration.

title: Neurogenic bladder model for spinal cord injury: spinal cord microdialysis and chronic urodynamics.
abstract: Neurogenic bladder dysfunction represents a major clinical complication following spinal cord injury (SCI), yet the underlying neurochemical mechanisms remain poorly understood. We developed a combined approach utilizing spinal cord microdialysis with chronic urodynamic monitoring to investigate neurotransmitter dynamics in a rat model of SCI-induced neurogenic bladder. Adult female Sprague-Dawley rats (n=24) underwent complete T10 spinal cord transection, with sham-operated controls (n=12). Four weeks post-injury, microdialysis probes were implanted in the L6-S1 spinal cord segments, and chronic bladder catheters were placed for simultaneous urodynamic recordings. Extracellular levels of acetylcholine, norepinephrine, serotonin, and GABA were measured during spontaneous voiding cycles over 72-hour periods. SCI animals demonstrated characteristic neurogenic bladder patterns including detrusor hyperreflexia, reduced bladder capacity (2.1±0.3 mL vs 4.8±0.4 mL in controls, p<0.001), and increased voiding frequency. Microdialysis revealed significantly elevated acetylcholine release during detrusor contractions in SCI rats (284±41% above baseline) compared to controls (156±23%, p<0.01). Norepinephrine levels were markedly reduced in SCI animals (38±12% of control values, p<0.001), while GABA concentrations showed compensatory increases (167±28% of controls, p<0.05). Serotonin levels remained unchanged between groups. These findings demonstrate that SCI-induced neurogenic bladder involves complex alterations in spinal neurotransmitter balance, with enhanced cholinergic activity and impaired noradrenergic modulation. This experimental model provides a valuable platform for investigating pharmacological interventions targeting specific neurotransmitter systems in neurogenic bladder dysfunction.

title: Curcumin ameliorates reserpine-induced pain-depression dyad: behavioural, biochemical, neurochemical and molecular evidences.
abstract: Pain and depression frequently co-occur in clinical practice, sharing common neurobiological pathways involving monoaminergic dysfunction and neuroinflammation. Reserpine, a monoamine-depleting agent, has been shown to induce both nociceptive hypersensitivity and depressive-like behaviors in rodents, providing a valuable model for studying pain-depression comorbidity. Curcumin, the active constituent of turmeric, possesses anti-inflammatory, antioxidant, and monoamine-modulating properties. The present study investigated the therapeutic potential of curcumin in ameliorating reserpine-induced pain-depression dyad in male Wistar rats. Animals received reserpine (1 mg/kg, s.c.) for three consecutive days, followed by curcumin treatment (25, 50, or 100 mg/kg, p.o.) for 14 days. Behavioral assessments included thermal hyperalgesia (hot plate test), mechanical allodynia (von Frey filaments), and depressive-like behaviors (forced swim test, sucrose preference). Biochemical analyses measured oxidative stress markers, pro-inflammatory cytokines (TNF-α, IL-1β, IL-6), and monoamine levels (5-HT, DA, NE) in prefrontal cortex and hippocampus. Molecular studies examined BDNF, p-CREB, and NF-κB expression. Reserpine administration induced significant thermal hyperalgesia, mechanical allodynia, increased immobility time, and anhedonia. Curcumin dose-dependently reversed these behavioral alterations. Biochemically, curcumin restored depleted monoamine levels, reduced oxidative stress markers and pro-inflammatory cytokines, while upregulating BDNF and p-CREB expression and suppressing NF-κB activation. These findings suggest that curcumin effectively ameliorates reserpine-induced pain-depression comorbidity through multiple mechanisms involving monoaminergic restoration, anti-inflammatory actions, and neuroprotective effects, supporting its potential as a therapeutic intervention for pain-depression dyad.

title: Fundamental and clinical neuropharmacology, a terra incognita with constantly expanding frontiers.
abstract: The field of neuropharmacology continues to evolve rapidly, yet significant knowledge gaps persist in understanding drug-brain interactions across clinical and preclinical domains. This comprehensive review synthesizes current literature from 2015-2021 to identify emerging trends, methodological advances, and translational challenges in fundamental and clinical neuropharmacology research. We conducted a systematic analysis of 847 peer-reviewed articles from major neuropharmacology journals, categorizing studies by therapeutic area, methodological approach, and translational outcomes. Additionally, we surveyed 156 researchers across 23 countries to assess perceived research priorities and barriers to clinical translation. Our analysis revealed exponential growth in neuroimaging-based pharmacodynamic studies (312% increase), optogenetic applications in drug discovery (287% increase), and precision medicine approaches targeting neuropsychiatric disorders (198% increase). However, translation rates from preclinical to clinical success remained low (8.3%), with significant disparities between therapeutic areas. Alzheimer's disease research showed the highest preclinical activity but lowest clinical success rates (3.1%), while addiction pharmacotherapy demonstrated improved translational efficiency (15.7%). Survey responses identified inadequate disease models (78% of respondents), insufficient funding for negative results (65%), and regulatory barriers (52%) as primary obstacles. Emerging areas including psychedelic-assisted therapy, neuromodulation pharmacology, and gut-brain axis interventions represent promising frontiers. These findings underscore neuropharmacology's dynamic landscape while highlighting persistent translational challenges. Enhanced collaboration between basic and clinical researchers, improved animal models, and standardized outcome measures are essential for advancing this rapidly expanding field toward meaningful therapeutic breakthroughs.

title: Closed-loop Neuropharmacology for Epilepsy: Distant Dream or Future Reality?
abstract:
Epilepsy affects over 65 million people worldwide, with approximately 30% of patients experiencing drug-resistant seizures despite optimal medical management. Traditional antiepileptic drug therapy relies on continuous systemic administration, often resulting in subtherapeutic or toxic levels and significant side effects. Closed-loop neuropharmacology represents a paradigm shift toward precision medicine, combining real-time seizure detection with automated, localized drug delivery. This review examines current technological advances and clinical feasibility of closed-loop systems for epilepsy management.

We conducted a comprehensive analysis of recent developments in seizure prediction algorithms, implantable drug delivery platforms, and biocompatible reservoir systems. Key studies from 2015-2019 were evaluated, including preclinical models and early-phase clinical trials utilizing responsive neurostimulation data, microfluidic drug delivery devices, and machine learning-based seizure forecasting.

Current closed-loop systems demonstrate 70-85% accuracy in seizure detection within 10-30 seconds of onset, with response latencies of 2-5 seconds for drug delivery. Microinfusion pumps can deliver precise drug volumes (nanoliters to microliters) directly to epileptogenic zones, achieving local concentrations 10-100 times higher than systemic administration while minimizing off-target effects. Battery life and biocompatibility remain technical challenges, with current devices requiring replacement every 3-5 years. Clinical pilot studies show 40-60% seizure reduction in drug-resistant patients with minimal systemic side effects.

Closed-loop neuropharmacology for epilepsy represents a promising therapeutic frontier that addresses limitations of conventional treatment. While technical hurdles remain, rapid advances in biosensors, drug delivery systems, and artificial intelligence suggest clinical implementation within the next decade is increasingly realistic rather than aspirational.

title: A primer on confidence intervals in psychopharmacology.
abstract: The interpretation of clinical trial results in psychopharmacology has traditionally relied heavily on p-values and statistical significance testing, often leading to oversimplified binary conclusions about treatment efficacy. Confidence intervals (CIs) provide a more nuanced approach to data interpretation by estimating the range of plausible values for treatment effects, yet their application remains underutilized in psychiatric research. This primer examines the theoretical foundations and practical applications of confidence intervals in psychopharmacological studies. We reviewed methodological approaches for constructing and interpreting CIs across common study designs in psychopharmacology, including randomized controlled trials, meta-analyses, and dose-response studies. Using examples from recent antidepressant and antipsychotic trials, we demonstrate how CIs can enhance interpretation of treatment effects beyond traditional significance testing. Key applications include assessing clinical meaningfulness of observed differences, evaluating equivalence and non-inferiority in comparative effectiveness research, and improving transparency in meta-analytic syntheses. Our analysis reveals that CIs provide crucial information about precision of estimates and potential clinical significance that p-values alone cannot convey. For instance, a statistically non-significant result may still indicate clinically meaningful benefit when CI bounds encompass relevant effect sizes. Furthermore, CIs facilitate better communication of uncertainty to clinicians and patients, supporting evidence-based decision-making. We conclude that routine reporting and interpretation of confidence intervals alongside traditional statistical measures would substantially improve the quality and clinical utility of psychopharmacological research. Training programs should emphasize CI interpretation to enhance statistical literacy among psychiatric researchers and clinicians.

title: In vivo voltammetry: from wire to wireless measurements.
abstract: In vivo voltammetry has emerged as a powerful technique for real-time monitoring of neurotransmitter dynamics in the living brain, yet traditional wired recording systems impose significant limitations on behavioral studies and chronic measurements. Current cable-tethered approaches restrict animal movement, introduce electrical artifacts, and preclude naturalistic behavioral paradigms. To address these constraints, we developed and validated a novel wireless voltammetry system for continuous monitoring of dopamine release in freely moving rats. The system incorporates a miniaturized potentiostat (2.1 g) with integrated radio frequency transmission capabilities, coupled with carbon fiber microelectrodes implanted in the nucleus accumbens. We compared the performance of wireless recordings with conventional wired fast-scan cyclic voltammetry during pharmacological challenges and behavioral tasks over 14 days post-implantation. Wireless measurements demonstrated equivalent sensitivity (detection limit: 50 nM dopamine) and temporal resolution (100 ms sampling) compared to wired controls, with signal-to-noise ratios exceeding 15:1 for stimulated dopamine release. Chronic recordings revealed stable baseline measurements and preserved responses to amphetamine administration (2 mg/kg, i.p.) and electrical stimulation of the ventral tegmental area. The wireless system eliminated movement artifacts present in tethered recordings and enabled dopamine monitoring during complex social behaviors previously inaccessible with conventional approaches. This wireless voltammetry platform represents a significant methodological advancement for neuropharmacological research, facilitating investigation of neurotransmitter function in naturalistic settings while maintaining the high temporal and chemical resolution essential for mechanistic studies of brain neurochemistry.

title: Microdialysis applications in neuroscience.
abstract: Microdialysis has emerged as a powerful in vivo sampling technique that enables real-time monitoring of neurotransmitters, metabolites, and drug concentrations in discrete brain regions of awake, freely moving animals. This review examines recent applications of cerebral microdialysis in neuroscience research, highlighting methodological advances and novel experimental paradigms developed over the past decade. We analyzed 127 peer-reviewed studies published between 2000-2007 that employed microdialysis to investigate neurotransmitter systems, drug pharmacokinetics, and pathophysiological mechanisms. Major applications included monitoring dopamine, serotonin, acetylcholine, and glutamate release in response to pharmacological challenges, behavioral paradigms, and disease models. Technological improvements have enhanced temporal resolution to sub-minute sampling intervals and reduced probe size to minimize tissue damage. Novel probe designs incorporating biosensors and online analytical systems have enabled simultaneous measurement of multiple analytes with improved sensitivity. Microdialysis studies have provided critical insights into addiction mechanisms, demonstrating drug-induced alterations in mesolimbic dopamine signaling, and have elucidated neurotransmitter changes in models of Alzheimer's disease, Parkinson's disease, and stroke. Recent applications in clinical settings show promise for monitoring brain chemistry in neurosurgical patients. Despite limitations including delayed equilibration and potential tissue trauma, microdialysis remains an invaluable tool for investigating dynamic neurochemical processes. Future developments in probe miniaturization, analytical sensitivity, and wireless telemetry systems will likely expand applications in translational neuroscience research and clinical neuropharmacology.

title: Simple behavioral methods to assess the effect of drugs or toxins on sensory experience.
abstract: Behavioral assessment of sensory function represents a critical component in neuropharmacological research, yet many current methods require extensive training, sophisticated equipment, or invasive procedures. We developed and validated a battery of simple, non-invasive behavioral assays to evaluate drug and toxin effects on visual, auditory, tactile, and olfactory sensory systems in laboratory rats. The protocol consists of four standardized tests: (1) visual cliff avoidance for depth perception, (2) acoustic startle response amplitude for auditory sensitivity, (3) adhesive tape removal latency for somatosensory function, and (4) buried food pellet discovery time for olfactory detection. Male Sprague-Dawley rats (n=8 per group) were tested following administration of known sensory-impairing agents: atropine (1-10 mg/kg, i.p.) for visual impairment, kanamycin (400 mg/kg, i.p.) for ototoxicity, capsaicin (50 mg/kg, s.c.) for nociceptive dysfunction, and zinc sulfate (intranasal) for anosmia. All compounds produced dose-dependent sensory deficits detectable within 30-60 minutes post-administration. Atropine significantly increased visual cliff crossing latency (p<0.01), kanamycin reduced startle amplitude by 65% (p<0.001), capsaicin doubled tape removal time (p<0.01), and zinc sulfate increased food-finding latency 4-fold (p<0.001). Inter-rater reliability exceeded 0.85 for all measures, and test-retest correlation was >0.90 across a two-week interval. These simple behavioral methods provide researchers with accessible, quantitative tools for screening sensory toxicity and monitoring therapeutic interventions without requiring specialized training or equipment, making them particularly suitable for laboratories with limited resources.

title: Unraveling neuronal dopamine transporter mechanisms with rotating disk electrode voltammetry.
abstract: Herein we describe how the rotating disk electrode voltammetric technique can be used to examine the mechanism(s) of the inward transport of dopamine by the neuronal transporter for dopamine (DAT). The usefulness of making measurements kinetically resolving dopamine transport, interpretations of changes in Km and Vmax, approaches to defining pre-steady-state binding of dopamine to DAT, interactions between competing inhibitors, chemical modification of functional groups within DAT, and a presentation of a hypothetical multi-state model of dopamine transport are presented and discussed.

title: Identifying novel protein-protein interactions using co-immunoprecipitation and mass spectroscopy.
abstract: Proteomics has evolved from genomic science due to the convergence of advances in protein chemistry, separations, mass spectroscopy, and peptide and protein databases. Where identifying protein-protein interactions was once limited to yeast two-hybrid analyses or empirical data, protein-protein interactions can now be examined in both cells and native tissues by precipitation of the protein complex of interest. Coupling this field to receptor pharmacology has recently allowed for the identification of proteins that differentially and selectively interact with receptors and are integral to their biological effects. It is becoming increasingly apparent that receptors in neurons do not exist as singular independent units, but rather are part of large macromolecular complexes of interacting proteins. It is a primary quest of neuroscience to piece together these interactions and to characterize the regulatory signalplexes of all proteins. This unit presents co-immunoprecipitation-coupled mass spectroscopy as one way of identifying signalplex partners.

title: "Novel Psychopharmacology for Depressive Disorders".
abstract: Major depressive disorder carries a significant burden and a high risk for suicide. The need for more effective, safer, and faster-acting drugs is, therefore, compelling. The present chapter briefly assesses the most promising agents, focusing on non-monoamine-targeting compounds, namely, the glutamate antagonist ketamine and its enantiomer esketamine. A critical overview of the evidence and the pitfalls associated with current antidepressant drug development is likewise provided in the following text.
journal: Advances in experimental medicine and biology

title: Challenges Faced by New Psychiatric-Mental Health Nurse Practitioner Prescribers.
abstract: One of the main challenges that psychiatric-mental health nurse practitioner (PMNHP) students experience is preparing to prescribe medications by demonstrating psychopharmacological competency. To examine the challenges as they relate to this issue, self-reflective journaling narratives were evaluated from two cohorts of Post-Master's PMHNP program graduates, across each of two semesters of pediatric and adult clinical experience. The most prominent challenges reported by students were in regard to medication treatment adherence, decision making, and monitoring symptom-related outcomes. The narratives also demonstrate that reflection, combined with faculty- and preceptor-supported clinical education, assists PMHNP students in developing psychopharmacological competency. All PMHNP students described in this article were nurse practitioners before they began the program. By seeking to augment their competencies and through continued self-reflective learning and practice, they will improve access to mental health care for the populations they serve. [Journal of Psychosocial Nursing and Mental Health Services, 58(10), 7-11.].
journal: Journal of psychosocial nursing and mental health services

title: Effect of ethanolic extract of Rubia peregrina L. (Rubiaceae) on monoamine-mediated behaviour.
abstract: In the traditional system of medicine in Sardinia, Rubia peregrina (RP) is reported as an aphrodisiac herb. Since aphrodisiacs may also have dopaminergic activity, and there can be a reciprocal relationship between dopaminergic and serotonergic functions in the central nervous system, the aim here was to study the effect of the ethanolic extract of the aerial parts of R. peregrina on monoamine-mediated behaviour. The RP (100 and 200 mg kg(-1) intraperitoneally) significantly inhibited haloperidol (1 mg kg(-1) i.p.) induced catalepsy in mice (p < 0.01, dopamine-mediated response) lithium sulphate induced head twitches in rats (serotonin-mediated response) and was without effect on clonidine-induced hypothermia in rats (noradrenaline-mediated response). The study concludes that R. peregrina improves the dopaminergic function, diminishes the serotonin-mediated function and is devoid of any effect on the noradrenergic function. The study suggests that we should carry out further experiments to investigate the psychopharmacological profile of R. peregrina.

title: Comparative analysis of inhibitory effects of caged ligands for the NMDA receptor.
abstract: Photolytic release of neurotransmitters from caged precursors is a useful method to study synaptic processes with high temporal and spatial resolution. At present, the two most widely used classes of caged precursors for studies on glutamate receptors are based on derivatives of the 2-nitrobenzyl caging group (alpha-carboxy-2-nitrobenzyl, CNB) and the nitroindoline caging group (7-nitroindoline, NI, and 4-methoxy-7-nitroindoline, MNI). Besides NI- and MNI-caged amino acids being thermally more stable than the CNB-caged amino acids, there have been no other major advantages reported of using compounds from either of these two classes. Here, we show inhibitory effects of CNB-glutamate and a number of other CNB-caged agonists on N-methyl-D-aspartate (NMDA) receptors at non-saturating concentrations of the co-agonist glycine. In contrast, NI- and MNI-glutamate and most other NI-/MNI-caged agonists that we tested were inert under these conditions. Furthermore, we demonstrate that carboxynitroindoline-caged glycine (CNI-glycine), which was previously found to inhibit glycine receptors, has no such effect on NMDA receptors. Together, these findings underline the usefulness of NI- and MNI-caged ligands and show that CNB-caged compounds should be avoided in studies involving NMDA receptors.

title: Roles of transmembrane domain 2 and the first intracellular loop in human noradrenaline transporter function: pharmacological and SCAM analysis.
abstract: The aim was to investigate the roles of transmembrane domain 2 and the adjacent region of the first intracellular loop in determining human noradrenaline transporter (hNET) function by pharmacological and substituted-cysteine accessibility method (SCAM) analyses. It was first necessary to establish a suitable background NET for SCAM. Alanine mutants of endogenous hNET cysteines, hC86A, hC131A and hC339A, were examined and showed no marked effects on expression or function. hNET and the mutants were also resistant to methanethiosulfonate (MTS), ethylammonium (MTSEA) and MTStrimethylammonium (MTSET). Hence, wild-type hNET is an appropriate background for production of cysteine mutants for SCAM. Pharmacological investigation showed that all mutants except hT99C and hL109C showed reduced cell-surface expression, while all except hM107C showed a reduction in functional activity. The mutations did not markedly affect the apparent affinities of substrates, but apparent affinities of cocaine were decreased 7-fold for hP97C and 10-fold for hF101C and increased 12-fold for hY98C. [3H]Nisoxetine binding affinities were decreased 13-fold for hP97C and 5-fold for hF101C. SCAM analysis revealed that only hL102C was sensitive to 1.25 mm MTSEA, and this sensitivity was protected by noradrenaline, nisoxetine and cocaine. The results suggest that this region of hNET is important for interactions with antidepressants and cocaine, but it is probably not involved in substrate translocation mechanisms.

title: Two novel combined drug treatments for women with hypoactive sexual desire disorder.
abstract: Hypoactive sexual desire disorder (HSDD) affects approximately 10% of women and represents the most common form of female sexual dysfunction, yet effective pharmacological interventions remain limited. Current monotherapies targeting dopaminergic, noradrenergic, or melanocortin pathways have shown modest efficacy with significant side effects. This study investigated two novel combination therapies designed to enhance sexual motivation through synergistic neurotransmitter modulation. A randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled trial was conducted in 180 premenopausal women diagnosed with HSDD. Participants were randomized to receive either bupropion (300mg/day) combined with buspirone (30mg/day), flibanserin (100mg/day) combined with low-dose testosterone gel (300μg/day), or placebo for 12 weeks. Primary outcomes included changes in sexual desire as measured by the Female Sexual Function Index (FSFI) desire domain and Sexual Desire Inventory-2 (SDI-2). Secondary measures assessed sexual satisfaction, distress levels, and adverse events. Both combination treatments demonstrated significantly greater improvements in sexual desire compared to placebo (p<0.001). The bupropion-buspirone combination increased mean FSFI desire scores by 2.4 points (95% CI: 1.8-3.0), while flibanserin-testosterone improved scores by 2.1 points (95% CI: 1.5-2.7). Sexual satisfaction improved in both active treatment groups with minimal adverse effects, primarily mild nausea and dizziness. These findings suggest that combination pharmacotherapies targeting multiple neurotransmitter systems may offer superior efficacy over monotherapy approaches for treating HSDD, warranting larger phase III trials to establish optimal dosing regimens and long-term safety profiles.

title: History of modern psychopharmacology: a personal view with an emphasis on antidepressants.
abstract: The development of modern psychopharmacology has fundamentally transformed psychiatric practice over the past five decades, with antidepressant medications representing one of the most significant therapeutic advances in medicine. This historical review examines the evolution of antidepressant pharmacotherapy from the serendipitous discovery of imipramine's antidepressant properties in 1957 through the introduction of selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors (SSRIs) in the 1980s and 1990s. A comprehensive analysis of landmark clinical trials, regulatory milestones, and theoretical developments was conducted using published literature, FDA records, and personal communications with key researchers in the field. The tricyclic antidepressants, beginning with imipramine and amitriptyline, established the foundation for understanding monoaminergic mechanisms in depression, despite significant anticholinergic and cardiovascular side effects. The monoamine oxidase inhibitors, though effective, were limited by dietary restrictions and drug interactions. The introduction of fluoxetine in 1987 marked a paradigm shift, offering comparable efficacy with improved tolerability profiles. Subsequent SSRIs including sertraline, paroxetine, and fluvoxamine expanded treatment options, while novel mechanisms including dual-action agents like venlafaxine emerged in the 1990s. Clinical response rates consistently demonstrated 60-70% efficacy across drug classes, though remission rates remained suboptimal. The evolution of antidepressant pharmacotherapy illustrates the critical importance of serendipitous observations, rigorous clinical investigation, and mechanistic understanding in advancing psychiatric therapeutics. Future directions must address treatment-resistant depression and personalized medicine approaches to optimize patient outcomes.

title: Stereochemistry in clinical medicine: a neurological perspective.
abstract: Stereochemistry plays a crucial role in neuropharmacology, yet its clinical implications remain incompletely understood in neurological practice. Many neurotropic medications exist as stereoisomers with potentially different pharmacokinetic and pharmacodynamic properties. We conducted a comprehensive review of stereochemical considerations in commonly prescribed neurological medications and analyzed clinical outcomes data from 847 patients treated at our tertiary neurology center between 1998-2002. We examined racemic versus enantiopure formulations of key drug classes including anticonvulsants, anti-parkinsonian agents, and psychotropic medications, correlating stereochemical properties with therapeutic efficacy and adverse event profiles. Our analysis revealed significant differences in clinical outcomes between stereoisomeric formulations. S-citalopram demonstrated superior efficacy compared to racemic citalopram in treating post-stroke depression (response rate 73% vs 58%, p<0.05). Levodopa combined with carbidopa showed enhanced bioavailability when the L-enantiomer was used exclusively versus racemic preparations. Among anticonvulsants, the S-enantiomer of phenytoin exhibited reduced cognitive side effects while maintaining seizure control in 89% of patients switched from racemic formulations. Pharmacokinetic modeling revealed that hepatic metabolism, protein binding, and blood-brain barrier penetration varied significantly between enantiomers of the same compound. These findings suggest that stereochemical optimization of neurotropic medications could improve therapeutic outcomes while minimizing adverse effects. Greater attention to stereochemistry in drug development and clinical practice may enhance precision in neurological therapeutics, though larger controlled trials are needed to establish definitive treatment guidelines.
journal: Journal of clinical neuroscience : official journal of the Neurosurgical Society of Australasia

title: Attention: oscillations and neuropharmacology.
abstract: Attention-related neural oscillations in the gamma frequency range (30-100 Hz) have been implicated in selective attention and conscious perception, yet their underlying neuropharmacological mechanisms remain poorly understood. We investigated the effects of GABAergic and cholinergic modulation on gamma oscillations during visual attention tasks in awake, behaving macaque monkeys. Local field potentials were recorded from area V4 while animals performed a cued spatial attention task. Iontophoretic application of the GABA_A antagonist bicuculline (10-50 nA) significantly increased gamma power (147 ± 23% of baseline, p < 0.001) and enhanced attentional modulation of neuronal responses by 34 ± 12%. Conversely, muscimol (GABA_A agonist, 20-80 nA) suppressed gamma oscillations (62 ± 18% of baseline, p < 0.01) and reduced attention-related firing rate modulation. The acetylcholinesterase inhibitor physostigmine (15-40 nA) enhanced gamma synchronization during attended conditions (128 ± 19% of baseline, p < 0.05) and improved behavioral performance on attention-demanding trials. The nicotinic receptor antagonist mecamylamine (25-60 nA) disrupted attention-related gamma enhancement and impaired discrimination accuracy. Cross-correlation analysis revealed that pharmacologically induced changes in gamma power correlated strongly with attentional performance (r = 0.73, p < 0.001). These findings demonstrate that gamma oscillations underlying visual attention depend critically on the balance between GABAergic inhibition and cholinergic excitation. Our results suggest that attention-related gamma rhythms emerge from local GABAergic interneuron networks modulated by ascending cholinergic projections, providing a neurochemical framework for understanding attentional dysfunction in neuropsychiatric disorders.

title: Operant self-administration models for testing the neuropharmacological basis of ethanol consumption in rats.
abstract: Operant self-administration paradigms represent the gold standard for investigating the neurobiological mechanisms underlying ethanol reinforcement and consumption patterns. These models provide translational validity by allowing laboratory animals to voluntarily consume ethanol through learned behaviors that parallel human drinking patterns. The current protocol describes established methods for training rats to self-administer ethanol using operant conditioning chambers, enabling systematic evaluation of pharmacological interventions on ethanol-seeking and consumption behaviors. Male Wistar rats (n=48) were trained to lever-press for 10% ethanol solutions using a fixed-ratio 1 schedule with concurrent water availability. Following acquisition, animals underwent pharmacological challenges with the μ-opioid receptor antagonist naltrexone (0.1-3.0 mg/kg, s.c.), the dopamine D2/D3 receptor antagonist raclopride (0.01-0.1 mg/kg, i.p.), and the GABAB receptor agonist baclofen (1.0-5.0 mg/kg, i.p.) administered 30 minutes prior to test sessions. Naltrexone dose-dependently reduced ethanol self-administration (F=12.4, p<0.001) with significant effects observed at 1.0 and 3.0 mg/kg doses without affecting water consumption. Raclopride similarly decreased ethanol lever-pressing (F=8.7, p<0.01) at 0.1 mg/kg. Baclofen produced mixed effects, reducing consumption at 2.5 mg/kg but causing motor impairment at higher doses. These findings demonstrate the utility of operant self-administration models for characterizing neurotransmitter systems mediating ethanol reinforcement, particularly opioidergic and dopaminergic pathways. This methodology provides a robust platform for screening potential pharmacotherapeutics for alcohol use disorders and investigating the neurochemical basis of ethanol reward.

title: From systems biology to dynamical neuropharmacology: proposal for a new methodology.
abstract: Traditional neuropharmacological approaches have primarily relied on reductionist methodologies that examine isolated molecular targets, often failing to capture the complex, dynamic interactions within neural networks. The emergence of systems biology presents an unprecedented opportunity to revolutionize drug discovery and therapeutic intervention in neurological disorders by integrating multi-scale biological data and network dynamics. We propose a novel methodological framework termed "dynamical neuropharmacology" that combines high-throughput genomic and proteomic data with computational modeling to predict drug effects on neural circuit function. Our approach utilizes differential equation-based models to simulate temporal dynamics of neurotransmitter systems, incorporating feedback loops, crosstalk between signaling pathways, and emergent network properties. We applied this methodology to analyze the effects of selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors (SSRIs) on hippocampal-prefrontal circuits using integrated datasets from microarray expression profiles, protein-protein interaction networks, and electrophysiological recordings from rat brain slices. The dynamical models successfully predicted biphasic dose-response relationships and identified previously unrecognized off-target effects involving GABAergic interneurons. Validation experiments confirmed 78% of model predictions regarding synaptic plasticity changes following chronic SSRI treatment. Our results demonstrate that dynamical neuropharmacology can reveal non-intuitive drug mechanisms and predict therapeutic windows more accurately than conventional approaches. This systems-level methodology offers a paradigm shift toward personalized neurotherapeutics and may accelerate the development of more effective treatments for psychiatric and neurological disorders by leveraging the inherent complexity of neural systems rather than oversimplifying them.

title: The future of acute care and prevention in headache.
abstract: Headache disorders represent a significant burden on healthcare systems worldwide, with current therapeutic approaches often providing suboptimal outcomes in acute management and prevention. This review examines emerging pharmacological strategies and technological innovations that may reshape headache treatment paradigms. We conducted a comprehensive analysis of recent clinical trials, experimental studies, and technological developments in headache medicine published between 2002-2007. Novel acute interventions include selective 5-HT1F receptor agonists showing efficacy without vasoconstriction, inhaled dihydroergotamine formulations for rapid onset, and targeted CGRP antagonists demonstrating promising results in migraine termination. Preventive strategies have expanded beyond traditional approaches to include botulinum toxin type A injections for chronic migraine, topiramate for episodic prevention, and emerging anti-epileptic drugs with dual mechanisms. Technological advances encompass transcranial magnetic stimulation devices, implantable occipital nerve stimulators for refractory cases, and telemedicine platforms enabling remote monitoring and early intervention. Pharmacogenomic approaches are beginning to personalize treatment selection based on individual metabolic profiles and drug responsiveness patterns. Early clinical data suggest that CGRP pathway modulation may provide breakthrough efficacy with improved tolerability profiles compared to current triptans and ergot derivatives. The integration of biomarker identification, personalized medicine approaches, and novel drug delivery systems represents a paradigm shift toward precision headache medicine. These developments promise to transform acute care through more effective, rapidly acting interventions and prevention through targeted, individualized prophylactic strategies, potentially reducing both attack frequency and healthcare utilization in headache populations.
journal: Neurological sciences : official journal of the Italian Neurological Society and of the Italian Society of Clinical Neurophysiology

title: Validation of a fluorescence-based high-throughput assay for the measurement of neurotransmitter transporter uptake activity.
abstract: Neurotransmitter transporters play critical roles in synaptic transmission by regulating extracellular concentrations of dopamine, serotonin, and norepinephrine. Current methods for measuring transporter uptake activity, including radioligand binding assays and electrophysiological techniques, are often labor-intensive and unsuitable for high-throughput screening applications. We developed and validated a fluorescence-based assay using the fluorescent substrate 4-(4-(dimethylamino)styryl)-N-methylpyridinium (ASP+) to quantify uptake activity of the dopamine transporter (DAT), serotonin transporter (SERT), and norepinephrine transporter (NET) in a 96-well plate format. Human embryonic kidney 293 cells stably transfected with individual transporters were incubated with ASP+ (5 μM) for 30 minutes at 37°C, and fluorescence accumulation was measured using a microplate reader (excitation 485 nm, emission 590 nm). The assay demonstrated excellent linearity (r² > 0.95) across a range of cell densities and incubation times. Kinetic analysis revealed apparent Km values of 8.2 ± 1.4 μM for DAT, 12.1 ± 2.1 μM for SERT, and 9.7 ± 1.8 μM for NET. Selective inhibitors including GBR12909, fluoxetine, and desipramine produced concentration-dependent inhibition with IC50 values consistent with literature reports. The assay showed high reproducibility (CV < 10%) and was successfully used to screen a library of 480 compounds, identifying novel transporter modulators. This fluorescence-based method provides a robust, cost-effective alternative to radioligand assays for neurotransmitter transporter research and drug discovery applications.

title: Developmental exposure to a serotonin agonist produces subsequent behavioral and neurochemical changes in the adult male prairie vole.
abstract: Prairie voles (Microtus ochrogaster) exhibit complex social behaviors mediated by multiple neurotransmitter systems, including serotonin, which plays a critical role in social attachment and anxiety-related behaviors. Early developmental exposure to pharmacological agents can produce lasting alterations in adult behavior and neurochemistry. The present study examined whether neonatal exposure to the serotonin 1A receptor agonist 8-OH-DPAT affects adult behavioral phenotypes and serotonergic function in male prairie voles. Male pups received daily subcutaneous injections of 8-OH-DPAT (0.5 mg/kg) or saline vehicle from postnatal days 1-14. At 8-10 weeks of age, subjects underwent behavioral testing including elevated plus maze, partner preference, and allogrooming assays. Subsequently, brains were analyzed for serotonin and 5-HIAA concentrations in the prefrontal cortex, nucleus accumbens, and hypothalamus using HPLC. 8-OH-DPAT-treated males exhibited significantly increased anxiety-like behavior in the elevated plus maze, spending less time in open arms compared to controls (P < 0.01). These animals also demonstrated impaired partner preference formation and reduced allogrooming duration toward familiar partners. Neurochemical analysis revealed decreased serotonin concentrations in the prefrontal cortex (32% reduction, P < 0.05) and nucleus accumbens (28% reduction, P < 0.05) of treated animals, while 5-HIAA/serotonin ratios were elevated in all brain regions examined. These findings demonstrate that developmental serotonergic manipulation produces persistent alterations in adult social behavior and anxiety, accompanied by region-specific changes in serotonergic neurotransmission. Such developmental programming may contribute to individual variation in social behavioral phenotypes.

title: Effects of infliximab on brain neurochemistry of adults with bipolar depression.
abstract: To explore the relationship between inflammation and neuronal metabolism in bipolar disorder (BD) by evaluating the neurochemical effects of the tumor necrosis factor-α (TNF-α) antagonist infliximab among individuals with bipolar depression METHODS: This is a post-hoc, exploratory analysis from a 12-week, randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled trial with infliximab for adults with bipolar depression. We assessed the effects of infliximab on concentration of metabolites in the prefrontal cortex, using proton-magnetic resonance spectroscopy (1H-MRS), as well as its association with clinical outcomes (i.e. depressive symptom severity and cognitive function). Eighteen participants in the placebo and 15 in the infliximab group were included in this analysis. In the pre-specified primary outcome, there were no significant effects of treatment on prefrontal concentrations of N-acetylaspartate (NAA; p = 0.712). In the secondary analyses, there was a significant treatment by time interaction for glutamate (Glx; p = 0.018), indicating that Glx levels decreased in infliximab-treated patients, relative to placebo. Treatment group significantly moderated the association between changes in Glx levels and changes in a neurocognitive test (i.e. Digit Symbol Substitution Test; p = 0.014), indicating that in infliximab-treated participants reductions in Glx were associated with cognitive improvement. Treatment with infliximab did not affect prefrontal NAA concentration in adults with BD. Exploratory analysis suggested a potential effect of treatment on the glutamate system, a finding that should be confirmed and validated by additional studies.

title: Optimization of LC apparatus for determinations in neurochemistry with an emphasis on microdialysis samples.
abstract: The popularity of in vivo microdialysis sampling of low-molecular-weight substances has focused attention on improved liquid chromatography procedures for such studies. The complexity of the in vivo experiment, coupled with the complexity of LC, has discouraged some workers from developing this capability in their laboratory. Many small-volume dialysate samples are collected over hours of experiments. Proper handling of the animal (anaesthesia, temperature control, probe size and placement, choice of perfusion media) is critical. Simultaneously giving equal attention to sample collection and storage, derivatization, analysis precision, calibration for accuracy, and preventive maintenance of LC pumps, columns, and detectors is difficult for many laboratories. For reliable LC-based assays for microliter volumes of dialysates minimum human interaction with the samples is desirable. Automation strategies and selection of LC components which we have adopted are described for routine analytes such as biogenic amines, amino acids, choline/acetylcholine, and glucose.

title: Neurobiological models of obsessive-compulsive disorder.
abstract: Obsessive-compulsive disorder (OCD) represents a prevalent psychiatric condition characterized by intrusive thoughts and repetitive behaviors, yet its underlying neurobiological mechanisms remain poorly understood. Recent advances in neuroimaging and pharmacological research have implicated dysfunction within specific brain circuits, particularly involving the orbitofrontal cortex, anterior cingulate cortex, and caudate nucleus. The present review synthesizes current neurobiological models of OCD based on clinical, pharmacological, and emerging neuroimaging evidence. We examined data from positron emission tomography studies, cerebrospinal fluid analyses, and treatment response patterns to selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors (SSRIs) in OCD patients. Converging evidence supports a model in which hyperactivity within the orbitofrontal-subcortical circuit, specifically the orbitofrontal cortex-caudate-thalamic loop, underlies the pathophysiology of OCD. Neurochemical investigations reveal dysregulation of serotonergic transmission, with secondary involvement of dopaminergic and GABAergic systems. PET studies consistently demonstrate increased metabolic activity in the orbitofrontal cortex and caudate nucleus that normalizes following successful treatment with SSRIs or behavioral therapy. Additionally, pharmacological challenge studies with meta-chlorophenylpiperazine suggest heightened sensitivity of postsynaptic serotonin receptors in OCD patients. These findings support a neurobiological model wherein serotonergic dysfunction leads to disinhibition of the orbitofrontal-subcortical circuit, resulting in the repetitive thoughts and behaviors characteristic of OCD. This model provides a framework for understanding treatment mechanisms and developing novel therapeutic interventions targeting specific neurotransmitter systems and brain circuits in obsessive-compulsive disorder.

title: Neuropsychiatric aspects of Parkinson disease psychopharmacology: Insights from circuit dynamics.
abstract: Neuropsychiatric manifestations in Parkinson disease (PD) represent a complex therapeutic challenge, with current pharmacological interventions often producing paradoxical effects on motor and non-motor symptoms. Recent advances in circuit-based neuroimaging and electrophysiological techniques have revealed distinct patterns of dysfunction within cortico-basal ganglia-thalamic loops that underlie these psychiatric comorbidities. This review synthesizes emerging evidence linking dopaminergic medication effects to specific neural circuit alterations in PD patients with depression, anxiety, psychosis, and impulse control disorders. We analyzed data from 847 PD patients across 23 neuroimaging studies published between 2015-2018, examining correlations between pharmacological interventions and circuit-specific functional connectivity patterns. Dopamine replacement therapy demonstrated differential effects on limbic versus associative circuits, with levodopa preferentially normalizing motor circuit dysfunction while potentially exacerbating ventral striatal hyperdopaminergia associated with impulse control disorders. Dopamine agonists showed stronger correlations with altered activity in orbitofrontal-striatal circuits (r=0.73, p<0.001) compared to levodopa (r=0.42, p<0.05). Antipsychotic medications, while effectively treating PD psychosis, produced significant reductions in striatal dopamine D2 receptor availability and compensatory increases in prefrontal-parietal network connectivity. Deep brain stimulation of the subthalamic nucleus demonstrated complex effects on emotional processing circuits, with high-frequency stimulation modulating amygdala-prefrontal connectivity patterns. These findings suggest that circuit-specific approaches to PD neuropsychiatric symptoms may optimize therapeutic outcomes while minimizing adverse effects, supporting the development of personalized pharmacological strategies based on individual circuit dysfunction profiles.

title: CSF neurochemicals during tryptophan depletion in individuals with remitted depression and healthy controls.
abstract: Tryptophan depletion has been shown to induce depressive relapse in individuals with remitted major depression, suggesting persistent alterations in serotonergic function. However, the neurochemical mechanisms underlying this vulnerability remain poorly understood. This study examined cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) concentrations of serotonin metabolites and related neurotransmitter systems during acute tryptophan depletion in remitted depressed patients compared to healthy controls. Twenty-four medication-free individuals with remitted major depression (≥6 months symptom-free) and 22 matched healthy controls underwent lumbar puncture before and 7 hours after consuming either a tryptophan-depleting amino acid mixture or balanced placebo mixture in a randomized, double-blind, crossover design. CSF samples were analyzed for 5-hydroxyindoleacetic acid (5-HIAA), homovanillic acid (HVA), and 3-methoxy-4-hydroxyphenylglycol (MHPG). Mood ratings were obtained using the Hamilton Depression Rating Scale. Tryptophan depletion significantly reduced CSF 5-HIAA concentrations in both groups, but the reduction was 40% greater in remitted patients compared to controls (p=0.012). This differential response correlated with transient mood deterioration (r=0.48, p=0.019). CSF HVA and MHPG levels remained unchanged following depletion in both groups. No significant changes occurred during placebo sessions. These findings demonstrate that individuals with remitted depression exhibit heightened sensitivity to tryptophan depletion at the central serotonergic level, providing neurochemical evidence for persistent alterations in serotonin metabolism that may confer vulnerability to depressive relapse. The results support the serotonin hypothesis of depression and suggest that CSF 5-HIAA response to tryptophan depletion may serve as a biomarker for depression susceptibility.
journal: European neuropsychopharmacology : the journal of the European College of Neuropsychopharmacology

title: William L. Woolverton: a case history in unraveling the behavioral pharmacology of stimulants.
abstract: The behavioral pharmacology of psychostimulants has been fundamentally shaped by the systematic research approach pioneered by William L. Woolverton over three decades. This retrospective analysis examines key methodological innovations and findings from Woolverton's laboratory that advanced our understanding of stimulant reinforcement mechanisms and abuse liability assessment. We reviewed seminal publications from 1978-2012 focusing on self-administration paradigms, choice procedures, and pharmacokinetic-pharmacodynamic relationships in nonhuman primate models. Woolverton's laboratory established critical methodological standards for intravenous self-administration studies, demonstrating that cocaine and amphetamine derivatives maintain responding across species through dopaminergic mechanisms. The introduction of concurrent schedules of reinforcement revealed that stimulant choice behavior follows matching law principles, with relative reinforcing efficacy determined by dose, alternative reinforcer magnitude, and pharmacokinetic profiles. Studies with novel stimulants, including modafinil, methylphenidate analogs, and dopamine transporter inhibitors, demonstrated predictive validity between preclinical self-administration data and clinical abuse potential. Investigations of pharmacokinetic variables revealed that rapid onset and short duration of action enhance reinforcing strength, explaining differential abuse liability among chemically similar compounds. The integration of behavioral economics principles further elucidated how environmental factors modulate stimulant reinforcement. Woolverton's systematic approach established the nonhuman primate self-administration model as the gold standard for abuse liability assessment, directly informing regulatory decisions for controlled substance scheduling. This body of work exemplifies how rigorous behavioral pharmacology research can bridge basic neuroscience mechanisms with clinical and public health applications in substance abuse.

title: The application of functional magnetic resonance imaging to neuropharmacology.
abstract: The technique of functional magnetic resonance imaging (fMRI) has the capacity to acquire data with spatial and temporal resolution that far exceeds other currently available methods of non-invasive investigation of brain function. This coupled with its ability for serial studies makes it an attractive prospect for investigating the effects of pharmacological agents in the brain. Recent advances in fMRI have been made in the areas of reward and dependence, brain trauma and injury, psychotropic drugs and pain using small animals. Although the use of fMRI in pharmacological studies is becoming popular, there are various associated complications, such as the possible interference of drugs with the mechanisms that give rise to the pharmacological fMRI signal, and local or global cardiovascular changes that might produce functional responses unrelated to neural activity. Consideration of these concerns, coupled with careful attention to experimental detail and verification procedures, promises to make pharmacological fMRI use a valuable tool for understanding the actions of drugs in the brain.

title: [New directions in GABAergic pharmacology of anxiety and depression].
abstract: GABAergic dysfunction has been increasingly implicated in the pathophysiology of anxiety and depressive disorders, yet traditional benzodiazepine therapies present significant limitations including tolerance, dependence, and cognitive impairment. This review examines emerging therapeutic approaches targeting the GABAergic system through novel mechanisms. We analyzed preclinical and clinical data from 1995-2003 investigating selective GABA receptor subunit modulators, neurosteroid analogues, and GABA transporter inhibitors. Particular attention was given to compounds demonstrating anxiolytic or antidepressant properties without classical benzodiazepine side effects. Selective α2/α3 subunit-preferring compounds showed promising anxiolytic efficacy in animal models while producing minimal sedation and reduced tolerance liability compared to non-selective agents. Synthetic neurosteroids, particularly 3α-hydroxy ring A-reduced pregnane derivatives, demonstrated rapid-onset antidepressant effects in forced swim and learned helplessness paradigms, potentially mediated through positive allosteric modulation of GABAA receptors and HPA axis regulation. GABA transporter-1 selective inhibitors exhibited both anxiolytic and antidepressant properties in multiple preclinical models, suggesting therapeutic potential through enhanced GABAergic tone. Clinical pilot studies with several compounds confirmed preliminary efficacy signals, though larger controlled trials remain necessary. These findings indicate that subunit-selective GABAA receptor modulators, neurosteroid-based therapies, and GABA reuptake inhibitors represent promising new directions for treating anxiety and depression. Such approaches may overcome limitations of current GABAergic medications while exploiting the therapeutic potential of this crucial inhibitory neurotransmitter system.

title: Neuropsychopharmacologic challenge in biological psychiatry.
abstract: Neuropsychopharmacologic challenge paradigms have emerged as valuable research tools for investigating the pathophysiology of psychiatric disorders and elucidating central neurotransmitter function in vivo. These approaches utilize selective pharmacologic agents to probe specific neurotransmitter systems while monitoring behavioral, neuroendocrine, and physiologic responses. We reviewed the current status of challenge strategies in biological psychiatry, focusing on serotonergic, dopaminergic, and noradrenergic probe compounds. Data from 127 published studies conducted between 1985-1993 were systematically analyzed to evaluate the clinical utility and methodologic considerations of major challenge paradigms. Fenfluramine challenge, measuring prolactin and cortisol responses as indices of serotonergic function, demonstrated consistent abnormalities in patients with depression, bulimia nervosa, and personality disorders characterized by impulsivity and aggression. Growth hormone responses to clonidine challenge revealed blunted noradrenergic function in depression and panic disorder. Dopaminergic challenge with apomorphine or L-DOPA showed altered growth hormone responses in schizophrenia and mood disorders. Methodologic factors significantly influencing challenge outcomes included subject selection criteria, drug dosing protocols, sampling intervals, and control for confounding variables such as age, gender, and medication status. These findings support the utility of neuropsychopharmacologic challenges as research tools for characterizing neurotransmitter dysfunction in psychiatric populations. However, standardization of protocols and careful attention to methodologic variables are essential for reliable interpretation of results. Future applications may include treatment prediction and monitoring of therapeutic interventions targeting specific neurotransmitter systems.

title: The psychopharmacological management of RLS in psychiatric conditions: a review of the literature.
abstract: Restless legs syndrome (RLS) is a common neurological disorder characterized by uncomfortable sensations in the legs and an irresistible urge to move them, particularly during periods of rest. The prevalence of RLS is significantly elevated in patients with psychiatric conditions, including depression, anxiety disorders, and attention-deficit/hyperactivity disorder, yet optimal treatment strategies for this comorbid population remain poorly defined. This comprehensive literature review examined current evidence regarding psychopharmacological management of RLS in psychiatric patients. A systematic search of PubMed, CINAHL, and PsycINFO databases was conducted covering publications from 2000-2012, yielding 847 initial articles, of which 42 met inclusion criteria for analysis. Studies consistently demonstrated that traditional psychiatric medications, particularly selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors and tricyclic antidepressants, can exacerbate RLS symptoms in up to 65% of patients. Conversely, dopamine agonists (pramipexole, ropinirole) showed efficacy for both RLS and depressive symptoms, with response rates of 70-85% for RLS improvement and significant mood benefits in 60% of patients. Anticonvulsants, especially gabapentin and pregabalin, demonstrated dual benefits for RLS and anxiety symptoms while showing minimal psychiatric side effects. Iron supplementation proved particularly effective in psychiatric patients with concurrent iron deficiency, present in 40% of the reviewed cases. These findings suggest that integrated treatment approaches considering both psychiatric and RLS symptomatology may optimize patient outcomes. Psychiatric nurses should routinely screen for RLS symptoms and collaborate with prescribers to select medications that address both conditions simultaneously.
journal: Journal of the American Psychiatric Nurses Association

title: Quantitative EEG in human neuropharmacology. Rationale, history, and recent developments.
abstract: CNS availability of compounds with neurological or nonneurological indication, CNS bioequivalence of different compounds/formulations, and CNS drug interactions can be estimated reliably in man by application of quantitative EEG procedures in controlled experimental conditions. Mapping approaches increase the visibility of drug-mediated EEG modifications with limited or selective scalp distribution, thus increasing the control on individual variability and allowing the study of interactions between drugs effect and background EEG pattern, including EEG concomitants of pathological substrata. Correlative structural/functional studies (EEG, CT, NMR, rCBF) are made possible. The pharmaco-EEG potentialities are therefore incremented in the study of compounds of new generation and in nontraditional clinical/therapeutic conditions (cognitive impairments, brain aging, dementia, epilepsies).

title: Psychedelic Psychiatry's Brave New World.
abstract: The resurgence of psychedelic research has transformed our understanding of psychiatric therapeutics, yet the underlying neural mechanisms remain poorly characterized. Here, we investigated the molecular and circuit-level effects of psilocybin, a 5-HT2A receptor agonist, in treatment-resistant depression using a combination of single-cell RNA sequencing, electrophysiology, and functional connectivity analysis in both mouse models and human subjects. Single-cell profiling revealed that psilocybin induces rapid transcriptional changes in cortical pyramidal neurons, particularly upregulating genes associated with synaptic plasticity including BDNF, Arc, and Egr1. Electrophysiological recordings demonstrated increased dendritic spine formation and enhanced long-term potentiation in prefrontal cortex within 24 hours of treatment. Notably, we identified a critical period of heightened neuroplasticity lasting 2-4 weeks post-administration, characterized by elevated mTOR signaling and increased protein synthesis. In a randomized, double-blind clinical trial (n=89), patients receiving psilocybin-assisted therapy showed significant improvements in depression scores (HAM-D reduction of 18.3 ± 3.2 points) compared to placebo controls (4.1 ± 2.8 points, p<0.001). Functional MRI revealed increased connectivity between default mode network regions and decreased amygdala hyperactivity that correlated with therapeutic outcomes. These effects persisted for at least 6 months, suggesting sustained neuroplastic remodeling. Our findings reveal that psychedelics leverage endogenous plasticity mechanisms to enable rapid and durable psychiatric improvements, establishing a neurobiological framework for precision psychedelic medicine.

title: Psychoneuroimmunology meets neuropsychopharmacology: translational implications of the impact of inflammation on behavior.
abstract: The bidirectional communication between the immune system and central nervous system has emerged as a critical pathway linking peripheral inflammation to neuropsychiatric disorders. Growing evidence indicates that pro-inflammatory cytokines can access the brain and alter neurotransmitter metabolism, neuroendocrine function, and synaptic plasticity, ultimately leading to behavioral changes reminiscent of depression and anxiety. This review synthesizes current understanding of inflammatory mechanisms underlying mood disorders and examines therapeutic implications for neuropsychopharmacology. We conducted a comprehensive analysis of preclinical and clinical studies investigating cytokine-induced behavioral alterations and anti-inflammatory treatment strategies. Animal models utilizing lipopolysaccharide, cytokine administration, and chronic stress paradigms consistently demonstrate that peripheral immune activation produces depressive-like behaviors including anhedonia, social withdrawal, and cognitive impairment. Mechanistically, inflammatory mediators disrupt monoaminergic signaling through enhanced tryptophan catabolism via indoleamine 2,3-dioxygenase, reduced tetrahydrobiopterin availability, and increased monoamine transporter expression. Clinical studies reveal elevated inflammatory markers in subsets of patients with major depression, particularly those with treatment-resistant illness. Importantly, anti-inflammatory interventions including cyclooxygenase-2 inhibitors, tumor necrosis factor-α antagonists, and minocycline show therapeutic efficacy in both preclinical models and preliminary clinical trials. These findings suggest that inflammatory biomarkers may identify patient subgroups most likely to respond to anti-inflammatory augmentation strategies. The convergence of psychoneuroimmunology and neuropsychopharmacology offers promising avenues for developing personalized medicine approaches targeting immune-mediated mechanisms in psychiatric disorders, potentially improving outcomes for treatment-refractory patients.
journal: Neuropsychopharmacology : official publication of the American College of Neuropsychopharmacology

title: The rotational model and microdialysis: Significance for dopamine signalling, clinical studies, and beyond.
abstract: The detailed anatomy of the monoamine pathways of the rat by the students of Nils-Ake Hillarp provided the basis for a neurocircuitry targeting pharmacology. Further progress was achieved by the introduction of 6-hydroxydopamine as a tool for performing specific lesions, leading to the first stereotaxic mapping of the monoamine pathways in the rat brain by Urban Ungerstedt at the Karolinska Institutet, Stockholm, Sweden. Unilateral intracerebral injections with 6-hydroxydopamine led to the proposal of 'Rotational Behaviour', as a classical model for screening drugs useful for alleviating Parkinson's disease and other neuropathologies. The direction of the rotational behaviour induced by drugs administrated to lesioned rats reveals their mechanisms of action on dopamine synapses, as demonstrated when rotational behaviour was combined with microdialysis. The model was useful for proposing a role of dopamine receptors in the gating of the flow of information through different efferent pathways of the basal ganglia. It is established now that the coupling of dopamine receptors is regulated by a number of proteins acting as GTPases, the regulators of G-protein signalling (RGS) family. More than 20 RGS proteins have been identified, organised into subfamilies based on structural features and specificity for different G-protein subunits. These protein subfamilies represent alternative pathways gating the flow of information generated in the basal ganglia. Microdialysis has been developed as a general tool for studying tissue and organ chemistry, leading to a truly translational venture as microdialysis is brought into clinical use, monitoring energy metabolism following global or focal ischemia in the neurosurgery and general medicine scenario.

title: The neuropsychopharmacology of attention-deficit/hyperactivity disorder.
abstract: Attention-deficit/hyperactivity disorder (ADHD) affects approximately 3-7% of school-age children and represents one of the most common neurodevelopmental disorders. While stimulant medications remain first-line treatments, the precise neurobiological mechanisms underlying ADHD pathophysiology and therapeutic response remain incompletely understood. This review synthesizes current knowledge of the neuropsychopharmacological basis of ADHD, examining evidence from neuroimaging, neurochemical, and genetic studies. Converging evidence implicates dysregulation of catecholamine neurotransmission, particularly dopamine and norepinephrine systems, in ADHD symptomatology. Neuroimaging studies demonstrate altered activity in prefrontal cortex, anterior cingulate cortex, and striatal regions, areas rich in catecholamine innervation and critical for executive function and attention. Dopamine transporter (DAT) and receptor polymorphisms show modest associations with ADHD risk and treatment response. Stimulant medications, including methylphenidate and amphetamines, enhance synaptic dopamine and norepinephrine availability through distinct mechanisms of action on monoamine transporters. Non-stimulant alternatives such as atomoxetine selectively inhibit norepinephrine reuptake, supporting the role of noradrenergic dysfunction in ADHD. Recent findings suggest additional involvement of cholinergic and GABAergic systems in attention regulation. While current pharmacological treatments effectively reduce core symptoms in 70-80% of patients, individual variability in treatment response remains substantial. Future research integrating pharmacogenomic approaches, advanced neuroimaging techniques, and novel therapeutic targets may yield more personalized and effective interventions for this heterogeneous disorder.

title: Microneurography in rats: a minimally invasive method to record single C-fiber action potentials from peripheral nerves in vivo.
abstract: Microneurography is a method suitable for recording intraneural single or multiunit action potentials in conscious subjects. Microneurography has rarely been applied to animal experiments, where more invasive methods, like the teased fiber recording technique, are widely used. We have tested the feasibility of microneurographic recordings from the peripheral nerves of rats. Tungsten microelectrodes were inserted into the sciatic nerve at mid-thigh level. Single or multiunit action potentials evoked by regular electrical stimulation were recorded, digitized and displayed as a raster plot of latencies. The method allows unambiguous recording and recognition of single C-fiber action potentials from an in vivo preparation, with minimal disruption of the nerve being recorded. Multiple C-fibers can be recorded simultaneously for several hours, and if the animal is allowed to recover, repeated recording sessions can be obtained from the same nerve at the same level over a period of weeks or months. Also, single C units can be functionally identified by their changes in latency to natural stimuli, and insensitive units can be recognized as 'silent' nociceptors or sympathetic efferents by their distinctive profiles of activity-dependent slowing during repetitive electrical stimulation, or by the effect on spontaneous efferent activity of a proximal anesthetic block. Moreover, information about the biophysical properties of C axons can be obtained from their latency recovery cycles. Finally, we show that this preparation is potentially suitable for the study of C-fiber behavior in models of neuropathies and nerve lesions, both under resting conditions and in response to drug administration.

title: Effects of zona incerta lesions on striatal neurochemistry and behavioral asymmetry in 6-hydroxydopamine-lesioned rats.
abstract: The zona incerta (ZI) has emerged as a potential modulator of basal ganglia function, yet its role in Parkinson's disease pathophysiology remains poorly understood. This study investigated whether ZI lesions influence striatal neurochemistry and motor asymmetry in the unilateral 6-hydroxydopamine (6-OHDA) rat model of Parkinson's disease. Adult male Sprague-Dawley rats (n=48) received unilateral 6-OHDA lesions of the medial forebrain bundle, followed by either sham surgery or electrolytic ZI lesions ipsilateral to the dopaminergic lesion. Behavioral asymmetry was assessed using amphetamine-induced rotation testing at 2, 4, and 6 weeks post-lesion. Striatal tissue was analyzed for dopamine, DOPAC, HVA, and serotonin content using high-performance liquid chromatography. Immunohistochemistry was performed to quantify tyrosine hydroxylase-positive neurons in the substantia nigra. ZI lesions significantly reduced amphetamine-induced rotational behavior compared to 6-OHDA-only controls (327 ± 45 vs. 612 ± 78 rotations/hour at 6 weeks, p<0.001). Striatal dopamine depletion was similar between groups (>90% reduction), but ZI-lesioned animals showed increased striatal serotonin levels (142% of control vs. 98% in 6-OHDA-only group, p<0.05) and elevated serotonin turnover ratios. No differences in nigral cell loss were observed between groups. These findings suggest that ZI lesions ameliorate parkinsonian motor asymmetry through mechanisms independent of dopamine restoration, potentially involving serotonergic compensation. The ZI may represent a novel therapeutic target for modulating basal ganglia output in movement disorders.

title: Photochemical and pharmacological evaluation of 7-nitroindolinyl-and 4-methoxy-7-nitroindolinyl-amino acids as novel, fast caged neurotransmitters.
abstract: Caged neurotransmitters have emerged as powerful tools for investigating synaptic transmission and neural circuit function with high temporal and spatial resolution. However, existing caged compounds often suffer from slow photolysis kinetics and limited tissue penetration, restricting their utility in physiological studies. We synthesized and characterized two novel classes of caged amino acid neurotransmitters based on 7-nitroindolinyl and 4-methoxy-7-nitroindolinyl protecting groups conjugated to glutamate, GABA, and glycine. Photochemical properties were assessed using flash photolysis with UV laser pulses (355 nm), while biological activity was evaluated in acute hippocampal slices from adult rats using whole-cell patch-clamp recordings from CA1 pyramidal neurons. The 7-nitroindolinyl-caged compounds exhibited photolysis time constants of 45-65 μs, representing a 3-4 fold improvement over conventional o-nitrobenzyl derivatives. The 4-methoxy-7-nitroindolinyl analogs demonstrated even faster kinetics (τ = 25-40 μs) with enhanced two-photon cross-sections at 740 nm. Caged glutamate and GABA showed minimal biological activity prior to photolysis (<2% of uncaged response) and complete restoration of native receptor responses following UV illumination. Quantum yields ranged from 0.15-0.28, comparable to existing caged compounds. The improved photolysis kinetics and maintained biological fidelity of these 7-nitroindolinyl-based caged neurotransmitters make them promising tools for high-resolution studies of fast synaptic transmission and enable more precise temporal control in neuropharmacological investigations of receptor function and neural network dynamics.

title: Cholinergic effects on fear conditioning I: the degraded contingency effect is disrupted by atropine but reinstated by physostigmine.
abstract: The cholinergic system has been shown to modulate contextual fear conditioning. However, with the exception of trace conditioning studies, most of the available data have focussed on independent context, i.e., context that do not compete with the conditioned stimulus to control for the conditioned response (interactive context). In the present series of experiments, the effects of the muscarinic antagonist, atropine, were assessed when contextual fear memory interacts with cued fear memory to regulate conditioned response, using a Pavlovian degraded contingency preparation in rats. This preparation not only afforded an insight into simple Pavlovian associations but also enabled us to test for the processes of competition that made use of these associations to make an appropriate response to a stimulus [degraded contingency effect (DCE)]. In experiment 1, three doses of atropine [2.5, 5.0, and 10.0 mg/kg, intraperitoneally (i.p.)] were evaluated on male Sprague-Dawley rats. In experiment 2, physostigmine (0.037-0.3 mg/kg, i.p.) was injected after the administration of 5 mg/kg of atropine. Experiment 1A and its partial replication (experiment 1B) showed that at asymptotic level of training, atropine did not alter contextual and cued fear memories when the subjects were directly tested for them, whereas it suppressed the DCE for a 5 mg/kg dose. Indeed, atropine-induced suppression of the DCE was found to be an inverted U-shaped dose-response curve. Experiment 2 showed that physostigmine caused a dose-dependent reversal of the atropine-induced alleviation of the DCE, without altering the expression of simple cued and contextual fear memories. These results evidence at asymptotic level of training a cholinergic modulation of the processing of interactive context, but not of independent ones. They are discussed in the framework of the mechanisms that are involved in both types of contextual processing.

title: Resting state functional connectivity: its physiological basis and application in neuropharmacology.
abstract: Brain structures do not work in isolation; they work in concert to produce sensory perception, motivation and behavior. Systems-level network activity can be investigated by resting state magnetic resonance imaging (rsMRI), an emerging neuroimaging technique that assesses the synchrony of the brain's ongoing spontaneous activity. Converging evidence reveals that rsMRI is able to consistently identify distinct spatiotemporal patterns of large-scale brain networks. Dysregulation within and between these networks has been implicated in a number of neurodegenerative and neuropsychiatric disorders, including Alzheimer's disease and drug addiction. Despite wide application of this approach in systems neuroscience, the physiological basis of these fluctuations remains incompletely understood. Here we review physiological studies in electrical, metabolic and hemodynamic fluctuations that are most pertinent to the rsMRI signal. We also review recent applications to neuropharmacology - specifically drug effects on resting state fluctuations. We speculate that the mechanisms governing spontaneous fluctuations in regional oxygenation availability likely give rise to the observed rsMRI signal. We conclude by identifying several open questions surrounding this technique. This article is part of the Special Issue Section entitled 'Neuroimaging in Neuropharmacology'.

title: Discovery of novel tetrahydro-pyrazolo [4,3-c] pyridines for the treatment of neuropathic pain: synthesis and neuropharmacology.
abstract: We disclose the discovery of a novel series of tetrahydropyrido-pyrazoles that are potent inhibitors of tumour necrosis factor-alpha (TNF-α), nitric oxide and cannabinoid receptor subtype 1 (CB₁). We report herein the synthesis and neuropharmacological screening results of the titled compounds in two acute pain and two neuropathic pain models in rodents. Particularly the analogue N-(4-bromophenyl)-3-tert-butyl-5-ethyl-4,5,6,7-tetrahydro-1H-pyrazolo[4,3-c]pyridine-1-carboxamide (8a) exhibited pronounced acute antinociceptive efficacy, also being effective in chronic constriction injury (ED₅₀ = 23.8 mg/kg) and partial sciatic nerve injury (ED₅₀ = 29.0 mg/kg) models with CB₁ receptor activity (IC₅₀ = 49.6 nM) and inhibitory effect on TNF-α (86.4% inhibition at 100 mg/kg). These results suggest the importance of the development of this lead as multi-targeted treatment strategy for neuropathic pain.

title: Noradrenergic dysfunction and the psychopharmacology of posttraumatic stress disorder.
abstract: The catecholamine norepinephrine is a critical effector of the mammalian stress response and has been implicated in the pathophysiology of posttraumatic stress disorder (PTSD)-a syndrome intrinsically related to the experience of extraordinary stress.
