Symptom-linked hypernoradrenergic derangements have been observed in PTSD and several studies have examined the potential therapeutic effects of agents that dampen the centrally hyperactive noradrenergic state. These agents include compounds that decrease norepinephrine release (e.g. centrally acting alpha(2) agonists such as clonidine) and those which block post-synaptic norepinephrine receptors (e.g. centrally acting alpha(1) or beta receptor antagonists such as prazosin or propranolol). In this article, we review studies of central noreadrenergic hyperactivity under both basal and challenge conditions and explore the evidence for these derangements as potential psychopharmacologic targets in patients with PTSD. Given the significant involvement of CNS norepinephrine hyperactivity in PTSD, and its link to intrusive and hyperarousal symptoms, it is not surprising that interventions directed at this system have therapeutic potential in PTSD. The utility of these anti-adrenergics in the clinical treatment of PTSD remains to be determined, though it is possible that they may prove to have primary roles in a disorder that is only modestly responsive to antidepressant treatment.

title: Quantitative in vitro phosphor imaging using [3H] and [18F] radioligands: the effects of chronic desipramine treatment on serotonin 5-HT2 receptors.
abstract: Traditionally, autoradiography of neuroreceptors is performed in vitro using tritiated ligands and low sensitivity X-ray film, requiring long exposure times. In vivo imaging of neuroreceptors using positron emission tomography (PET) suffers poor spatial resolution, but in vitro PET autoradiography is difficult with film due to the short half-life of the isotopes. Storage phosphor screens provide an extremely sensitive alternative to film. To demonstrate and validate quantitative in vitro phosphor imaging with PET and tritiated ligands, we treated rats chronically with the antidepressant desipramine, which results in decreased binding to serotonin 5-HT(2) receptors. Serotonin 5-HT(2) binding decreased significantly in all cortical regions examined as measured by both [(3)H]ketanserin and [(18)F]setoperone. The data from the two radioligands were not significantly different, and the distribution of the receptors was in agreement with previous reports. We also present data on the reusability of tritium-sensitive phosphor screens, and show that the use of simple corrections allows receptor binding data with PET ligands to be compared across different days. The results indicate that phosphor imaging is a valid, fast, and quantifiable technique for measuring neuroreceptor regulation, and that it provides an excellent tool to corroborate in vivo PET data in vitro at higher resolution.

title: Neuropharmacology of memory consolidation and reconsolidation: Insights on central cholinergic mechanisms.
abstract: Memory consolidation and reconsolidation represent distinct neurobiological processes that transform labile memories into stable engrams and render retrieved memories susceptible to modification, respectively. While considerable evidence implicates cholinergic neurotransmission in memory formation, the specific roles of central cholinergic mechanisms in consolidation versus reconsolidation remain poorly understood. Here we investigated the differential contributions of muscarinic and nicotinic acetylcholine receptors to these memory processes using auditory fear conditioning in adult male Sprague-Dawley rats. Animals received bilateral infusions of the muscarinic antagonist scopolamine (10 μg/side), the nicotinic antagonist mecamylamine (5 μg/side), or vehicle into the basolateral amygdala either immediately post-training (consolidation) or post-retrieval (reconsolidation). Long-term memory was assessed 24 hours later through freezing behavior measurements. Scopolamine administration impaired both consolidation and reconsolidation, reducing freezing responses by 65% and 58%, respectively, compared to vehicle controls (p<0.001). Conversely, mecamylamine selectively disrupted consolidation (47% reduction, p<0.01) while leaving reconsolidation intact. Immunohistochemical analysis revealed increased c-Fos expression in cholinergic neurons of the medial septal complex following both consolidation and reconsolidation phases. These findings demonstrate that muscarinic receptors are critically involved in both memory consolidation and reconsolidation, whereas nicotinic receptors appear specifically required for initial memory stabilization. Our results provide novel insights into the neurochemical basis of memory processing and suggest that cholinergic modulation of memory involves receptor subtype-specific mechanisms that differentially regulate distinct phases of memory formation and updating.

title: The discovery of chemical neurotransmitters.
abstract: The elucidation of chemical neurotransmission represents one of the most fundamental paradigm shifts in neuroscience, transitioning from purely electrical models of neural communication to our current understanding of synaptic transmission. This historical review examines the key experimental discoveries and methodological advances that established the chemical basis of interneuronal signaling from the early 20th century through the molecular era. We analyzed seminal studies beginning with Loewi's 1921 frog heart experiments demonstrating "Vagusstoff," through Dale's acetylcholine work, Eccles' intracellular recordings at the neuromuscular junction, and subsequent identification of major neurotransmitter systems including catecholamines, amino acids, and neuropeptides. Critical methodological developments included bioassay techniques, electron microscopy revealing synaptic vesicles, subcellular fractionation methods, and receptor binding assays. The convergence of evidence from these approaches definitively established that neurons communicate primarily through calcium-dependent vesicular release of specific chemical messengers that bind to postsynaptic receptors. Key findings included the quantal nature of transmitter release, the existence of distinct receptor subtypes, and the complex machinery governing synaptic vesicle fusion and recycling. These discoveries fundamentally transformed our understanding of neural computation, learning and memory, and neurological disease mechanisms. The identification of chemical neurotransmitters provided the conceptual framework for modern psychopharmacology and continues to drive therapeutic drug development. This historical perspective illuminates how methodological innovation and cross-disciplinary collaboration established the chemical foundations of nervous system function.

title: Early polyneuropharmacologic intervention in brain injury agitation.
abstract: To evaluate clinical outcomes observed in the treatment of posttraumatic brain injury agitation with multiple medications. Longitudinal evaluation of effect of treatment intervention on patients admitted to a level 1 trauma center with traumatic brain injury. Eleven adult patients referred for physiatry consultation to manage posttraumatic brain injury agitation were treated with a combination of medications and other agents (antipsychotics and sedatives) were discontinued. The Rancho Los Amigos scale was rated on initial consultation and follow-up, ranging from 1 to 3 wk. All 11 patients demonstrated an average improvement of three Rancho Los Amigos levels within an average treatment duration of 10 days. Simultaneously, 9 of 11 patients were discontinued from treatment and prescribed antipsychotic and sedative agents. The findings suggest that administration of multiple neuropharmacologic agents used early in the treatment of posttraumatic brain injury agitation may be an effective therapeutic intervention in which behavioral and, possibly, cognitive benefits occur. These results were noted as patients were discontinued from other agents more commonly employed in the acute care setting for the treatment of posttraumatic brain injury agitation. Further research is needed to determine the most effective medication combinations and the best time to initiate treatment.
journal: American journal of physical medicine & rehabilitation

title: Neurochemical profiles of some novel psychoactive substances.
abstract: Fourteen substances from the class of drugs sometimes known as "legal highs" were screened against a battery of human receptors in binding assays, and their potencies as inhibitors of monoamine uptake determined in functional in vitro assays. Thirteen of the test substances acted as inhibitors of monoamine uptake at submicromolar concentrations, including 9 potent inhibitors of the dopamine transporter (DAT), 12 potent inhibitors of the norepinephrine transporter (NET) and 4 potent inhibitors of the serotonin transporter (SERT). Seven compounds acted as submicromolar inhibitors of both DAT and NET, and three substances 1-(benzofuran-5-yl)propan-2-amine (5-APB), 1-naphthalen-2-yl-2-pyrrolidin-1-ylpentan-1-one hydrochloride ("naphyrone") and 1-naphthalen-1-yl-2-pyrrolidin-1-ylpentan-1-one hydrochloride ("1-naphyrone") were submicromolar inhibitors of all three monoamine transporters. There was a lack of correlation between results of functional uptake experiments and in vitro binding assays for the monoamine transporters. There was also no correlation between the human behavioral effects of the substances and the results of bindings assays for a range of receptor targets, although 1-(benzofuran-5-yl)propan-2-amine (5-APB), 1-(benzofuran-6-yl)propan-2-amine hydrochloride (6-APB) and 5-iodo-2,3-dihydro-1H-inden-2-amine hydrochloride (5-iodo-aminoindane) exhibited <100 nM affinities for 5HT(2B) and α(2C) receptors. Functional assays revealed that 5-APB and 6-APB were potent full agonists at 5HT(2B) receptors.

title: Quantitative assessment of the microstructure of rat behavior: I, f(d), the extension of the scaling hypothesis.
abstract: Recent advances in behavioral pharmacology have emphasized the importance of analyzing fine-grained temporal patterns of behavior rather than relying solely on traditional rate measures. The scaling hypothesis, originally proposed for simple operant responses, suggests that drug effects on behavior follow predictable mathematical relationships across different time scales. This study extends this framework by introducing f(d), a novel quantitative measure that characterizes the microstructure of behavioral sequences as a function of temporal resolution (d). Male Sprague-Dawley rats (n=48) were trained on a free-operant lever pressing schedule and subsequently administered acute doses of amphetamine (0.5, 1.0, 2.0 mg/kg, i.p.), chlorpromazine (1.0, 2.0, 4.0 mg/kg, i.p.), or saline vehicle. Behavioral sessions were recorded at 10-ms resolution and analyzed using computer-automated detection of response clusters, inter-response times, and pause durations. The f(d) function was calculated by measuring behavioral complexity across logarithmically spaced time windows from 0.01 to 100 seconds. Results demonstrated that f(d) follows a power-law relationship under control conditions, with amphetamine producing systematic shifts in the scaling exponent (increased from 0.73±0.08 to 1.24±0.12 at 2.0 mg/kg), while chlorpromazine decreased the exponent (0.51±0.09 at 4.0 mg/kg). These findings suggest that psychoactive drugs produce characteristic "fingerprints" in behavioral microstructure that are not apparent in conventional analyses. The f(d) measure provides a sensitive tool for detecting drug effects and may offer insights into the temporal organization of dopaminergic and other neurotransmitter systems underlying motivated behavior.

title: Time-dependent behavioral, neurochemical, and metabolic dysregulation in female C57BL/6 mice caused by chronic high-fat diet intake.
abstract: Obesity and metabolic dysfunction are increasingly recognized as risk factors for neuropsychiatric disorders, yet the temporal relationship between diet-induced metabolic changes and behavioral alterations remains poorly understood, particularly in females. This study investigated time-dependent effects of chronic high-fat diet (HFD) consumption on behavioral, neurochemical, and metabolic parameters in female C57BL/6 mice. Eight-week-old female mice were fed either standard chow (10% fat) or HFD (60% fat) for 2, 8, or 16 weeks (n=12-15/group). Behavioral assessments included open field test, elevated plus maze, forced swim test, and novel object recognition. Neurochemical analyses measured monoamine levels in prefrontal cortex, hippocampus, and striatum using HPLC. Metabolic parameters included body weight, glucose tolerance, insulin sensitivity, and serum leptin levels. HFD mice showed progressive weight gain and glucose intolerance by 2 weeks, with insulin resistance developing by 8 weeks. Anxiety-like behavior emerged at 8 weeks, accompanied by reduced dopamine and serotonin levels in prefrontal cortex and hippocampus. Depressive-like behavior and cognitive impairment were evident only after 16 weeks of HFD exposure, coinciding with further reductions in hippocampal BDNF expression and elevated neuroinflammatory markers. Serum leptin levels were elevated throughout all time points but showed diminished central leptin sensitivity after 8 weeks. These findings demonstrate that chronic HFD consumption in female mice produces sequential metabolic dysfunction, anxiety, and finally depression with cognitive deficits, suggesting distinct temporal windows for intervention strategies targeting diet-induced neuropsychiatric complications.

title: The psychopharmacology of violence: making sensible decisions.
abstract: Violent behavior associated with mental disorders is a common reason for admission to a psychiatric inpatient unit. Once hospitalized, patients may continue to be intermittently agitated and have persistent aggressive behaviors, preventing their discharge back into the community. Managing agitation quickly with effective pharmacological agents can avoid further escalation to aggression and violence. In the acute setting, this usually involves the parenteral use of antipsychotics, with or without benzodiazepines. Within the past decade, short-acting intramuscular formulations of second-generation antipsychotics have become available and provide a means to induce calm with a substantially lower risk of acute dystonia or akathisia compared with haloperidol. New alternative formulations that avoid injections include inhalation and sublingual administration. Longer-term management of persistent aggressive behavior by reducing the frequency and intensity of future episodes of agitation is more complex. In contrast to agitation associated with schizophrenia or bipolar mania, no agents have yet been approved by regulatory agencies for the treatment of persistent aggressive behavior. The strongest evidence supports the use of clozapine as an antihostility agent, followed by olanzapine. Adjunctive strategies with anticonvulsants and beta-adrenergic agents may also be worthwhile to consider.

title: Exploring the relationship between serotonin and brain-derived neurotrophic factor: analysis of BDNF protein and extraneuronal 5-HT in mice with reduced serotonin transporter or BDNF expression.
abstract: Serotonin (5-HT) has been proposed to promote neuronal plasticity during the treatment of mood and anxiety disorders and following neurodegenerative insult by altering the expression of critical genes including brain-derived neurotrophic factor (BDNF). In this study, mice with constitutive reductions in the serotonin transporter (SERT) or BDNF were investigated to further assess the functional relationship between serotonin neurotransmission and BDNF expression. Using a modified extraction procedure and a commercial enzyme-linked immunosorbant assay, 50% decreases in BDNF protein in hippocampus, frontal cortex and brain stem were confirmed in 4-month-old mice lacking one copy of the BDNF gene (BDNF(+/-)). By contrast, 4-month-old male and female mice with partial (SERT(+/-)) or complete (SERT(-/-)) reductions in SERT expression showed no differences in BDNF protein levels compared to SERT(+/+) mice, although male SERT knockout mice of all genotypes had higher BDNF levels in hippocampus, frontal cortex, and brain stem than female animals. Microdialysis also was performed in BDNF(+/-) mice. In addition to other phenotypic aspects suggestive of altered serotonin neurotransmission, BDNF(+/-) mice show accelerated age-related degeneration of 5-HT forebrain innervation. Nevertheless, extracellular 5-HT levels determined by zero net flux microdialysis were similar between BDNF(+/+) and BDNF(+/-) mice in striatum and frontal cortex at 8-12 months of age. These data illustrate that a 50% decrease in BDNF does not appear to be sufficient to cause measurable changes in basal extracellular 5-HT concentrations and, furthermore, that constitutive reductions in SERT expression are not associated with altered BDNF protein levels at the ages and in the brain regions examined in this study.

title: Dorsal raphe vs. median raphe serotonergic antagonism. Anatomical, physiological, behavioral, neuroendocrinological, neuropharmacological and clinical evidences: relevance for neuropharmacological therapy.
abstract: The serotonergic system comprises anatomically and functionally distinct dorsal raphe (DR) and median raphe (MR) nuclei that exhibit differential responses to pharmacological interventions and may contribute differently to psychiatric disorders. This study examined the contrasting effects of selective serotonergic modulation in DR versus MR regions using combined anatomical, electrophysiological, behavioral, and neuroendocrine approaches. Male Sprague-Dawley rats received microinjections of the 5-HT1A receptor antagonist WAY-100635 (0.1-1.0 μg) into either DR or MR nuclei, followed by assessment of anxiety-related behaviors in elevated plus maze and open field tests, measurement of plasma corticosterone and ACTH levels, and single-unit recordings from hippocampal CA1 pyramidal neurons. Immunohistochemical analysis revealed distinct 5-HT1A receptor distribution patterns between DR and MR regions. DR antagonism produced anxiogenic effects, elevated stress hormone levels, and increased hippocampal theta rhythm power, while MR antagonism resulted in anxiolytic responses, reduced HPA axis activation, and decreased theta activity. Electrophysiological recordings demonstrated that DR manipulation primarily affected dorsal hippocampal neuronal firing, whereas MR modulation influenced ventral hippocampal activity. These findings provide compelling evidence for functional antagonism between DR and MR serotonergic systems, with DR primarily mediating stress responses and anxiety, while MR appears to regulate emotional processing and behavioral inhibition. The differential therapeutic targets identified suggest that anatomically-selective serotonergic interventions may offer improved treatment strategies for anxiety disorders and depression, potentially explaining variable clinical responses to current serotonergic medications.

title: Psychopharmacological possibilities in the acute disaster setting.
abstract: This article focuses on possible psychopharmacological interventions in the immediate post disaster setting. As there is little evidence for the efficacy or effectiveness of such interventions-given the difficulty in performing randomized, double-blind, placebo controlled studies with these populations-the article will delineate the neurobiological basis for pathological sequelae and theoretical drug interventions targeting putative disease mechanisms.
journal: The Psychiatric clinics of North America

title: Victor P. Whittaker (1919-2016).
abstract: This Obituary honors Victor P. Whittaker, one of the pioneers in the field of neurochemistry. Victor Whittaker died on 5th July 2016 aged 97 in Cambridge (UK) after a short illness. Victor is best known for his landmark advances in the subcellular fractionation of brain tissue which led to the isolation of synaptosomes and subsequently synaptic vesicles at the beginning of the 1960s and for the cellular and molecular analysis of the cholinergic synapse.

title: Pathological laughing and crying : epidemiology, pathophysiology and treatment.
abstract: Pathological laughing and crying (PLC) is characterized by frequent, brief, intense paroxysms of uncontrollable crying and/or laughing due to a neurological disorder. When sufficiently frequent and severe, PLC may interfere with the performance of activities of daily living, interpersonal functioning, or both, and is a source of distress for affected patients and their families. PLC is also often misunderstood by patients and their families, and is under-recognized by the clinicians caring for patients with this disorder. However, this syndrome is easily recognized when understood properly and is highly responsive to treatment with a variety of pharmacological agents. This review aims to facilitate the diagnosis and treatment of patients with PLC, and begins by providing definitions of mood and affect that will help clinicians distinguish between mood disorders, such as major depression and mania, and disorders of affect, such as PLC. In addition, the various terms used to describe this syndrome are reviewed and a recommendation for the use of the term PLC is made. The core clinical features of PLC are also presented and the epidemiology of this syndrome is reviewed. A discussion of the pathophysiology of PLC, including the neuroanatomic and neurochemical bases, is provided. Finally, the evaluation and treatment of patients with PLC is described. Based on the pathophysiology of PLC and on a detailed review of published treatment studies, SSRIs are recommended as first-line pharmacotherapy for this disorder. When SSRIs are ineffective or poorly tolerated, other treatment options, including TCAs, noradrenergic reuptake inhibitors, novel antidepressants, dopaminergic agents and uncompetitive NMDA receptor antagonists may be useful second-line treatments.

title: Recent advances in neuropharmacology of cutaneous nociceptors.
abstract: Cutaneous nociceptors are peripheral receptive endings of primary sensory neurons activated by noxious stimuli. Nociceptors detect and signal the presence of tissue-damaging stimuli or the existence of tissue damage. In this short review, we will focus on the molecular mechanism of maintenance, activation, inhibition and sensitization in cutaneous nociceptors. Neurotrophic factors are essential to the development of nociceptors during embryogenesis. Recent evidences have indicated that nociceptors in the adult are maintained by either nerve growth factor (NGF) or glial cell line-derived neurotrophic factor (GDNF). A selective activator of nociceptors is capsaicin, natural product of capsicum peppers. Recently, the receptor for capsaicin (the vanilloid receptor 1: VR1) has been cloned, identified and characterized. VR1 seems to play an important role in the activation and sensitization of nociceptors. In contrast, peripheral endogenous cannabinoids such as anandamide are novel candidates for mediators that inhibit the excitation of nociceptors. Intracellular messengers and the mechanisms of signal transduction in nociceptors have also been studied. Our recent findings provide evidences demonstrate that an activation of both cAMP- and cGMP-second messenger systems is required to induce the sensitization of nociceptors. Such emerging evidences reviewed here would make a significant contribution to further understanding of the molecular mechanism of nociceptors.

title: Fast Hadamard transform capillary electrophoresis for on-line, time-resolved chemical monitoring.
abstract: The rapid detection and quantification of neurotransmitters and their metabolites in biological systems remains a critical challenge in neuropharmacology research. Traditional analytical methods often lack the temporal resolution necessary to monitor dynamic neurochemical processes occurring on millisecond to second timescales. We developed a novel fast Hadamard transform capillary electrophoresis (FHT-CE) method for real-time chemical monitoring with enhanced signal-to-noise ratio and temporal resolution. The system employs multiplexed sample injection patterns based on Hadamard encoding matrices, followed by mathematical deconvolution to reconstruct individual electropherograms. Validation studies were performed using standard solutions of dopamine, serotonin, norepinephrine, and their primary metabolites (DOPAC, 5-HIAA, MHPG) in artificial cerebrospinal fluid. The FHT-CE method demonstrated a 4-fold improvement in signal-to-noise ratio compared to conventional CE, with detection limits of 50 nM for dopamine and 75 nM for serotonin. Temporal resolution was enhanced to 2.3 seconds per analysis cycle, representing a 10-fold improvement over standard methods. Application to microdialysis samples from rat striatum during pharmacological stimulation revealed rapid fluctuations in dopamine levels (15-340 nM) with concurrent changes in DOPAC concentrations. The method successfully monitored neurotransmitter dynamics following acute amphetamine administration, detecting peak dopamine release within 45 seconds of drug delivery. This FHT-CE approach provides a powerful analytical tool for investigating rapid neurochemical processes and evaluating pharmacokinetic profiles of neuroactive compounds in real-time biological systems.

title: Neuropharmacology of drugs and alcohol in mother and fetus.
abstract: Epidemiological evidence suggests that an adverse prenatal environment can have profound long-term health consequences throughout postnatal life. This chapter discusses the underlying mechanisms implicated in the consumption of mood-altering recreational drugs and teratogenicity in the fetus. The way metabolic parameters in pregnancy influence the pharmacokinetic characteristics of drugs and alcohol and the developmental stage of neurotoxicity are reviewed. The general underlying mechanisms that link multifaceted interactions between drug characteristics, gene polymorphisms, dietary deficiencies, changed endocrine indices and fetal programming are outlined, with specific examples throughout the text. As developmental injury is of significant social concern, the final section questions whether society provides adequate support for making appropriate and informed lifestyle choices to alleviate preventable transgenerational harm.

title: Utilizing resources of neuropsychopharmacology to address the opioid overdose crisis.
abstract: North America is facing a severe public health crisis in the form of excessive numbers of deaths due to overdose from self-administration of opioids by individuals who are dependent on these substances. There are many factors that must be addressed in order to gain control over this tragedy. Of particular relevance to neuropsychopharmacology is the fact that the problem is due in part to misuse of pharmaceuticals and especially the illicit production of the powerful synthetic opioids, fentanyl, and carfentanil. The development and adoption of appropriate pharmacotherapies are of critical importance. We discuss specific options to deal effectively with both withdrawal from opioid dependence and substitution of clinically approved drugs in place of illicit substances. Hopefully, this crisis will reinvigorate both basic and clinical neuropsychopharmacological research leading to the develop new and more effective options for dealing with the many and varied elements of the current opioid crisis as described in the present commentary.

title: Inside-out neuropharmacology of nicotinic drugs.
abstract: Traditional neuropharmacological approaches to nicotinic acetylcholine receptors (nAChRs) have focused on extracellular drug binding sites, yet emerging evidence suggests that intracellular drug interactions may play crucial roles in receptor modulation. We investigated the inside-out neuropharmacology of nicotinic drugs using patch-clamp electrophysiology on inside-out membrane patches from rat hippocampal neurons and heterologous expression systems containing α4β2 and α7 nAChR subtypes. Bath application of nicotine (1-100 μM) to the intracellular membrane surface produced concentration-dependent potentiation of acetylcholine-evoked currents in α4β2 receptors (EC50 = 12.4 ± 2.1 μM) but had minimal effects on α7 receptors. Conversely, intracellular mecamylamine (10-300 μM) inhibited both receptor subtypes with greater potency than extracellular application (IC50 values: 45.2 ± 8.7 μM vs 127.3 ± 15.4 μM for α4β2). Site-directed mutagenesis of putative intracellular binding residues in the M3-M4 cytoplasmic loop significantly reduced intracellular drug sensitivity. Molecular docking simulations identified distinct intracellular binding pockets with nanomolar to micromolar affinities for tested compounds. Functional assays demonstrated that intracellular drug effects were independent of extracellular competitive antagonism and persisted in the presence of saturating extracellular nicotine concentrations. These findings reveal a previously uncharacterized dimension of nicotinic receptor pharmacology, suggesting that drug penetration across cellular membranes and subsequent intracellular receptor interactions contribute significantly to overall pharmacological profiles. Understanding inside-out nicotinic pharmacology may inform the development of more selective therapeutic agents for neurological disorders involving cholinergic dysfunction.

title: Quantitative assessment of the microstructure of rat behavior: I, f(d), the extension of the scaling hypothesis.
abstract: Previous studies demonstrated that drug effects on the movement sequences of rats in unconditioned motor activity paradigms can be quantified by scaling measures that describe the average relationship between a variable of interest and an experimental parameter. However, rats engage in a wide variety of geometrically distinct movements that can be influenced differentially by drugs. In this investigation, the extended scaling approach is presented to capture quantitatively the relative contributions of geometrically distinct movement sequences to the overall path structure. The calculation of the spectrum of local spatial scaling exponents, f(d), is based on ensemble methods used in statistical physics. Results of the f(d) analysis confirm that the amount of motor activity is not correlated with the geometrical structure of movement sequences. Changes in the average spatial scaling exponent, d, correspond to shifting the entire f(d) function, and indicate overall changes in path structure. With the extended scaling approach, straight movement sequences are assessed independently from highly circumscribed movements. Thus, the f(d) function identifies drug effects on particular ranges of movement sequences as defined by the geometrical structure of movements. More generally, the f(d) function quantifies the relationship between microscopically recorded variables, in this paradigm consecutive (x,y) locations, and the macroscopic behavioral patterns that constitute the animal's response topography.

title: Noradrenergic dysfunction and the psychopharmacology of posttraumatic stress disorder.
abstract: Posttraumatic stress disorder (PTSD) is characterized by dysregulation of multiple neurotransmitter systems, with growing evidence implicating noradrenergic hyperactivity in core symptom manifestation. While selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors remain first-line pharmacotherapy, their limited efficacy suggests involvement of additional neurochemical pathways. This study examined noradrenergic system alterations in PTSD and evaluated targeted pharmacological interventions. We conducted a comprehensive review of preclinical and clinical literature from 1990-2007, analyzing noradrenergic biomarkers, neuroimaging findings, and treatment response data. Additionally, we performed a 12-week open-label trial of prazosin, an α1-adrenergic antagonist, in 28 veterans with combat-related PTSD experiencing prominent hyperarousal symptoms and sleep disturbances. Literature analysis revealed consistent evidence of elevated norepinephrine levels in cerebrospinal fluid and plasma of PTSD patients, along with altered α2-adrenergic receptor sensitivity and enhanced noradrenergic responsivity to trauma-related stimuli. Neuroimaging studies demonstrated hyperactivation in noradrenergic projection areas including the amygdala and prefrontal cortex. In our clinical trial, prazosin treatment (mean dose 6.8±2.4 mg/day) resulted in significant improvements in Clinician-Administered PTSD Scale scores (baseline 78.3±12.1 vs. endpoint 52.7±15.8, p<0.001), with particularly robust effects on sleep quality and nightmare frequency. Treatment was well-tolerated with minimal adverse effects. These findings support a central role for noradrenergic dysfunction in PTSD pathophysiology and suggest that α1-adrenergic antagonism represents a promising therapeutic strategy, particularly for hyperarousal symptoms resistant to conventional serotonergic interventions.

title: Partial response, nonresponse, and relapse with selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors in major depression: a survey of current "next-step" practices.
abstract: Despite the widespread use of selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors (SSRIs) as first-line treatment for major depressive disorder, substantial numbers of patients experience partial response, nonresponse, or relapse during treatment. However, limited data exist regarding clinicians' approaches to these treatment challenges in routine clinical practice. We conducted a nationwide survey of 847 psychiatrists and primary care physicians to examine current "next-step" strategies when patients fail to achieve or maintain adequate response to SSRI therapy. Participants completed a structured questionnaire detailing their management approaches for three clinical scenarios: partial response after 8-12 weeks of adequate SSRI dosing, complete nonresponse after 8-12 weeks, and relapse during maintenance therapy. Response rate was 73% (n=618). For partial responders, 54% of clinicians reported dose optimization as their preferred strategy, followed by augmentation with bupropion (23%) or lithium (18%). In cases of nonresponse, 67% favored switching to another SSRI, while 28% preferred switching to a non-SSRI antidepressant. For relapse scenarios, 71% increased the SSRI dose, 19% added augmentation therapy, and 10% switched medications. Significant differences emerged between psychiatrists and primary care physicians, with psychiatrists more likely to utilize augmentation strategies and combination therapies. Geographic variations were also observed, with urban practitioners showing greater willingness to employ complex regimens. These findings highlight substantial heterogeneity in clinical decision-making for SSRI treatment failures and underscore the need for evidence-based guidelines to optimize outcomes in this challenging patient population.

title: [Current trends in clinical psychiatric research].
abstract: The landscape of clinical psychiatric research has undergone significant transformation during the 1990s, driven by advances in neuropharmacology and neuroimaging technologies. This review examines emerging methodological approaches and therapeutic targets in contemporary psychiatric investigation. We analyzed 347 clinical trials published between 1995-1998 across major psychiatric journals, focusing on study design innovations, novel pharmacological agents, and integration of biological markers. Additionally, we surveyed 125 research centers across Europe and North America to assess current research priorities and methodological trends. Our analysis reveals a marked shift toward multi-modal treatment approaches, with 68% of recent studies incorporating both pharmacological and psychosocial interventions. Selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors remain the predominant focus (42% of trials), though research into atypical antipsychotics has increased substantially (23% vs. 8% in 1990-1994). Notably, 31% of contemporary studies now utilize neuroimaging techniques, compared to 12% in earlier investigations. Genetic polymorphism analysis is emerging as a significant research component, appearing in 19% of recent pharmacogenetic studies. Quality-of-life measures have become standard outcome assessments, present in 76% of trials. The integration of placebo-controlled designs with active comparator arms has become increasingly common, reflecting enhanced methodological rigor. These trends indicate a paradigm shift toward personalized medicine approaches and dimensional rather than categorical diagnostic frameworks. Future psychiatric research will likely emphasize biomarker-guided treatment selection and the development of mechanism-based therapeutic interventions targeting specific neurobiological pathways.

title: Are You Ready for Change?
abstract: The implementation of new psychopharmacological interventions in psychiatric care settings requires careful assessment of organizational readiness and staff preparedness. This study examined factors influencing healthcare providers' readiness to adopt evidence-based medication management protocols in community mental health centers. A cross-sectional survey was conducted among 247 nurses, psychiatrists, and mental health technicians across 12 community mental health facilities in the Midwest. Participants completed validated instruments measuring organizational change readiness, knowledge of psychopharmacology principles, attitudes toward medication adherence monitoring, and perceived barriers to implementation. Data were analyzed using descriptive statistics, correlation analysis, and multiple regression modeling. Results indicated that 68% of participants reported moderate to high readiness for implementing new medication management protocols. Significant predictors of change readiness included years of psychiatric nursing experience (β = 0.34, p < 0.001), organizational support perception (β = 0.42, p < 0.001), and baseline psychopharmacology knowledge scores (β = 0.28, p < 0.01). Primary barriers identified were insufficient training time (73%), concerns about increased workload (61%), and uncertainty about clinical outcomes (45%). Nurses with specialized psychiatric certifications demonstrated significantly higher readiness scores compared to non-certified staff (M = 4.2 vs 3.6, p < 0.05). These findings suggest that successful implementation of psychopharmacological innovations requires targeted educational interventions, adequate organizational support, and consideration of staff experience levels. Mental health administrators should prioritize comprehensive training programs and address identified barriers before implementing new medication management protocols.
journal: Journal of psychosocial nursing and mental health services

title: Psychopharmacology of the endocannabinoids: far beyond anandamide.
abstract: The endocannabinoid system has emerged as a critical neuromodulatory network, yet research has predominantly focused on anandamide (AEA) while neglecting other bioactive lipid mediators. This review synthesizes current understanding of the broader endocannabinoid pharmacology beyond classical eCB signaling. We conducted a comprehensive analysis of recent literature examining 2-arachidonoylglycerol (2-AG), palmitoylethanolamide (PEA), oleoylethanolamide (OEA), and N-arachidonoyldopamine (NADA) across preclinical models of neuropsychiatric disorders. Unlike AEA's predominant CB1 receptor activity, 2-AG demonstrates distinct biosynthetic pathways via diacylglycerol lipase and enhanced synaptic plasticity modulation. PEA and OEA, while lacking direct cannabinoid receptor affinity, exhibit potent anti-inflammatory and feeding regulatory properties through peroxisome proliferator-activated receptor-α (PPAR-α) and GPR119 mechanisms. NADA uniquely activates both cannabinoid and vanilloid receptor systems, suggesting novel therapeutic targets. Pharmacokinetic analyses reveal differential enzymatic degradation patterns, with fatty acid amide hydrolase (FAAH) and monoacylglycerol lipase (MAGL) displaying substrate selectivity that impacts tissue-specific endocannabinoid tone. Behavioral studies demonstrate that selective modulation of non-AEA pathways produces distinct anxiolytic, analgesic, and cognitive effects, often with improved therapeutic windows compared to direct CB1 agonism. These findings highlight the endocannabinoid system's remarkable pharmacological diversity and suggest that therapeutic interventions targeting specific eCB metabolic pathways may offer superior clinical outcomes with reduced psychoactive liability compared to traditional cannabinoid approaches.

title: Effect of short-term intake of high- and low-concentrations of sucrose solution on the neurochemistry of male and female mice.
abstract: Chronic high sucrose consumption has been extensively studied for its neurochemical effects, yet the acute impact of varying sucrose concentrations on brain neurotransmitter systems remains poorly characterized. This study investigated sex-specific neurochemical responses following short-term exposure to different sucrose concentrations in adult C57BL/6J mice. Male and female mice (n=12 per group) were randomly assigned to receive either high-concentration sucrose (20% w/v), low-concentration sucrose (5% w/v), or water for 7 days ad libitum. Brain tissue samples from the prefrontal cortex, hippocampus, and striatum were analyzed using high-performance liquid chromatography to quantify dopamine, serotonin, norepinephrine, and their metabolites. Behavioral assessments included sucrose preference tests and locomotor activity measurements. High-concentration sucrose significantly increased dopamine levels in the striatum of both sexes (p<0.01), with males showing greater elevation than females (2.3-fold vs. 1.7-fold increase). Serotonin turnover in the prefrontal cortex was enhanced in female mice receiving high-concentration sucrose (p<0.05) but remained unchanged in males. Low-concentration sucrose produced minimal neurochemical alterations compared to controls. Locomotor activity increased dose-dependently with sucrose concentration, particularly in female subjects. These findings demonstrate that short-term sucrose exposure induces rapid, concentration-dependent neurochemical changes with notable sex differences in neurotransmitter responses. The differential sensitivity between males and females suggests distinct neural mechanisms underlying sugar reward processing, with implications for understanding sex-specific vulnerabilities to dietary sugar overconsumption and related metabolic disorders.

title: Love and attachment: the psychobiology of social bonding.
abstract: Social bonding represents a fundamental aspect of human behavior with significant implications for mental health and neuropsychiatric disorders. While the neurobiological mechanisms underlying attachment have been extensively studied in animal models, translational research examining the psychobiology of human social bonding remains limited. This review synthesizes current understanding of the neurochemical and neuroanatomical substrates of love and attachment, integrating findings from preclinical studies with emerging human neuroimaging data. We examined peer-reviewed literature from 1990-2008 focusing on the roles of oxytocin, vasopressin, dopamine, and endogenous opioids in pair bonding and attachment behaviors. Prairie vole studies demonstrate that oxytocin and vasopressin receptor expression in the nucleus accumbens and ventral pallidum critically regulate pair bond formation, while dopaminergic signaling in mesolimbic reward circuits mediates the reinforcing aspects of social contact. Human neuroimaging studies reveal activation of similar reward pathways during romantic attachment, including the ventral tegmental area, caudate nucleus, and anterior cingulate cortex. Oxytocin administration enhances trust, empathy, and social recognition in healthy volunteers, supporting its role in human bonding. Dysregulation of these systems may contribute to attachment disorders, depression, and autism spectrum disorders. The neurochemistry of love involves complex interactions between neuropeptide, monoamine, and opioid systems that evolved to promote selective social bonds essential for reproductive success and offspring care. Understanding these mechanisms provides insight into the biological basis of human relationships and offers potential therapeutic targets for disorders characterized by impaired social functioning.

title: New approaches to chemotherapy-induced nausea and vomiting: from neuropharmacology to clinical investigations.
abstract: Chemotherapy-induced nausea and vomiting (CINV) remains a significant dose-limiting toxicity affecting patient quality of life and treatment compliance. While 5-HT3 receptor antagonists have improved antiemetic therapy, breakthrough emesis continues to affect 30-50% of patients receiving highly emetogenic chemotherapy. This review examines emerging neuropharmacological targets and translational approaches for CINV management. We analyzed preclinical data from animal models of cisplatin and cyclophosphamide-induced emesis, focusing on novel receptor systems including neurokinin-1 (NK-1), cannabinoid CB1/CB2, and GABA-B receptors in the chemoreceptor trigger zone and nucleus tractus solitarius. Clinical trial data from 2000-2006 were systematically reviewed, emphasizing phase II and III studies of combination antiemetic regimens. Preclinical studies demonstrate that NK-1 receptor antagonists significantly reduce both acute and delayed emesis when combined with 5-HT3 antagonists, with aprepitant showing 65-80% complete response rates in delayed CINV. Cannabinoid receptor modulators exhibit promise in refractory cases, while GABA-B agonists show efficacy in anticipatory nausea models. Clinical investigations reveal that triple combination therapy (5-HT3 antagonist, NK-1 antagonist, and dexamethasone) achieves complete protection rates exceeding 70% for highly emetogenic regimens. Patient stratification based on genetic polymorphisms in CYP2D6 and 5-HT3 receptor subunits may optimize individual responses. These findings support a multi-target approach to CINV, with NK-1 receptor antagonists representing the most significant advance since 5-HT3 antagonists. Future directions include personalized antiemetic therapy and investigation of central sensitization mechanisms in treatment-refractory patients.

title: Consensus Guidelines for Therapeutic Drug Monitoring in Neuropsychopharmacology: Update 2017.
abstract: Therapeutic drug monitoring (TDM) is the quantification and interpretation of drug concentrations in blood to optimize pharmacotherapy. It considers the interindividual variability of pharmacokinetics and thus enables personalized pharmacotherapy. In psychiatry and neurology, patient populations that may particularly benefit from TDM are children and adolescents, pregnant women, elderly patients, individuals with intellectual disabilities, patients with substance abuse disorders, forensic psychiatric patients or patients with known or suspected pharmacokinetic abnormalities. Non-response at therapeutic doses, uncertain drug adherence, suboptimal tolerability, or pharmacokinetic drug-drug interactions are typical indications for TDM. However, the potential benefits of TDM to optimize pharmacotherapy can only be obtained if the method is adequately integrated in the clinical treatment process. To supply treating physicians and laboratories with valid information on TDM, the TDM task force of the Arbeitsgemeinschaft für Neuropsychopharmakologie und Pharmakopsychiatrie (AGNP) issued their first guidelines for TDM in psychiatry in 2004. After an update in 2011, it was time for the next update. Following the new guidelines holds the potential to improve neuropsychopharmacotherapy, accelerate the recovery of many patients, and reduce health care costs.

title: Human brain imaging and substance abuse.
abstract: Over the past decade, functional neuroimaging has contributed greatly to our knowledge about the neuropharmacology of substance misuse in man. Techniques such as functional magnetic resonance imaging, positron emission tomography (PET) or single photon emission tomography (SPET) can measure changes in regional cerebral activity, whereas changes in neuropharmacological parameters (e.g. receptor number and neurotransmitter levels) can be directly measured only with PET and SPET. Recently, a series of studies have shown common effects of substances of misuse on the brain, such as an acute increase in dopamine release (followed by hypofunction after chronic use) and cue exposure-induced activation of the frontal cortex, as well as specific effects, such as ecstasy-induced decreases in serotonergic transporters and occupancy of opiate receptors by substitute drugs (e.g. methadone and buprenorphine) in opiate addicts.

title: Proton magnetic resonance spectroscopy detection of neurotransmitters in dorsomedial medulla correlate with spontaneous baroreceptor reflex function.
abstract: Control of heart rate variability via modulation of sympathovagal balance is a key function of nucleus tractus solitarii and the dorsal motor nucleus of the vagus localized in the dorsomedial medulla oblongata. Normal blood pressure regulation involves precise balance of glutamate (Glu)-glutamine-gamma-aminobutyric acid transmitter systems, and angiotensin II modulates these transmitters to produce tonic suppression of reflex function. It is not known, however, whether other brain transmitters/metabolites are indicators of baroreflex function. This study establishes the concept that comprehensive baseline transmitter/metabolite profiles obtained using in vivo (1)H magnetic resonance spectroscopy in rats with well-characterized differences in resting blood pressure and baroreflex function can be used as indices of autonomic balance or baroreflex sensitivity. Transgenic rats with over-expression of renin [m(Ren2)27] or under-expression of glial-angiotensinogen (ASrAogen) were compared with Sprague-Dawley rats. Glu concentration in the dorsal medulla is significantly higher in ASrAogen rats compared with either Sprague-Dawley or (mRen2)27 rats. Glu levels and the ratio of Glu:glutamine correlated positively with indices of higher vagal tone consistent with the importance of these neurotransmitters in baroreflex function. Interestingly, the levels of choline-containing metabolites showed a significant positive correlation with spontaneous baroreflex sensitivity and a negative correlation with sympathetic tone. Thus, we demonstrate the concept that noninvasive assessment of neurochemical biomarkers may be used as an index of baroreflex sensitivity.

title: Functional genomics in neuropsychiatric disorders and in neuropharmacology.
abstract: The rapidly accumulating amount of information concerning gene and protein expression patterns produced by functional genomics, proteomics and bioinformatics is presently providing new targets for drug development. Furthermore, the analysis of gene expression in cells and tissues affected by a disease may reveal the underlying metabolic pathways and cellular processes affected. Finally, changes in gene expression may be used in either diagnostics or the monitoring of drug responses. This review focuses on advances in the use of functional genomics in neurological and neuropsychiatric diseases and neuropsychopharmacology. Although the number of published studies in this field is still limited, it already appears that this strategy may become a fruitful means in the analysis of the aetiology of neuropsychiatric disorders and the search for novel neuropharmacological drugs.

title: A short historical review of SCNP with special reference to the UKU.
abstract: The development of systematic approaches to assess side effects of psychotropic medications has been crucial for advancing neuropsychopharmacological research and clinical practice. The Scandinavian Committee on Neuropsychopharmacology (SCNP), established in the 1970s, played a pivotal role in standardizing adverse event reporting through the development of the UKU Side Effect Rating Scale (Udvalg for Kliniske Undersøgelser). This historical review examines the evolution of the SCNP and its contribution to systematic side effect assessment in psychiatric treatment. We conducted a comprehensive literature review spanning 1970-2014, analyzing key publications, meeting proceedings, and collaborative research initiatives involving SCNP members. Historical documents and personal communications with founding members were also examined to trace the development of the UKU scale. The SCNP emerged from the need for standardized clinical trial methodologies in Nordic countries, with early focus on antipsychotic and antidepressant efficacy studies. The UKU scale, developed between 1983-1987, represented a breakthrough in systematic side effect documentation, incorporating 48 items across four domains: psychological, neurological, autonomic, and other symptoms. The scale demonstrated excellent inter-rater reliability (κ=0.74-0.89) and became widely adopted internationally, translated into over 20 languages. Validation studies confirmed its utility across diverse psychiatric populations and treatment modalities. The SCNP's collaborative model influenced subsequent international research networks, while the UKU scale remains a gold standard for comprehensive side effect assessment. This historical perspective highlights how Nordic cooperation in neuropsychopharmacology contributed significantly to evidence-based psychiatric treatment and patient safety monitoring worldwide.

title: Proteomic approaches in brain research and neuropharmacology.
abstract: Numerous applications of genomic technologies have enabled the assembly of unprecedented inventories of genes, expressed in cells under specific physiological and pathophysiological conditions. Complementing the valuable information generated through functional genomics with the integrative knowledge of protein expression and function should enable the development of more efficient diagnostic tools and therapeutic agents. Proteomic analyses are particularly suitable to elucidate posttranslational modifications, expression levels and protein-protein interactions of thousands of proteins at a time. In this review, two-dimensional polyacrylamide gel electrophoresis (2D-PAGE) investigations of brain tissues in neurodegenerative diseases such as Alzheimer's disease, Down syndrome and schizophrenia, and the construction of 2D-PAGE proteome maps of the brain are discussed. The role of the Human Proteome Organization (HUPO) as an international coordinating organization for proteomic efforts, as well as challenges for proteomic technologies and data analysis are also addressed. It is expected that the use of proteomic strategies will have significant impact in neuropharmacology over the coming decade.

title: Glutamate-based antidepressants: preclinical psychopharmacology.
abstract: Recent clinical findings demonstrating rapid antidepressant effects of the NMDA receptor antagonist ketamine have generated considerable interest in glutamatergic mechanisms underlying depression and antidepressant action. Traditional monoaminergic antidepressants require weeks to achieve therapeutic efficacy, highlighting the urgent need for novel rapid-acting treatments. We investigated the preclinical psychopharmacological profiles of glutamate-modulating compounds with potential antidepressant properties using established rodent behavioral paradigms. Male Sprague-Dawley rats underwent chronic unpredictable stress (CUS) for 4 weeks to induce depressive-like behaviors. Animals received acute or chronic treatment with ketamine (10 mg/kg), the mGluR2/3 antagonist LY341495 (1-10 mg/kg), the AMPA receptor positive allosteric modulator LY451646 (0.1-1 mg/kg), or vehicle. Behavioral assessments included forced swim test, sucrose preference, novelty-suppressed feeding, and locomotor activity. Western blot analysis examined BDNF, phospho-mTOR, and synaptic protein expression in prefrontal cortex and hippocampus. Acute ketamine and LY341495 administration produced rapid antidepressant-like effects within 24 hours, reversing CUS-induced behavioral deficits without affecting locomotion. Chronic LY451646 treatment showed delayed but sustained antidepressant efficacy. All compounds increased BDNF expression and mTOR phosphorylation, with ketamine producing the most robust synaptic protein upregulation. These findings support the therapeutic potential of glutamate receptor modulators as novel antidepressants and suggest that enhancing AMPA receptor function or blocking NMDA/mGluR2/3 receptors may represent viable alternatives to traditional monoaminergic approaches, potentially offering more rapid symptom relief through glutamate-dependent synaptic plasticity mechanisms.

title: The origins and early history of neurochemistry and its societies.
abstract: The field of neurochemistry emerged in the early 20th century as investigators began applying biochemical techniques to understand brain function, yet a comprehensive historical analysis of its origins and institutional development remains lacking. This review examines the foundational period of neurochemistry from 1900-1970, analyzing primary sources including scientific publications, society records, and correspondence to trace the discipline's evolution and the establishment of its professional organizations. Systematic literature searches were conducted across historical databases, with particular attention to seminal works by pioneers such as Thudichum, McIlwain, and Richter. Institutional archives from the International Society for Neurochemistry (ISN) and American Society for Neurochemistry (ASN) provided insights into organizational founding principles and early membership patterns. Our analysis reveals that neurochemistry crystallized as a distinct discipline following three key developments: the application of enzymatic assays to neural tissue (1920s-1930s), the discovery of neurotransmitter synthesis pathways (1940s-1950s), and the advent of subcellular fractionation techniques (1950s-1960s). The ISN, established in 1963, emerged from informal networks of researchers who recognized the need for specialized forums to discuss brain chemistry distinct from general biochemistry or neurophysiology. Early society proceedings demonstrate rapid expansion from 200 founding members to over 1,200 by 1970, with significant geographic diversification. This historical framework demonstrates how neurochemistry's institutional identity was shaped by methodological innovations and the unique challenges of studying brain metabolism, providing context for understanding contemporary directions in neuropharmacological research and professional organization within the neurosciences.

title: Reduced computational models of serotonin synthesis, release, and reuptake.
abstract: Serotonergic dysfunction underlies numerous psychiatric and neurological disorders, yet computational modeling of serotonin (5-HT) dynamics remains limited by model complexity and computational cost. We developed reduced-order mathematical models of 5-HT synthesis, release, and reuptake to enable efficient simulation of serotonergic neurotransmission while preserving biological fidelity. Starting from detailed biophysical models incorporating tryptophan hydroxylase kinetics, vesicular packaging, calcium-dependent exocytosis, and serotonin transporter (SERT) function, we applied model reduction techniques including quasi-steady-state approximations and singular perturbation analysis. The resulting reduced models were validated against experimental data from rat dorsal raphe nucleus recordings and compared to full-order simulations. Our simplified models captured 5-HT synthesis dynamics with 85% accuracy while reducing computational time by two orders of magnitude. The reduced release model accurately reproduced stimulus-evoked 5-HT transients measured by fast-scan cyclic voltammetry, including both peak amplitudes and decay kinetics. Reuptake dynamics were well-preserved across physiological and pharmacologically-relevant SERT inhibition conditions, with less than 10% deviation from full models. Parameter sensitivity analysis revealed that synthesis rate and SERT density were the most critical determinants of steady-state 5-HT levels. These computationally efficient models enable real-time simulation of serotonergic circuits and provide a foundation for closed-loop neuromodulation systems targeting 5-HT dysfunction. The reduced-order framework presented here can be readily extended to other monoaminergic systems and integrated into larger network models of brain function.

title: The use of biomarkers in psychiatric research: how serotonin transporter occupancy explains the dose-response curves of SSRIs.
abstract: This column reviews why signal detection in psychiatric research has been problematic, how the use of biomarkers can help, how and why fixed dose studies are done, and how these studies differ from what clinicians do in practice. The fixed dose studies done with selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors (SSRIs) are used to illustrate general points about clinical trial research methodology relevant to clinical practice. Studies with SSRIs have yielded flat dose-response curves with regard to efficacy but ascending dose-response curves with regard to discontinuation due to adverse effects. These clinical trial findings are explained by studies using serotonin transporter inhibition or occupancy as a surrogate marker for SSRI efficacy and tolerability. Initially, these studies were conducted ex vivo using human platelets as the model system; however, they have now been extended to in vivo measurement of serotonin transporter occupancy in patients using positron emission tomography. The conclusion from this work is that the usually effective, minimum dose of each marketed SSRI produces 70%-80% inhibition or occupancy (depending on the methodology used) of the serotonin transporter; higher rates of inhibition or occupancy do not on average increase efficacy but instead increase early discontinuation rates due to adverse effects. These increased discontinuation rates offset any gain in efficacy when the results are analyzed using the last-observation-carried-forward approach. An understanding of these principles also provides an explanation for what initially may appear to be a conundrum: why some patients can benefit from a dose increase even though, in fixed dose clinical trials, the drug had a flat dose-efficacy curve.

title: S32504, a novel naphtoxazine agonist at dopamine D3/D2 receptors: III. Actions in models of potential antidepressive and anxiolytic activity in comparison with ropinirole.
abstract: The novel naphtoxazine derivative S32504 displays preferential affinity for dopamine D3 vs D2 receptors and shows promise as a potential therapeutic agent. This study evaluated the antidepressant and anxiolytic properties of S32504 in comparison with the D2/D3 agonist ropinirole using established rodent behavioral models. Male Wistar rats and Swiss mice received S32504 (0.16-10.0 mg/kg, s.c.), ropinirole (0.63-10.0 mg/kg, s.c.), or vehicle 30 min prior to testing. In the forced swim test, S32504 dose-dependently reduced immobility time in both rats (ED50 = 2.1 mg/kg) and mice (ED50 = 1.8 mg/kg), with efficacy comparable to imipramine (15 mg/kg, i.p.). Ropinirole showed weaker antidepressant-like activity (ED50 = 6.4 mg/kg in rats). In the elevated plus-maze, S32504 (0.63-2.5 mg/kg) significantly increased open arm entries and time spent in open arms, indicating anxiolytic effects. These actions were blocked by the D3-preferring antagonist S33084 (10 mg/kg, s.c.) but only partially attenuated by the D2-selective antagonist L-741,626 (10 mg/kg, s.c.). Ropinirole produced less pronounced anxiolytic effects that were fully reversed by both antagonists. S32504 did not impair motor coordination at active doses, unlike ropinirole which caused rotational behavior at higher doses. These findings suggest that S32504's preferential D3 receptor activation underlies its robust antidepressant and anxiolytic properties, supporting the therapeutic potential of selective D3 receptor agonists for mood and anxiety disorders.
journal: The Journal of pharmacology and experimental therapeutics

title: Imaging of phosphatidylcholines in the adult rat brain using MALDI-TOF MS.
abstract: Phosphatidylcholines (PCs) are the most abundant constituents of lipid in the brain. PCs function as major structural components of cell membranes and as important sources for signaling molecules. In the brain, three kinds of PCs, dipalmitoyl PC, palmitoyloleoyl PC, and stearoyloleoyl PC have been reported to be major species. They have different chemical and biological characteristics depending on the length of alkyl chains and the degree of saturation, suggesting that the abundance of PCs might be important to keep specialized membrane structures in the brain, such as myelin and synaptic membranes. However, detailed imaging of PCs in the total rat brain has not done yet. Thus, using imaging technology by matrix-assisted laser desorption/ionization mass spectrometry (MALDI-MS), we investigated the total distribution of PC32:0, PC34:1, and PC36:1 in the rat brain. PC32:0 and PC34:1 were more abundantly observed in the gray matter areas than in the white matter areas throughout the central nervous system (CNS), while PC36:1 was evenly seen at low levels in both areas. In addition, we found that PC32:0 and PC34:1 were detected at very high levels in the granular layer of the olfactory bulb, piriform cortex, insular cortex, and molecular layer of the cerebellum, which are known for areas showing high neuronal plasticity. The present imaging data clearly show that various PCs are differentially distributed throughout the rat CNS, and suggest that these differential distributions of various PCs are necessary to keep normal brain functions.

title: Pharmacotherapy for neuropathic pain.
abstract: Refractory neuropathic pain can be devastating to a patient's quality of life. Ideally, the primary goal of therapy would be to prevent the pain, yet even the most appropriate treatment strategy may be only able to reduce the pain to a more tolerable level. Pharmacotherapy is currently the mainstay of treatment in patients with neuropathic pain, although at present the drugs are used on a mainly "off-label" basis. A wide variety of agents are used, especially antidepressants (ie, tricyclic antidepressants, selective serotonin-reuptake inhibitors) and anticonvulsants, but also opioids and tramadol, topical agents (eg, lidocaine), systemic local anesthetics, and anti-inflammatories. Even so, effective pain relief is achieved in less than half of patients with chronic neuropathic pain. In refractory patients, combination therapy using two agents with synergistic mechanisms of action may offer greater pain relief without compromising the side-effect profile of each agent.
journal: Pain practice : the official journal of World Institute of Pain

title: Psychopharmacology of autistic spectrum disorders in children and adolescents.
abstract: Autism spectrum disorders (ASDs) affect approximately 1 in 110 children and are characterized by impaired social communication, restricted interests, and repetitive behaviors. While behavioral interventions remain the cornerstone of treatment, pharmacological management of associated symptoms has become increasingly important in clinical practice. This review examines the current evidence base for psychopharmacological interventions in children and adolescents with ASDs. A comprehensive literature search was conducted using PubMed, Cochrane Library, and PsycINFO databases from 1990 to 2011, focusing on randomized controlled trials and systematic reviews of pharmacological treatments in pediatric ASD populations. Key outcome measures included core autism symptoms, associated behavioral problems, and adverse effects. Evidence supports the use of atypical antipsychotics, particularly risperidone and aripiprazole, for managing irritability, aggression, and self-injurious behaviors, with both agents receiving FDA approval for these indications in pediatric ASD patients. Selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors show mixed efficacy for repetitive behaviors and anxiety symptoms, with fluoxetine demonstrating some benefit in adolescents. Stimulant medications can effectively treat comorbid attention-deficit/hyperactivity disorder symptoms, though response rates may be lower than in typically developing children. Alpha-2 agonists show promise for hyperactivity and sleep disturbances. However, no medications have demonstrated significant improvement in core social communication deficits. While pharmacological interventions can provide meaningful symptom relief for associated behavioral problems in children and adolescents with ASDs, treatment decisions must carefully weigh potential benefits against risks of adverse effects, with close monitoring essential for optimal outcomes.

title: The role of 5-HT₁A receptors in fish oil-mediated increased BDNF expression in the rat hippocampus and cortex: a possible antidepressant mechanism.
abstract: Omega-3 fatty acids have demonstrated antidepressant-like effects in both clinical and preclinical studies, yet the underlying neurobiological mechanisms remain poorly understood. Brain-derived neurotrophic factor (BDNF) upregulation and serotonergic system modulation have been proposed as potential mediators of these effects. This study investigated whether fish oil-induced increases in hippocampal and cortical BDNF expression are mediated through 5-HT₁A receptor signaling pathways. Male Sprague-Dawley rats (n=8-10 per group) received daily oral administration of fish oil (300 mg/kg) or vehicle for 21 days. To examine 5-HT₁A receptor involvement, separate groups received co-treatment with the selective 5-HT₁A antagonist WAY-100635 (0.5 mg/kg, s.c.). BDNF mRNA and protein levels were quantified using qRT-PCR and Western blot analysis in hippocampal and prefrontal cortical tissues. Immunohistochemical analysis was performed to assess regional BDNF distribution. Fish oil treatment significantly increased BDNF mRNA expression in the hippocampus (2.3-fold, p<0.01) and prefrontal cortex (1.8-fold, p<0.05) compared to controls. Corresponding increases in BDNF protein levels were observed in both regions. Co-administration of WAY-100635 completely abolished fish oil-induced BDNF upregulation in the hippocampus and partially attenuated the response in the prefrontal cortex. Immunohistochemical analysis revealed that fish oil enhanced BDNF immunoreactivity primarily in CA1 and CA3 pyramidal neurons and dentate gyrus granule cells. These findings suggest that 5-HT₁A receptors play a critical role in mediating fish oil-induced BDNF expression, providing novel mechanistic insight into the antidepressant properties of omega-3 fatty acids.

title: Neurochemical, endocrine and immunological responses to stress in young and old Fischer 344 male rats.
abstract: Two experiments were performed. In the first, a 20 min conditioned emotional response (CER) paradigm was used to compare the neurochemical, endocrine and immunological responses to stress of 7- and 22-month-old Fischer 344 (F344) male rats. In the second, corticosterone levels 20 min following ether stress, and regional brain type I and II corticosterone receptor densities were examined using 7- and 17.5-month-old F344 male rats. Dopamine (DA) metabolism in old nonstressed rats was significantly reduced in the medial frontal cortex, neostriatum, nucleus accumbens and hypothalamus, but not in the amygdala. The CER procedure, nevertheless, increased medial frontal cortical, nucleus accumbens and amygdaloid DA turnover in both the young and old rats. The young and old nonstressed rats did not evidence differences in norepinephrine (NE) and serotonin (5-HT) concentrations. However, stress resulted in a decrease in medial frontal cortical 5-hydroxyindoleacetic acid (5-HIAA) and hypothalamic 5-HT levels in old but not in young animals. These observations suggest age-related differences in the response of central NE and 5-HT systems to stress. Ether and the CER procedure led to exaggerated corticosterone responses in the old rats (17.5 and 22 month, respectively). Hippocampal type I but not type II corticosterone receptors were decreased by 47% in the 17.5-month-old rats. Thus, age-related changes in hippocampal corticosterone receptor types do not occur in unison, and the exacerbated corticosterone response to stress precedes the reported down-regulation of hippocampal type II corticosterone receptors in aged rats. Age-related changes were not observed in the concentrations of corticosterone receptors in other brain regions, or in the prolactin response to stress. The old rats, however, evidenced a reduction in the availability of the renin substrate, angiotensinogen, and in stress-induced renin secretion. Immune function was impaired in the old nonstressed rats, and further compromised by exposure to the CER procedure. In comparison to the young control rats, the old nonstressed rats showed an increased percentage of splenic large granular lymphocytes, reduced splenic natural killer cytotoxicity, and impaired Con-A-stimulated splenic T lymphocyte proliferation. Reductions in T splenic cell proliferation and natural killer cytotoxicity were observed in the young rats subjected to the CER paradigm, but not to the same extent as in the old rats. These observations indicate that aging male F344 rats evidence major alterations in basal central monoamine, endocrine and immune functions, and an increased sensitivity of these systems to stress.

title: The neurochemistry of agitation in Alzheimer's disease: a systematic review.
abstract: Agitation represents one of the most prevalent and distressing behavioral symptoms in Alzheimer's disease (AD), affecting up to 70% of patients and contributing significantly to caregiver burden and institutionalization. Despite its clinical importance, the underlying neurochemical mechanisms remain poorly understood. We conducted a systematic review to synthesize current knowledge on the neurochemical basis of agitation in AD. We searched PubMed, EMBASE, and PsycINFO databases from inception to December 2017 for studies investigating neurochemical correlates of agitation in AD patients. Studies were included if they examined cerebrospinal fluid, neuroimaging, or post-mortem brain tissue analyses in relation to validated agitation measures. Twenty-eight studies met inclusion criteria, encompassing 2,847 AD patients. Converging evidence implicated dysregulation of multiple neurotransmitter systems. Reduced cholinergic activity, particularly in frontal and temporal regions, was consistently associated with increased agitation severity across neuroimaging and post-mortem studies. Dopaminergic dysfunction, evidenced by decreased striatal dopamine transporter binding and altered D2 receptor density, correlated with agitated behaviors. Serotonergic abnormalities, including reduced 5-HT1A receptor binding and elevated inflammatory markers affecting serotonin metabolism, were linked to aggression and irritability. Additionally, increased noradrenergic activity and elevated cortisol levels suggested heightened stress responses in agitated patients. GABAergic deficits in frontal regions were associated with disinhibited behaviors. These findings suggest agitation in AD results from complex interactions between cholinergic depletion, monoaminergic imbalances, and stress system activation. Understanding these mechanisms may inform targeted therapeutic approaches for managing this challenging symptom.

title: Psychopharmacological possibilities in the acute disaster setting.
abstract: The September 11, 2001 terrorist attacks and recent natural disasters have highlighted the urgent need for evidence-based psychopharmacological interventions in acute disaster settings. While psychological first aid remains the primary intervention, certain populations may benefit from targeted pharmacological support to prevent the development of chronic psychiatric sequelae. This review examines the current literature on acute pharmacological interventions following traumatic exposure and their potential applications in mass casualty events. A comprehensive literature search was conducted using MEDLINE and PsycINFO databases from 1990-2004, focusing on randomized controlled trials and case series examining pharmacological interventions within 30 days of trauma exposure. Studies were evaluated for methodological quality and clinical applicability in disaster scenarios. Limited but promising evidence supports the use of selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors, particularly sertraline and paroxetine, in preventing post-traumatic stress disorder when initiated within 72 hours of exposure in high-risk individuals. Beta-blockers, specifically propranolol, show potential for disrupting traumatic memory consolidation when administered within 6 hours of the event, though replication studies are needed. Short-term benzodiazepine use may provide symptomatic relief but should be limited to 2-3 days due to concerns about dependence and potential interference with natural recovery processes. Atypical antipsychotics may be beneficial for individuals experiencing acute psychotic symptoms or severe agitation. While pharmacological interventions should not replace psychological support, targeted use of specific agents in carefully selected populations may reduce the risk of chronic psychiatric morbidity following mass traumatic events.
journal: The Psychiatric clinics of North America

title: Imaging dopamine transmission in cocaine dependence: link between neurochemistry and response to treatment.
abstract: Previous research has shown that dopamine signaling in the limbic striatum is crucial for selecting adaptive, motivated behavior and that disrupted dopamine transmission is associated with impulsive and maladaptive behavior. In humans, positron emission tomography (PET) imaging studies have shown that cocaine dependence is associated with the dysregulation of striatal dopamine signaling, which is linked to cocaine-seeking behavior. The goal of the present study was to investigate whether this association applies to the treatment setting. The authors hypothesized that dopamine signaling in the limbic striatum would be associated with response to a behavioral treatment that uses positive reinforcement to replace impulsive cocaine use with constructive personal goals. Prior to treatment, cocaine-dependent subjects underwent two PET scans using [(11)C]raclopride, before and after the administration of a stimulant (methylphenidate), for measurement of striatal dopamine D(2/3) receptor binding and presynaptic dopamine release. Both of the outcome measures were lower in the volunteers who did not respond to treatment than in those who experienced a positive treatment response.
