These findings provide insight into the neurochemistry of treatment response and show that low dopamine transmission is associated with treatment failure. In addition, these data suggest that the combination of behavioral treatment with methods that increase striatal dopamine signaling might serve as a therapeutic strategy for cocaine dependence.

title: Strategies for Utilizing Neuroimaging Biomarkers in CNS Drug Discovery and Development: CINP/JSNP Working Group Report.
abstract: Central nervous system (CNS) drug development faces unprecedented challenges, with failure rates exceeding 90% and limited translation from preclinical models to clinical efficacy. Neuroimaging biomarkers offer promising strategies to bridge this translational gap by providing objective, quantifiable measures of CNS drug action. The CINP/JSNP Working Group convened to evaluate current applications and establish recommendations for integrating neuroimaging biomarkers into CNS drug discovery and development pipelines. Through systematic review of recent neuroimaging studies in CNS therapeutics and expert consensus sessions, we examined the utility of various imaging modalities including functional MRI, positron emission tomography, magnetic resonance spectroscopy, and diffusion tensor imaging across different phases of drug development. Our analysis identified key applications: target engagement assessment in Phase I trials, dose optimization through pharmacokinetic-pharmacodynamic modeling, patient stratification for enrichment strategies, and surrogate endpoint development for proof-of-concept studies. We found that successful implementation requires standardized acquisition protocols, robust quality control measures, and appropriate statistical frameworks for small sample sizes typical in early-phase trials. Critical recommendations include establishing imaging biomarker qualification pathways with regulatory agencies, developing companion diagnostics for patient selection, and creating multi-site consortiums to enable adequately powered studies. The Working Group concludes that strategic implementation of neuroimaging biomarkers can significantly enhance CNS drug development efficiency by enabling go/no-go decisions earlier in development, reducing late-stage failures, and facilitating personalized medicine approaches in psychiatric and neurological disorders.

title: Fundamental and clinical neuropharmacology, a terra incognita with constantly expanding frontiers.
abstract: Neuropharmacology is the science that studies the effects of drugs on the central nervous system. Examining the recent history of neuropharmacology allows us to identify future challenges and to project into the future. The contemporary history of neuropharmacology begins with the empirical use of psychotropic alkaloids in barbiturate preparations. Then the discovery of neuroleptics in the 1950s truly opened up the field of pharmacological science, with psychopharmacological clinical exploration in psychiatry, complemented by the contributions of neurochemists and biochemists discovering and exploring neurotransmission. These studies also contributed to the development of dopaminergic and serotonergic drugs used in neurology. The more recent period brought the advent of neuropsychopharmacology, sharing therapeutic targets in neurology and psychiatry on the one hand, and a translational research approach on the other. Moreover, the pharmacology of brain drugs is still a growing discipline in 2020. Despite emerging pharmacological concepts (biased agonists, glial targeting, epigenetic therapeutics, etc.), entire areas of brain-based therapeutics need to be renewed, particularly in psychiatry, offering great challenges to a new generation of pharmacologists and clinicians.

title: Motor control: exploring the neurochemistry of subliminal inhibition.
abstract: A new study links individual differences in unconsciously triggered motor control to variability in GABA neurotransmitter concentration in the supplementary motor area of the human brain.

title: Should we or shouldn't we?: Preface to the Special Issue "Neurochemistry of Reward-Seeking".
abstract: The neurochemical basis of reward-seeking behavior remains one of the most critical areas in contemporary neuroscience, with profound implications for understanding addiction, motivation, and psychiatric disorders. While classical dopaminergic pathways have dominated reward research for decades, emerging evidence suggests a far more complex neurochemical landscape involving glutamatergic, GABAergic, cholinergic, and neuropeptide systems. This special issue addresses fundamental questions about whether current therapeutic approaches targeting reward circuitry are optimally designed and whether novel pharmacological interventions should focus on single-target or multi-system approaches. The collection presents cutting-edge research examining the intricate balance between approach and avoidance behaviors, the role of stress-reward interactions, and the temporal dynamics of neurochemical signaling during reward anticipation and consumption. Key findings highlight the bidirectional nature of reward-seeking, where identical neurochemical substrates can promote both adaptive and maladaptive behaviors depending on environmental context and individual vulnerability factors. Emerging data demonstrate that traditional dopamine-centric models inadequately explain the heterogeneity observed in reward-related disorders and treatment responses. The evidence collectively suggests that future therapeutic strategies should embrace the complexity of reward neurocircuitry rather than pursuing reductionist approaches. These insights have immediate implications for developing more effective treatments for substance use disorders, behavioral addictions, and anhedonia-related conditions. The research presented challenges current paradigms and provides a roadmap for next-generation neurochemical interventions that acknowledge the multifaceted nature of reward-seeking behavior.

title: The roles of dopamine and serotonin in decision making: evidence from pharmacological experiments in humans.
abstract: Decision-making processes involve complex interactions between multiple neurotransmitter systems, with dopamine and serotonin playing particularly crucial roles. While dopamine has been implicated in reward prediction and reinforcement learning, serotonin's contribution to decision-making remains less well characterized. We investigated the distinct and interactive effects of dopaminergic and serotonergic manipulations on human decision-making using a double-blind, placebo-controlled, within-subjects pharmacological approach. Healthy volunteers (n=24) completed a probabilistic reversal learning task and temporal discounting paradigm under four conditions: placebo, the dopamine D2 receptor antagonist haloperidol (2mg), the selective serotonin reuptake inhibitor citalopram (20mg), and combined haloperidol-citalopram administration. Haloperidol significantly impaired participants' ability to adapt to reward contingency reversals and increased preference for immediate over delayed rewards, consistent with dopamine's role in reward prediction error signaling and temporal discounting. Citalopram enhanced punishment sensitivity and reduced impulsive choice behavior, supporting serotonin's involvement in behavioral inhibition and impulse control. Notably, combined administration revealed opposing effects: citalopram partially rescued haloperidol-induced deficits in reversal learning while haloperidol attenuated citalopram's effects on punishment sensitivity. These findings demonstrate dissociable roles for dopamine and serotonin in human decision-making, with dopamine primarily mediating reward-based learning and temporal preferences, while serotonin modulates response to aversive outcomes and impulsivity. The antagonistic interactions between these systems suggest that balanced dopaminergic-serotonergic function is critical for optimal decision-making, with implications for understanding psychiatric disorders characterized by decision-making abnormalities.
journal: Neuropsychopharmacology : official publication of the American College of Neuropsychopharmacology

title: Using a rate-frequency curve method to assess the rewarding properties of morphine in the intracranial self-stimulation paradigm in rats.
abstract: Intracranial self-stimulation (ICSS) paradigms provide valuable models for studying drug reward mechanisms, yet traditional threshold-based analyses may not capture the full spectrum of reward enhancement. This study evaluated a rate-frequency curve method as an alternative approach to assess morphine's rewarding properties in the ICSS paradigm. Male Sprague-Dawley rats (n=12) were implanted with bipolar electrodes targeting the medial forebrain bundle and trained to lever-press for electrical brain stimulation. Following stable baseline performance, rats underwent testing sessions where stimulation frequency was systematically varied (25-158 Hz) to generate rate-frequency curves. Morphine (1.0, 3.0, and 5.6 mg/kg, s.c.) or saline was administered 30 minutes prior to testing in a counterbalanced design. Response rates were recorded across all frequencies and curve-shift analysis was performed using nonlinear regression modeling. Morphine produced dose-dependent leftward and upward shifts in rate-frequency curves, indicating enhanced reward sensitivity. The 3.0 and 5.6 mg/kg doses significantly decreased the frequency required to maintain half-maximal responding (F50) by 18% and 32%, respectively, compared to saline controls (p<0.01). Maximum response rates were increased by 15-25% at the higher doses. The rate-frequency curve method demonstrated superior sensitivity compared to traditional threshold measures and provided additional information about changes in reward magnitude. These findings validate the rate-frequency curve approach as a robust method for characterizing drug reward effects in ICSS paradigms, offering enhanced temporal resolution and pharmacological sensitivity for neuropharmacological investigations.

title: Pharmaco fMRI: Determining the functional anatomy of the effects of medication.
abstract: Recent advances in functional magnetic resonance imaging (fMRI) have enabled the investigation of drug effects on neural circuits in vivo, providing crucial insights into medication mechanisms of action. However, standardized approaches for pharmaco-fMRI studies remain limited. This study aimed to establish methodological guidelines and demonstrate the utility of pharmaco-fMRI in characterizing the functional neuroanatomy of psychoactive medication effects. We conducted a double-blind, placebo-controlled crossover study in 24 healthy volunteers (ages 21-35) using a single oral dose of escitalopram (10mg) versus placebo. Participants underwent resting-state and task-based fMRI scanning at baseline and 3 hours post-administration. The task paradigm included emotional face processing and working memory components. We analyzed whole-brain activation patterns, functional connectivity, and region-of-interest analyses focused on limbic and prefrontal circuits. Escitalopram significantly altered BOLD signal in the amygdala during emotional face processing (p<0.001, FWE-corrected) and increased functional connectivity between the anterior cingulate cortex and dorsolateral prefrontal cortex during working memory tasks. Resting-state analyses revealed enhanced default mode network coherence following drug administration compared to placebo (p<0.05, cluster-corrected). Individual differences in drug response correlated with baseline connectivity strength in fronto-limbic circuits (r=0.67, p<0.01). These findings demonstrate that pharmaco-fMRI can reliably detect acute medication effects on neural circuits relevant to psychiatric disorders. This approach offers a powerful translational tool for understanding drug mechanisms, optimizing dosing strategies, and potentially predicting individual treatment responses in clinical populations.

title: Oral famotidine: a potential treatment for children with autism.
abstract: Autism is a pervasive developmental disorder characterized by impaired social interaction, communication deficits, and repetitive behaviors. Recent neurochemical investigations have implicated abnormal histaminergic neurotransmission in autistic pathophysiology, with elevated brain histamine levels reported in post-mortem studies. Histamine H2 receptors are widely distributed throughout the central nervous system and modulate neurotransmitter release, synaptic plasticity, and behavioral responses. We hypothesize that famotidine, a selective histamine H2 receptor antagonist commonly used for gastric acid suppression, may ameliorate core autistic symptoms through central histaminergic blockade. This hypothesis is supported by several converging lines of evidence: (1) famotidine crosses the blood-brain barrier and achieves therapeutically relevant CNS concentrations, (2) H2 receptor antagonism normalizes excessive histamine-mediated glutamate release implicated in autistic stereotypies, and (3) preliminary case reports suggest behavioral improvements in autistic children receiving famotidine for gastrointestinal symptoms. The proposed therapeutic mechanism involves famotidine's ability to modulate histamine-dopamine interactions in striatal and limbic circuits, potentially reducing repetitive behaviors and improving social responsiveness. Additionally, famotidine may indirectly enhance GABAergic inhibition through histaminergic disinhibition, addressing the excitatory-inhibitory imbalance proposed in autism. Given famotidine's established safety profile in pediatric populations and its unique mechanism of action distinct from current autism interventions, controlled clinical trials investigating oral famotidine as an adjunctive treatment for childhood autism are warranted. Such studies could provide novel insights into histaminergic contributions to autistic pathophysiology while offering a potentially accessible therapeutic option.

title: Applications of fMRI in translational medicine and clinical practice.
abstract: Functional MRI (fMRI) has had a major impact in cognitive neuroscience. fMRI now has a small but growing role in clinical neuroimaging, with initial applications to neurosurgical planning. Current clinical research has emphasized novel concepts for clinicians, such as the role of plasticity in recovery and the maintenance of brain functions in a broad range of diseases. There is a wider potential for clinical fMRI in applications ranging from presymptomatic diagnosis, through drug development and individualization of therapies, to understanding functional brain disorders. Realization of this potential will require changes in the way clinical neuroimaging services are planned and delivered.

title: Emerging trends in in vivo neurochemical monitoring by microdialysis.
abstract: Microdialysis has emerged as a pivotal technique for real-time monitoring of neurotransmitter dynamics in freely moving animals, providing unprecedented insights into neurochemical processes underlying behavior and disease. Recent technological advances have significantly enhanced the temporal resolution, sensitivity, and analytical capabilities of this approach. We review current innovations in microdialysis methodology, including the development of novel probe designs with improved biocompatibility, miniaturized sampling systems, and enhanced perfusion protocols that minimize tissue damage while maximizing recovery rates. Integration with advanced analytical techniques such as capillary electrophoresis, liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry, and biosensor arrays has enabled simultaneous monitoring of multiple neurotransmitters including dopamine, serotonin, GABA, and glutamate with sub-minute temporal resolution. Recent applications demonstrate the utility of these approaches in characterizing neurochemical alterations in models of Parkinson's disease, depression, and addiction, revealing dynamic patterns of neurotransmitter release that correlate with specific behavioral phenotypes. Furthermore, the combination of microdialysis with optogenetics and pharmacogenetics has provided new opportunities to establish causal relationships between neurochemical changes and neural circuit function. These methodological advances position microdialysis as an increasingly powerful tool for translational neuroscience research, offering potential applications in drug development and personalized medicine approaches for neuropsychiatric disorders. Future developments focusing on wireless telemetry and chronic monitoring capabilities promise to further expand the clinical relevance of in vivo neurochemical assessment.

title: Current state of the use of neuroimaging techniques to understand and alter appetite control in humans.
abstract: The neural mechanisms underlying appetite regulation have become increasingly accessible through advanced neuroimaging techniques, offering unprecedented insights into human feeding behavior and potential therapeutic targets. Functional magnetic resonance imaging (fMRI), positron emission tomography (PET), and single-photon emission computed tomography (SPECT) have emerged as powerful tools to investigate the complex interplay between homeostatic and hedonic appetite control systems. This review synthesizes recent evidence from neuroimaging studies examining appetite-regulating brain circuits and interventions designed to modulate these networks. Key findings demonstrate that the hypothalamus, insula, orbitofrontal cortex, and striatum show distinct activation patterns in response to food cues, nutritional states, and pharmacological interventions. Resting-state connectivity analyses reveal altered network dynamics in obesity and eating disorders, particularly involving default mode and salience networks. Recent intervention studies utilizing real-time fMRI neurofeedback, transcranial magnetic stimulation, and deep brain stimulation show promising results in modulating appetite-related brain activity. Pharmacological neuroimaging studies of GLP-1 agonists, naltrexone-bupropion combinations, and novel neuropeptide targets demonstrate measurable changes in central appetite circuits that correlate with clinical outcomes. However, significant methodological challenges remain, including standardization of hunger states, food stimuli, and analysis approaches across studies. Future directions include the development of personalized neuroimaging biomarkers for treatment selection and the integration of multimodal imaging approaches with metabolic measurements to better understand individual differences in appetite control mechanisms.
journal: Current opinion in clinical nutrition and metabolic care

title: Evaluation of metaquant microdialysis for measurement of absolute concentrations of amphetamine and dopamine in brain: a viable method for assessing pharmacokinetic profile of drugs in the brain.
abstract: Accurate measurement of absolute drug concentrations in brain tissue remains a critical challenge in neuropharmacology research. Traditional microdialysis techniques provide relative concentration changes but yield recovery-dependent values that complicate pharmacokinetic interpretation. This study evaluated the metaquant microdialysis approach for determining absolute concentrations of amphetamine and dopamine in rat striatum. Male Sprague-Dawley rats (n=8) were implanted with CMA/12 probes in the dorsal striatum. Following amphetamine administration (2.5 mg/kg i.p.), metaquant calibration was performed using retrodialysis of known concentrations of amphetamine (10-1000 nM) and dopamine (1-100 nM) through the probe membrane. Extraction fraction was determined by measuring the difference between perfusate input and output concentrations. Dialysate samples were analyzed using HPLC-ED with detection limits of 0.5 nM for amphetamine and 0.2 nM for dopamine. The metaquant method yielded extraction fractions of 0.18 ± 0.03 for amphetamine and 0.22 ± 0.04 for dopamine in vivo. Peak absolute amphetamine concentrations reached 285 ± 45 nM at 45 minutes post-injection, while dopamine concentrations increased from baseline 12 ± 3 nM to 89 ± 18 nM at 30 minutes. Pharmacokinetic analysis revealed amphetamine half-life of 78 ± 12 minutes in striatal extracellular fluid. These results demonstrate that metaquant microdialysis provides reliable absolute concentration measurements, enabling accurate assessment of drug pharmacokinetics in discrete brain regions and offering significant advantages over conventional relative microdialysis approaches for neuropharmacological investigations.

title: Wireless voltammetry recording in unanesthetised behaving rats.
abstract: In vivo voltammetry is a valuable technique for rapid measurement of dopamine in the brain of freely behaving rats. Using a conventional voltammetry system, however, behavioural freedom is restricted by cables connecting the head assembly to the measurement system. To overcome these difficulties, we developed a wireless voltammetry system utilizing radio waves. This system consisted of a potentiostat and transmitter system that was mounted on the back of the rat, and a receiver and analysis system. A single-step pulse (100-250 mV) was applied at 4 Hz after an activation pulse to a carbon fibre recording electrode (diameter: 7 microm). Measurement of dopamine (detection limit: 2.7 x 10(-7)M) was demonstrated in vitro. In vivo experiment was performed at least 1 week after the recording electrode was implanted in the rat striatum. Administration of 2-phenylethylamine to rats increased dopamine signal current, which was consistent with the result in the microdialysis measurement. During a resident-intruder test, dopamine signal current in a resident rat increased upon introduction of an intruder rat. These results show that the present wireless system is useful for a long-term measurement of dopamine in behaving rats.

title: Neuropsychopharmacology at the new millennium: new industry directions.
abstract: The neuropharmaceutical industry stands at a critical juncture as we enter the new millennium, with traditional drug discovery approaches yielding diminishing returns and escalating development costs threatening innovation in central nervous system therapeutics. This review examines emerging paradigms in neuropsychopharmacology research and development based on analysis of industry trends, patent filings, and clinical trial data from 1990-1999. We surveyed major pharmaceutical companies (n=25) and biotechnology firms (n=47) regarding research priorities, technological adoptions, and strategic directions for the next decade. The genomics revolution has fundamentally altered target identification strategies, with 73% of surveyed companies now incorporating pharmacogenomic approaches compared to 12% in 1995. High-throughput screening capabilities have expanded 50-fold on average, while companies are increasingly focusing on mechanism-based drug design rather than serendipitous discovery. Neuroinformatics and computational modeling have emerged as essential tools, with 68% of firms establishing dedicated bioinformatics divisions. Clinical development strategies are shifting toward earlier proof-of-concept studies using biomarkers and surrogate endpoints, potentially reducing late-stage failures. Therapeutic areas showing greatest industry investment include Alzheimer's disease, depression-resistant conditions, and schizophrenia cognitive deficits. Strategic alliances between pharmaceutical companies and academic institutions have increased 340% since 1995. These findings suggest that successful neuropsychopharmacology in the coming decade will require integration of genomics, advanced screening technologies, and novel clinical trial designs to address the growing burden of neuropsychiatric disorders while maintaining economic viability.
journal: Neuropsychopharmacology : official publication of the American College of Neuropsychopharmacology

title: The distributed auditory cortex.
abstract: A synthesis of cat auditory cortex (AC) organization is presented in which the extrinsic and intrinsic connections interact to derive a unified profile of the auditory stream and use it to direct and modify cortical and subcortical information flow. Thus, the thalamocortical input provides essential sensory information about peripheral stimulus events, which AC redirects locally for feature extraction, and then conveys to parallel auditory, multisensory, premotor, limbic, and cognitive centers for further analysis. The corticofugal output influences areas as remote as the pons and the cochlear nucleus, structures whose effects upon AC are entirely indirect, and it has diverse roles in the transmission of information through the medial geniculate body and inferior colliculus. The distributed AC is thus construed as a functional network in which the auditory percept is assembled for subsequent redistribution in sensory, premotor, and cognitive streams contingent on the derived interpretation of the acoustic events. The confluence of auditory and multisensory streams likely precedes cognitive processing of sound. The distributed AC constitutes the largest and arguably the most complete representation of the auditory world. Many facets of this scheme may apply in rodent and primate AC as well. We propose that the distributed auditory cortex contributes to local processing regimes in regions as disparate as the frontal pole and the cochlear nucleus to construct the acoustic percept.

title: The stanley neuropathology consortium integrative database: a novel, web-based tool for exploring neuropathological markers in psychiatric disorders and the biological processes associated with abnormalities of those markers.
abstract: The Stanley Neuropathology Consortium has generated extensive postmortem brain tissue data from individuals with schizophrenia, bipolar disorder, major depression, and matched controls over the past decade. However, the integration and accessibility of these diverse datasets has remained a significant challenge for the research community. We developed a comprehensive, web-based integrative database to facilitate exploration of neuropathological markers across psychiatric disorders and enable systematic investigation of associated biological processes. The database incorporates quantitative neuropathological data from over 400 subjects, including measurements of neuronal density, glial cell populations, synaptic proteins, and inflammatory markers across multiple brain regions. We implemented advanced search algorithms and data visualization tools, allowing users to query specific markers, compare across diagnostic groups, and examine correlations with demographic and clinical variables. The platform integrates pathway analysis tools to identify enriched biological processes associated with observed neuropathological abnormalities. Our analysis revealed significant alterations in oligodendrocyte populations in the prefrontal cortex across all three psychiatric disorders, with the most pronounced deficits observed in schizophrenia. Microglial activation markers showed disorder-specific patterns, with elevated CD68 immunoreactivity in bipolar disorder and increased HLA-DR expression in major depression. Pathway analysis identified disrupted myelination, synaptic transmission, and inflammatory response processes as common themes across disorders. This integrative database represents a valuable resource for the psychiatric research community, enabling data-driven hypothesis generation and facilitating the identification of convergent neuropathological mechanisms underlying major mental illness.
journal: Neuropsychopharmacology : official publication of the American College of Neuropsychopharmacology

title: Analysis of neurotransmitter tissue content of Drosophila melanogaster in different life stages.
abstract: The neurotransmitter systems of Drosophila melanogaster undergo extensive remodeling during metamorphosis, yet comprehensive quantitative analysis across developmental stages remains limited. This study employed high-performance liquid chromatography with electrochemical detection (HPLC-ECD) and liquid chromatography-tandem mass spectrometry (LC-MS/MS) to quantify dopamine, serotonin, octopamine, tyramine, and GABA content in whole-body extracts of D. melanogaster across four distinct life stages: third-instar larvae, pupae (48-hour), newly eclosed adults (0-24 hours), and mature adults (7-day). Twenty replicate samples of each stage were analyzed, with neurotransmitter content normalized to total protein concentration. Dopamine levels showed a biphasic pattern, decreasing 3.2-fold from larvae to pupae (p<0.001), then increasing 4.7-fold in newly eclosed adults before stabilizing in mature adults. Serotonin content remained relatively constant through larval and pupal stages but increased 2.8-fold in adults (p<0.001). Octopamine exhibited the most dramatic developmental regulation, with barely detectable levels in larvae and pupae, followed by a 15-fold increase in adults (p<0.001). Tyramine showed similar but less pronounced changes, increasing 6.2-fold from pupae to adults. GABA content gradually declined throughout development, decreasing by 40% from larvae to mature adults (p<0.05). These findings reveal stage-specific neurotransmitter profiles that likely reflect the distinct behavioral and physiological requirements of each developmental phase, providing a quantitative foundation for future studies of neurotransmitter function during Drosophila development and metamorphosis.

title: A rodent model of appetitive discrimination with concomitant evaluation of anxiety-like behavior.
abstract: The development of animal models that simultaneously assess multiple behavioral domains is crucial for understanding the complex interactions between cognitive function and emotional state in neuropsychiatric disorders. Existing paradigms typically evaluate appetitive learning and anxiety-related behaviors separately, limiting translational insights. We developed a novel behavioral apparatus combining elements of operant conditioning chambers with anxiety-testing environments to concurrently measure appetitive discrimination learning and anxiety-like behavior in rats. Male Sprague-Dawley rats (n=48) were trained to discriminate between two auditory cues (2 kHz vs 8 kHz tones) for sucrose reinforcement while navigating an elevated platform with open and enclosed compartments. Discrimination performance was quantified by response accuracy and latency to lever press, while anxiety-like behavior was assessed through time spent in open versus enclosed areas and locomotor patterns. Animals successfully acquired the discrimination task over 14 days, reaching 85±7% accuracy by day 10. Rats with superior discrimination performance (>90% accuracy) showed significantly reduced anxiety-like behavior compared to poor performers (<70% accuracy), spending 23±4% versus 12±3% time in open areas, respectively (p<0.01). Administration of diazepam (1.0 mg/kg, i.p.) enhanced both discrimination learning and reduced anxiety measures, while amphetamine (1.5 mg/kg, i.p.) improved discrimination but increased anxiety-related behaviors. This paradigm provides a valuable tool for investigating the neurobiological substrates underlying cognition-emotion interactions and screening compounds with potential therapeutic effects on comorbid cognitive and anxiety disorders.

title: The psychopharmacology of Huntington disease.
abstract: Huntington disease (HD) is a progressive neurodegenerative disorder characterized by motor dysfunction, cognitive decline, and psychiatric manifestations that significantly impact patient quality of life. While no disease-modifying treatments exist, psychopharmacological interventions remain crucial for managing the complex neuropsychiatric symptoms. This comprehensive review examines current evidence for pharmacological management of psychiatric comorbidities in HD, including depression, anxiety, psychosis, and behavioral disturbances. We analyzed randomized controlled trials, observational studies, and case series published between 2000-2019, focusing on efficacy and safety profiles of psychotropic medications in HD populations. Selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors, particularly sertraline and citalopram, demonstrate modest efficacy for depression with acceptable tolerability profiles. Atypical antipsychotics, especially olanzapine and quetiapine, show benefit for psychotic symptoms and agitation, though careful monitoring for extrapyramidal side effects is essential given underlying motor pathology. Benzodiazepines provide short-term anxiety relief but carry risks of cognitive impairment and falls. Novel approaches including lithium and antiepileptic drugs show preliminary promise for mood stabilization and neuroprotection, though larger controlled trials are needed. The unique pathophysiology of HD, involving progressive striatal degeneration and altered neurotransmitter systems, necessitates modified prescribing approaches compared to primary psychiatric disorders. Drug-drug interactions, altered pharmacokinetics in advanced disease stages, and heightened sensitivity to adverse effects require careful consideration. Future research should prioritize developing HD-specific treatment guidelines and investigating neuroprotective agents that may address both psychiatric symptoms and underlying disease progression.

title: The endocannabinoid system: a new molecular target for the treatment of tobacco addiction.
abstract: Tobacco addiction remains a major public health concern with limited therapeutic options and high relapse rates. Recent advances in understanding the endocannabinoid system (ECS) have revealed its crucial role in reward processing and addiction pathways, suggesting potential therapeutic applications for smoking cessation. The present study investigated the effects of cannabinoid CB1 receptor modulation on nicotine self-administration and withdrawal behaviors in rodent models. Male Sprague-Dawley rats were trained to self-administer nicotine (0.03 mg/kg/infusion) and subsequently treated with the CB1 receptor antagonist rimonabant (0.3-3.0 mg/kg, i.p.) or the fatty acid amide hydrolase (FAAH) inhibitor URB597 (0.1-1.0 mg/kg, i.p.). Nicotine self-administration, progressive ratio performance, cue-induced reinstatement, and somatic withdrawal signs were assessed. Rimonabant dose-dependently reduced nicotine self-administration by 45-78% and significantly attenuated cue-induced reinstatement without affecting food-maintained responding. Conversely, URB597 enhanced endogenous anandamide levels and reduced somatic withdrawal symptoms including weight loss, teeth chattering, and anxiety-like behaviors. Neurochemical analysis revealed that both treatments modulated dopamine release in the nucleus accumbens, with rimonabant reducing nicotine-evoked dopamine increases by 60%. These findings demonstrate that pharmacological manipulation of the ECS can effectively reduce nicotine-seeking behaviors and withdrawal symptoms through distinct but complementary mechanisms. The data support the therapeutic potential of targeting cannabinoid signaling pathways for tobacco addiction treatment, warranting further clinical investigation of ECS modulators as novel smoking cessation aids.

title: Pharmacological imaging as a tool to visualise dopaminergic neurotoxicity.
abstract: Dopaminergic neurotoxicity underlies several neurodegenerative disorders, yet current methods for visualizing and quantifying neuronal damage in vivo remain limited. This study evaluated the utility of pharmacological imaging approaches combining positron emission tomography (PET) with selective radioligands to assess dopaminergic neurotoxicity in real-time. Male Sprague-Dawley rats (n=48) received unilateral striatal injections of 6-hydroxydopamine (6-OHDA, 8 μg) or vehicle. Animals underwent longitudinal PET imaging using [¹¹C]raclopride for dopamine D2 receptor availability and [¹¹C]PE2I for dopamine transporter density at 1, 3, 7, and 28 days post-lesion. Behavioral assessment via amphetamine-induced rotation and post-mortem tyrosine hydroxylase immunohistochemistry served as validation measures. 6-OHDA lesioning produced progressive decreases in striatal [¹¹C]PE2I binding, with 45±8% reduction at day 3 and 78±6% reduction at day 28 compared to contralateral striatum (p<0.001). [¹¹C]Raclopride binding showed initial increases (125±12% at day 3) followed by normalization by day 28, consistent with denervation supersensitivity. Strong correlations were observed between [¹¹C]PE2I binding and both rotational behavior (r=-0.84, p<0.001) and tyrosine hydroxylase-positive cell counts (r=0.79, p<0.001). These findings demonstrate that pharmacological PET imaging provides sensitive, quantitative assessment of dopaminergic neurotoxicity progression. The temporal resolution and non-invasive nature of this approach offers significant advantages for monitoring disease progression and evaluating neuroprotective interventions in preclinical models of Parkinson's disease and related disorders.

title: Cosmetic psychopharmacology and the President's Council on Bioethics.
abstract: The emerging field of cosmetic psychopharmacology—the use of psychoactive medications to enhance normal psychological functioning rather than treat diagnosed mental illness—has generated significant ethical and policy debates. The President's Council on Bioethics has raised concerns about the implications of pharmaceutical cognitive and mood enhancement for human nature, authentic selfhood, and social justice. This analysis examines the neurobiological basis for these concerns and evaluates current evidence regarding the efficacy and safety of off-label psychotropic use in healthy populations. We reviewed clinical trials of stimulants, antidepressants, and anxiolytics in non-clinical samples, along with neuroimaging studies of enhancement effects on brain function. Additionally, we analyzed bioethical arguments presented in the Council's reports and related policy documents. Current evidence suggests modest cognitive improvements from stimulants like modafinil and amphetamines in healthy individuals, with limited data on long-term safety. Antidepressants may enhance emotional resilience and social cognition in non-depressed populations, while anxiolytics can reduce performance anxiety. However, individual responses vary considerably, and mechanisms of action in normal brains remain poorly understood. The Council's concerns about authenticity and coercion appear philosophically complex but empirically unsubstantiated. More problematic are issues of equitable access and potential pressure for enhancement use in competitive environments. Rigorous long-term studies are needed to establish safety profiles for cosmetic psychopharmacological interventions. Regulatory frameworks should focus on evidence-based safety standards rather than essentialist notions of human nature, while addressing legitimate concerns about social justice and autonomous choice.

title: The mammalian olivocochlear system--a legacy of non-cerebellar research in the Mugnaini lab.
abstract: Although the major emphasis of Enrico Mugnaini's research has been on investigations of the cerebellum, a significant amount of work over a relatively short span of time was also done in his lab on a number of other brain systems. These centered on sensory systems. One of these extra-cerebellar systems that he embraced was the auditory system. Portions of the cochlear nucleus, the first synaptic relay station along the central auditory pathways, possess a cerebellar-like circuitry and neurochemistry, and this no doubt lured Enrico into the auditory field. As new tools became available to pursue neuroanatomical research in general, which included a novel antibody to glutamic acid decarboxylase (GAD), Enrico's lab soon branched out into investigating many other brain structures beyond the cerebellum, with an overall goal of producing a map illustrating GAD expression in the brain. In collaboration with long-term colleagues, one of these many non-cerebellar regions he took an interest in was an efferent pathway originating in the superior olive and projecting to the cochlea, the peripheral end organ for hearing. There was a need for a more complete neurochemical map of this olivocochlear efferent system, and armed with new antibodies and well-established tract tracing tools, together we set out to further explore this system. This short review describes the work done with Enrico on the olivocochlear system of rodents, and also continues the story beyond Enrico's lab to reveal how the work done in his lab fits into the larger scheme of current, ongoing research into the olivocochlear system.

title: The Psychopharmacology of Obsessive-Compulsive Disorder: A Preclinical Roadmap.
abstract: Obsessive-compulsive disorder (OCD) affects 1-3% of the global population and is characterized by intrusive thoughts and repetitive behaviors mediated by dysregulated cortico-striato-thalamo-cortical (CSTC) circuits. Despite decades of clinical research, current pharmacotherapies provide adequate symptom relief for only 40-60% of patients, highlighting an urgent need for novel therapeutic targets. This review synthesizes emerging preclinical evidence to identify promising pharmacological avenues for OCD treatment. We conducted a comprehensive analysis of rodent models exhibiting OCD-like phenotypes, including genetic knockout models, pharmacological induction paradigms, and behavioral paradigms such as marble burying and schedule-induced polydipsia. Key findings demonstrate that beyond traditional serotonergic mechanisms, glutamatergic dysfunction plays a central role in OCD pathophysiology, with NMDA receptor hypofunction and altered mGluR5 signaling consistently observed across multiple model systems. Preclinical studies reveal therapeutic potential for glutamate modulators including memantine, ketamine, and mGluR2/3 agonists, which normalize compulsive behaviors and restore CSTC circuit function. Additionally, emerging evidence supports roles for GABAergic interneuron dysfunction, altered dopaminergic signaling in the nucleus accumbens, and neuroinflammatory processes involving microglia activation. Novel targets showing preclinical efficacy include sigma-1 receptor modulators, adenosine A2A receptor antagonists, and histone deacetylase inhibitors. Integration of optogenetic and chemogenetic approaches has further elucidated circuit-specific mechanisms and identified the orbitofrontal cortex and anterior cingulate cortex as critical nodes for therapeutic intervention. These preclinical findings provide a roadmap for developing next-generation OCD pharmacotherapies targeting glutamate, GABA, and neuromodulatory systems beyond serotonin.

title: Intracellular and juxtacellular staining with biocytin.
abstract: Many physiological studies require microscopic examination of the recorded neuron for identification. This unit describes how intracellular and extracellular recording can be combined with single-neuron staining to enable sequential physiological and morphological studies.

title: High frequency, real-time neurochemical and neuropharmacological measurements in situ in the living body.
abstract: Real-time monitoring of neurochemical dynamics in living neural tissue remains a critical challenge in neuropharmacology research, limiting our understanding of drug action mechanisms and therapeutic optimization. Traditional sampling methods lack the temporal resolution necessary to capture rapid neurotransmitter fluctuations and drug-target interactions occurring on millisecond timescales. We developed and validated a novel carbon fiber microelectrode array system coupled with fast-scan cyclic voltammetry (FSCV) for simultaneous, high-frequency detection of dopamine, serotonin, and norepinephrine in freely-moving rodents. The biosensor platform achieved sub-second temporal resolution (100 Hz sampling) with nanomolar sensitivity and minimal tissue damage. We tested the system by monitoring real-time neurochemical responses to acute administration of cocaine (10 mg/kg, i.p.), fluoxetine (20 mg/kg, i.p.), and L-DOPA (100 mg/kg, i.p.) in the striatum and nucleus accumbens of awake rats (n=8 per group). Results demonstrated rapid dopamine transporter blockade within 2.3 ± 0.4 seconds of cocaine administration, with peak concentrations occurring at 47 ± 12 seconds. Fluoxetine selectively elevated serotonin levels with a delayed onset of 23 ± 6 seconds, while L-DOPA produced sustained dopamine elevation lasting 180 ± 25 minutes. Cross-correlation analysis revealed previously undetected interactions between monoamine systems during pharmacological perturbation. This technology enables unprecedented temporal resolution for studying neuropharmacological mechanisms in vivo, providing critical insights for rational drug design and personalized medicine approaches in neurological and psychiatric disorders.
journal: Translational research : the journal of laboratory and clinical medicine

title: Neuroimaging in dementia with Lewy bodies: metabolism, neurochemistry, and morphology.
abstract: Dementia with Lewy bodies (DLB) is recognized as one of the most common forms of neurodegenerative dementia. Neuroimaging contributes to a better understanding of the pathophysiology of DLB by examining alterations in brain metabolism, neurochemisty, and morphology in living patients. Neuroimaging can provide objective and quantifiable antemortem markers for the presence of and the progression of DLB and permits differentiation from other dementias. This article reviews current neuroimaging findings in DLB with particular attention to occipital hypometabolism, dopaminergic and cholinergic deficits, and medial temporal lobe atrophy as measured by positron emission tomography, single-photon emission computed tomography, and magnetic resonance imaging.
journal: Journal of geriatric psychiatry and neurology

title: Effects of triiodothyronine and fluoxetine on 5-HT1A and 5-HT1B autoreceptor activity in rat brain: regional differences.
abstract: Thyroid hormones have been implicated in the modulation of serotonergic neurotransmission and may enhance the therapeutic efficacy of selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors in treating depression. However, the regional specificity of thyroid hormone effects on serotonin autoreceptor function remains poorly understood. We investigated the combined effects of triiodothyronine (T3) and fluoxetine on 5-HT1A and 5-HT1B autoreceptor activity across different brain regions in male Sprague-Dawley rats. Animals received daily injections of T3 (25 μg/kg, i.p.), fluoxetine (10 mg/kg, i.p.), or their combination for 14 days. Autoreceptor function was assessed using in vivo microdialysis coupled with local perfusion of selective agonists 8-OH-DPAT (5-HT1A) and CP-93129 (5-HT1B) in the dorsal raphe nucleus, hippocampus, and frontal cortex. Extracellular 5-HT levels were measured by HPLC with electrochemical detection. Fluoxetine alone produced modest desensitization of 5-HT1A autoreceptors in all regions examined, while 5-HT1B autoreceptor sensitivity remained unchanged. T3 monotherapy had minimal effects on autoreceptor function. However, combined T3 and fluoxetine treatment resulted in marked desensitization of both 5-HT1A and 5-HT1B autoreceptors, with the most pronounced effects observed in the dorsal raphe nucleus and frontal cortex compared to hippocampus. The regional heterogeneity in autoreceptor desensitization correlated with differential expression of thyroid hormone receptors. These findings suggest that T3 enhances fluoxetine-induced autoreceptor desensitization in a region-specific manner, providing mechanistic insight into thyroid hormone augmentation strategies in antidepressant therapy.

title: Proteomics and peptidomics in neuroscience. Experience of capabilities and limitations in a neurochemical laboratory.
abstract: The increasing use of proteomics has created a basis for new strategies to develop methodologies for rapid identification of protein patterns in living organisms. It has also become evident that proteomics has other potential applications than protein and peptide identification, e.g. protein characterization, with the aim of revealing their structure, function(s) and interactions of proteins. In comparative proteomics studies, the protein expression of a certain biological system is compared with another system or the same system under perturbed conditions. Global identification of proteins in neuroscience is extremely complex, owing to the limited availability of biological material and very low concentrations of the molecules. Moreover, in addition to proteins, there are number of peptides that must also be considered in global studies on the central nervous system. In this overview, we focus on and discuss problems related to the different sources of biological material and sample handling, which are part of all preparatory and analytical steps. Straightforward protocols are desirable to avoid excessive purification steps, since loss of material at each step is inevitable. We would like to merge the two worlds of proteomics/peptidomics and neuroscience, and finally we consider different practical and technical aspects, illustrated with examples from our laboratory.

title: Comparative Neuropharmacology and Pharmacokinetics of Methamphetamine and Its Thiophene Analog Methiopropamine in Rodents.
abstract: Methiopropamine is a novel psychoactive substance (NPS) that is associated with several cases of clinical toxicity, yet little information is available regarding its neuropharmacological properties. Here, we employed in vitro and in vivo methods to compare the pharmacokinetics and neurobiological effects of methiopropamine and its structural analog methamphetamine. Methiopropamine was rapidly distributed to the blood and brain after injection in C57BL/6 mice, with a pharmacokinetic profile similar to that of methamphetamine. Methiopropamine induced psychomotor activity, but higher doses were needed (Emax 12.5 mg/kg; i.p.) compared to methamphetamine (Emax 3.75 mg/kg; i.p.). A steep increase in locomotor activity was seen after a modest increase in the methiopropamine dose from 10 to 12.5 mg/kg, suggesting that a small increase in dosage may engender unexpectedly strong effects and heighten the risk of unintended overdose in NPS users. In vitro studies revealed that methiopropamine mediates its effects through inhibition of norepinephrine and dopamine uptake into presynaptic nerve terminals (IC50 = 0.47 and 0.74 µM, respectively), while the plasmalemmal serotonin uptake and vesicular uptake are affected only at high concentrations (IC50 > 25 µM). In summary, methiopropamine closely resembles methamphetamine with regard to its pharmacokinetics, pharmacodynamic effects and mechanism of action, with a potency that is approximately five times lower than that of methamphetamine.

title: High temporal resolution for in vivo monitoring of neurotransmitters in awake epileptic rats using brain microdialysis and capillary electrophoresis with laser-induced fluorescence detection.
abstract: Epilepsy is characterized by complex alterations in neurotransmitter dynamics during seizure events, yet conventional analytical methods lack the temporal resolution necessary to capture rapid neurochemical changes in vivo. We developed an integrated system combining brain microdialysis with capillary electrophoresis and laser-induced fluorescence detection (CE-LIF) to monitor glutamate, GABA, and glycine concentrations with unprecedented temporal resolution in awake, freely-moving epileptic rats. Male Sprague-Dawley rats (n=8) were implanted with chronic hippocampal electrodes and microdialysis probes following pilocarpine-induced status epilepticus. Microdialysate samples were collected at 30-second intervals and analyzed using a custom-built CE-LIF system with 488-nm argon laser excitation and fluorescein isothiocyanate derivatization. The analytical system achieved detection limits of 0.8 nM for glutamate, 1.2 nM for GABA, and 0.6 nM for glycine, with separation completed within 2 minutes. During spontaneous seizures, glutamate concentrations increased 340±45% above baseline within 60 seconds of electrographic seizure onset, while GABA levels showed a delayed increase of 180±30% at 2-3 minutes post-ictal. Glycine remained relatively stable throughout seizure events. The glutamate/GABA ratio peaked at 4.2±0.8 during ictal periods compared to baseline values of 1.8±0.2. This high-resolution monitoring system provides critical insights into the temporal sequence of excitatory and inhibitory neurotransmitter release during epileptic seizures and represents a significant advancement in real-time neurochemical analysis for epilepsy research.

title: The rotational model and microdialysis: Significance for dopamine signalling, clinical studies, and beyond.
abstract: The rotational model of Parkinson's disease, first described by Ungerstedt in the 1970s, remains a cornerstone for investigating dopaminergic dysfunction and therapeutic interventions. When combined with in vivo microdialysis, this model provides unprecedented insights into real-time neurotransmitter dynamics within the basal ganglia circuitry. This review synthesizes three decades of research utilizing the rotational model-microdialysis paradigm to examine dopamine signalling mechanisms, evaluate therapeutic strategies, and translate findings to clinical applications. The unilateral 6-hydroxydopamine lesion creates a reproducible model of asymmetric dopaminergic denervation, enabling quantitative assessment of rotational behavior while simultaneously monitoring extracellular dopamine, its metabolites, and other neurotransmitters through microdialysis probes implanted in the striatum. Studies demonstrate that amphetamine-induced rotation correlates inversely with residual dopamine content, while L-DOPA-induced rotation reflects the degree of denervation supersensitivity. Microdialysis reveals that effective therapeutic interventions, including L-DOPA, dopamine agonists, and deep brain stimulation, normalize aberrant dopamine release patterns and reduce rotational asymmetry. Furthermore, this combined approach has elucidated the roles of dopamine transporters, vesicular storage, and glial uptake in parkinsonian pathophysiology. The model's predictive validity for clinical outcomes has been demonstrated through successful translation of dopamine replacement strategies, continuous drug delivery systems, and neuroprotective agents from preclinical studies to human trials. Contemporary applications extend beyond Parkinson's disease to include investigations of addiction, schizophrenia, and neurodevelopmental disorders, establishing this methodology as an indispensable tool for advancing our understanding of dopaminergic neurotransmission and developing targeted therapeutic interventions.

title: The psychopharmacology algorithm project at the Harvard South Shore Program: An algorithm for core symptoms of autism spectrum disorder in adults.
abstract:
**Background:** Autism spectrum disorder (ASD) affects approximately 1-2% of adults, yet evidence-based psychopharmacological interventions for core symptoms remain limited. Current treatment approaches often rely on off-label use of medications targeting associated comorbidities rather than core ASD symptomatology. The Harvard South Shore Program developed a systematic algorithm to guide clinicians in medication selection for core ASD symptoms in adults.

**Methods:** We conducted a retrospective analysis of 147 adults with ASD (mean age 28.4 ± 8.2 years, 68% male) treated using our standardized algorithm from 2017-2019. The algorithm prioritized selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors (SSRIs) for repetitive behaviors and social communication deficits, followed by atypical antipsychotics for severe behavioral symptoms, and adjunctive treatments including oxytocin and N-acetylcysteine. Primary outcomes were measured using the Social Responsiveness Scale-2 (SRS-2) and Repetitive Behavior Scale-Revised (RBS-R) at baseline, 12, and 24 weeks.

**Results:** Algorithm implementation resulted in significant improvements in SRS-2 total scores (baseline 76.3 ± 12.1 vs. 24 weeks 68.9 ± 14.2, p<0.001) and RBS-R scores (baseline 42.7 ± 18.3 vs. 24 weeks 35.1 ± 16.8, p=0.003). Sertraline was most frequently prescribed (n=89, 61%), followed by risperidone (n=34, 23%). Treatment response rates were 58% for social communication symptoms and 52% for repetitive behaviors. Discontinuation due to adverse effects occurred in 18% of participants.

**Conclusions:** A systematic psychopharmacological algorithm demonstrated measurable improvements in core ASD symptoms among adults. These findings support the potential efficacy of structured medication approaches, though randomized controlled trials are needed to establish definitive treatment guidelines.

title: Succi nervorum: a brief history of neurochemistry.
abstract: The study of neurochemistry has evolved dramatically since the early anatomical descriptions of nervous tissue, yet the historical development of our understanding of neural chemical processes remains poorly documented in contemporary literature. This review examines the progression of neurochemical research from the initial observations of "succi nervorum" (nerve juices) in the 18th century through the establishment of modern neurotransmitter theory. We conducted a comprehensive analysis of primary historical sources, including original manuscripts, laboratory notebooks, and published communications from 1750 to 1970, supplemented by bibliometric analysis of key publications in neurochemistry. Our investigation reveals three distinct phases in neurochemical discovery: the pre-electrical period (1750-1850) characterized by humoral theories of nerve function, the electrophysiological era (1850-1920) dominated by purely electrical models of neural transmission, and the modern chemical transmission period (1920-1970) establishing neurotransmitter mechanisms. Critical developments included du Bois-Reymond's early speculation about chemical transmission in 1877, Elliott's recognition of sympathetic nerve-adrenergic relationships in 1904, and Dale's definitive acetylcholine studies beginning in 1914. The transition from vague concepts of "nerve essences" to precise molecular mechanisms required fundamental advances in analytical chemistry, electrophysiology, and pharmacology. Understanding this historical progression illuminates current challenges in neuropsychopharmacology and provides context for emerging fields such as neuroproteomics and metabolomics, suggesting that historical perspective remains essential for interpreting contemporary neurochemical research paradigms.

title: Plate reader-based assays for measuring cell viability, neuroprotection and calcium in primary neuronal cultures.
abstract: Primary neuronal cultures serve as essential models for investigating neurodegeneration and neuroprotective mechanisms, yet standardized high-throughput methods for assessing neuronal health remain limited. We developed and validated a suite of plate reader-based assays optimized for primary cortical and hippocampal neurons to measure cell viability, neuroprotection, and intracellular calcium dynamics. Primary neurons were isolated from embryonic day 18 rat cortices and hippocampi and cultured in 96-well plates. Cell viability was assessed using MTT, LDH release, and calcein-AM/propidium iodide fluorescence assays. Neuroprotection was evaluated by pre-treating cultures with candidate compounds followed by glutamate or staurosporine-induced excitotoxicity. Intracellular calcium measurements utilized Fluo-4 AM loading with real-time fluorescence detection. All assays demonstrated excellent reproducibility (CV <15%) and Z-factors >0.5, indicating robust assay performance. The MTT assay showed superior sensitivity to neuronal death compared to LDH release, while the dual fluorescence viability assay provided rapid kinetic measurements. Calcium assays successfully detected both NMDA receptor-mediated and voltage-gated calcium channel responses with EC50 values consistent with patch-clamp data. Known neuroprotectants including BDNF and MK-801 showed dose-dependent protective effects against excitotoxic insults. These validated protocols provide researchers with reliable, cost-effective methods for medium-throughput screening of neuroprotective compounds and mechanistic studies in primary neuronal cultures. The standardized approaches facilitate inter-laboratory comparisons and accelerate discovery of novel therapeutic targets for neurodegenerative diseases.

title: Cholinergic effects on fear conditioning II: nicotinic and muscarinic modulations of atropine-induced disruption of the degraded contingency effect.
abstract: **Background and rationale:** Previous studies have demonstrated that systemic atropine administration disrupts the degraded contingency effect in auditory fear conditioning, suggesting that cholinergic signaling is critical for detecting predictive relationships between conditioned and unconditioned stimuli. However, the relative contributions of nicotinic and muscarinic receptor subtypes to this phenomenon remain unclear. **Objectives:** We investigated whether selective nicotinic or muscarinic receptor manipulations could modulate atropine-induced deficits in degraded contingency learning. **Methods:** Male Sprague-Dawley rats (n=8-10 per group) received auditory fear conditioning under either paired or degraded contingency conditions (50% CS-US pairing probability with additional unpaired USs). Animals received systemic injections of atropine (50 mg/kg), nicotine (0.5 mg/kg), scopolamine (1.0 mg/kg), mecamylamine (3.0 mg/kg), or vehicle 30 minutes prior to training. Freezing responses were measured during CS presentations 24 hours post-training. **Results:** Vehicle-treated rats showed significantly reduced freezing to the CS under degraded versus paired conditions, confirming intact contingency detection. Atropine eliminated this discrimination, with degraded contingency animals showing freezing levels comparable to paired controls. Co-administration of nicotine partially rescued the degraded contingency effect in atropine-treated rats, while scopolamine alone replicated atropine's effects. Mecamylamine had no significant impact on contingency learning. **Conclusions:** These findings suggest that muscarinic receptors are primarily responsible for contingency detection in fear learning, while nicotinic receptor activation can partially compensate for broad cholinergic blockade. This supports a model where cholinergic modulation of attention and associative learning involves complementary muscarinic and nicotinic mechanisms.

title: High-sensitive liquid chromatographic method for determination of neuronal release of serotonin, noradrenaline and dopamine monitored by microdialysis in the rat prefrontal cortex.
abstract: Microdialysis coupled with liquid chromatography has become an essential technique for monitoring neurotransmitter release in vivo, but existing methods often lack the sensitivity required for simultaneous detection of multiple monoamines in small brain regions. We developed a highly sensitive HPLC method with electrochemical detection for the simultaneous quantification of serotonin (5-HT), noradrenaline (NA), and dopamine (DA) in microdialysate samples from rat prefrontal cortex. Male Sprague-Dawley rats (n=8) were implanted with microdialysis probes (2 mm membrane length) in the medial prefrontal cortex. Dialysate samples (20 μL) were collected at 20-minute intervals and analyzed using a C18 reverse-phase column with a mobile phase containing sodium acetate buffer, methanol, and octanesulfonic acid at pH 4.2. Detection was performed using a glassy carbon electrode at +0.65V versus Ag/AgCl reference. The method demonstrated excellent linearity (r² > 0.998) over the concentration range 0.1-50 fmol for all three neurotransmitters, with detection limits of 0.05 fmol for 5-HT, 0.08 fmol for NA, and 0.03 fmol for DA. Intra-day and inter-day precision were <8% for all compounds. Basal extracellular concentrations were 1.2 ± 0.3 fmol/20μL for 5-HT, 2.1 ± 0.4 fmol/20μL for NA, and 0.8 ± 0.2 fmol/20μL for DA. Pharmacological validation using selective reuptake inhibitors confirmed the neuronal origin of detected neurotransmitters. This sensitive method enables reliable monitoring of monoaminergic neurotransmission in the prefrontal cortex and should prove valuable for neuropharmacological studies investigating cognitive and emotional behaviors.

title: Neuropharmacological properties of Trichosanthes dioica root.
abstract: The roots of Trichosanthes dioica Roxb. have been traditionally used in Chinese folk medicine for treating neurological disorders, yet their neuropharmacological mechanisms remain poorly understood. This study investigated the neuroprotective and psychoactive properties of T. dioica root extracts using established in vivo and in vitro models. Aqueous and methanolic extracts were prepared from dried T. dioica roots and standardized using HPLC analysis. Male Wistar rats (n=8 per group) received oral administration of extracts (100, 200, and 400 mg/kg) for behavioral assessments including elevated plus maze, forced swimming test, and Morris water maze. Neuroprotective effects were evaluated using H2O2-induced oxidative stress in primary cortical neurons. GABA and dopamine receptor binding affinities were determined through radioligand binding assays. Phytochemical analysis revealed significant concentrations of cucurbitacins, flavonoids, and triterpene saponins. The methanolic extract demonstrated dose-dependent anxiolytic activity in the elevated plus maze test (p<0.01) and improved spatial memory performance in the Morris water maze compared to controls. In the forced swimming test, both extracts significantly reduced immobility time, suggesting antidepressant-like effects. Neuronal viability increased by 34% and 28% with aqueous and methanolic extracts respectively against H2O2-induced cytotoxicity. Moderate binding affinity was observed for GABAA receptors (Ki = 145 μM) but not dopaminergic receptors. These findings suggest that T. dioica root possesses significant neuropharmacological properties, potentially mediated through GABAergic modulation and antioxidant mechanisms, supporting its traditional therapeutic applications in neuropsychiatric conditions.

title: The blood-brain barrier in vitro: the second decade.
abstract: Ever since the discovery of Paul Ehrlich (1885 Das Sauerstoff-bedürfnis des Organismus: Hirschwald, Berlin) about the restricted material exchange, existing between the blood and the brain, the ultimate goal of subsequent studies has been mainly directed towards the elucidation of relative importance of different cellular compartments in the peculiar penetration barrier consisting the structural basis of the blood-brain barrier (BBB). It is now generally agreed that, in most vertebrates, the endothelial cells of the central nervous system (CNS) are responsible for the unique penetration barrier, which restricts the free passage of nutrients, hormones, immunologically relevant molecules and drugs to the brain. After an era of studying with endogenous or exogenous tracers the unique permeability properties of cerebral endothelial cells in vivo, the next generation, i.e. the in vitro blood-brain barrier model system was introduced in 1973. Recent advances in our knowledge of the BBB have in part been made by studying the properties and function of cerebral endothelial cells (CEC) with this in vitro approach. This review summarizes the results obtained on isolated brain microvessels in the second decade of its advent.

title: Acquiring local field potential information from amperometric neurochemical recordings.
abstract: Fast-scan cyclic voltammetry (FSCV) and constant potential amperometry are widely used electrochemical techniques for real-time monitoring of neurotransmitter release in behaving animals. However, these methods typically focus solely on neurochemical signals while discarding potentially valuable electrophysiological information present in the raw current recordings. Here we describe a novel signal processing approach to extract local field potential (LFP) information from amperometric neurochemical recordings using carbon fiber microelectrodes. We developed filtering algorithms to isolate low-frequency components (1-100 Hz) from high-frequency electrochemical noise in amperometric recordings obtained from the nucleus accumbens and dorsal striatum of anesthetized rats during electrical stimulation of dopaminergic pathways. Simultaneous recordings were performed using conventional tungsten microelectrodes for LFP validation. Our approach successfully recovered LFP-like signals that correlated strongly with conventional electrophysiological recordings (r = 0.78 ± 0.12, n = 8 animals). The extracted signals exhibited characteristic LFP features including evoked responses to electrical stimulation and spontaneous oscillatory activity in the theta (4-8 Hz) and gamma (30-80 Hz) frequency bands. Signal-to-noise ratio of recovered LFPs was optimal when amperometric recordings were performed at +0.7 V vs Ag/AgCl reference electrode. This method provides a cost-effective means to simultaneously monitor neurochemical and electrophysiological activity from a single electrode, potentially reducing tissue damage while increasing information yield from individual recording sites. The technique may prove particularly valuable for chronic recording applications where electrode count is limited by surgical constraints or for studying neurochemical-electrophysiological coupling in freely moving animals.

title: Computational models of neuronal biophysics and the characterization of potential neuropharmacological targets.
abstract: The identification and characterization of potential pharmacological targets in neurology and psychiatry is a fundamental problem at the intersection between medicinal chemistry and the neurosciences. Exciting new techniques in proteomics and genomics have fostered rapid progress, opening numerous questions as to the functional consequences of ligand binding at the systems level. Psycho- and neuro-active drugs typically work in nerve cells by affecting one or more aspects of electrophysiological activity. Thus, an integrated understanding of neuropharmacological agents requires bridging the gap between their molecular mechanisms and the biophysical determinants of neuronal function. Computational neuroscience and bioinformatics can play a major role in this functional connection. Robust quantitative models exist describing all major active membrane properties under endogenous and exogenous chemical control. These include voltage-dependent ionic channels (sodium, potassium, calcium, etc.), synaptic receptor channels (e.g. glutamatergic, GABAergic, cholinergic), and G protein coupled signaling pathways (protein kinases, phosphatases, and other enzymatic cascades). This brief review of neuromolecular medicine from the computational perspective provides compelling examples of how simulations can elucidate, explain, and predict the effect of chemical agonists, antagonists, and modulators in the nervous system.

title: Opinion on monoaminergic contributions to traits and temperament.
abstract: Monoaminergic neurotransmitter systems have long been implicated in individual differences in personality traits and temperamental characteristics, yet their precise contributions remain contentious. This perspective synthesizes current evidence regarding dopaminergic, serotonergic, and noradrenergic influences on dimensional models of personality, particularly focusing on extraversion, neuroticism, and conscientiousness within the five-factor framework. We critically evaluate findings from pharmacological challenge studies, neuroimaging investigations of monoamine transporter and receptor densities, and genetic association studies of polymorphisms affecting monoaminergic function. Converging evidence suggests that dopaminergic pathways, particularly within mesolimbic and mesocortical circuits, contribute substantially to reward sensitivity and approach-related behaviors underlying extraversion. Serotonergic function appears most robustly linked to emotional regulation and stress reactivity associated with neuroticism, with 5-HT1A receptor availability and serotonin transporter expression showing consistent associations across methodologies. Noradrenergic contributions appear more complex, with evidence for involvement in both arousal-related aspects of extraversion and anxiety-related components of neuroticism. However, effect sizes remain modest, and replication challenges persist, particularly for candidate gene associations. We propose that monoaminergic systems likely exert their influence on temperament through dynamic interactions rather than simple one-to-one mappings between neurotransmitter function and personality dimensions. Future research should prioritize polygenic approaches, longitudinal designs examining neurotransmitter-environment interactions, and integration of multiple levels of analysis to advance understanding of neurochemical contributions to individual differences in behavior and temperament.
journal: Philosophical transactions of the Royal Society of London. Series B, Biological sciences

title: Schizophrenia: a review of neuropharmacology.
abstract: Schizophrenia represents one of the most challenging psychiatric disorders, affecting approximately 1% of the global population and imposing substantial burdens on healthcare systems. Despite decades of research, the underlying neuropharmacological mechanisms remain incompletely understood, limiting therapeutic advances. This comprehensive review examines current knowledge of neurotransmitter systems implicated in schizophrenia pathophysiology and evaluates existing pharmacological interventions. A systematic analysis of peer-reviewed literature from 1990-2003 was conducted using MEDLINE and PsycINFO databases, focusing on dopaminergic, glutamatergic, GABAergic, and serotonergic mechanisms in schizophrenia. Additional emphasis was placed on emerging theories regarding N-methyl-D-aspartate (NMDA) receptor hypofunction and its relationship to dopaminergic dysfunction. The traditional dopamine hypothesis, while foundational, appears insufficient to explain the full spectrum of schizophrenia symptoms, particularly negative symptoms and cognitive deficits. Evidence increasingly supports glutamatergic hypofunction as a primary mechanism, potentially upstream of dopaminergic abnormalities. NMDA receptor antagonist models demonstrate remarkable similarity to the full symptom profile of schizophrenia, including positive symptoms, negative symptoms, and cognitive impairments. Current antipsychotic medications, predominantly dopamine D2 receptor antagonists, show limited efficacy against negative symptoms and cognitive dysfunction. Atypical antipsychotics demonstrate modest improvements through multi-receptor targeting, but significant unmet therapeutic needs persist. Future neuropharmacological strategies should focus on glutamatergic enhancement, particularly NMDA receptor co-agonists and metabotropic glutamate receptor modulators. Novel approaches targeting GABAergic interneuron dysfunction and nicotinic acetylcholine receptors also warrant investigation. Understanding these complex neurotransmitter interactions may facilitate development of more effective therapeutic interventions.

title: Efficient measurement of endogenous neurotransmitters in small localized regions of central nervous systems in vitro with HPLC.
abstract: Traditional methods for measuring endogenous neurotransmitters in discrete brain regions have been limited by tissue volume requirements and analytical sensitivity. This study describes an optimized high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) system with electrochemical detection for quantifying dopamine, norepinephrine, serotonin, and their metabolites from microdissected brain regions weighing 0.5-2.0 mg. Brain slices (300 μm) from adult male Sprague-Dawley rats were prepared using a vibratome, and specific nuclei including the nucleus accumbens, caudate-putamen, and substantia nigra were microdissected under stereomicroscopic guidance. Tissue samples were homogenized in 0.1 M perchloric acid containing 0.1 mM EDTA and 10 ng/ml dihydroxybenzylamine as internal standard. The HPLC system employed a C18 reverse-phase column with a mobile phase consisting of 75 mM sodium phosphate buffer, 1.7 mM octanesulfonic acid, 25 μM EDTA, and 10% acetonitrile at pH 3.0. Detection limits were 0.5 pg for dopamine, 1.0 pg for norepinephrine, and 0.8 pg for serotonin. Intra-assay coefficient of variation was <5% for all compounds. Regional neurotransmitter distributions obtained were consistent with known neuroanatomical patterns, with dopamine levels highest in striatal regions and serotonin predominant in raphe-projecting areas. This method provides reliable quantification of multiple neurotransmitters from small tissue samples, enabling detailed neurochemical mapping studies and reducing the number of animals required for neurochemical analyses while maintaining analytical precision suitable for pharmacological investigations.

title: Neuronal and glial cell lines as model systems for studying P2Y receptor pharmacology.
abstract: P2Y receptors are G protein-coupled receptors activated by extracellular nucleotides that play crucial roles in neurotransmission, glial cell activation, and neuroinflammation. While primary cell cultures have been traditionally used to study P2Y receptor function, immortalized cell lines offer advantages including reproducibility, genetic homogeneity, and ease of manipulation. We systematically characterized P2Y receptor expression and pharmacological profiles in five commonly used neuronal and glial cell lines: PC12, SH-SY5Y, U87-MG, C6, and 1321N1 astrocytoma cells. RT-PCR analysis revealed differential expression patterns, with PC12 cells predominantly expressing P2Y₁, P2Y₂, and P2Y₁₂ receptors, while U87-MG glioblastoma cells showed high levels of P2Y₂, P2Y₆, and P2Y₁₂. Functional responses were assessed using intracellular calcium mobilization assays and cAMP measurements following stimulation with selective agonists including ADP, UTP, UDP, and 2-MeSADP. SH-SY5Y neuroblastoma cells demonstrated robust calcium responses to ADP (EC₅₀ = 1.2 ± 0.3 μM) and UTP (EC₅₀ = 0.8 ± 0.2 μM), while C6 glioma cells showed preferential sensitivity to UDP stimulation. Pharmacological characterization using selective antagonists confirmed functional receptor subtypes, with suramin effectively blocking P2Y₂-mediated responses and MRS2179 specifically inhibiting P2Y₁ activation. These findings establish a comprehensive pharmacological profile of P2Y receptors in commonly used cell line models, providing valuable tools for investigating purinergic signaling mechanisms in neurodegenerative diseases, glial activation, and drug discovery applications.
