title: Volume versus wiring transmission in the brain: a new theoretical frame for neuropsychopharmacology.
abstract: A volume transmission mode of communication in brain was implicit already in the early work of Golgi, who postulated the existence of electrical signals in the extracellular fluid (ECF) based on Volta's "wet conductor" made by solutions. The term volume transmission is taken from the term volume conduction describing the flow of ionic currents in the ECF as a basis for the electrocorticogram. The slow VT mode includes also chemical signals and is opposed to the fast synaptic (wiring) transmission. Every neuron may function in a dual mode, the synaptic and the volume transmission mode, when considering the autocrine and synaptic classes of communication. The paracrine- and neuroendocrine-like classes only involve the VT mode in the latter case including the CSF as a route. The chemical signals for VT are the neuropeptides, but also the classical transmitters, the monoamines, acetylcholine, GABA, and glutamate can participate, when they operate via slow, high affinity G protein coupled receptors. Ions such as K+, Ca++, and H+ also function as VT signals. The hypothesis is also introduced that CO2 can act as a multifacit long-distance VT and WT regulator besides being part of the CO2/HCO3 buffer. CO2 via regulating NMDA receptor sensitivity can also regulate NO formation, which represents a paracrine and fast VT signal. The therapy of CNS disorders is also discussed in the frame of the wiring and VT concept. Two therapeutical approaches can therefore be developed, one based on increasing WT and one based on increasing VT. In contrast to the WT therapy, which must preserve the electrotemporal code, the VT therapy can operate also with postsynaptic agonists. Therefore, a therapeutic effect with such a drug indicates that the deficiency in the communication process operates via VT. In view of the lack of very effective negative feedbacks in VT vs. WT, VT therapy may produce less tolerance and drug dependency.

title: The neuropharmacological basis for the use of memantine in the treatment of Alzheimer's disease.
abstract: Alzheimer's disease (AD) represents a progressive neurodegenerative disorder characterized by cognitive decline, memory impairment, and eventual loss of functional independence. Current therapeutic approaches have focused primarily on cholinesterase inhibition, yet this strategy addresses only one aspect of the complex pathophysiology underlying AD. Emerging evidence suggests that excitotoxicity mediated by excessive N-methyl-D-aspartate (NMDA) receptor activation contributes significantly to neuronal death in AD. Memantine, a low-affinity, voltage-dependent NMDA receptor antagonist, offers a novel therapeutic mechanism by selectively blocking pathological receptor overactivation while preserving physiological glutamatergic neurotransmission. This review examines the neuropharmacological rationale for memantine use in AD treatment through analysis of preclinical studies, receptor binding assays, electrophysiological recordings, and clinical trial data from recent phase II and III studies. Preclinical investigations demonstrate that memantine exhibits neuroprotective properties in models of excitotoxic neuronal injury and reduces amyloid-beta-induced toxicity. Clinical studies involving moderate to severe AD patients reveal significant improvements in cognitive function, activities of daily living, and global clinical measures compared to placebo treatment. The drug's unique pharmacological profile, characterized by fast blocking kinetics and moderate affinity for NMDA receptors, allows for therapeutic intervention without interfering with normal synaptic transmission. These findings support memantine as a promising therapeutic agent that targets the glutamatergic system in AD, representing a complementary approach to existing cholinergic therapies and potentially offering disease-modifying benefits in addition to symptomatic improvement.

title: On the prescribing analyst.
abstract: The value of medication for some patients in psychoanalysis serves to highlight the potential challenges of the medical analyst and invites exploration into possible motivations for assuming the prescribing role. Prescribing medication is one way in which the medical analyst integrates the dual identities of physician and analyst while dealing with significant cultural influences and intrapsychic tensions. Technical challenges posed by assuming the prescribing role are explored, as are the potential benefits of split treatment. The educational implications of this argument are discussed in relation to identity formation for candidates who are physicians.

title: Rett syndrome: clinical characteristics and recent genetic advances.
abstract: Rett syndrome is a neurodevelopmental disorder that occurs almost exclusively in females. In recent years there has been increased knowledge concerning the multidisciplinary management of individuals with Rett syndrome. The aim of this paper is to provide an update of the clinical phenotype, natural history and current genetic understanding of the disorder. Rett syndrome is thought to be the second most common cause of severe mental retardation in females after Down syndrome. it now appears that females with RS present with a much broader phenotype than originally described. Recently, mutations in the MECP2 gene encoding X-linked methyl-CpG-binding-protein 2 have been identified in some females with Rett syndrome.

title: New treatments for ocular hypertension.
abstract: Ocular hypertension remains a significant risk factor for the development of primary open-angle glaucoma, necessitating effective therapeutic interventions to reduce intraocular pressure (IOP). Current first-line treatments, including topical beta-blockers and prostaglandin analogs, demonstrate limitations in efficacy and tolerability in certain patient populations. This study investigated the ocular hypotensive effects of a novel dual-action compound, NK-347, which combines selective alpha-2 adrenergic agonism with nitric oxide donation properties. Twenty-four New Zealand white rabbits were randomized to receive topical NK-347 (0.1%, 0.2%, or 0.5%), timolol 0.5%, or vehicle control twice daily for 14 days. IOP measurements were obtained using Goldmann applanation tonometry at baseline and at 2, 4, 6, and 8 hours post-dosing on days 1, 7, and 14. Aqueous humor dynamics were assessed using fluorophotometry, and autonomic innervation of ciliary tissues was evaluated through immunohistochemistry for tyrosine hydroxylase and neuronal nitric oxide synthase. NK-347 0.2% and 0.5% demonstrated significant IOP reduction compared to vehicle (p<0.001), with maximal effects observed at 4 hours post-dosing (mean reduction: 6.2±1.4 mmHg and 8.1±1.8 mmHg, respectively). The compound enhanced aqueous humor outflow facility by 34% (p<0.01) and reduced aqueous humor production by 18% (p<0.05). Immunohistochemical analysis revealed preserved sympathetic and nitrergic innervation patterns. These findings suggest NK-347 represents a promising therapeutic approach for ocular hypertension management through dual mechanisms targeting both aqueous humor dynamics and vascular regulation.

title: Current and Future Neuropharmacological Options for the Treatment of Essential Tremor.
abstract: Essential tremor (ET) is the most prevalent movement disorder, affecting approximately 1% of the global population and up to 5% of individuals over 65 years of age. Despite its high prevalence, current pharmacological treatments remain limited and often provide suboptimal symptom control. This comprehensive review examines the current neuropharmacological landscape for ET management and explores emerging therapeutic targets. Traditional first-line treatments include propranolol, a non-selective β-adrenergic antagonist, and primidone, an anticonvulsant, which demonstrate moderate efficacy in 50-70% of patients but are associated with significant side effects limiting long-term adherence. Second-line options such as topiramate, gabapentin, and benzodiazepines show variable effectiveness with considerable inter-individual variability. Recent mechanistic insights into ET pathophysiology have identified cerebellar dysfunction and altered GABAergic/glutamatergic neurotransmission as key therapeutic targets. Novel approaches under investigation include T-type calcium channel modulators, particularly zonisamide and 3,4-diaminopyridine, which have shown promising results in early-phase clinical trials. GABA-A receptor positive allosteric modulators and metabotropic glutamate receptor antagonists represent additional emerging strategies. Furthermore, personalized medicine approaches utilizing pharmacogenomic profiling may optimize treatment selection and dosing strategies. Deep brain stimulation remains the most effective intervention for medication-refractory cases, though surgical risks limit widespread application. The integration of precision medicine principles with novel pharmacological targets offers considerable promise for improving therapeutic outcomes in ET patients, potentially transforming the treatment paradigm for this common yet undertreated neurological condition.

title: Commentary: physician-scientist's frustrations fester.
abstract: The declining population of physician-scientists in neuropharmacology research represents a critical threat to translational medicine and drug discovery for neuropsychiatric disorders. This commentary examines the systemic barriers contributing to career dissatisfaction among physician-scientists in academic neuropharmacology from 2005-2008. We conducted structured interviews with 47 physician-scientists at various career stages across 12 academic medical centers, supplemented by analysis of NIH funding patterns and publication metrics. Participants reported increasing administrative burdens (89%), inadequate protected research time (83%), and insufficient institutional support for translational research (76%) as primary sources of frustration. Clinical revenue pressures resulted in mean research time declining from 65% to 41% over the study period. Early-career physician-scientists expressed particular concern about competing with PhD scientists for limited grant funding while maintaining clinical responsibilities. Senior investigators noted decreased mentorship capacity due to expanding administrative duties. NIH R01 success rates for physician-scientists in neuropharmacology fell from 23% to 17% during this period, compared to relatively stable rates for PhD investigators. Publication output per physician-scientist decreased by 28%, while time from bench discovery to clinical application increased significantly. These findings highlight the urgent need for institutional reforms including enhanced protected time policies, dedicated physician-scientist funding mechanisms, and reduced administrative burdens. Without systematic intervention, the continued exodus of physician-scientists threatens to create an irreversible gap between neuroscience discovery and clinical application, ultimately compromising patient care and therapeutic innovation in neuropsychiatric medicine.
journal: Neuropsychopharmacology : official publication of the American College of Neuropsychopharmacology

title: Drug-gut microbiota interactions: implications for neuropharmacology.
abstract: The fate and activity of drugs are frequently dictated not only by the host per se but also by the microorganisms present in the gastrointestinal tract. The gut microbiome is known to, both directly and indirectly, affect drug metabolism. More evidence now hints at the effects that drugs can have on the function and composition of the gut microbiome. Both microbiota-mediated alterations in drug metabolism and drug-mediated alterations in the gut microbiome can have beneficial or detrimental effects on the host. Greater insights into the mechanisms driving these reciprocal drug-gut microbiota interactions are needed to guide the development of microbiome-targeted dietary or pharmacological interventions, which may have the potential to enhance drug efficacy or reduce drug side effects. In this review, we explore the relationship between drugs and the gut microbiome, with a specific focus on potential mechanisms underpinning the drug-mediated alterations on the gut microbiome and the potential implications for psychoactive drugs. LINKED ARTICLES: This article is part of a themed section on When Pharmacology Meets the Microbiome: New Targets for Therapeutics? To view the other articles in this section visit http://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1111/bph.v175.24/issuetoc.

title: An in vivo technique for pharmacological manipulation of Drosophila brain during optical recording.
abstract: The fruit fly Drosophila melanogaster has emerged as a powerful model system for investigating neural circuits and behavior, yet current methods for simultaneous pharmacological manipulation and optical monitoring of brain activity in vivo remain limited. We developed a novel technique combining targeted drug delivery with real-time calcium imaging to enable pharmacological dissection of neural function in the intact Drosophila brain. Our approach utilizes a custom-designed perfusion chamber that maintains fly viability while allowing precise application of pharmacological agents to exposed brain regions. We incorporated a moveable micropipette system for localized drug delivery coupled with two-photon calcium imaging of genetically encoded indicators (GCaMP) in identified neurons. We validated this system using GABA receptor modulators (picrotoxin, muscimol) and demonstrated selective modulation of neuronal activity in mushroom body Kenyon cells and antennal lobe projection neurons. Picrotoxin application (10-100 μM) increased spontaneous calcium transient frequency by 340 ± 45%, while muscimol (50-200 μM) decreased activity by 78 ± 12% compared to saline controls (n=8-12 flies per condition). The preparation remained stable for over 2 hours, with consistent pharmacological responses and preserved circuit architecture as verified by post-hoc immunohistochemistry. This technique provides temporal resolution of seconds and spatial precision of individual dendritic compartments. Our method significantly advances the toolkit available for Drosophila neuropharmacology research and will facilitate mechanistic studies of neural circuit function, neurotransmitter systems, and drug screening applications in this genetically tractable model organism.

title: Nicotinic acetylcholine receptor redux: Discovery of accessories opens therapeutic vistas.
abstract: Nicotinic acetylcholine receptors (nAChRs) have long been considered promising therapeutic targets for neurological and psychiatric disorders, yet clinical translation has been hampered by limited selectivity and off-target effects. Recent structural and functional studies have revealed that nAChRs exist in dynamic complexes with auxiliary proteins that modulate their pharmacology, trafficking, and signaling properties. Here, we systematically characterized the composition and functional impact of nAChR-associated protein complexes in human brain tissue and heterologous systems using proximity labeling proteomics, electrophysiology, and pharmacological profiling. We identified 47 high-confidence nAChR-interacting proteins, including previously uncharacterized chaperones, scaffolding proteins, and regulatory enzymes. Notably, the auxiliary protein NACHO (neuronal acetylcholine receptor-associated protein) enhanced α7 nAChR surface expression 3.2-fold and shifted sensitivity to the positive allosteric modulator PNU-120596 by 15-fold in hippocampal neurons. Co-expression of the scaffolding protein PSD-95 with α4β2 nAChRs increased receptor clustering and altered desensitization kinetics, reducing IC50 values for novel selective antagonists by 8-fold. Viral-mediated knockdown of key auxiliary proteins in mouse prefrontal cortex significantly impaired working memory performance and altered nicotine reward behaviors. These findings demonstrate that nAChR auxiliary proteins represent unexplored pharmacological targets that could enable development of more selective therapeutic interventions. Our results suggest that targeting nAChR-accessory protein interactions, rather than the receptors themselves, may overcome historical limitations in nAChR-based drug discovery and open new therapeutic avenues for addiction, cognitive disorders, and neuropsychiatric diseases.

title: Twenty-Year Trends in the Psychopharmacological Treatment of Outpatients with Borderline Personality Disorder: A Cross-Sectional Naturalistic Study in Spain.
abstract: Although no psychotropic drugs have been officially approved for the treatment of borderline personality disorder (BPD), medications are routinely prescribed for these patients. The primary aim of this study was to evaluate changes in the pharmacological management of patients with BPD treated in an outpatient specific unit in Spain over the past 20 years, while a secondary aim was to identify the factors associated with the prescription. Observational and cross-sectional study of all patients with a primary diagnosis of BPD (n = 620) consecutively admitted to a BPD outpatient program in Barcelona, Spain, from 2001 through 2020. We examined trends in the prescription of antidepressants, benzodiazepines, mood stabilizers, and antipsychotics. For the analysis, prescription data were grouped into four 5-year periods (2001-2005, 2006-2010, 2011-2015, and 2016-2020). Logistic regression models were performed to identify sociodemographic and clinical variables associated with pharmacological prescription and polypharmacy. The percentage of patients receiving pharmacotherapy decreased over time. Antidepressant prescription rates remained high and stable over time (74% of patients), while benzodiazepine use decreased significantly during the study period (from 77 to 36%) and second-generation antipsychotic (SGA) use increased from 15 to 32%. Psychiatric comorbidity was the main factor associated with pharmacological treatment (odds ratio 2.5, 95% confidence interval 1.5-4.2) and polypharmacy, although a high percentage of patients without comorbidity were also taking medications. Over the past 20 years, the pharmacological treatment of BPD outpatients has undergone important changes, most notably the decrease in benzodiazepines and increase in SGAs. The findings of this study demonstrate that pharmacotherapy is much more prevalent in patients with BPD than recommended in most clinical guidelines.

title: Lobeline inhibits the neurochemical and behavioral effects of amphetamine.
abstract: Lobeline, an alkaloid derived from Lobelia inflata, has been shown to interact with neuronal nicotinic acetylcholine receptors and vesicular monoamine transporters. Given the structural similarities between lobeline and amphetamine, we investigated whether lobeline could modulate amphetamine's neurochemical and behavioral effects. Male Sprague-Dawley rats received intraperitoneal injections of lobeline (1, 3, or 10 mg/kg) 30 minutes prior to d-amphetamine (2 mg/kg). Extracellular dopamine levels in the nucleus accumbens were measured using in vivo microdialysis, while locomotor activity was assessed in automated activity chambers. Additionally, we examined lobeline's effects on amphetamine-induced stereotypy and conditioned place preference. Pretreatment with lobeline dose-dependently attenuated amphetamine-induced increases in accumbal dopamine release, with the highest dose reducing peak dopamine levels by 68% compared to amphetamine alone. Correspondingly, lobeline significantly inhibited amphetamine-induced hyperlocomotion and stereotypic behaviors in a dose-related manner. The 10 mg/kg dose of lobeline completely blocked the development of amphetamine-conditioned place preference. When administered alone, lobeline produced minimal effects on dopamine release or locomotor activity. These inhibitory effects were not attributable to general motor impairment, as lobeline did not affect rotarod performance or food-motivated behavior. Our findings suggest that lobeline acts as a functional antagonist of amphetamine's rewarding and stimulant properties, possibly through interference with vesicular dopamine storage or release mechanisms. These results support the potential therapeutic application of lobeline or related compounds in treating psychostimulant abuse disorders.
journal: The Journal of pharmacology and experimental therapeutics

title: Current evaluation of the millennium phytomedicine- ginseng (II): Collected chemical entities, modern pharmacology, and clinical applications emanated from traditional Chinese medicine.
abstract: This review, a sequel to part 1 in the series, collects about 107 chemical entities separated from the roots, leaves and flower buds of Panax ginseng, quinquefolius and notoginseng, and categorizes these entities into about 18 groups based on their structural similarity. The bioactivities of these chemical entities are described. The 'Yin and Yang' theory and the fundamentals of the 'five elements' applied to the traditional Chinese medicine (TCM) are concisely introduced to help readers understand how ginseng balances the dynamic equilibrium of human physiological processes from the TCM perspectives. This paper concerns the observation and experimental investigation of biological activities of ginseng used in the TCM of past and present cultures. The current biological findings of ginseng and its medical applications are narrated and critically discussed, including 1) its antihyperglycemic effect that may benefit type II diabetics; in vitro and in vivo studies demonstrated protection of ginseng on beta-cells and obese diabetic mouse models. The related clinical trial results are stated. 2) its aphrodisiac effect and cardiovascular effect that partially attribute to ginseng's bioactivity on nitric oxide (NO); 3) its cognitive effect and neuropharmacological effect that are intensively tested in various rat models using purified ginsenosides and show a hope to treat Parkinson's disease (PD); 4) its uses as an adjuvant or immunotherapeutic agent to enhance immune activity, appetite and life quality of cancer patients during their chemotherapy and radiation. Although the apoptotic effect of ginsenosides, especially Rh2, Rg3 and Compound K, on various tumor cells has been shown via different pathways, their clinical effectiveness remains to be tested. This paper also updates the antioxidant, anti-inflammatory, anti-apoptotic and immune-stimulatory activities of ginseng, its ingredients and commercial products, as well as common side effects of ginseng mainly due to its overdose, and its pharmacokinetics.

title: Neurobiology and neuroimmunology of Tourette's syndrome: an update.
abstract: Tourette's syndrome is a childhood-onset neuropsychiatric disorder characterized by the presence of both multiple motor and vocal tics. While the pathogenesis at a molecular and cellular level remains unknown, structural and functional neuroimaging studies point to the involvement of the basal ganglia and related cortico-striato-thalamo-cortical circuits as the neuroanatomical site for Tourette's syndrome. Moreover, Tourette's syndrome has a strong genetic component, and considerable progress has been made in understanding the mode of transmission and in identifying potential genomic loci. Summaries of recent findings in these areas will be reviewed, followed by a critical overview of findings both supporting and challenging the proposed autoimmune hypothesis of Tourette's syndrome. We conclude that Tourette's syndrome is a heterogeneous disorder, and that immune factors may indeed be involved in some patients.
journal: Cellular and molecular life sciences : CMLS

title: Effects of triiodothyronine and fluoxetine on 5-HT1A and 5-HT1B autoreceptor activity in rat brain: regional differences.
abstract: The thyroid hormone triiodothyronine (T3) augments and accelerates the effects of antidepressant drugs. Although the majority of studies showing this have used tricyclics, a few studies have shown similar effects with the selective serotonin re-uptake inhibitor (SSRI) fluoxetine. In this study we investigated the effects of fluoxetine (5 mg/kg), T3 (20 microg/kg) and the combination of these drugs, each administered daily for 7 days, on serotonergic function in the rat brain, using in vivo microdialysis. Fluoxetine alone induced a trend towards desensitization of 5-HT1A autoreceptors as shown by a reduction in the effect of 8-OH-DPAT to lower 5-HT levels in frontal cortex, and desensitized 5-HT1B autoreceptors in frontal cortex. The combination of fluoxetine and T3 induced desensitization of 5-HT1B autoreceptors in hypothalamus. Since there is evidence linking hypothalamic function and depression, we suggest that this effect may partly account for the therapeutic efficacy of the combination of an SSRI and T3.

title: LRRK2 mouse models: dissecting the behavior, striatal neurochemistry and neurophysiology of PD pathogenesis.
abstract: Mutations in leucine-rich repeat kinase 2 (LRRK2) are the most common genetic cause of familial Parkinson's disease (PD), resembling the sporadic disorder. Intensive effort has been directed toward LRRK2 mouse modeling and investigation, aimed at reproducing the human disease to inform mechanistic studies of pathogenesis and design of neuroprotective therapies. The physiological function of LRRK2 is still under exploration, but a clear role in striatal neurophysiology and animal behavior has emerged. Alterations in LRRK2 impair dopamine (DA) transmission, regulation and signaling, in addition to corticostriatal synaptic plasticity. Consistently, several subtle abnormalities in motor and nonmotor abilities have been demonstrated in LRRK2 genetic mouse models, generally paralleling preclinical symptoms of early DA dysfunction. However, the variability in model design and phenotypes observed requires a critical approach in interpreting the results, adapting the model used to the specific research question. Etiologically appropriate knockin mice might represent the ultimate animal model in which to study early disease mechanisms and therapies as well as to investigate drug effectiveness and off-target consequences.

title: Neuropharmacological modulation of cognitive deficits after brain damage.
abstract: Cognitive impairments following traumatic brain injury, stroke, and neurodegenerative diseases represent a major clinical challenge with limited therapeutic options. Recent advances in understanding the neurochemical basis of cognitive dysfunction have identified potential pharmacological targets for intervention. This review examines current evidence for neuropharmacological approaches to treating post-injury cognitive deficits, focusing on cholinergic, dopaminergic, and glutamatergic systems. We analyzed data from randomized controlled trials and case studies published between 1995-2004 involving patients with acquired brain injury. Cholinesterase inhibitors, particularly donepezil and rivastigmine, demonstrated modest but significant improvements in attention and memory function in 60-70% of patients with traumatic brain injury-related cognitive impairment. Dopaminergic agents including bromocriptine and methylphenidate showed efficacy in enhancing executive function and processing speed, with effect sizes ranging from 0.3-0.6. Modafinil emerged as a promising treatment for post-injury fatigue and attention deficits, with response rates approaching 65% in preliminary studies. AMPA receptor potentiators and NMDA receptor modulators showed encouraging results in animal models but remain in early-phase human trials. Combination therapies targeting multiple neurotransmitter systems demonstrated superior outcomes compared to monotherapy in limited studies. While current pharmacological interventions provide meaningful but moderate cognitive benefits, optimal treatment protocols remain undefined. Future research should focus on biomarker-guided personalized medicine approaches and novel neuroprotective strategies to maximize therapeutic potential in this vulnerable population.

title: Obsessive-compulsive disorder and tic syndromes.
abstract: The phenomenology of OCD and TS seem to match perfectly with the existing conceptualization of the functional relationship between frontal cortical and subcortical circuits. Failed editing of thoughts and impulses, perseverative patterns, and inhibitory deficits are the most convenient descriptors of the symptoms, and some operationalized measures can capture evidence for such deficits in TS and OCD patients. Beyond these expectations borne from conceptual models and some broad patterns of distributed metabolic disturbances in neuroimaging studies, a specific causal pathology within CSPT circuitry needs to be identified in these disorders. This is not a criticism of the existing studies of TS and OCD; to the contrary, the scarcity of pathologic material, the limits of resolution of existing technologies, and the heterogeneity of the phenotypes make the accomplishments of these studies more impressive. As clinicians strive to integrate clinical and scientific findings into coherent models for the pathophysiology of OCD and TS, it is useful to identify practical and effective strategies for therapeutic interventions.
journal: The Medical clinics of North America

title: Laboratory of receptor pharmacology.
abstract: G-protein coupled receptors (GPCRs) represent the largest class of membrane proteins targeted by therapeutic agents, yet the molecular mechanisms underlying ligand-receptor interactions and downstream signaling remain incompletely understood. We investigated the pharmacological properties and signaling pathways of dopamine D2 and serotonin 5-HT2A receptors using a combination of radioligand binding assays, functional cAMP measurements, and calcium mobilization studies in HEK293 cells stably transfected with human receptor constructs. Saturation binding experiments with [³H]spiperone and [³H]ketanserin revealed Kd values of 0.89 ± 0.12 nM and 2.3 ± 0.4 nM for D2 and 5-HT2A receptors, respectively. Competition binding studies with a panel of 15 structurally diverse ligands demonstrated significant differences in binding affinity profiles between receptor subtypes. Functional assays showed that D2 receptor activation by quinpirole inhibited forskolin-stimulated cAMP accumulation with an EC50 of 45 ± 8 nM, while 5-HT2A receptor stimulation by DOI increased intracellular calcium with an EC50 of 127 ± 23 nM. Pre-treatment with pertussis toxin abolished D2-mediated cAMP inhibition, confirming Gi/o protein coupling. Interestingly, several atypical antipsychotic drugs exhibited partial agonist properties at both receptors with varying efficacy profiles. These findings provide detailed pharmacological characterization of D2 and 5-HT2A receptors and support the hypothesis that functional selectivity at these targets may contribute to the therapeutic profiles of antipsychotic medications. Our receptor pharmacology platform offers valuable tools for drug discovery and mechanistic studies of GPCR signaling.

title: Nicotinic acetylcholine receptor redux: Discovery of accessories opens therapeutic vistas.
abstract: The neurotransmitter acetylcholine (ACh) acts in part through a family of nicotinic ACh receptors (nAChRs), which mediate diverse physiological processes including muscle contraction, neurotransmission, and sensory transduction. Pharmacologically, nAChRs are responsible for tobacco addiction and are targeted by medicines for hypertension and dementia. Nicotinic AChRs were the first ion channels to be isolated. Recent studies have identified molecules that control nAChR biogenesis, trafficking, and function. These nAChR accessories include protein and chemical chaperones as well as auxiliary subunits. Whereas some factors act on many nAChRs, others are receptor specific. Discovery of these regulatory mechanisms is transforming nAChR research in cells and tissues ranging from central neurons to spinal ganglia to cochlear hair cells. Nicotinic AChR-specific accessories also enable drug discovery on high-confidence targets for psychiatric, neurological, and auditory disorders.

title: Interaction Between the Trace Amine-Associated Receptor 1 and the Dopamine D2 Receptor Controls Cocaine's Neurochemical Actions.
abstract: The trace amine-associated receptor 1 (TAAR1) has emerged as a potential modulator of dopaminergic neurotransmission, but its precise interaction with dopamine D2 receptors (D2R) in mediating cocaine's effects remains unclear. We investigated the functional relationship between TAAR1 and D2R in controlling cocaine-induced neurochemical responses using pharmacological and molecular approaches in rodent models. Wild-type and TAAR1 knockout mice received acute cocaine administration (15 mg/kg, i.p.) with or without selective TAAR1 agonist RO5263397 (10 mg/kg) or D2R antagonist raclopride (1 mg/kg). Extracellular dopamine levels in the nucleus accumbens were measured using in vivo microdialysis, while D2R binding and cAMP signaling were assessed using radioligand binding and biochemical assays. Cocaine increased extracellular dopamine by 340% in wild-type mice, but this response was potentiated to 485% in TAAR1 knockout animals. TAAR1 activation with RO5263397 significantly reduced cocaine-induced dopamine efflux and attenuated D2R-mediated signaling through enhanced receptor internalization. Co-immunoprecipitation studies revealed direct protein-protein interactions between TAAR1 and D2R in striatal tissue. Functional coupling experiments demonstrated that TAAR1 activation inhibited D2R-mediated adenylyl cyclase suppression in a dose-dependent manner. These findings establish that TAAR1 functions as a negative allosteric modulator of D2R signaling and serves as an endogenous brake on cocaine's dopaminergic effects. The TAAR1-D2R interaction represents a novel regulatory mechanism that could be therapeutically targeted for substance use disorders, suggesting TAAR1 agonists may have clinical utility in treating cocaine addiction.

title: Extracellular trypsin increases ASIC1a selectivity for monovalent versus divalent cations.
abstract: Acid-sensing ion channels (ASICs) are proton-gated cation channels widely distributed throughout the nervous system and implicated in synaptic transmission, mechanosensation, and pathological conditions including ischemic brain injury. ASIC1a, the predominant brain isoform, exhibits relatively low selectivity between monovalent and divalent cations under physiological conditions. Previous studies have shown that extracellular proteases can modulate ASIC function, though the underlying mechanisms remain poorly understood. Here we investigated whether trypsin treatment affects the ionic selectivity of recombinant human ASIC1a channels expressed in Chinese hamster ovary (CHO) cells using whole-cell patch-clamp electrophysiology. Cells were treated with bovine pancreatic trypsin (10-100 μg/ml) for 2-15 minutes before recording acid-evoked currents (pH 6.0) in solutions containing various concentrations of Na+, K+, Ca2+, or Mg2+. Relative permeability ratios were calculated from reversal potential measurements using the Goldman-Hodgkin-Katz equation. Trypsin treatment (50 μg/ml, 5 min) significantly increased PNa+/PCa2+ from 2.1 ± 0.3 to 8.7 ± 1.2 (n=12, p<0.001) and PK+/PMg2+ from 1.8 ± 0.4 to 6.9 ± 0.8 (n=10, p<0.001). The effect was dose- and time-dependent, reversible upon trypsin removal, and blocked by the serine protease inhibitor PMSF. Current amplitude and pH sensitivity remained unchanged. These findings demonstrate that extracellular trypsin selectively enhances ASIC1a permeability to monovalent cations while reducing divalent cation flux, suggesting proteolytic modification of channel selectivity filter regions. This mechanism may be physiologically relevant during tissue injury when both acidosis and protease release occur simultaneously.

title: Cell-Specific Neuropharmacology.
abstract: The advent of modern molecular tools has revealed unprecedented cellular heterogeneity within neural circuits, challenging traditional pharmacological approaches that broadly target receptor subtypes across diverse cell populations. While classical neuropharmacology has provided valuable therapeutic interventions, the lack of cell-type specificity often results in unwanted side effects and limits therapeutic precision. Here we review emerging strategies that enable cell-specific drug targeting within the nervous system, including chemogenetic approaches, optopharmacology, and targeted drug delivery systems. Designer receptors exclusively activated by designer drugs (DREADDs) have demonstrated remarkable success in selectively modulating genetically-defined neuronal populations in both preclinical models and early clinical applications. Optopharmacological tools, combining photochemistry with pharmacology, allow spatiotemporal control of drug action with cellular precision. Additionally, antibody-drug conjugates and nanoparticle-based delivery systems show promise for targeting specific neural cell types based on surface marker expression. Recent applications in treating neuropsychiatric disorders, chronic pain, and neurodegenerative diseases highlight the therapeutic potential of cell-specific approaches. For instance, selective targeting of medium spiny neurons in Huntington's disease models has shown improved efficacy compared to conventional treatments. However, challenges remain in translation to clinical settings, including delivery across the blood-brain barrier, potential immunogenicity, and long-term safety profiles. Cell-specific neuropharmacology represents a paradigm shift toward precision medicine in neurology and psychiatry, offering opportunities to enhance therapeutic efficacy while minimizing off-target effects. Continued technological advances and interdisciplinary collaboration will be essential for realizing the full potential of this rapidly evolving field.

title: A simple, sensitive and widely applicable ELISA for S100B: Methodological features of the measurement of this glial protein.
abstract: S100B expression, particularly extracellular S100B, is used as a parameter of glial activation and/or death in several situations of brain injury. Several immunoassays for S100B measurement are available, which differ with regard to specificity, sensitivity, sample application, and, of course, economic costs. We standardized two protocols for S100B measurement (range between 1.9pg and 10ng/mL) in human and rat samples from brain and adipose tissues, blood serum, cerebrospinal fluid, urine and cell culture. Abundance and secretion of this protein in adipose tissue reinforces the caution about its origin in blood serum. Interestingly, S100B recognition was affected by the redox status of the protein. This aspect should be considered in S100B measurement, assuming that oxidized and reduced forms possibly coexist in vivo and the equilibrium can be modified by oxidative stress of physiological or pathological conditions or even by obtaining sample conditions.

title: Examining the 'psychopharmacology revolution' (1950-1980) through the advertising of psychoactive drugs in the British Medical Journal.
abstract: Many modern pharmaceutical products were launched during 1950-1980, as reflected in advertisements in the British Medical Journal ( BMJ). One of the first therapeutic areas to benefit from novel effective medications was psychiatry. We examined BMJ advertising material between 1950 and 1980, including every other issue over six-month periods (October-March) in 1950/1951, 1955/1956, 1957/1958, 1960/1961, 1962/1963, 1965/1966, 1967/1968, 1970/1971, 1972/1973, 1975/1976, 1980/1981. We recorded numbers of adverts for all pharmaceutical products and for psychiatric drugs; we also recorded trade names, generic names and marketing company. Advertising in BMJ peaked in the 1960s and declined markedly in the 1970s. Adverts for psychiatric drugs as a percentage of total pharmaceutical product advertising was broadly similar during 1955-1980, but with peaks in 1960/1961, 1970/1971 and 1975/1976, reflecting the entry of several novel compounds into the market. The peak marketing of antipsychotic drugs, sedatives and anxiolytic drugs was in 1960 and of antidepressants 1970. The time course of the rise of tricyclics and the switch from barbiturates to benzodiazepines can be seen. Drugs for psychiatry rose from ninth (1955/1956) to fourth (1975/1976) in terms of the number of products in the top 10 therapeutic areas. There is no evidence that they were advertised more aggressively (number of adverts/number of products). The birth of modern psychopharmacology is reflected in th e advertising of psychiatric drugs in BMJ. Many drugs currently used, or their closely related successors, were launched in the early to mid-1960s. This rise in modern pharmaceuticals preceded several other major therapeutic areas.

title: Microdialysis applications in neuroscience.
abstract: Microdialysis is a technique to monitor extracellular changes in living tissue. Substances present in the extracellular space, such as neurotransmitters and metabolites transported between cells and capillaries in the extracellular fluid (ECF), are major object. Since its introduction to the research of the nervous system, microdialysis has become a popular method for the measurements of brain chemistry and greatly affected in the fields of neuropharmacology, neuroanatomy and neurophysiology. Most of published papers using microdialysis have focused on the area of neuroscience, recently more biomedical application. In this review, we focused on cerebral microdialysis as a monitoring tool for physiologic and pathophysiologic changes in chemical processes in the brain. Then we presented the principle and various applications of cerebral microdialysis.

title: Team Evans and Watkins: Excitatory Amino Acid research at Bristol University 1973-1981.
abstract: Between 1973 and 1981, the research teams led by Evans and Watkins at Bristol University made seminal contributions to understanding excitatory amino acid neurotransmission, fundamentally shaping modern neuropharmacology. This historical review examines their pioneering work identifying glutamate receptors and establishing the pharmacological framework that underlies contemporary NMDA, AMPA, and kainate receptor classification. The Bristol group's systematic investigation of L-glutamate and L-aspartate as excitatory neurotransmitters began with electrophysiological studies on spinal neurons, demonstrating that these amino acids produced dose-dependent depolarization distinct from other neurotransmitter systems. Their development of selective antagonists, including 2-amino-5-phosphonovalerate (AP5) and 6,7-dinitroquinoxaline-2,3-dione (DNQX), provided crucial pharmacological tools for dissecting glutamatergic transmission. Subsequent binding studies and receptor autoradiography revealed heterogeneous glutamate binding sites throughout the central nervous system, with particularly high densities in hippocampus, cerebral cortex, and cerebellum. The team's characterization of N-methyl-D-aspartate (NMDA) as a selective agonist and their discovery of the glycine modulatory site established the foundation for understanding NMDA receptor function in synaptic plasticity, learning, and excitotoxicity. These discoveries directly influenced the development of neuroprotective strategies for stroke and neurodegenerative diseases, while their receptor classification system remains the standard framework for glutamate pharmacology. The Evans-Watkins collaboration exemplifies how systematic neuropharmacological research can transform our understanding of brain function and provide therapeutic targets for neurological disorders.

title: A temporary local energy pool coupled to neuronal activity: fluctuations of extracellular lactate levels in rat brain monitored with rapid-response enzyme-based sensor.
abstract: A successfully developed enzyme-based lactate microsensor with rapid response time allows the direct and continuous in vivo measurement of lactic acid concentration with high temporal resolution in brain extracellular fluid. The fluctuations coupled to neuronal activity in extracellular lactate concentration were explored in the dentate gyrus of the hippocampus of the rat brain after electrical stimulation of the perforant pathway. Extracellular glucose and oxygen levels were also detected simultaneously by coimplantation of a fast-response glucose sensor and an oxygen electrode, to provide novel information of trafficking of energy substances in real time related to local neuronal activity. The results first give a comprehensive picture of complementary energy supply and use of lactate and glucose in the intact brain tissue. In response to acute neuronal activation, the brain tissue shifts immediately to significant energy supply by lactate. A local temporary fuel "reservoir" is established behind the blood-brain barrier, evidenced by increased extracellular lactate concentration. The pool can be depleted rapidly, up to 28% in 10-12 s, by massive, acute neuronal use after stimulation and can be replenished in approximately 20 s. Glutamate-stimulated astrocytic glycolysis and the increase of regional blood flow may regulate the lactate concentration of the pool in different time scales to maintain local energy homeostasis.

title: Dementia: the neurochemical basis of putative transmitter orientated therapy.
abstract: The progressive cognitive decline characteristic of dementia syndromes has been increasingly linked to dysfunction of specific neurotransmitter systems, particularly cholinergic, noradrenergic, and serotonergic pathways. This review examines the neurochemical alterations underlying dementia and evaluates the therapeutic potential of transmitter-specific interventions. We analyzed postmortem brain tissue from 45 patients with Alzheimer's disease, 23 patients with multi-infarct dementia, and 32 age-matched controls using high-performance liquid chromatography to quantify neurotransmitter levels and enzymatic activities. Additionally, we assessed the efficacy of cholinesterase inhibitors, noradrenergic modulators, and serotonergic agents in 127 dementia patients across multiple clinical sites over 12 months. Alzheimer's disease patients showed marked reductions in cortical choline acetyltransferase activity (68% decrease, p<0.001) and acetylcholine levels (72% decrease, p<0.001) compared to controls. Norepinephrine concentrations were reduced by 45% (p<0.01), while serotonin levels decreased by 38% (p<0.05). Multi-infarct dementia exhibited similar but less pronounced deficits. Clinical trials demonstrated that physostigmine and tetrahydroaminoacridine produced modest but significant improvements in cognitive test scores (15-22% improvement, p<0.05), while combined cholinergic-noradrenergic therapy showed enhanced efficacy. These findings support the neurochemical basis of dementia and suggest that rational pharmacological interventions targeting specific transmitter deficits may offer therapeutic benefits. However, the modest clinical improvements indicate the need for more selective agents and combination therapies addressing multiple neurotransmitter systems simultaneously.

title: Behavioral and neurochemical effects in mice after one-generation exposure to low doses of manganese: Focus on offspring development.
abstract: The risk of exposure to toxic metals is a known concern to human populations. The overexposure to Mn can lead to a pathological condition, with symptoms similar to Parkinson's disease. Although toxicity of Mn has been reported, studies in neonates are scarce but necessary, as Mn can cross biological barriers. The present study evaluated if chronic perinatal exposure to Mn at low doses lead to neurotoxic effects in mice, after direct and indirect exposure. Couples of mice were exposed to Mn (0.013, 0.13, and 1.3 mg kg-1.day-1) for 60 days prior to mating, as well as during gestation and lactation. The offspring was distributed into two groups: animals that were not exposed after weaning - parental exposure only (PE); and animals subject to additional 60-day exposure through gavages after weaning - parental and direct exposure (PDE). Neurological effects were evaluated by Mn quantification, behavior tests and biochemical markers in the brain. PDE animals had alterations in short/long-term memory and increased anxiety-like behavior. Exposure to Mn triggered a decrease of glutathione-s-transferase and increase of cholinesterase activity in different regions of the brain. These findings highlight the risk of exposure to low doses of Mn over a generation and at early stages of development.

title: A conversation with Paul Greengard. Interview by Eric J Nestler.
abstract: Paul Greengard was born in New York City in 1925. After completing high school, he served three years in the US Navy during World War II and then completed his bachelor's degree at Hamilton College where he majored in physics and mathematics. He obtained a PhD in biophysics from Johns Hopkins University in 1953 and pursued postdoctoral training with Wilhelm Feldberg at the National Institute for Medical Research in England. After eight years as head of biochemistry at Geigy, and sabbaticals at Albert Einstein College of Medicine and Vanderbilt University, he joined the Yale University faculty as a full professor of pharmacology in 1968. While he was at Yale, Greengard's laboratory performed groundbreaking research, which demonstrated a role for cyclic nucleotides, protein kinases and protein phosphatases, and their protein substrates in the regulation of synaptic transmission. In 1983, Greengard moved to The Rockefeller University, where he has since served as the Vincent Astor Professor and Head of the Laboratory of Molecular and Cellular Neuroscience. Greengard's paradigm-shifting research has continued at Rockefeller and has informed our understanding and possible treatment of a host of brain disorders, including schizophrenia, Alzheimer's disease, Parkinson's disease, and depression. He is the author of more than 950 research articles and reviews. Greengard has received numerous awards and honors, including the Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine in 2000, the Metropolitan Life Foundation Award for Medical Research, The National Academy of Sciences Award in Neuroscience, the Ralph W. Gerard Prize in Neuroscience for the Society for Neuroscience, and the Karolinska Institutet's Bicentennial Gold Medal. He is a member of the US National Academy of Sciences and the Institute of Medicine of the National Academies. The following interview was conducted on May 29, 2012.
journal: Annual review of pharmacology and toxicology

title: In vivo monitoring of extracellular glutamate in the brain with a microsensor.
abstract: Dysregulation of glutamate neurotransmission has been implicated in numerous neurological disorders including stroke, epilepsy, and neurodegenerative diseases. Current methods for measuring extracellular glutamate in vivo, such as microdialysis, suffer from poor temporal resolution and tissue damage. We developed a novel glutamate-selective microsensor for real-time monitoring of extracellular glutamate concentrations in the living brain. The microsensor consists of a platinum microelectrode (25 μm diameter) coated with glutamate oxidase and poly(o-phenylenediamine) to confer selectivity and minimize interferents. The sensor was calibrated in vitro and demonstrated linear response to glutamate (10 μM to 1 mM, r² = 0.98) with minimal interference from ascorbic acid, dopamine, and GABA. Response time was < 2 seconds with detection limit of 5 μM. In anesthetized Sprague-Dawley rats (n=8), microsensors were stereotaxically implanted into the striatum and hippocampus. Baseline glutamate levels were 12.3 ± 2.1 μM in striatum and 8.7 ± 1.8 μM in hippocampus. Local application of potassium chloride (100 mM) produced rapid glutamate increases of 340 ± 45% in striatum and 280 ± 38% in hippocampus, returning to baseline within 5 minutes. Focal ischemia induced by middle cerebral artery occlusion resulted in sustained glutamate elevation (peak: 850 ± 120% of baseline) in the ischemic penumbra. This microsensor technology provides unprecedented temporal resolution for monitoring glutamate dynamics and offers valuable insights into excitotoxic mechanisms underlying neurological pathology.

title: Beyond the reward hypothesis: alternative functions of nucleus accumbens dopamine.
abstract: The nucleus accumbens (NAcc) has been predominantly studied within the framework of reward processing and addiction, where dopamine release is viewed as encoding reward prediction error and motivational salience. However, accumulating evidence suggests that NAcc dopamine serves broader computational functions beyond hedonic processing. This review synthesizes recent findings challenging the traditional reward hypothesis and proposes alternative roles for accumbens dopamine signaling. We examined literature from 2000-2005 encompassing electrophysiological recordings, microdialysis studies, and behavioral pharmacology experiments in rodents and non-human primates. Lesion studies utilizing 6-OHDA and pharmacological manipulations with dopamine receptor antagonists were analyzed alongside emerging optogenetic approaches. Our analysis reveals that NAcc dopamine responds robustly to aversive stimuli, uncertainty, and novelty independent of reward value. Dopaminergic signaling in the shell and core subregions exhibits distinct temporal dynamics during action selection, with phasic release preceding motor initiation rather than following reward delivery. Furthermore, dopamine depletion impairs effort-based decision making and behavioral switching without abolishing reward preference. These findings indicate that NAcc dopamine functions as a general motivational signal that energizes behavior and facilitates adaptive responding to environmental demands. Rather than simply encoding "wanting" or reward prediction, accumbens dopamine appears to regulate behavioral activation, attention allocation, and cognitive flexibility. This reconceptualization has important implications for understanding psychiatric disorders characterized by motivational deficits, including depression and schizophrenia, and suggests novel therapeutic targets beyond traditional reward-based frameworks.

title: [Research progress in neuropsychopharmacology updated for the post-genomic era].
abstract: The completion of the Human Genome Project in 2003 has fundamentally transformed neuropsychopharmacology research, shifting the paradigm from traditional phenotype-driven approaches to genotype-based drug discovery and personalized medicine strategies. This review examines the current state of neuropsychopharmacological research in the post-genomic era, analyzing developments from 2004 to 2009. We conducted a comprehensive literature analysis of major neuropsychopharmacology publications, focusing on genomics-based drug discovery, pharmacogenomics applications, and novel therapeutic targets identified through genomic approaches. Database searches included PubMed, Web of Science, and Japanese medical databases, encompassing studies on major psychiatric disorders including schizophrenia, bipolar disorder, major depression, and anxiety disorders. Our analysis reveals significant progress in several key areas: identification of over 150 novel CNS drug targets through genome-wide association studies (GWAS), development of pharmacogenomic testing for antidepressant and antipsychotic medications, and emergence of epigenetic-based therapeutic strategies. Notably, cytochrome P450 genotyping has become clinically relevant for personalizing psychotropic drug dosing, while GWAS have identified new susceptibility loci for psychiatric disorders. However, the translation of genomic discoveries into approved therapeutics remains limited, with only three genomics-derived neuropsychiatric drugs reaching clinical trials by 2009. The post-genomic era has revolutionized our understanding of psychiatric disorders' molecular basis, yet significant challenges remain in bridging genomic knowledge to clinical applications. Future research must focus on functional validation of genomic targets and development of more sophisticated personalized treatment algorithms to fully realize the potential of genomics-based neuropsychopharmacology.
journal: Nihon shinkei seishin yakurigaku zasshi = Japanese journal of psychopharmacology

title: Chemogenetics drives paradigm change in the investigation of behavioral circuits and neural mechanisms underlying drug action.
abstract: The advent of chemogenetic tools has revolutionized the field of neuropharmacology by enabling precise temporal and spatial control over neuronal activity in behaving animals. Traditional approaches to studying drug action and behavioral circuits have relied heavily on pharmacological interventions and lesion studies, which often lack specificity and reversibility. Designer Receptors Exclusively Activated by Designer Drugs (DREADDs) represent a transformative methodology that allows researchers to selectively modulate genetically-defined neuronal populations with high temporal precision. This review examines how chemogenetic approaches have advanced our understanding of neural circuits underlying addiction, reward processing, and drug-seeking behaviors over the past decade. We analyze key studies demonstrating the application of both excitatory (hM3Dq) and inhibitory (hM4Di) DREADDs in dissecting the functional roles of specific brain regions and cell types in behavioral responses to drugs of abuse. Chemogenetic manipulation of dopaminergic neurons in the ventral tegmental area, GABAergic interneurons in the nucleus accumbens, and glutamatergic projections from the prefrontal cortex has provided unprecedented insights into the circuit-level mechanisms of addiction and relapse. Furthermore, the development of next-generation chemogenetic tools, including light-sensitive variants and improved selectivity profiles, continues to expand experimental possibilities. The integration of chemogenetics with advanced behavioral paradigms, electrophysiology, and neuroimaging techniques has established new standards for mechanistic studies in behavioral neuroscience. These technological advances promise to accelerate the translation of basic research findings into targeted therapeutic interventions for substance use disorders and other neuropsychiatric conditions.

title: Psychopharmacology of traumatic brain injury.
abstract: Traumatic brain injury (TBI) represents a significant clinical challenge with complex pathophysiology involving excitotoxicity, neuroinflammation, and disrupted neurotransmitter systems. Current pharmacological interventions remain limited, necessitating comprehensive evaluation of existing psychotropic medications in TBI populations. This systematic review analyzed clinical trials and observational studies from 2010-2018 examining psychopharmacological treatments in TBI patients (n=4,847 across 23 studies). We evaluated efficacy and safety profiles of antidepressants, anticonvulsants, dopaminergic agents, and cognitive enhancers for post-TBI neuropsychiatric symptoms and cognitive dysfunction. Sertraline and citalopram demonstrated superior efficacy for post-TBI depression compared to tricyclic antidepressants, with fewer anticholinergic side effects potentially impairing cognitive recovery. Methylphenidate showed modest improvements in attention and processing speed (effect size 0.42, p<0.05) but increased seizure risk in acute phases. Levetiracetam emerged as the preferred anticonvulsant, showing neuroprotective properties and lower cognitive impairment compared to phenytoin or valproate. Amantadine administration within 30 days post-injury accelerated functional recovery in severe TBI patients (median recovery time 14 vs 21 days, p<0.01). However, modafinil showed inconsistent results for fatigue management. Notable safety concerns included increased intracranial pressure with certain antipsychotics and delayed wound healing with high-dose corticosteroids. These findings support a precision medicine approach to post-TBI psychopharmacology, emphasizing careful drug selection based on injury severity, timeline, and individual risk factors. Further research is needed to establish optimal dosing protocols and long-term safety profiles in this vulnerable population.

title: Affinity labeling the dopamine transporter ligand binding site.
abstract: The dopamine transporter (DAT) plays a crucial role in terminating dopaminergic neurotransmission by removing dopamine from the synaptic cleft, and represents a primary target for psychostimulants such as cocaine and amphetamine. Despite extensive pharmacological characterization, the precise molecular architecture of the DAT ligand binding site remains poorly understood. We employed photoaffinity labeling techniques to identify amino acid residues within the cocaine and dopamine binding domains of the human DAT. A series of photoreactive analogues of cocaine and dopamine were synthesized, including [³H]3β-[4-(4-azido-3-iodophenyl)methyl]tropane-2β-carboxylic acid methyl ester (RTI-82) and [³H]2-azido-dopamine. Membrane preparations from HEK293 cells stably expressing human DAT were incubated with photolabels under UV irradiation conditions. Covalently modified transporters were isolated by immunoprecipitation and subjected to proteolytic digestion followed by peptide mapping and Edman degradation sequencing. RTI-82 specifically labeled Asp79 in transmembrane domain 1 and Phe320 in transmembrane domain 6, while 2-azido-dopamine primarily modified Ser422 in transmembrane domain 8. Competition experiments with unlabeled ligands confirmed specificity of labeling. Mutational analysis revealed that substitution of labeled residues significantly reduced binding affinity for both substrate and inhibitor ligands. These results provide direct biochemical evidence for a multi-domain binding pocket involving transmembrane segments 1, 6, and 8, and suggest overlapping but distinct binding sites for dopamine and cocaine within the DAT structure. This photoaffinity mapping approach offers valuable insights into transporter structure-function relationships and may facilitate rational drug design efforts.

title: The Psychopharmacology of Obsessive-Compulsive Disorder: A Preclinical Roadmap.
abstract: This review evaluates current knowledge about obsessive-compulsive disorder (OCD), with the goal of providing a roadmap for future directions in research on the psychopharmacology of the disorder. It first addresses issues in the description and diagnosis of OCD, including the structure, measurement, and appropriate description of the disorder and issues of differential diagnosis. Current pharmacotherapies for OCD are then reviewed, including monotherapy with serotonin reuptake inhibitors and augmentation with antipsychotic medication and with psychologic treatment. Neuromodulatory therapies for OCD are also described, including psychosurgery, deep brain stimulation, and noninvasive brain stimulation. Psychotherapies for OCD are then reviewed, focusing on behavior therapy, including exposure and response prevention and cognitive therapy, and the efficacy of these interventions is discussed, touching on issues such as the timing of sessions, the adjunctive role of pharmacotherapy, and the underlying mechanisms. Next, current research on the neurobiology of OCD is examined, including work probing the role of various neurotransmitters and other endogenous processes and etiology as clues to the neurobiological fault that may underlie OCD. A new perspective on preclinical research is advanced, using the Research Domain Criteria to propose an adaptationist viewpoint that regards OCD as the dysfunction of a normal motivational system. A systems-design approach introduces the security motivation system (SMS) theory of OCD as a framework for research. Finally, a new perspective on psychopharmacological research for OCD is advanced, exploring three approaches: boosting infrastructure facilities of the brain, facilitating psychotherapeutic relearning, and targeting specific pathways of the SMS network to fix deficient SMS shut-down processes. SIGNIFICANCE STATEMENT: A significant proportion of patients with obsessive-compulsive disorder (OCD) do not achieve remission with current treatments, indicating the need for innovations in psychopharmacology for the disorder. OCD may be conceptualized as the dysfunction of a normal, special motivation system that evolved to manage the prospect of potential danger. This perspective, together with a wide-ranging review of the literature, suggests novel directions for psychopharmacological research, including boosting support systems of the brain, facilitating relearning that occurs in psychotherapy, and targeting specific pathways in the brain that provide deficient stopping processes in OCD.

title: Methodological approaches to exploring epileptic disorders in the human brain in vitro.
abstract: Brain surgery, and in particular epilepsy surgery, offers the unique opportunity to study viable human central nervous tissue in vitro. This does not only open a window to address the basic mechanisms underlying human disease, such as epilepsy, but it allows to venture into investigating neurophysiological functions per se. In the present paper, we describe the most commonly used methods in the electrophysiological (and, at least to some extent, also histochemical and molecular) analysis of human tissue in vitro. In addition, we consider the pitfalls and limitations of such studies, in particular regarding the issue of tissue sampling procedures and control experiments.

title: Different role of cAMP dependent protein kinase and CaMKII in H3 receptor regulation of histamine synthesis and release.
abstract: The histamine H3 receptor functions as an autoreceptor regulating histamine synthesis and release in the central nervous system, but the intracellular signaling mechanisms mediating these effects remain incompletely understood. We investigated the differential roles of cAMP-dependent protein kinase (PKA) and Ca2+/calmodulin-dependent protein kinase II (CaMKII) in H3 receptor-mediated regulation of histaminergic function using rat hypothalamic slices and cultured histaminergic neurons. H3 receptor activation with imetit (1-100 nM) dose-dependently decreased histidine decarboxylase (HDC) activity and histamine release, effects blocked by the H3 antagonist thioperamide. Co-immunoprecipitation studies revealed constitutive association between H3 receptors and both PKA regulatory subunits and CaMKII. Selective PKA inhibition with H-89 (10 μM) completely prevented imetit-induced suppression of HDC activity and histamine synthesis, while having no effect on histamine release inhibition. Conversely, CaMKII inhibition with KN-93 (10 μM) selectively blocked H3-mediated reduction of histamine release without affecting HDC regulation. Western blot analysis showed that H3 activation decreased PKA-mediated phosphorylation of HDC at Ser320, while simultaneously reducing CaMKII autophosphorylation at Thr286. Immunofluorescence microscopy revealed distinct subcellular localizations of H3-PKA and H3-CaMKII complexes, with PKA predominantly cytosolic and CaMKII enriched at synaptic terminals. These findings demonstrate that H3 autoreceptors utilize spatially segregated signaling pathways, with PKA specifically regulating histamine synthesis through HDC phosphorylation, while CaMKII selectively controls histamine release mechanisms. This dual-pathway organization may allow for independent fine-tuning of histaminergic neurotransmission under different physiological conditions.

title: A history of the Collegium Internationale Neuro-Psychopharmacologicum (1957-2004).
abstract: The Collegium Internationale Neuro-Psychopharmacologicum (CINP) was established in 1957 as the first international organization dedicated to advancing the emerging field of neuropsychopharmacology. This historical analysis examines the evolution of CINP from its founding through 2004, documenting its pivotal role in shaping modern psychopharmacological research and clinical practice. We conducted a comprehensive review of CINP archives, meeting proceedings, published abstracts, and interviews with founding members and past presidents to trace the organization's scientific and organizational development. Key milestones were identified through systematic analysis of biennial congress themes, membership growth patterns, and publication records. From its initial membership of 65 scientists in 1958, CINP expanded to over 1,200 members by 2004, representing 45 countries across six continents. The organization facilitated critical advances in understanding neurotransmitter systems, culminating in the development of selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors, atypical antipsychotics, and novel anxiolytics. CINP congresses provided essential forums for disseminating breakthrough research, including early presentations on chlorpromazine's mechanism of action (1960), dopamine hypothesis of schizophrenia (1972), and GABA receptor pharmacology (1984). The establishment of specialized task forces led to influential treatment guidelines that shaped international psychiatric practice standards. CINP's commitment to fostering international collaboration and mentoring young investigators proved instrumental in globalizing neuropsychopharmacology research. This historical perspective demonstrates CINP's fundamental contribution to transforming psychiatry from a predominantly psychoanalytic discipline into an evidence-based medical specialty grounded in neuroscientific principles.

title: Antipsychotic drugs: evolving mechanisms of action with improved therapeutic benefits.
abstract: The therapeutic landscape of antipsychotic medications has undergone significant evolution since the introduction of chlorpromazine, with newer agents demonstrating improved efficacy and reduced adverse effects through novel mechanisms of action. This review examines the pharmacological profiles of second-generation antipsychotics and their enhanced therapeutic benefits compared to conventional neuroleptics. We analyzed the receptor binding profiles, clinical efficacy data, and adverse effect profiles of risperidone, olanzapine, quetiapine, and ziprasidone relative to haloperidol and chlorpromazine using published clinical trials and preclinical studies from 1990-2003. Second-generation antipsychotics demonstrate broader receptor antagonism beyond dopamine D2 receptors, including significant activity at serotonin 5-HT2A, 5-HT2C, α1-adrenergic, and histamine H1 receptors. This polypharmacological approach correlates with superior efficacy in treating negative symptoms and cognitive deficits in schizophrenia, with effect sizes ranging from 0.3-0.6 compared to conventional agents. Additionally, the reduced D2 receptor occupancy threshold (65-70% vs 80-85% for typical antipsychotics) and enhanced 5-HT2A/D2 antagonism ratio contribute to significantly lower incidence of extrapyramidal side effects and tardive dyskinesia. Clinical studies demonstrate 40-60% reduction in treatment discontinuation rates and improved quality of life measures. These findings support the hypothesis that balanced neurotransmitter modulation, rather than selective dopamine blockade, provides superior therapeutic outcomes in psychotic disorders. The evolving understanding of antipsychotic mechanisms continues to guide rational drug development toward more effective and tolerable treatments for schizophrenia and related conditions.
journal: Current drug targets. CNS and neurological disorders

title: The functional logic of cortico-pulvinar connections.
abstract: The pulvinar is an 'associative' thalamic nucleus, meaning that most of its input and output relationships are formed with the cerebral cortex. The function of this circuitry is little understood and its anatomy, though much investigated, is notably recondite. This is because pulvinar connection patterns disrespect the architectural subunits (anterior, medial, lateral and inferior pulvinar nuclei) that have been the traditional reference system. This article presents a simplified, global model of the organization of cortico-pulvinar connections so as to pursue their structure-function relationships. Connections between the cortex and pulvinar are topographically organized, and as a result the pulvinar contains a 'map' of the cortical sheet. However, the topography is very blurred. Hence the pulvinar connection zones of nearby cortical areas overlap, allowing indirect transcortical communication via the pulvinar. A general observation is that indirect cortico-pulvino-cortical circuits tend to mimic direct cortico-cortical pathways: this is termed 'the replication principle'. It is equally apt for certain pairs (or groups) of nearby cortical areas that happen not to connect with each other. The 'replication' of this non-connection is achieved by discontinuities and dislocations of the cortical topography within the pulvinar, such that the associated pair of connection zones do not overlap. Certain of these deformations can be used to divide the global cortical topography into specific sub-domains, which form the natural units of a connectional subdivision of the pulvinar. A substantial part of the pulvinar also expresses visual topography, reflecting visual maps in occipital cortex. There are just two well-ordered visual maps in the pulvinar, that both receive projections from area V1, and several other occipital areas; the resulting duplication of cortical topography means that each visual map also acts as a separate connection domain. In summary, the model identifies four topographically ordered connection domains, and reconciles the coexistence of visual and cortical maps in two of them. The replication principle operates at and below the level of domain structure. It is argued that cortico-pulvinar circuitry replicates the pattern of cortical circuitry but not its function, playing a more regulatory role instead. Thalamic neurons differ from cortical neurons in their inherent rhythmicity, and the pattern of cortico-thalamic connections must govern the formation of specific resonant circuits. The broad implication is that the pulvinar acts to coordinate cortical information processing by facilitating and sustaining the formation of synchronized trans-areal assemblies; a more pointed suggestion is that, owing to the considerable blurring of cortical topography in the pulvinar, rival cortical assemblies may be in competition to recruit thalamic elements in order to outlast each other in activity.
journal: Philosophical transactions of the Royal Society of London. Series B, Biological sciences

title: The pediatric psychopharmacology of autism spectrum disorder: A systematic review - Part I: The past and the present.
abstract: Autism spectrum disorder (ASD) affects approximately 1 in 54 children, yet evidence-based pharmacological interventions remain limited. While no medications are approved for core ASD symptoms, psychotropic drugs are frequently prescribed off-label to manage associated behavioral symptoms. This systematic review synthesizes the historical evolution and current state of pediatric psychopharmacological approaches in ASD. We conducted a comprehensive search of PubMed, Embase, and Cochrane databases from inception through December 2020, including randomized controlled trials, open-label studies, and case series examining pharmacological interventions in children and adolescents with ASD. Two independent reviewers extracted data on study design, participant characteristics, interventions, outcome measures, and safety profiles. Of 2,847 initially identified studies, 156 met inclusion criteria, encompassing 8,923 pediatric participants. Antipsychotics, particularly risperidone and aripiprazole, demonstrated the strongest evidence for reducing irritability and aggressive behaviors, with FDA approval for these indications. Selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors showed mixed results for repetitive behaviors and anxiety, with concerns regarding behavioral activation in younger children. Stimulants effectively addressed comorbid attention-deficit/hyperactivity disorder symptoms but required careful monitoring for adverse effects. Alpha-2 agonists and anticonvulsants showed promise in preliminary studies. However, significant heterogeneity in outcome measures, small sample sizes, and limited long-term safety data constrain clinical recommendations. Current pediatric psychopharmacology in ASD remains symptom-focused rather than addressing core deficits. Future research should prioritize standardized outcome measures, longer-term studies, and investigation of novel therapeutic targets to advance evidence-based treatment approaches for this vulnerable population.

title: Serial neurochemical measurement of cerebrospinal fluid during the human sexual response cycle.
abstract: The neurochemical basis of human sexual response remains poorly understood due to methodological limitations in studying central nervous system dynamics during sexual arousal. Previous animal studies have implicated dopamine, oxytocin, and endogenous opioids in sexual behavior, but direct measurement in humans has been limited to peripheral samples or post-coital assessments. We developed a novel protocol to serially sample cerebrospinal fluid during controlled sexual stimulation in healthy volunteers. Eight participants (4 male, 4 female, ages 22-34) underwent lumbar catheter placement under local anesthesia, followed by standardized visual and tactile sexual stimulation while monitoring physiological arousal markers. CSF samples (2 ml) were collected at baseline, during early arousal, at peak arousal, and during resolution phases. High-performance liquid chromatography with electrochemical detection was used to quantify dopamine, norepinephrine, serotonin, and their metabolites. Oxytocin and β-endorphin levels were measured using radioimmunoassay. Dopamine concentrations increased 340% from baseline during peak arousal (p<0.001), while norepinephrine showed biphasic elevation during arousal and resolution phases. Oxytocin levels rose significantly in all participants during peak arousal (280% increase, p<0.01), with higher absolute concentrations in females. β-endorphin demonstrated delayed elevation during the resolution phase. Serotonin levels remained relatively stable throughout the response cycle. These findings provide the first direct neurochemical evidence of dopaminergic and oxytocinergic activation during human sexual response, supporting translational relevance of animal models and offering insights into potential therapeutic targets for sexual dysfunction.

title: Annual Research Review: New frontiers in developmental neuropharmacology: can long-term therapeutic effects of drugs be optimized through carefully timed early intervention?
