abstract: Migraine-associated vertigo (MAV) represents a frequently underdiagnosed neurological condition affecting approximately 1% of the general population, with higher prevalence among migraine sufferers. The pathophysiological mechanisms linking vestibular dysfunction to migraine remain poorly understood, complicating both diagnosis and therapeutic management. This comprehensive review examines current diagnostic criteria and evidence-based treatment approaches for MAV based on analysis of 127 published studies from 2000-2009. We evaluated diagnostic methodologies including vestibular testing, neuroimaging, and clinical assessment tools, alongside therapeutic interventions ranging from lifestyle modifications to pharmacological treatments. Our analysis reveals significant heterogeneity in diagnostic approaches, with only 34% of studies utilizing standardized criteria such as those proposed by the International Headache Society. Vestibular function testing showed abnormalities in 68% of MAV patients, most commonly involving reduced caloric responses and abnormal vestibulo-ocular reflex parameters. Treatment efficacy varied considerably across interventions, with tricyclic antidepressants demonstrating response rates of 65-78%, anticonvulsants showing 52-71% efficacy, and calcium channel blockers achieving 45-62% improvement in vertigo symptoms. Combination therapies incorporating dietary modifications and vestibular rehabilitation exercises enhanced overall outcomes by 23-35%. These findings highlight the need for standardized diagnostic criteria and individualized treatment protocols. Future research should focus on elucidating the neurobiological mechanisms underlying MAV and developing targeted therapeutic strategies to improve patient outcomes in this challenging clinical population.

title: Neuropharmacological profile of aqueous extract of Anaphe venata larva (Notondotidae) in rats.
abstract: **Background:** Anaphe venata larva, commonly consumed as food in West African communities, has been associated with seasonal ataxic syndrome in humans, suggesting significant neurological activity. Despite its widespread consumption and reported neurotoxic effects, the comprehensive neuropharmacological profile of this edible caterpillar remains poorly characterized. This study investigated the neurobehavioral effects of aqueous extract of A. venata larva in experimental rats. **Methods:** Adult Wistar rats (n=48) were randomly assigned to four groups receiving oral administration of A. venata aqueous extract at doses of 100, 200, and 400 mg/kg body weight, or distilled water (control) for 14 days. Neurobehavioral assessments included open field test, rotarod performance, beam walking test, and elevated plus maze. Motor coordination, anxiety-like behavior, locomotor activity, and cognitive function were evaluated. Histopathological examination of brain tissues was performed using hematoxylin and eosin staining. **Results:** A. venata extract produced dose-dependent neurobehavioral alterations. Significant impairment in motor coordination was observed at 200 and 400 mg/kg doses (p<0.05), characterized by reduced rotarod retention time and increased beam walking errors. Locomotor activity was markedly decreased at the highest dose (p<0.01). Anxiety-like behavior increased significantly in treated groups. Histopathological analysis revealed cerebellar Purkinje cell degeneration and neuronal vacuolation in the hippocampus, particularly at higher doses. **Conclusions:** Aqueous extract of A. venata larva exhibits significant neurotoxic properties in rats, producing motor dysfunction and cerebellar damage consistent with human seasonal ataxic syndrome. These findings provide experimental evidence supporting the neurotoxic potential of A. venata consumption.
journal: African journal of traditional, complementary, and alternative medicines : AJTCAM

title: Single-fiber recordings of unmyelinated afferents in pig.
abstract: Microneurography has provided valuable data on single-fiber characteristics in healthy volunteers and patients, featuring a unique setting that allows linking discharge of single fibers to percept. This advantage is of particular value, when pain patients are examined. Latest results on specific changes of axonal excitability differentially expressed in various C-fiber classes have incited studies linking axonal excitability changes to the mechanism of chronic pain. These studies are mainly based on investigating patients rather than establishing animal models and have led to specific questions on the molecular mechanisms underlying axonal excitability changes. However, pharmacological interventions are limited in human microneurography and therefore an animal model is required. Ideally, the distribution of fiber classes and their sensory and axonal characteristics should be comparable to human. Here we report on corresponding C-fiber classes between human and pig. Nociceptive and non-nociceptive fiber classes found in pig correlate with human fiber classes, in both distribution and axonal excitability changes. It is suggested that the pig is an attractive model for studying modulation of excitability in nociceptive and non-nociceptive C-fiber classes that correspond to those in man.

title: Power spectra reveal distinct BOLD resting-state time courses in white matter.
abstract: Accurate characterization of the time courses of blood-oxygen-level-dependent (BOLD) signal changes is crucial for the analysis and interpretation of functional MRI data. While several studies have shown that white matter (WM) exhibits distinct BOLD responses evoked by tasks, there have been no comprehensive investigations into the time courses of spontaneous signal fluctuations in WM. We measured the power spectra of the resting-state time courses in a set of regions within WM identified as showing synchronous signals using independent components analysis. In each component, a clear separation between voxels into two categories was evident, based on their power spectra: one group exhibited a single peak, and the other had an additional peak at a higher frequency. Their groupings are location specific, and their distributions reflect unique neurovascular and anatomical configurations. Importantly, the two categories of voxels differed in their engagement in functional integration, revealed by differences in the number of interregional connections based on the two categories separately. Taken together, these findings suggest WM signals are heterogeneous in nature and depend on local structural-vascular-functional associations.
journal: Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences of the United States of America

title: How evolutionary psychiatry can advance psychopharmacology .
abstract: The prevailing paradigm for psychopharmacology focuses on understanding brain mechanisms as the key to finding new medications and improving clinical outcomes, but frustration with slow progress has inspired many pleas for new approaches. Evolutionary psychiatry brings in an additional basic science that poses new questions about why natural selection left us vulnerable to so many mental disorders, and new insights about how drugs work. The integration of neuroscience with evolutionary psychiatry is synergistic, going beyond reductionism to provide a model like the one used by the rest of medicine. It recognizes negative emotions as symptoms, that are, like pain and cough, useful defenses whose presence should initiate a search for causes. An integrative evolutionary approach explains why agents that block useful aversive responses are usually safe, and how to anticipate when they may cause harm. More generally, an evolutionary framework suggests novel practical strategies for finding and testing new drugs. . El paradigma común para la psicofarmacología se centra en entender los mecanismos cerebrales como la clave para encontrar nuevos medicamentos y mejorar los resultados clínicos, pero la frustración con el lento progreso ha inspirado muchos motivos para nuevas aproximaciones. La psiquiatría evolutiva conlleva una ciencia básica adicional que plantea nuevas preguntas sobre el por qué la selección natural nos dejó vulnerables a tantos trastornos mentales y aporta nuevas perspectivas sobre cómo funcionan los fármacos. La integración de las neurociencias con la psiquiatría evolutiva es sinérgica, y va más allá del reduccionismo para proporcionar un modelo como el que utiliza el resto de la medicina. Reconoce las emociones negativas como síntomas, que son, como el dolor y la tos, defensas útiles cuya presencia debe iniciar una búsqueda de causas. Un enfoque evolutivo integrador explica por qué los agentes que bloquean las respuestas aversivas útiles suelen ser seguros y cómo anticipar cuándo pueden causar daño. De manera más general, un marco evolutivo sugiere nuevas estrategias prácticas para encontrar y probar nuevos medicamentos. Le modèle prévalent de la psychopharmacologie se concentre sur la compréhension des mécanismes du cerveau comme la clé pour découvrir de nouveaux médicaments et améliorer les résultats cliniques, mais la frustration ressentie devant les lents progrès de cette méthode a inspiré de nombreux plaidoyers pour d’autres approches. La psychiatrie évolutionniste apporte une connaissance scientifique fondamentale supplémentaire qui pose de nouvelles questions sur la raison pour laquelle la sélection naturelle nous a laissé si vulnérables à tant de troubles mentaux, et des idées nouvelles sur la façon dont fonctionnent les médicaments. L’intégration de la psychiatrie évolutionniste aux neurosciences est source de synergies, allant bien au-delà du réductionnisme en fournissant un modèle semblable à celui utilisé par le reste de la médecine. Il reconnaît les émotions négatives comme des symptômes, exactement comme le sont la douleur et la toux, défenses habituelles qui doivent initier une recherche des causes. Une approche évolutionniste intégrative explique pourquoi des agents qui bloquent des réactions pertinentes d’aversion sont généralement sans danger, et comment anticiper lorsqu’ils pourraient causer un préjudice. Plus généralement, un modèle évolutionniste propose de nouvelles stratégies pratiques pour trouver et tester de nouveaux médicaments.

title: The neurophysiological and neurochemical effects of alcohol on the brain are inconsistent with current evidence based models of sleepwalking.
abstract: The DSM-5 and ICSD-3 have removed alcohol from the list of potential triggers for sleepwalking due to the lack of empirical evidence. Recent imaging and EEG based studies of sleepwalking and confusional arousals have provided a more data-based method of examining if alcohol is compatible with what is known about the neurophysiology and neurochemistry of sleepwalking. These studies have demonstrated a deactivation of the frontal areas of the brain, while the cingulate or motor cortex remains active and characterized activation in the form of beta EEG. This increase in activation is attributed to a decrease in the inhibitory activity the neurotransmitter GABAA. This cerebral excitability of the cingulate cortex of sleepwalkers is also present in the brains of sleepwalkers during wakefulness compared to normal controls. Alcohol is well established to have an inhibitory effect on the brain and specifically on the motor areas via the inhibitory effects of increased GABAA activity. Thus, the empirical data show sleepwalking is characterized by a decrease in the inhibitory activity of GABAA - permitting or facilitating motor activity while alcohol has the opposite effect of increasing GABAA and inhibiting motor activity. This is inconsistent with theories that alcohol is somehow a trigger or facilitator for sleepwalking.

title: Psychopharmacology: neuroimmune signaling in psychiatric disease-developing vaccines against abused drugs using toll-like receptor agonists.
abstract: Since substance use disorders have few or no effective pharmacotherapies, researchers have developed vaccines as immune-therapies against nicotine, cocaine, methamphetamine, and opioids including fentanyl. We focus on enhancing antibody (AB) production through stimulation of toll-like receptor-5 (TLR5) during active vaccination. The stimulating adjuvant is Entolimod, a novel protein derivative of flagellin. We review the molecular and cellular mechanisms underlying Entolimod's actions on TLR5. Entolimod shows excellent efficacy for increasing AB levels to levels well beyond those produced by anti-addiction vaccines alone in animal models and humans. These ABs also significantly block the behavioral effects of the targeted drug of abuse. The TLR5 stimulation involves a wide range of immune cell types such as dendritic, antigen presenting, T and B cells. Entolimod binding to TLR5 initiates an intracellular signaling cascade that stimulates cytokine production of tumor necrosis factor and two interleukins (IL-6 and IL-12). While cytokine release can be catastrophic in cytokine storm, Entolimod produces a modulated release with few side effects even at doses 30 times greater than doses needed in these vaccine studies. Entolimod has markedly increased AB responses to all of our anti-addiction vaccines in rodent models, and in normal humans. Entolimod and TLR5 stimulation has broad application to vaccines and potentially to other psychiatric disorders like depression, which has critical inflammatory contributions that Entolimod could reduce.

title: [Nanopsychiatry. The potential role of nanotechnologies in the future of psychiatry. A systematic review].
abstract: Nanomedicine is defined as the area using nanotechnology's concepts for the benefit of human beings, their health and well being. The field of nanotechnology opened new unsuspected fields of research a few years ago. To provide an overview of nanotechnology application areas that could affect care for psychiatric illnesses. We conducted a systematic review using the PRISMA criteria (preferred reporting items for systematic reviews and meta-analysis). Inclusion criteria were specified in advance: all studies describing the development of nanotechnology in psychiatry. The research paradigm was: "(nanotechnology OR nanoparticles OR nanomedicine) AND (central nervous system)" Articles were identified in three research bases, Medline (1966-present), Web of Science (1975-present) and Cochrane (all articles). The last search was carried out on April 2, 2012. Seventy-six items were included in this qualitative review. The main applications of nanotechnology in psychiatry are (i) pharmacology. There are two main difficulties in neuropharmacology. Drugs have to pass the blood brain barrier and then to be internalized by targeted cells. Nanoparticles could increase drugs' bioavailability and pharmacokinetics, especially improving safety and efficacy of psychotropic drugs. Liposomes, nanosomes, nanoparticle polymers, nanobubbles are some examples of this targeted drug delivery. Nanotechnologies could also add new pharmacological properties, like nanohells and dendrimers; (ii) living analysis. Nanotechnology provides technical assistance to in vivo imaging or metabolome analysis; (iii) central nervous system modeling. Research teams have modelized inorganic synapses and mimicked synaptic behavior, essential for further creation of artificial neural systems. Some nanoparticle assemblies present the same small world and free-scale network architecture as cortical neural networks. Nanotechnologies and quantum physics could be used to create models of artificial intelligence and mental illnesses. Even if nanotechnologies are promising, their safety is still tricky and this must be kept in mind. We are not about to see a concrete application of nanomedicine in daily psychiatric practice. However, it seems essential that psychiatrists do not forsake this area of research the perspectives of which could be decisive in the field of mental illness.

title: The engagement of brain cytochrome P450 in the metabolism of endogenous neuroactive substrates: a possible role in mental disorders.
abstract: Brain cytochrome P450 (CYP) enzymes have been increasingly recognized as key regulators of central nervous system homeostasis through their involvement in the metabolism of endogenous neuroactive compounds. While peripheral CYP function in drug metabolism is well-established, the role of brain-expressed CYP isoforms in neuropsychiatric pathophysiology remains poorly understood. This review synthesizes current evidence regarding brain CYP-mediated metabolism of endogenous substrates including neurosteroids, fatty acid derivatives, and biogenic amines, with particular emphasis on their potential contribution to mental disorders. We systematically analyzed published literature from 1995-2017 examining brain CYP expression, enzymatic activity, and substrate specificity using PubMed and Web of Science databases. Critical evaluation focused on CYP2D6, CYP2E1, CYP46A1, and CYP19A1 isoforms based on their neuronal expression patterns and substrate profiles. Evidence indicates that brain CYP enzymes metabolize cholesterol, arachidonic acid metabolites, neurosteroids such as pregnenolone and allopregnanolone, and catecholamine neurotransmitters. Dysregulation of these metabolic pathways correlates with altered levels of neuroactive metabolites in depression, schizophrenia, and anxiety disorders. CYP46A1-mediated cholesterol hydroxylation influences synaptic plasticity, while CYP19A1 aromatase activity affects local estrogen synthesis and neuroprotection. Genetic polymorphisms in brain-expressed CYP genes show associations with psychiatric phenotypes and treatment responses. These findings suggest that brain CYP enzymes represent previously underappreciated therapeutic targets for mental health disorders, warranting further investigation into their pharmacological modulation as novel treatment strategies.

title: Advances in paediatric neuropsychopharmacology: an overview.
abstract: The field of paediatric neuropsychopharmacology has experienced remarkable growth over the past decade, driven by increased recognition of childhood psychiatric disorders and the unique pharmacological challenges they present. This comprehensive review examines recent advances in the use of psychotropic medications in children and adolescents, focusing on developmental considerations, efficacy data, and safety profiles. A systematic literature review was conducted using MEDLINE and PsycINFO databases from 1990-2001, supplemented by recent conference proceedings and regulatory documents. Key therapeutic areas examined include attention-deficit/hyperactivity disorder (ADHD), childhood depression, anxiety disorders, and early-onset psychotic disorders. Significant progress has been made in understanding the pharmacokinetics and pharmacodynamics of psychotropic agents in developing populations. Stimulant medications for ADHD demonstrate robust efficacy with well-characterized safety profiles, while selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors show promise for childhood anxiety and depression, though with important caveats regarding suicidal ideation monitoring. Atypical antipsychotics have emerged as valuable treatments for severe behavioural disturbances and early-onset schizophrenia, despite concerns about metabolic side effects. Critical gaps remain in our understanding of long-term developmental effects of psychotropic medications, particularly regarding growth, sexual maturation, and cognitive development. The paucity of controlled trials in paediatric populations continues to challenge evidence-based prescribing. Future research priorities include dose-finding studies, long-term safety surveillance, and investigation of pharmacogenomic factors affecting drug response in children. Enhanced regulatory frameworks and industry incentives are essential to advance this critical field.

title: [Use of the antisense oligonucleotide technique in neuropsychopharmacology research].
abstract: The technique using antisense oligonucleotides has been widely used in the study of specific functions of targeted protein molecules. This technique has enabled us to perform experiments which were previously impossible in the field of neuropsychopharmacology. Some chemically modified oligonucleotide analogues (i.e., phosphorothioate, S-oligo), which are relatively nuclease-resistant, are now available. However, the molecular mechanisms of action, pharmacokinetics, and kinetics of cellular uptake of antisense oligonucleotides, have not yet been fully understood. Recently, a number of reports have described the use of antisense oligonucleotides in inhibiting gene expression in the living brain, using intact laboratory animals. In the present article, after a brief discussion of mechanisms of action of antisense oligonucleotides, the methodological aspects of antisense experiments using the "mini osmotic pump" as the oligonucleotide delivery system are reviewed. The choice, design, mode of delivery, and dose are also discussed. In particular, we address the advantages and disadvantages of the antisense oligonucleotide technique as well as the control experiments required. This technique is a powerful method and one of the most important tools in neuropsychopharmacology research.
journal: Nihon shinkei seishin yakurigaku zasshi = Japanese journal of psychopharmacology

title: Listening to the brain: microelectrode biosensors for neurochemicals.
abstract: Chemical signalling underlies every function of the nervous system, from those of which we are unaware, for example, control of the heart, to higher cognitive functions, such as emotions, learning and memory. Neurotransmitters and neuromodulators mediate communication between neurons and between neurons and non-neural cells such as glia and muscle. In the past, the means for studying the production and release of these signalling agents directly has been limited in its temporal and spatial resolution relative to the dynamics of chemical signalling and the structures of interest in the brain. Now microelectrode biosensors are becoming available that give unprecedented spatial and temporal resolution, enabling, for the first time, direct measurement in real time of the chemical conversations between cells in the nervous system.

title: The intersection of stress and reward: BNST modulation of aversive and appetitive states.
abstract: The bed nucleus of the stria terminalis (BNST) is widely acknowledged as a brain structure that regulates stress and anxiety states, as well as aversive and appetitive behaviours. The diverse roles of the BNST are afforded by its highly modular organisation, neurochemical heterogeneity, and complex intrinsic and extrinsic circuitry. There has been growing interest in the BNST in relation to psychopathologies such as anxiety and addiction. Although research on the human BNST is still in its infancy, there have been extensive preclinical studies examining the molecular signature and hodology of the BNST and their involvement in stress and reward seeking behaviour. This review examines the neurochemical phenotype and connectivity of the BNST, as well as electrophysiological correlates of plasticity in the BNST mediated by stress and/or drugs of abuse.

title: S(-)Propranolol as a discriminative stimulus and its comparison to the stimulus effects of cocaine in rats.
abstract: The β-adrenergic antagonist propranolol exists as two enantiomers with distinct pharmacological profiles, with S(-)propranolol demonstrating membrane-stabilizing properties independent of β-blockade. Previous studies suggest that propranolol's discriminative stimulus properties may involve non-adrenergic mechanisms. The present study investigated whether S(-)propranolol could serve as a discriminative stimulus in rats and examined potential cross-generalization with cocaine, given both compounds' membrane-stabilizing effects. Male Sprague-Dawley rats (n=8) were trained to discriminate S(-)propranolol (10 mg/kg, IP) from saline in a two-lever operant conditioning paradigm using a FR10 schedule reinforced with food pellets. Following acquisition, generalization tests were conducted with various doses of S(-)propranolol (1-20 mg/kg), racemic propranolol (2.5-15 mg/kg), R(+)propranolol (5-20 mg/kg), and cocaine (2.5-20 mg/kg). Rats successfully acquired the S(-)propranolol discrimination within 45±8 sessions. Dose-response analysis revealed full generalization to the training dose occurred at 7.5-10 mg/kg S(-)propranolol (ED50 = 4.2 mg/kg). Racemic propranolol produced partial generalization (maximum 65% drug-lever responding), while R(+)propranolol showed minimal generalization (<25%). Notably, cocaine demonstrated significant cross-generalization with S(-)propranolol, producing dose-dependent increases in drug-lever responding (ED50 = 8.7 mg/kg) with maximum generalization of 78% at 15 mg/kg. These findings suggest that S(-)propranolol's discriminative stimulus effects are mediated by mechanisms distinct from β-adrenergic blockade and share common interoceptive properties with cocaine, likely involving sodium channel blockade or other membrane-stabilizing actions.

title: Blended learning: how can we optimise undergraduate student engagement?
abstract: Blended learning is a combination of online and face-to-face learning and is increasingly of interest for use in undergraduate medical education. It has been used to teach clinical post-graduate students pharmacology but needs evaluation for its use in teaching pharmacology to undergraduate medical students, which represent a different group of students with different learning needs. An existing BSc-level module on neuropharmacology was redesigned using the Blended Learning Design Tool (BLEnDT), a tool which uses learning domains (psychomotor, cognitive and affective) to classify learning outcomes into those taught best by self-directed learning (online) or by collaborative learning (face-to-face). Two online courses were developed, one on Neurotransmitters and the other on Neurodegenerative Conditions. These were supported with face-to-face tutorials. Undergraduate students' engagement with blended learning was explored by the means of three focus groups, the data from which were analysed thematically. Five major themes emerged from the data 1) Purpose and Acceptability 2) Structure, Focus and Consolidation 3) Preparation and workload 4) Engagement with e-learning component 5) Future Medical Education. Blended learning was acceptable and of interest to undergraduate students learning this subject. They expressed a desire for more blended learning in their courses, but only if it was highly structured, of high quality and supported by tutorials. Students identified that the 'blend' was beneficial rather than purely online learning.

title: Neuropharmacology of performance monitoring.
abstract: Performance monitoring, the ability to detect and respond to errors and conflicts during goal-directed behavior, represents a fundamental cognitive control mechanism mediated by the anterior cingulate cortex (ACC) and associated neural networks. While extensive neuroimaging and electrophysiological studies have characterized the neural correlates of performance monitoring, including the error-related negativity (ERN) and conflict-related activity, the underlying neuropharmacological mechanisms remain poorly understood. This review synthesizes current knowledge regarding the neurochemical basis of performance monitoring, with particular emphasis on dopaminergic, GABAergic, and glutamatergic systems. We examine evidence from pharmacological challenge studies in humans and animals, focusing on how acute manipulations of neurotransmitter systems affect behavioral and neural indices of error detection and conflict monitoring. Dopamine appears to play a critical role in signaling prediction errors and modulating ACC activity during performance monitoring tasks, with D2 receptor blockade consistently impairing error detection. GABAergic modulation through benzodiazepine administration reduces conflict-related ACC activation and impairs behavioral adjustments following errors. Glutamatergic NMDA receptor antagonism similarly disrupts performance monitoring, suggesting involvement of cortical excitatory networks. Additionally, we review emerging evidence for acetylcholine and norepinephrine contributions to attention-related aspects of performance monitoring. The convergent findings indicate that performance monitoring depends on complex interactions between multiple neurotransmitter systems, with dopamine and GABA playing particularly prominent roles. Understanding these neurochemical mechanisms has important implications for psychiatric disorders characterized by performance monitoring deficits, including schizophrenia, ADHD, and obsessive-compulsive disorder.

title: Should we or shouldn't we?: Preface to the Special Issue "Neurochemistry of Reward-Seeking".
abstract: Reward-seeking is critical for survival. Learning about the relationship between actions and outcomes helps us to make decisions and select behaviours that result in a specific outcome. This special issue entitled "The Neurochemistry of Reward-Seeking" addresses this crucial facet of behaviour and brings together a number of key thought-leaders to provide a timely update on the circuitry, chemistry and mechanisms underlying different aspects of reward-seeking. The reviews in this issue canvass unanswered questions in the field and provide a degree of forethought about how we may advance our understanding of reward-seeking by embracing novel technology alongside existing scholarship. This issue also highlights the neurochemical complexity of reward-seeking, and the reader will uncover both distinct and shared circuits and transmitters driving various forms of reward-seeking. Accordingly, we hope that this special issue will provide a valuable resource for the field and trigger future research on this topic.

title: The effect of alprostadil on ischemia-reperfusion injury of peripheral nerve in rats.
abstract: To determine the effect of alprostadil on the ischemia-reperfusion (I/R) injury of the sciatic nerve in rats. Pre-reperfusion administration of alprostadil (0.05 microg kg(-1)) was assessed in the I/R injury model of the rat sciatic nerve. In this model, blood samples were investigated for the I/R injury markers namely malondialdehyde (MDA), an indicator of lipid peroxidation, and nitrite/nitrate levels, products of nitric oxide (NO) metabolism. A significant decrease in MDA, and increase in NO levels were observed in the groups which received alprostadil before reperfusion, when compared to their corresponding untreated controls (I/R only) at all time intervals (P=0.0001). There was a statistically significant difference in both MDA, and NO levels between certain time intervals. There was no statistical linear correlation between MDA and NO levels. Alprostadil may be suggested as a protective anti-inflammatory and a vasodilator pharmacological agent for I/R injury in peripheral nerves. Also, measurements of NO and MDA may be complementary to the generally accepted evaluation parameters of I/R injury including electromyography and nerve histopathology.

title: Blended learning: how can we optimise undergraduate student engagement?
abstract:
**Background:** Traditional lecture-based teaching in undergraduate neuroscience education often results in passive learning and suboptimal knowledge retention. Blended learning approaches, combining face-to-face instruction with digital technologies, have emerged as promising pedagogical strategies. However, optimal methods for maximizing student engagement in neuropharmacology courses remain poorly defined. This study investigated the effectiveness of a structured blended learning framework on undergraduate student engagement and academic performance in neuropharmacology education.

**Methods:** A randomized controlled trial was conducted with 156 third-year undergraduate students enrolled in a neuropharmacology course at two medical schools. Students were randomly assigned to either traditional lecture-based instruction (control, n=78) or a blended learning intervention (n=78). The blended approach incorporated pre-class online modules, interactive case-based discussions, virtual laboratory simulations, and post-class digital assessments. Student engagement was measured using the Student Engagement Questionnaire and behavioral analytics from the learning management system. Academic performance was assessed through standardized examinations and practical assessments.

**Results:** Students in the blended learning group demonstrated significantly higher engagement scores (7.2±1.1 vs 5.8±1.3, p<0.001) and increased online participation rates (85% vs 45%, p<0.001). Examination scores improved by 12.3% compared to controls (78.6±8.2 vs 70.1±9.4, p<0.001). Time spent on course materials increased by 34% in the intervention group. Student satisfaction ratings were significantly higher for blended learning participants (4.6±0.5 vs 3.8±0.7, p<0.001).

**Conclusions:** Structured blended learning significantly enhances undergraduate student engagement and academic performance in neuropharmacology education. These findings support the integration of digital technologies with traditional teaching methods to optimize learning outcomes in medical education.

title: Optofluidic control of rodent learning using cloaked caged glutamate.
abstract: The precise spatiotemporal control of neurotransmitter release remains a fundamental challenge in understanding neural circuit function and behavior. While optogenetics has revolutionized neuroscience research, existing approaches for controlling endogenous neurotransmitter signaling lack the temporal precision and spatial specificity required for dissecting complex learning behaviors. Here, we developed a novel optofluidic platform utilizing photocaged glutamate conjugated with near-infrared fluorescent cloaking molecules to achieve millisecond-precise, localized glutamate release in behaving rodents. The cloaking strategy prevents premature photolysis while enabling deep tissue penetration of 980-nm light for targeted uncaging. We validated this approach in adult C57BL/6 mice (n=48) performing a spatial memory task, delivering caged glutamate via chronically implanted microfluidic devices targeting the hippocampal CA1 region. Optical stimulation through implanted fiber optics triggered glutamate release with 2.3 ± 0.4 ms temporal precision and <50 μm spatial resolution. Mice receiving precisely timed glutamate uncaging during memory encoding showed significantly enhanced performance in the Morris water maze (escape latency: 18.2 ± 3.1 s vs 31.7 ± 4.8 s, p<0.001) compared to controls. Electrophysiological recordings confirmed selective enhancement of CA1 pyramidal cell activity and theta-gamma coupling during stimulation periods. Post-mortem immunohistochemistry revealed minimal tissue damage and preserved synaptic architecture. These results demonstrate that optofluidic control of caged neurotransmitters enables unprecedented precision in manipulating neural activity during complex behaviors, providing a powerful tool for dissecting the neurochemical basis of learning and memory with potential therapeutic applications for cognitive disorders.
journal: Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences of the United States of America

title: In vivo neurochemical correlates of cognitive processes: methodological and conceptual challenges.
abstract: The investigation of neurochemical substrates underlying cognitive processes in living organisms presents formidable methodological and theoretical obstacles that significantly impact data interpretation and translational relevance. Traditional approaches including microdialysis, voltammetry, and pharmacological manipulations have provided valuable insights into neurotransmitter dynamics during learning, memory, and attention tasks, yet each technique carries inherent limitations that constrain temporal and spatial resolution. We critically examine current methodological approaches used to assess in vivo neurochemical changes during cognitive performance, with particular emphasis on technical constraints affecting data quality and reproducibility. Microdialysis studies demonstrate significant variability in neurotransmitter sampling efficiency across brain regions, with recovery rates ranging from 15-40% depending on probe membrane characteristics and tissue properties. Fast-scan cyclic voltammetry offers superior temporal resolution but remains limited to select neurotransmitters and restricted anatomical locations. Pharmacological interventions, while providing mechanistic insights, often produce systemic effects that complicate attribution of cognitive changes to specific neural circuits. Furthermore, substantial inter-individual differences in baseline neurochemical activity and drug metabolism introduce considerable variance in experimental outcomes. Conceptual challenges include establishing causal relationships between neurochemical fluctuations and behavioral performance, accounting for compensatory mechanisms, and integrating data across multiple neurotransmitter systems. We propose that future advances require integration of complementary techniques, development of more selective pharmacological tools, and adoption of standardized protocols to enhance reproducibility. These methodological refinements are essential for advancing our understanding of the complex neurochemical basis of cognition and facilitating translation to clinical applications.

title: Chapter 11 - Solid lipid nanoparticles for brain tumors therapy: State of the art and novel challenges.
abstract: The treatment of brain tumors remains one of the most challenging aspects of oncology due to the blood-brain barrier (BBB) limiting drug penetration and the need for targeted delivery to minimize systemic toxicity. Solid lipid nanoparticles (SLNs) have emerged as promising drug delivery systems that can enhance brain bioavailability of therapeutic agents while reducing adverse effects. This review examines current developments in SLN-based drug delivery systems for brain tumor therapy and identifies key challenges for clinical translation. We analyzed recent literature on SLN formulations, surface modifications, and targeting strategies for brain tumor treatment, focusing on studies published between 2005-2009. Various approaches for BBB penetration have been investigated, including receptor-mediated transcytosis using transferrin and lactoferrin conjugation, adsorptive-mediated transcytosis through cationic surface modifications, and disruption of tight junctions. SLNs loaded with chemotherapeutic agents such as doxorubicin, paclitaxel, and temozolomide have demonstrated enhanced brain accumulation and improved therapeutic efficacy in preclinical models compared to conventional formulations. Surface functionalization with polyethylene glycol has shown to prolong circulation time and reduce recognition by the reticuloendothelial system. However, several challenges remain, including optimization of particle size and surface properties for maximum BBB permeability, prevention of drug leakage during storage and circulation, and scale-up manufacturing processes. Additionally, limited understanding of SLN biodistribution and long-term safety profiles necessitates comprehensive toxicological evaluation. Future research should focus on developing multifunctional SLNs combining therapeutic and diagnostic capabilities for personalized brain tumor therapy.

title: The cell cycle in Alzheimer disease: a unique target for neuropharmacology.
abstract: Several hypotheses have been proposed attempting to explain the pathogenesis of Alzheimer disease including, among others, theories involving amyloid deposition, tau phosphorylation, oxidative stress, metal ion dysregulation and inflammation. While there is strong evidence suggesting that each one of these proposed mechanisms contributes to disease pathogenesis, none of these mechanisms are able to account for all the physiological changes that occur during the course of the disease. For this reason, we and others have begun the search for a causative factor that predates known features found in Alzheimer disease, and that might therefore be a fundamental initiator of the pathophysiological cascade. We propose that the dysregulation of the cell cycle that occurs in neurons susceptible to degeneration in the hippocampus during Alzheimer disease is a potential causative factor that, together with oxidative stress, would initiate all known pathological events. Neuronal changes supporting alterations in cell cycle control in the etiology of Alzheimer disease include the ectopic expression of markers of the cell cycle, organelle kinesis and cytoskeletal alterations including tau phosphorylation. Such mitotic alterations are not only one of the earliest neuronal abnormalities in the disease, but as discussed herein, are also intimately linked to all of the other pathological hallmarks of Alzheimer disease including tau protein, amyloid beta protein precursor and oxidative stress, and even risk factors such as mutations in the presenilin genes. Therefore, therapeutic interventions targeted toward ameliorating mitotic changes would be predicted to have a profound and positive impact on Alzheimer disease progression.

title: Neuropharmacology of motion sickness and emesis. A review.
abstract: Histamine H1-receptors are involved in the development of the symptoms and signs of motion sickness, including emesis. On provocative motion stimulus, a signal for sensory conflict activates the histaminergic neuron system, and the histaminergic descending impulse stimulates H1-receptors in the emetic center of the brain stem. The histaminergic input to the emetic center through H1-receptors is independent of dopamine D2-receptors in the chemoreceptor trigger zone and serotonin 5HT3-receptors in the visceral afferent, which are also involved in the emetic reflex. Antihistamines block emetic H1-receptors to prevent motion sickness. Acetylcholine muscarinic receptors are involved in the generation of signals for sensory conflict. Anti-cholinergic drugs prevent motion sickness by modifying the neural store to facilitate the acquisition of habituation to provocative motion.

title: Listening to the brain: microelectrode biosensors for neurochemicals.
abstract: Real-time monitoring of neurochemical dynamics in the living brain remains a critical challenge in neuroscience research. Traditional analytical techniques lack the spatial and temporal resolution necessary to capture rapid neurotransmitter fluctuations at synaptic and circuit levels. Here we review recent advances in microelectrode biosensor technology that enable direct, continuous measurement of neurochemicals in vivo with unprecedented precision. Carbon fiber microelectrodes modified with selective enzymes or aptamers have emerged as powerful tools for detecting dopamine, serotonin, acetylcholine, and glutamate with sub-second temporal resolution and nanomolar sensitivity. Fast-scan cyclic voltammetry coupled with these biosensors allows discrimination between structurally similar compounds while minimizing tissue damage. We examined validation studies demonstrating sensor selectivity, stability, and biocompatibility across multiple species and brain regions. Recent applications include real-time monitoring of dopamine release in reward circuits during behavioral tasks, tracking glutamate dynamics during synaptic plasticity, and measuring neurotransmitter perturbations in disease models. Emerging multiplexed sensor arrays enable simultaneous detection of multiple analytes, providing unprecedented insight into neurochemical network interactions. However, challenges remain in sensor fouling, drift compensation, and standardization across laboratories. Despite these limitations, microelectrode biosensors represent a transformative technology for understanding brain chemistry in health and disease. Future developments integrating wireless telemetry and advanced nanomaterials promise to further revolutionize our ability to decode the brain's chemical language in real-time.

title: Metabolic changes detected by proton magnetic resonance spectroscopy in vivo and in vitro in a murin model of Parkinson's disease, the MPTP-intoxicated mouse.
abstract: Parkinson's disease (PD) is characterized by selective dopaminergic neuronal loss in the substantia nigra, yet the underlying metabolic alterations remain poorly understood. The 1-methyl-4-phenyl-1,2,3,6-tetrahydropyridine (MPTP) mouse model reproduces key features of PD pathology and provides an opportunity to investigate neurochemical changes associated with dopaminergic degeneration. We employed proton magnetic resonance spectroscopy (¹H-MRS) to characterize metabolic profiles in MPTP-treated C57BL/6 mice both in vivo and in vitro. Adult male mice received four subcutaneous injections of MPTP (20 mg/kg) at 2-hour intervals. In vivo ¹H-MRS was performed at 9.4T focusing on striatal regions at 1, 7, and 21 days post-treatment. Ex vivo high-resolution magic angle spinning ¹H-MRS was conducted on striatal and nigral tissue extracts. MPTP treatment resulted in significant reductions in N-acetylaspartate (NAA) levels in the striatum by day 7 (23% decrease, p<0.01), which persisted through day 21. Lactate concentrations were elevated 2-fold at day 1 but normalized by day 7. In vitro analysis revealed decreased glutamate and increased taurine levels in both striatal and nigral regions. Creatine and choline-containing compounds showed region-specific alterations, with striatal creatine decreasing by 18% while nigral levels remained unchanged. These findings demonstrate that MPTP-induced dopaminergic toxicity produces distinct metabolic signatures detectable by ¹H-MRS, with NAA reduction likely reflecting neuronal dysfunction or loss. The metabolic profiles provide insights into the temporal progression of MPTP toxicity and may serve as biomarkers for monitoring disease progression and therapeutic interventions in experimental PD.

title: Neuroglialpharmacology: white matter pathophysiologies and psychiatric treatments.
abstract: Emerging evidence suggests that white matter abnormalities and glial dysfunction play critical roles in the pathophysiology of major psychiatric disorders, yet current therapeutic approaches primarily target neuronal mechanisms. Recent neuroimaging studies have revealed widespread white matter microstructural alterations in schizophrenia, bipolar disorder, and major depression, while post-mortem analyses demonstrate significant oligodendrocyte pathology and altered myelin gene expression. This review synthesizes current understanding of neuroglial pharmacology and its therapeutic implications for psychiatric treatment. We examined preclinical and clinical literature from 1995-2011 focusing on white matter pathophysiology, glial cell biology, and emerging therapeutic targets. Database searches identified 247 relevant studies examining oligodendrocyte function, myelin dynamics, and glial-neuronal interactions in psychiatric contexts. Converging evidence indicates that psychiatric medications exert significant effects on glial cells beyond their primary neuronal targets. Lithium promotes oligodendrocyte survival and myelin repair through GSK-3β inhibition, while quetiapine enhances oligodendrocyte progenitor cell differentiation. Antidepressants modulate microglial activation and promote remyelination via BDNF-dependent mechanisms. Novel therapeutic approaches targeting white matter pathology show promise, including clemastine for myelin repair and minocycline for microglial modulation. Importantly, genetic variants affecting oligodendrocyte function predict treatment response to conventional psychiatric medications. These findings support a paradigm shift toward "neuroglialpharmacology" that considers both neuronal and glial targets. Future psychiatric drug development should incorporate white matter endpoints and glial biomarkers to optimize therapeutic efficacy. Understanding neuroglial mechanisms may explain individual treatment responses and guide personalized psychiatric medicine approaches.

title: Charles Darwin's emotional expression "experiment" and his contribution to modern neuropharmacology.
abstract: Charles Darwin's investigations into emotional expression, particularly his systematic observations documented in "The Expression of the Emotions in Man and Animals" (1872), represent an overlooked foundation for contemporary neuropharmacological research. While Darwin's work predates modern neurochemistry by nearly a century, his methodological approach and theoretical insights established crucial conceptual frameworks that continue to influence current understanding of emotion-drug interactions. This historical analysis examines Darwin's correspondence, experimental notebooks, and published observations to evaluate his contributions to the field. We analyzed Darwin's documentation of emotional responses across species, his early recognition of the physiological basis of emotional expression, and his proto-experimental methodology involving photographic documentation and cross-cultural surveys. Darwin's work demonstrated remarkable prescience in identifying the involuntary nature of emotional expressions, the role of the autonomic nervous system in emotional states, and the evolutionary conservation of emotional mechanisms across species. His observations of facial muscle contractions, cardiovascular responses, and respiratory changes during emotional states anticipated key targets of modern anxiolytic and antidepressant medications. Furthermore, his recognition that emotional expressions could be modified through conscious effort foreshadowed current research into cognitive-behavioral interventions and their neurochemical correlates. Darwin's systematic approach to studying emotional expression established methodological principles that remain relevant to contemporary neuropharmacological research, particularly in the development of animal models for psychiatric disorders and the assessment of drug efficacy in emotional regulation.
journal: Journal of the history of the neurosciences

title: Microelectrode array-based system for neuropharmacological applications with cortical neurons cultured in vitro.
abstract: In vitro neuronal networks cultured on microelectrode arrays (MEAs) represent a promising platform for neuropharmacological screening and mechanistic studies of neural activity modulation. However, existing MEA systems often lack the temporal resolution and signal processing capabilities required for comprehensive pharmacological analysis. We developed an integrated MEA-based system optimized for real-time monitoring of spontaneous and evoked electrical activity in primary rat cortical neurons. The system incorporates a 60-electrode planar array with 30 μm diameter electrodes spaced at 200 μm intervals, coupled to custom amplification circuitry and digital signal processing algorithms for spike detection and network burst analysis. Primary cortical neurons from embryonic day 18 Sprague-Dawley rats were cultured on poly-D-lysine coated MEAs and maintained for 14-21 days in vitro. We characterized baseline network activity patterns and tested acute responses to GABA_A receptor modulators (muscimol, bicuculline), glutamate receptor antagonists (CNQX, APV), and sodium channel blockers (TTX, lidocaine). The system reliably detected single-unit spikes with signal-to-noise ratios exceeding 4:1 and temporal resolution of 40 kHz. Pharmacological interventions produced dose-dependent, reversible changes in firing rates and network burst characteristics consistent with known mechanisms of action. Muscimol (1-50 μM) decreased spike rates by 65-95%, while bicuculline (10-100 μM) increased burst frequency by 200-400%. This MEA platform provides a robust, high-throughput approach for screening neuroactive compounds and investigating network-level effects of pharmacological interventions in vitro.

title: Effects of soman (pinacolyl methylphosphonofluoridate) on coronary blood flow and cardiac function in swine.
abstract: Organophosphorus compounds such as soman pose significant toxicological threats due to their irreversible inhibition of acetylcholinesterase, leading to cholinergic crisis. While the neurological and respiratory effects of soman poisoning are well-documented, the direct cardiovascular consequences remain poorly characterized. This study examined the acute effects of soman exposure on coronary hemodynamics and myocardial function in an anesthetized swine model. Twelve Yorkshire swine (25-30 kg) were instrumented with coronary flow probes, left ventricular pressure catheters, and ECG electrodes under pentobarbital anesthesia. Animals received either soman (0.5 × LD50, n=8) or saline control (n=4) via intravenous injection. Coronary blood flow, left ventricular pressure, heart rate, and cardiac output were continuously monitored for 120 minutes post-exposure. Soman administration produced rapid onset of cholinergic signs including bradycardia, hypotension, and cardiac arrhythmias within 5-10 minutes. Coronary blood flow decreased significantly by 35 ± 8% (p<0.01) compared to baseline, while coronary vascular resistance increased 2.3-fold. Left ventricular contractility (dP/dt max) declined by 42 ± 12% (p<0.001), and cardiac output fell to 60% of control values. These hemodynamic changes preceded the onset of severe respiratory distress and were partially reversed by atropine pretreatment in additional animals. The results demonstrate that soman exerts direct cardiotoxic effects independent of respiratory compromise, suggesting that cardiovascular support should be considered as an essential component of organophosphate poisoning treatment protocols.
journal: Fundamental and applied toxicology : official journal of the Society of Toxicology

title: [The way out from crisis--from the closing of the National Psychiatric and Neurologic Institute (OPNI) to Semmelweis University].
abstract: The closure of the National Psychiatric and Neurologic Institute (OPNI) in 2007 represented a critical juncture in Hungarian psychiatric care, necessitating comprehensive evaluation of patient outcomes and treatment continuity during the institutional transition to Semmelweis University. This retrospective cohort study analyzed clinical data from 847 patients with severe psychiatric disorders who were transferred from OPNI to the newly established Department of Psychiatry and Psychotherapy at Semmelweis University between January 2007 and December 2008. Primary outcomes included treatment adherence rates, hospitalization frequency, and clinical symptom severity assessed using the Brief Psychiatric Rating Scale (BPRS) and Global Assessment of Functioning (GAF). Secondary measures examined medication compliance, outpatient follow-up rates, and patient satisfaction scores. Despite initial concerns regarding service disruption, 78.3% of patients maintained stable treatment engagement within six months of transfer. Mean BPRS scores showed no significant deterioration (pre-transfer: 42.1±12.4 vs. post-transfer: 43.7±13.1, p=0.23), while GAF scores demonstrated modest improvement (51.2±15.8 vs. 54.3±16.2, p=0.04) at 12-month follow-up. Medication adherence rates remained stable at 71%, comparable to pre-closure levels. However, emergency department visits increased by 23% during the initial transition period before returning to baseline. The successful integration of OPNI services into Semmelweus University demonstrates that institutional transitions in psychiatric care can be achieved without compromising patient outcomes when implemented with adequate planning, resource allocation, and continuity protocols. These findings provide valuable insights for future healthcare system reorganizations in psychiatric services.
journal: Psychiatria Hungarica : A Magyar Pszichiatriai Tarsasag tudomanyos folyoirata

title: Increased levels of gamma-glutamylamines in Huntington disease CSF.
abstract: Transglutaminases (TGases) catalyze several reactions with protein substrates, including formation of gamma-glutamyl-epsilon-lysine cross-links and gamma-glutamylpolyamine residues. The resulting gamma-glutamylamines are excised intact during proteolysis. TGase activity is altered in several diseases, highlighting the importance of in situ enzymatic determinations. Previous work showed that TGase activity (as measured by an in vitro assay) and free gamma-glutamyl-epsilon-lysine levels are elevated in Huntington disease (HD) and that gamma-glutamyl-epsilon-lysine is increased in HD CSF. Although free gamma-glutamyl-epsilon-lysine was used in these studies as an index of in situ TGase activity, gamma-glutamylpolyamines may also be diagnostic. We have devised methods for the simultaneous determination of four gamma-glutamylamines in CSF: gamma-glutamyl-epsilon-lysine, gamma-glutamylspermidine, gamma-glutamylputrescine, and bis-gamma-glutamylputrescine and showed that all are present in normal human CSF at concentrations of approximately 150, 670, 40, and 240 nM, respectively. The high gamma-glutamylspermidine/gamma-glutamylputrescine and gamma-glutamylspermidine/bis-gamma-glutamylputrescine ratios presumably reflect in part the large spermidine to putrescine mole ratio in human brain. We also showed that all four gamma-glutamylamines are elevated in HD CSF. Our findings support the hypotheses that (i) gamma-glutamylpolyamines are reflective of TGase activity in human brain, (ii) polyamination is an important post-translational modification of brain proteins, and (iii) TGase-catalyzed modification of proteins is increased in HD brain.

title: The inferior temporal cortex: architecture, computation, and representation.
abstract: Neurons in the inferior temporal cortex (IT), an area crucially involved in visual object recognition in monkeys, show the visual response properties and anatomical/chemical nature which are distinct from those in the cortical areas that feed visual inputs to the IT. Earlier physiological studies showed that IT neurons have large receptive fields covering the center and contralateral (often bilateral) visual fields, stimulus selectivity for images of complex objects or shapes, and translation invariance of the stimulus selectivity. Recent studies have revealed new aspects of their properties such as invariant selectivity for shapes despite drastic changes in various physical attributes of stimuli, latent excitatory inputs masked by stimulus-specific GABAergic inhibition, selectivity for binocular disparity and 3-dimensional surface structures, profound effects of learning on the stimulus selectivity, and columnar clustering of neurons with similar stimulus selectivity for shapes and other object features. Another line of research using histological techniques have revealed that pyramidal neurons in the IT are larger in the size of dendritic arbors, in the number of dendritic branches and spines, and in the size and distribution of horizontal axonal arbors than those in the earlier areas, allowing them to integrate a larger population of afferents and process more diverse inputs. The concentration of several neurochemicals including those related to synaptic transmission or plasticity changes systematically towards the IT along the occipitotemporal pathway. Many of the characteristics of IT neurons parallel or explain certain aspects of visual object perception, although the behavioral relevance has yet to be addressed experimentally.

title: Psychopharmacology and ethnicity: A comparative study on Senegalese and Italian men.
abstract: Ethnic variations in drug metabolism and response represent critical considerations in psychopharmacological treatment, yet remain understudied in African populations. This study examined pharmacokinetic and pharmacodynamic differences in psychotropic medication response between Senegalese and Italian men. We conducted a randomized, double-blind crossover trial with 84 healthy male volunteers (42 Senegalese, 42 Italian) aged 18-35 years. Participants received single doses of risperidone (2mg), sertraline (50mg), and lorazepam (1mg) in separate sessions with 14-day washout periods. Blood samples were collected at predetermined intervals for 48 hours post-administration to assess plasma concentrations via LC-MS/MS. Cognitive and psychomotor assessments were performed using standardized batteries. Genotyping for CYP2D6, CYP2C19, and CYP3A4 polymorphisms was conducted. Senegalese participants demonstrated significantly higher plasma concentrations of risperidone (AUC: 1.47-fold increase, p<0.001) and its active metabolite, with delayed clearance compared to Italian subjects. Sertraline metabolism showed similar patterns (AUC: 1.32-fold increase, p<0.01). CYP2D6 poor metabolizer phenotypes were more prevalent in Senegalese participants (18.2% vs 7.1%, p<0.05). Lorazepam pharmacokinetics showed no significant ethnic differences. Cognitive performance decrements correlated with higher drug exposures in both groups. These findings highlight substantial ethnic differences in psychotropic drug metabolism between West African and European populations, primarily attributed to genetic polymorphisms in cytochrome P450 enzymes. Results support the need for ethnicity-guided dosing strategies and personalized medicine approaches in global psychiatric practice.
journal: The world journal of biological psychiatry : the official journal of the World Federation of Societies of Biological Psychiatry

title: Neuropharmacologic correlates of deglutition: lessons from fictive swallowing.
abstract: The neurochemical mechanisms underlying deglutition remain poorly understood despite the critical importance of swallowing function. Recent advances in fictive swallowing preparations have provided new opportunities to examine the neuropharmacologic basis of this complex motor behavior. We investigated the role of various neurotransmitter systems in the generation and modulation of fictive swallowing patterns using an in vitro brainstem preparation from adult cats. Fictive swallowing was induced by electrical stimulation of the superior laryngeal nerve while recording from hypoglossal and recurrent laryngeal nerve branches. Bath application of neurotransmitter agonists and antagonists was used to assess their effects on swallowing motor patterns. Serotonin (5-HT) application significantly enhanced the amplitude and duration of fictive swallowing bursts, while the 5-HT2 receptor antagonist ketanserin abolished these facilitatory effects. GABA application dose-dependently suppressed swallowing activity, with complete inhibition occurring at concentrations above 100 μM. The GABAA receptor antagonist bicuculline partially reversed GABA-induced suppression and spontaneously increased swallowing frequency. Dopamine produced biphasic effects, initially facilitating then suppressing swallowing patterns at higher concentrations. Acetylcholine consistently enhanced swallowing activity through muscarinic mechanisms, as atropine blocked these effects while nicotinic antagonists had minimal impact. These findings demonstrate that fictive swallowing is modulated by multiple neurotransmitter systems, with serotonin and acetylcholine providing excitatory drive while GABA exerts inhibitory control. Understanding these neuropharmacologic correlates may inform therapeutic approaches for dysphagia associated with neurologic disorders.

title: Photoinactivation of native AMPA receptors reveals their real-time trafficking.
abstract: AMPA receptors mediate fast excitatory synaptic transmission in the central nervous system and undergo constitutive trafficking between synaptic and extrasynaptic sites, yet the real-time dynamics of this process remain poorly understood. Here we developed a novel photoinactivation approach using the membrane-impermeant crosslinking reagent bis(sulfosuccinimidyl)suberate (BS³) coupled with caged compounds to irreversibly inactivate surface AMPA receptors in cultured hippocampal neurons. Following photoinactivation of existing surface receptors, we monitored the recovery of AMPA receptor-mediated currents using whole-cell patch-clamp recordings and tracked receptor insertion using immunofluorescence microscopy with GluR1 and GluR2 subunit-specific antibodies. Photoinactivation reduced AMPA receptor currents by 85-90% within 2 minutes, with minimal effects on NMDA or GABA receptor function. Recovery of synaptic AMPA currents followed biphasic kinetics, with a rapid phase (τ = 8.2 ± 1.4 min) representing 40% of recovery and a slower phase (τ = 47 ± 8 min) accounting for the remainder. Immunofluorescence revealed that newly inserted receptors initially appeared at extrasynaptic sites before accumulating at synaptic locations. Brefeldin A treatment blocked recovery, confirming dependence on the secretory pathway. Interestingly, synaptic activity enhanced both the rate and extent of recovery, with bicuculline treatment increasing the fast phase contribution to 65%. These results demonstrate that AMPA receptors undergo rapid, activity-dependent trafficking from intracellular pools to synapses, providing direct evidence for constitutive receptor cycling that may underlie homeostatic plasticity mechanisms.

title: Applying small quantities of multiple compounds to defined locations of in vitro brain slices.
abstract: When studying in vitro brain slices, rapidly applying multiple agonists, antagonists, drugs, or modulatory compounds is a significant technical problem. There are three major ways that multiple compounds are applied to slices: by bath, via a U-tube device, or by pressure application using a "puffer" pipette. Each of these methods has advantages and disadvantages, making each more appropriate for particular purposes. Because puffer pipettes have a small, sharp tip, they are best suited to apply a small quantity of a compound to a well-defined location within the slice. When used in this way, puffer pipettes have two shortcomings. Solution leaking from the tip of the pipette can contaminate the signal, and it is difficult to apply more than one test solution to exactly the same local area of the slice. We describe methods and newly designed devices aimed at overcoming those limitations. Relatively inexpensive approaches are described to apply eight different solutions to the same exact location deep within a brain slice. The validity of the approach is verified by measuring ligand-gated channel currents activated by glutamate (Glu), acetylcholine (ACh), and gamma-amino butyric acid (GABA).

title: [Nanopsychiatry. The potential role of nanotechnologies in the future of psychiatry. A systematic review].
abstract: Psychiatric disorders represent a major global health burden, yet current pharmacotherapeutic approaches remain limited by poor blood-brain barrier penetration, systemic side effects, and suboptimal drug targeting. Nanotechnology offers promising solutions through enhanced drug delivery systems and novel therapeutic modalities. This systematic review aimed to evaluate the current state and future potential of nanotechnology applications in psychiatric treatment. We conducted a comprehensive search of PubMed, Embase, and Web of Science databases from inception through December 2012, using terms related to nanotechnology, nanoparticles, drug delivery, and psychiatric disorders. Studies investigating nanocarrier systems, targeted drug delivery, and nanotechnology-based diagnostics in psychiatric contexts were included. Forty-three studies met inclusion criteria, comprising 28 preclinical studies and 15 theoretical frameworks. Polymeric nanoparticles, liposomes, and solid lipid nanoparticles demonstrated enhanced central nervous system penetration for antidepressants, antipsychotics, and anxiolytics in animal models. Targeted delivery systems using transferrin and lactoferrin receptors showed 2-4 fold increases in brain bioavailability compared to conventional formulations. Nanoparticle-mediated delivery reduced peripheral side effects by 40-60% while maintaining therapeutic efficacy. Emerging applications included quantum dots for neuroimaging and carbon nanotubes for neural interface technologies. Despite promising preclinical results, clinical translation remains limited by safety concerns and regulatory challenges. Nanopsychiatry represents an emerging field with significant potential to revolutionize psychiatric treatment through improved drug delivery, reduced side effects, and enhanced therapeutic precision. However, extensive safety evaluations and standardized regulatory frameworks are essential before clinical implementation.

title: EFNS guidelines for the diagnosis and management of Alzheimer's disease.
abstract: In 2008 a task force was set up to develop a revision of the European Federation of the Neurological Societies (EFNS) guideline for the diagnosis and management of Alzheimer's disease (AD) and other disorders associated with dementia, published in early 2007. The aim of this revised international guideline was to present a peer-reviewed evidence-based statement for the guidance of practice for clinical neurologists, geriatricians, psychiatrists, and other specialist physicians responsible for the care of patients with AD. Mild cognitive impairment and non-Alzheimer dementias are not included in this guideline. The task force working group reviewed evidence from original research articles, meta-analysis, and systematic reviews, published before May 2009. The evidence was classified and consensus recommendations graded (A, B, or C) according to the EFNS guidance. Where there was a lack of evidence, but clear consensus, good practice points were provided. The recommendations for clinical diagnosis, blood tests, neuropsychology, neuroimaging, electroencephalography, cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) analysis, genetic testing, disclosure of diagnosis, treatment of AD, behavioural and psychological symptoms in dementia, legal issues, counselling and support for caregivers were all revised as compared with the previous EFNS guideline. A number of new recommendations and good practice points are made, namely in CSF, neuropsychology, neuroimaging and reviewing non-evidence based therapies. The assessment, interpretation, and treatment of symptoms, disability, needs, and caregiver stress during the course of AD require the contribution of many different professionals. These professionals should adhere to these guideline to improve the diagnosis and management of AD.

title: History of modern psychopharmacology: a personal view with an emphasis on antidepressants.
abstract: This article provides the chemical basis for the molecular modification of H1 antihistamines in the rational development of some antidepressant and antipsychotic drugs. A review of the literature and personal experiences have been compiled. The contributions of many basic scientists, the crucial observations of clinicians, and the desire of the drug industry to make money have resulted in the currently available psychopharmacological treatments. The future development of psychopharmacology depends on better clinical research to generate new hypotheses of the chemical and behavioral pathology of mental disease. Psychosomatic medicine can make a unique contribution in its interdisciplinary role of stressing brain, body, and mind relationships.

title: Cognitive and neurodevelopmental effects of antiepileptic drugs.
abstract: Antiepileptic drugs (AEDs) are essential for seizure control in pediatric epilepsy, yet concerns persist regarding their potential impact on cognitive development. This systematic review examined the neurocognitive effects of commonly prescribed AEDs in children and adolescents with epilepsy. We conducted a comprehensive literature search of studies published between 1990-2010, identifying 47 controlled studies examining cognitive outcomes in pediatric patients receiving monotherapy with phenytoin, carbamazepine, valproate, lamotrigine, levetiracetam, or topiramate. Primary outcomes included measures of IQ, memory, attention, executive function, and academic achievement. Secondary analyses examined dose-dependent effects and reversibility of cognitive impairment. Results demonstrated significant heterogeneity across AEDs in their cognitive profiles. Traditional AEDs, particularly phenytoin and high-dose valproate, were associated with decreased processing speed (effect size d = -0.42, p < 0.01) and impaired verbal memory (d = -0.38, p < 0.05). Topiramate showed the most pronounced negative effects on language function and working memory (d = -0.65, p < 0.001). In contrast, lamotrigine and levetiracetam exhibited minimal cognitive impact, with some studies suggesting modest improvements in attention. Dose-response relationships were evident for most AEDs, with cognitive impairment becoming more pronounced at higher serum concentrations. Importantly, cognitive effects were largely reversible upon drug discontinuation, though recovery was variable. These findings underscore the importance of considering cognitive outcomes when selecting AEDs for pediatric patients, favoring newer agents with more favorable neurocognitive profiles when clinically appropriate.

title: New vistas in immunopsychopharmacology.
abstract: The emerging field of immunopsychopharmacology represents a paradigm shift in understanding the complex interactions between the immune system, central nervous system, and psychoactive drugs. Growing evidence suggests that traditional psychopharmacological agents may exert therapeutic effects partially through modulation of immune function, while immune mediators reciprocally influence neurochemical processes underlying psychiatric disorders. This review synthesizes current knowledge from both clinical and preclinical studies examining bidirectional neuroimmune communication in the context of psychotropic drug action. We analyzed data from recent investigations utilizing cytokine measurements, lymphocyte proliferation assays, and neuroendocrine challenge tests in patients receiving antidepressants, antipsychotics, and anxiolytics. Additionally, we examined animal studies employing immune stimulation models and psychopharmacological interventions. Results demonstrate that selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors and tricyclic antidepressants significantly alter interleukin-1β, tumor necrosis factor-α, and interleukin-6 levels in depressed patients, correlating with clinical improvement. Antipsychotic medications modulate T-helper cell responses and natural killer cell activity in schizophrenic patients. Benzodiazepines influence immunoglobulin production and complement activation. Conversely, immune challenges with lipopolysaccharide or interleukin-1 administration in animal models produce depression-like behaviors reversible by antidepressant treatment. These findings suggest that immunomodulation may represent a novel therapeutic target for psychiatric disorders. Understanding neuroimmune mechanisms could lead to development of more effective treatments combining traditional psychopharmacological approaches with immunotherapeutic strategies, potentially revolutionizing psychiatric medicine in the next millennium.

title: Traditional Chinese medicine and Western psychopharmacology: building bridges.
abstract: Traditional Chinese Medicine (TCM) and Western psychopharmacology have historically operated under distinct theoretical frameworks, yet both systems target neuropsychiatric disorders through modulation of neurotransmitter systems. This review examines potential integration points between TCM herbal formulations and evidence-based Western psychopharmacological approaches. We conducted a systematic analysis of 127 peer-reviewed studies published between 2000-2012, focusing on TCM compounds with demonstrated effects on major neurotransmitter pathways including serotonergic, dopaminergic, GABAergic, and glutamatergic systems. Particular attention was given to frequently prescribed TCM formulations for depression, anxiety, and cognitive disorders, including Gan Mai Da Zao Tang, An Shen Ding Zhi Wan, and Ding Zhi Wan. Analysis revealed that 68% of examined TCM formulations contained bioactive compounds with measurable effects on Western-defined neurotransmitter targets. Hypericum perforatum constituents showed significant serotonin reuptake inhibition comparable to conventional SSRIs, while Paeonia lactiflora demonstrated GABA receptor modulation similar to benzodiazepine mechanisms. Ginkgo biloba extracts exhibited acetylcholinesterase inhibition and neuroprotective properties relevant to cognitive enhancement. Notably, 43% of TCM formulations demonstrated multi-target activity, potentially offering advantages over single-target Western pharmaceuticals. However, standardization challenges, herb-drug interactions, and limited pharmacokinetic data remain significant barriers to integration. These findings suggest that systematic investigation of TCM compounds using Western pharmacological methodologies may identify novel therapeutic targets and combination strategies, potentially bridging traditional wisdom with contemporary neuropharmacological practice while maintaining rigorous safety and efficacy standards.

title: [The neurochemistry and neuropharmacology of frontotemporal dementia].
abstract: Frontotemporal dementia (FTD) represents a heterogeneous group of neurodegenerative disorders characterized by progressive deterioration of the frontal and temporal cortices, leading to profound changes in behavior, personality, and language. Despite its clinical significance as the second most common cause of presenile dementia, the neurochemical basis of FTD remains poorly understood compared to Alzheimer's disease. This review synthesizes current knowledge regarding the neurochemical alterations and pharmacological interventions in FTD. We conducted a comprehensive analysis of postmortem studies, neuroimaging investigations, and clinical trials published between 1990 and 2005, focusing on neurotransmitter systems, protein aggregation patterns, and therapeutic approaches. The neurochemical profile of FTD reveals significant disruptions in multiple neurotransmitter systems, including reduced cholinergic and GABAergic activity in affected regions, altered serotonergic function correlating with behavioral symptoms, and dysregulation of dopaminergic pathways contributing to motor manifestations. Protein aggregation studies demonstrate distinct patterns of tau and ubiquitin-positive inclusions, differentiating FTD subtypes from other tauopathies. Current pharmacological interventions show limited efficacy, with selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors providing modest benefits for behavioral symptoms and cholinesterase inhibitors showing inconsistent results. Novel therapeutic targets include modulation of tau phosphorylation, enhancement of GABAergic transmission, and neuroprotective strategies targeting oxidative stress. The complex neurochemical landscape of FTD necessitates development of targeted therapies addressing specific neurotransmitter deficits and pathological protein accumulation, moving beyond traditional approaches used in Alzheimer's disease.
