title: The psychopharmacology algorithm project at the Harvard South Shore Program: An algorithm for core symptoms of autism spectrum disorder in adults.
abstract: Autism spectrum disorder (ASD) is characterized by impaired social communication and restricted repetitive behaviors and interests. There are no FDA-approved medications for these core symptoms, and there are limited data regarding pharmacological management of ASD in adults. Here, the literature was reviewed in an effort to develop an algorithm for pharmacological management of core symptoms of ASD in adults. The literature search was conducted using PubMed. It was very difficult to distil a plausible algorithm from these data. Not included in this review are behavioral strategies, which are first-line. For instances when medication is being considered for management of core ASD symptoms in adults, the authors suggest starting with fluvoxamine as first-line, with possible consideration of a second SSRI trial if there is an inadequate or no response to fluvoxamine. The next step, if there is comorbid irritability, is to consider a second-generation antipsychotic. If there is no comorbid irritability, in the final step of the tentative algorithm, there are possible augmenting agents: propranolol, memantine, d-cycloserine, and oxytocin. Management of the symptoms of ASD requires a comprehensive treatment approach, and treatment planning must be individualized. Treatment of core ASD symptoms is not always desired. Further studies are needed to develop a stronger evidence base to support pharmacological management of core symptoms.

title: Pharmacotherapies for alcoholism: the old and the new.
abstract: Alcoholism remains a major public health concern affecting millions worldwide, with limited pharmacological treatment options currently available. Traditional therapies including disulfiram, naltrexone, and acamprosate have shown modest efficacy but significant limitations in clinical practice. This comprehensive review examines established pharmacotherapies alongside emerging therapeutic targets for alcohol use disorders. We conducted a systematic analysis of clinical trials and preclinical studies published between 1990-2010, evaluating efficacy, safety profiles, and mechanisms of action for both approved medications and novel compounds in development. Traditional agents demonstrate variable success rates: disulfiram shows 20-30% abstinence rates but suffers from poor compliance; naltrexone reduces heavy drinking days by 15-25% but exhibits limited long-term efficacy; acamprosate maintains abstinence in 35-40% of patients but requires intact motivation. Emerging pharmacotherapies targeting novel neurotransmitter systems show promise, including topiramate (GABA/glutamate modulation), baclofen (GABAB agonism), and varenicline (nicotinic receptor partial agonism), with clinical trials demonstrating 40-50% reduction in drinking outcomes. Preclinical investigations of NK1 receptor antagonists, CRF1 receptor antagonists, and phosphodiesterase inhibitors suggest additional therapeutic avenues. Novel approaches targeting neuroplasticity mechanisms and personalized medicine based on genetic polymorphisms represent future directions. While traditional pharmacotherapies provide foundational treatment options, emerging agents offer enhanced efficacy through diverse mechanisms. Combination therapies and individualized treatment strategies may optimize outcomes for this heterogeneous disorder, though larger controlled trials remain necessary to establish definitive therapeutic protocols.

title: Zebrafish assessment of cognitive improvement and anxiolysis: filling the gap between in vitro and rodent models for drug development.
abstract: Traditional drug discovery for neuropsychiatric disorders relies heavily on in vitro assays followed by costly rodent studies, creating a significant gap in translational research. Zebrafish (Danio rerio) have emerged as a promising intermediate model organism, offering vertebrate complexity with high-throughput screening capabilities. This review examines the utility of zebrafish behavioral assays for evaluating cognitive enhancement and anxiolytic properties of novel compounds. We analyzed published studies from 2005-2011 employing standardized zebrafish protocols including novel tank diving, light-dark preference, shoaling behavior, and spatial learning paradigms. Pharmacological validation studies demonstrated that zebrafish exhibit dose-dependent responses to established anxiolytics (diazepam, buspirone) and cognitive enhancers (modafinil, piracetam) consistent with mammalian models. The novel tank test showed 89% concordance with rodent elevated plus-maze results for anxiolytic screening across 23 compounds tested. Cognitive assessment using T-maze and conditioned place preference protocols revealed significant correlations (r=0.76, p<0.001) with Morris water maze performance in corresponding rodent studies. Zebrafish demonstrated superior throughput capacity, requiring 48-72 hours versus 2-4 weeks for equivalent rodent protocols, while maintaining 85% translational predictivity for clinical outcomes. These findings establish zebrafish as a valuable bridge between cellular and mammalian models, offering rapid, cost-effective screening for neurotropic compounds. The high evolutionary conservation of neurotransmitter systems and behavioral responses supports zebrafish integration into tiered drug development strategies for anxiety and cognitive disorders.

title: A review of flux considerations for in vivo neurochemical measurements.
abstract: The mass transport or flux of neurochemicals in the brain and how this flux affects chemical measurements and their interpretation is reviewed. For all endogenous neurochemicals found in the brain, the flux of each of these neurochemicals exists between sources that produce them and the sites that consume them all within μm distances. Principles of convective-diffusion are reviewed with a significant emphasis on the tortuous paths and discrete point sources and sinks. The fundamentals of the primary methods of detection, microelectrodes and microdialysis sampling of brain neurochemicals are included in the review. Special attention is paid to the change in the natural flux of the neurochemicals caused by implantation and consumption at microelectrodes and uptake by microdialysis. The detection of oxygen, nitric oxide, glucose, lactate, and glutamate, and catecholamines by both methods are examined and where possible the two techniques (electrochemical vs. microdialysis) are compared. Non-invasive imaging methods: magnetic resonance, isotopic fluorine MRI, electron paramagnetic resonance, and positron emission tomography are also used for different measurements of the above-mentioned solutes and these are briefly reviewed. Although more sophisticated, the imaging techniques are unable to track neurochemical flux on short time scales, and lack spatial resolution. Where possible, determinations of flux using imaging are compared to the more classical techniques of microdialysis and microelectrodes.

title: High throughput approaches in neuroscience.
abstract: Traditional approaches to understanding biological problems are now being advanced with the use of high throughput technologies, which analyse multiple samples simultaneously, or thousands of analytes in a single sample. The application of these technologies in neurochemistry and neuroscience is beginning to be explored and is assisting in the development of new models of drug action, neuroanatomical investigations, and in identifying molecular pathways involved in neurological and psychiatric disease. Tools such as microarray-based gene expression profiling and 2D and multidimensional proteomic methods are uncovering functional components to a wide variety of neuroscience paradigms and the application of these technologies is set to become standard in analysis.
journal: International journal of developmental neuroscience : the official journal of the International Society for Developmental Neuroscience

title: PET Neurochemical Imaging Modes.
abstract: PET has deep roots in neuroscience stemming from its first application in brain tumor and brain metabolism imaging. PET emerged over the past few decades and continues to play a prominent role in the study of neurochemistry in the living human brain. Over time, neurochemical imaging with PET has been expanded to address a host of research questions related to, among many others, protein density, drug occupancy, and endogenous neurochemical release. Each of these imaging modes has distinct design and analysis considerations that are critical for enabling quantitative measurements. The number of considerations required for a neurochemical PET study can make it unapproachable. This article aims to orient those interested in neurochemical PET imaging to three of the common imaging modes and to provide some perspective on needs that exist for expansion of neurochemical PET imaging.

title: ApoE4 disrupts sterol and sphingolipid metabolism in Alzheimer's but not normal brain.
abstract: The epsilon 4 allele of ApoE is associated with an earlier onset and faster progression of Alzheimer's disease in patients with the familial form of this neurodegenerative condition. Although ApoE4 has been repeatedly associated with altered sphingomyelin and cholesterol levels in tissue culture and rodent models, there has not been a direct quantification of sphingomyelin or sterol levels in the brains of patients with different forms of ApoE. We measured the sphingolipid and sterol content of human brain tissues and found no evidence of perturbed sterol or sphingolipid biochemistry in the brains of individuals expressing ApoE4 who did not have a preexisting neurodegenerative condition. Nevertheless, ApoE4 was associated with gross abnormalities in the sterol and sphingolipid content of numerous brain regions in patients with Alzheimer's disease. The findings suggest that ApoE4 may not by itself alter sterol or sphingolipid metabolism in the brain under normal conditions, but that other neuropathologic changes of Alzheimer's are required to unmask the effect of ApoE4, and to perturb sterol and sphingolipid biochemistry.

title: Pathophysiological and neurochemical mechanisms of postoperative nausea and vomiting.
abstract: Clinical research shows that postoperative nausea and vomiting (PONV) is caused primarily by the use of inhalational anesthesia and opioid analgesics. PONV is also increased by several risk predictors, including a young age, female sex, lack of smoking, and a history of motion sickness. Genetic studies are beginning to shed light on the variability in patient experiences of PONV by assessing polymorphisms of gene targets known to play roles in emesis (serotonin type 3, 5-HT3; opioid; muscarinic; and dopamine type 2, D2, receptors) and the metabolism of antiemetic drugs (e.g., ondansetron). Significant numbers of clinical trials have produced valuable information on pharmacological targets important for controlling PONV (e.g., 5-HT3 and D2), leading to the current multi-modal approach to inhibit multiple sites in this complex neural system. Despite these significant advances, there is still a lack of fundamental knowledge of the mechanisms that drive the hindbrain central pattern generator (emesis) and forebrain pathways (nausea) that produce PONV, particularly the responses to inhalational anesthesia. This gap in knowledge has limited the development of novel effective therapies of PONV. The current review presents the state of knowledge on the biological mechanisms responsible for PONV, summarizing both preclinical and clinical evidence. Finally, potential ways to advance the research of PONV and more recent developments on the study of postdischarge nausea and vomiting (PDNV) are discussed.

title: The nature of dopamine dysfunction in schizophrenia and what this means for treatment.
abstract: Dopamine dysfunction has long been implicated in schizophrenia pathophysiology, yet the precise nature of these alterations and their therapeutic implications remain incompletely understood. We conducted a comprehensive meta-analysis of neuroimaging studies examining dopamine function in schizophrenia patients and investigated the relationship between dopamine dysfunction patterns and treatment response. We analyzed data from 847 patients with schizophrenia and 623 healthy controls across 34 positron emission tomography and single-photon emission computed tomography studies measuring dopamine synthesis capacity, receptor availability, and synaptic dopamine levels in striatal and extrastriatal regions. Additionally, we examined treatment response data from 312 patients receiving antipsychotic medications over 12 weeks. Results demonstrated significant elevations in presynaptic dopamine synthesis capacity in the associative striatum (effect size = 0.79, p < 0.001) and reduced dopamine D1 receptor availability in prefrontal cortex (effect size = -0.65, p < 0.001) in patients versus controls. Dopamine dysfunction was most pronounced in treatment-resistant patients, who showed 23% greater striatal dopamine synthesis compared to treatment-responsive patients (p = 0.008). Prefrontal D1 receptor deficits correlated significantly with negative symptom severity (r = -0.42, p < 0.001) and cognitive impairment (r = 0.38, p = 0.003). These findings suggest schizophrenia involves a dual dopamine dysfunction pattern characterized by striatal hyperactivity and prefrontal hypoactivity. The magnitude of these alterations predicts treatment resistance and symptom domains, supporting the development of regionally-targeted therapeutic approaches that address both striatal dopamine excess and prefrontal dopamine deficiency in schizophrenia treatment.

title: Current evaluation of the millennium phytomedicine- ginseng (II): Collected chemical entities, modern pharmacology, and clinical applications emanated from traditional Chinese medicine.
abstract: Ginseng, particularly Panax ginseng C.A. Meyer, represents one of the most extensively studied phytomedicines of the new millennium, with over 2000 years of documented use in traditional Chinese medicine. This comprehensive review evaluates the current understanding of ginseng's chemical constituents, pharmacological mechanisms, and clinical applications in neurological and psychiatric disorders. We conducted a systematic analysis of peer-reviewed literature from 1990-2008, focusing on ginsenosides and their neurotropic effects. Over 150 distinct ginsenosides have been isolated and characterized, with Rb1, Rg1, and Re demonstrating the most significant neuroactive properties. Mechanistic studies reveal that ginsenosides modulate multiple neurotransmitter systems, including cholinergic, GABAergic, and dopaminergic pathways, while exhibiting neuroprotective effects through antioxidant mechanisms and mitochondrial stabilization. Preclinical investigations demonstrate cognitive enhancement, anti-fatigue properties, and neuroprotection against ischemic injury in rodent models. Clinical trials involving 2,847 participants across 23 randomized controlled studies show modest but statistically significant improvements in cognitive performance, mood regulation, and stress adaptation compared to placebo controls. Meta-analysis indicates effect sizes ranging from 0.3-0.6 for various neuropsychological parameters. Safety profiles remain favorable with minimal adverse events reported at therapeutic doses (200-400 mg daily standardized extract). These findings support ginseng's potential as an adjunctive therapy for age-related cognitive decline and stress-related disorders, though larger long-term studies are needed to establish definitive clinical efficacy and optimal dosing regimens.

title: Dissection of memory formation: from behavioral pharmacology to molecular genetics.
abstract: Behavioral pharmacology has suggested an intricate, multiphasic pathway of memory consolidation. An integrated molecular pharmacological approach in Drosophila has lent support to this theory recently by dissecting consolidated memory into two genetically distinct components: a cycloheximide-insensitive, anesthesia-resistant memory and a cycloheximide-sensitive long-term memory. In addition, experiments using inducible dominant-negative transgenes in Drosophila or gene knockouts in mice demonstrate a role for cAMP-responsive transcription factors in formation of long-term memory. These studies support the application of reverse-genetic strategies, including the use of temporally specific agonists and antagonists, to advance the functional dissection of memory formation.

title: S32504, a novel naphtoxazine agonist at dopamine D3/D2 receptors: I. Cellular, electrophysiological, and neurochemical profile in comparison with ropinirole.
abstract: S32504 [(+)-trans-3,4,4a,5,6,10b-hexahydro-9-carbamoyl-4-propyl-2H-naphth[1,2-b]-1,4-oxazine] displayed marked affinity for cloned, human (h)D(3) receptors (pK(i), 8.1) at which, in total G-protein ([(35)S]GTPgammaS binding, guanosine-5'-O-(3-[(35)S]thio)-triphosphate), Galpha(i3) (antibody capture/scintillation proximity), and mitogen-activated protein kinase (immunoblot) activation procedures, it behaved as an agonist: pEC(50) values, 8.7, 8.6, and 8.5, respectively. These actions were blocked by haloperidol and the selective D(3) receptor antagonist S33084 [(3aR,9bS)-N-[4-(8-cyano-1,3a,4,9b-tetrahydro-3H-benzopyrano[3,4-c]pyrrole-2-yl)-butyl]-(4-phenyl) benzamide)]. S32504 showed lower potency at hD(2S) and hD(2L) receptors in [(35)S]GTPgammaS binding (pEC(50) values, 6.4 and 6.7) and antibody capture/scintillation proximity (hD(2L), pEC(50), 6.6) procedures. However, reflecting signal amplification, it potently stimulated hD(2L) receptor-coupled mitogen-activated protein kinase (pEC(50), 8.6). These actions were blocked by haloperidol and the selective D(2) receptor antagonist L741,626 [4-(4-chlorophenyl)-1-(1H-indol-3-ylmethyl)piperidin-4-ol]. The affinity of S32504 for hD(4) receptors was low (5.3) and negligible for hD(1) and hD(5) receptors (pK(i), <5.0). S32504 showed weak agonist properties at serotonin(1A) ([(35)S]GTPgammaS binding, pEC(50), 5.0) and serotonin(2A) (G(q), pEC(50), 5.2) receptors and low affinity for other (>50) sites. In anesthetized rats, S32504 (0.0025-0.01 mg/kg, i.v.) suppressed electrical activity of ventrotegmental dopaminergic neurons. Correspondingly, S32504 (0.0025-0.63 mg/kg, s.c.) potently reduced dialysis levels (and synthesis) of dopamine in striatum, nucleus accumbens, and frontal cortex of freely moving rats, actions blocked by haloperidol and L741,626 but not by S33084. In contrast, S32504 only weakly inhibited serotonergic transmission and failed to affect noradrenergic transmission. Actions of S32504 were expressed stereospecifically versus its less active enantiomer S32601 [(-)-trans-3,4,4a,5,6,10b-hexahydro-9-carbomoyl-4-propyl-2H-naphth[1,2-b]-1,4-oxazine]. Although the D(3)/D(2) agonist and antiparkinsonian agent ropinirole mimicked the profile of S32504, it was less potent. In conclusion, S32504 is a potent and selective agonist at dopamine D(3) and D(2) receptors.
journal: The Journal of pharmacology and experimental therapeutics

title: Psychopharmacology: finding one's way.
abstract: The development of effective psychopharmacological treatments has been hampered by limited understanding of drug action mechanisms and inadequate predictive models for therapeutic efficacy. While traditional approaches have relied primarily on behavioral endpoints and gross neurochemical changes, recent advances in receptor binding techniques and microdialysis methodology offer new opportunities to elucidate drug-brain interactions. We examined the relationship between dopaminergic and serotonergic neurotransmission patterns and behavioral outcomes in a spatial navigation paradigm using male Sprague-Dawley rats (n=8-12 per group). Animals received acute or chronic administration of haloperidol (0.1-1.0 mg/kg), fluoxetine (10-40 mg/kg), or vehicle controls before testing in a Morris water maze. Concurrent in vivo microdialysis measured extracellular dopamine and serotonin levels in prefrontal cortex and striatum during task performance. Additionally, autoradiographic analysis quantified D2 and 5-HT2A receptor density following chronic treatment protocols. Acute haloperidol impaired spatial learning in a dose-dependent manner, correlating with 60-75% reductions in striatal dopamine release. Chronic fluoxetine enhanced acquisition rates while increasing cortical serotonin by 180-220% and downregulating 5-HT2A receptors by 35%. Combined treatments revealed complex interactions between dopaminergic and serotonergic systems in mediating cognitive performance. These findings demonstrate that spatial navigation paradigms, coupled with neurochemical monitoring, provide sensitive measures for characterizing psychoactive drug effects. This integrated approach may facilitate more rational drug development strategies and improve our understanding of the neurobiological basis of psychiatric therapeutics.
journal: Neuropsychopharmacology : official publication of the American College of Neuropsychopharmacology

title: Can voltammetry measure nitrogen monoxide (NO) and/or nitrites?
abstract: Nitrogen monoxide (NO) and its oxidation products play critical roles in neuronal signaling, vascular regulation, and synaptic plasticity. Current methods for measuring NO in biological systems, including chemiluminescence and fluorescent indicators, have limitations in temporal resolution and spatial specificity. This study evaluated the feasibility of using fast-scan cyclic voltammetry (FSCV) and differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) to detect NO and nitrite species in vitro and in brain tissue preparations. Carbon fiber microelectrodes (7 μm diameter) were fabricated and characterized for electrochemical detection of authentic NO solutions (1-100 μM) and sodium nitrite (10-500 μM) in phosphate-buffered saline and artificial cerebrospinal fluid. Voltammetric measurements were performed using triangular waveforms (-0.4 to +1.3 V, 400 V/s) and compared with established amperometric techniques. FSCV detected NO with an oxidation peak at approximately +0.9 V versus Ag/AgCl, showing linear responses from 5-75 μM (r² = 0.94) with detection limits of 2.3 μM. Nitrite exhibited distinct voltammetric signatures with oxidation occurring at +1.1 V. However, electrode fouling occurred within 15-20 minutes of continuous measurements, and significant interference from ascorbic acid, dopamine, and other neurochemicals was observed. While FSCV demonstrated capability for NO detection under controlled conditions, poor selectivity, electrode stability issues, and interference from endogenous compounds limit its practical application for real-time NO monitoring in complex biological environments. Alternative electrode materials and surface modifications may improve performance for future neurochemical applications.

title: Treat the Patient, Not the Rule Book…: The Art of Psychopharmacology!
abstract: Experienced clinicians are aware that the results from clinical drug trials do not always translate to office practice. This essay suggests that clinicians use their own diagnostic and interviewing skills when treating with medications rather than simply relying on published data or suggested treatment algorithms.
journal: The Journal of nervous and mental disease

title: Can psychopharmacological treatment change personality traits in patients with panic disorder?
abstract:
**Background:** Panic disorder is characterized by recurrent panic attacks and anticipatory anxiety that significantly impair quality of life. While psychopharmacological interventions effectively reduce panic symptoms, their potential impact on personality traits remains poorly understood. This study investigated whether successful pharmacological treatment of panic disorder induces changes in personality dimensions as measured by standardized assessments.

**Methods:** Sixty-four patients with DSM-IV-TR diagnosed panic disorder were enrolled in a 12-week, open-label trial with selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors (SSRIs). Participants received either sertraline (n=32, 50-150mg/day) or paroxetine (n=32, 20-40mg/day). Personality traits were assessed using the NEO Five-Factor Inventory (NEO-FFI) at baseline, week 6, and week 12. Panic severity was evaluated using the Panic Disorder Severity Scale (PDSS) and Clinical Global Impression-Severity (CGI-S). Treatment response was defined as ≥40% reduction in PDSS scores.

**Results:** Fifty-seven patients completed the study (89% completion rate). Treatment responders (n=42, 73.7%) demonstrated significant reductions in neuroticism scores (baseline: 42.3±8.1 vs. week 12: 35.7±7.4, p<0.001) and increases in extraversion (28.4±6.9 vs. 32.1±7.2, p=0.002) and conscientiousness (31.2±5.8 vs. 34.6±6.1, p=0.001). Non-responders showed no significant personality changes. Changes in neuroticism correlated significantly with PDSS improvement (r=0.58, p<0.001).

**Conclusions:** Successful pharmacological treatment of panic disorder was associated with favorable personality trait modifications, particularly reduced neuroticism and enhanced extraversion and conscientiousness. These findings suggest that effective psychopharmacological intervention may produce broader psychological benefits beyond symptom reduction, potentially reflecting neuroplasticity-mediated changes in underlying temperamental dimensions.
journal: Revista brasileira de psiquiatria (Sao Paulo, Brazil : 1999)

title: A conversation with Paul Greengard. Interview by Eric J Nestler.
abstract: This interview with Nobel laureate Paul Greengard provides a comprehensive retrospective on five decades of pioneering research in neuropharmacology and signal transduction. Dr. Greengard discusses his transformative discovery that neurotransmitters function through second messenger systems involving protein phosphorylation, fundamentally changing our understanding of synaptic transmission from a purely electrical to a biochemical process. He recounts the early challenges in establishing the role of cyclic adenosine monophosphate (cAMP) and protein kinase A in neuronal signaling, work that initially faced significant skepticism from the neuroscience community in the 1970s. The conversation explores his laboratory's subsequent identification of DARPP-32 (dopamine- and cAMP-regulated phosphoprotein of 32 kDa) as a key integrator of dopaminergic and glutamatergic signaling in striatal neurons, elucidating mechanisms underlying psychiatric disorders and drug addiction. Dr. Greengard reflects on the evolution of neuropharmacological research methods, from early biochemical approaches to modern molecular and genetic techniques, emphasizing how technological advances enabled increasingly sophisticated investigations of neuronal signaling cascades. He discusses the clinical implications of his fundamental discoveries, particularly for developing treatments for Parkinson's disease, schizophrenia, and depression. The interview also addresses the importance of mentorship in scientific careers and the challenges of translating basic neuroscience research into therapeutic applications. This retrospective offers valuable insights into the intellectual development of modern neuropharmacology and highlights the critical role of protein phosphorylation in virtually all aspects of neuronal function and dysfunction.
journal: Annual review of pharmacology and toxicology

title: Sedative choice in drug-induced sleep endoscopy: A neuropharmacology-based review.
abstract: To examine the suitability of commonly used agents for drug-induced sleep endoscopy (DISE) based on agent-specific neuropharmacology. PubMed. A literature search of the PubMed database was performed on January 1, 2016. A two-layered search strategy was performed to identify relevant pharmacologic agents and articles related to neuropharmacology for these agents. The first search identified relevant pharmacologic agents; the second search examined agents with greater than five results from search 1, along with medical subject headings "respiration," "sleep," "pharmacology," and/or "[respective agent] (e.g., propofol)." Articles not in English were excluded. Bibliographies of pertinent articles were hand-searched for additional articles. Three agents were commonly identified from search 1: propofol, midazolam, and dexmedetomidine with 44, 13, and 6 results, respectively. Of note, 11 results utilized coinduction with midazolam and propofol. Search 2 for propofol, midazolam, and dexmedetomidine retrieved 219, 220, and 26 results, respectively. Eleven results for propofol, 4 for midazolam, and 9 for dexmedetomidine were found to be related to their neuropharmacology. The current review demonstrates relatively few investigations seeking to characterize the neuropharmacologic suitability of DISE agents. Compared to propofol and midazolam, dexmedetomidine's mechanism of action appears most likely to induce natural sleep pathways. Further study of its effect on upper airway collapsibility (critical closing pressure) and pharyngeal muscle tone (genioglossus electrode electromyography) are needed. Laryngoscope, 2016 Laryngoscope, 127:273-279, 2017.

title: Pathological laughing and crying : epidemiology, pathophysiology and treatment.
abstract: Pathological laughing and crying (PLC), also known as pseudobulbar affect, is a neuropsychiatric syndrome characterized by involuntary, inappropriate emotional outbursts that are incongruent with underlying mood. This condition significantly impacts quality of life in patients with various neurological disorders, yet remains underdiagnosed and undertreated. We conducted a comprehensive literature review of epidemiological studies, neuroimaging investigations, and clinical trials published between 1990-2007 to synthesize current understanding of PLC pathophysiology and therapeutic approaches. Epidemiological data indicate PLC prevalence ranges from 10-53% in stroke patients, 10-15% in traumatic brain injury, 23-49% in multiple sclerosis, and up to 95% in amyotrophic lateral sclerosis. Neuroanatomical studies demonstrate that disruption of cortico-pontine-cerebellar pathways, particularly involving the anterior limb of the internal capsule and basis pontis, underlies PLC pathophysiology. Lesions affecting serotonergic and glutamatergic neurotransmission appear critical in symptom development. Treatment efficacy has been demonstrated with tricyclic antidepressants, selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors, and combination dextromethorphan/quinidine therapy. Double-blind, placebo-controlled trials show significant symptom reduction with amitriptyline (50-75mg daily), citalopram (20-30mg daily), and dextromethorphan/quinidine (20/10mg twice daily). Response rates typically range from 60-85% across different therapeutic agents. Recognition of PLC as a distinct neurological entity with specific anatomical substrates and effective pharmacological treatments should encourage clinicians to screen for this treatable condition in at-risk neurological populations.

title: [The processes of foresight and of the evaluation of behavioral results and their cellular neurochemical mechanisms].
abstract: Chemical sensitivity of the cat visual cortex neurons was studied in conditions of rewarded and unrewarded instrumental behaviour. A connexion was found between the processes of provision and estimation and the neurochemical mechanisms of the central neurons. The unit spike activity was found to depend on the protein synthesis in the neurons.

title: Corticosterone urinalysis and nicotinic receptor modulation in rats.
abstract: Chronic stress and nicotine exposure produce complex neuroadaptations that are difficult to assess using invasive sampling methods. Non-invasive biomarkers would facilitate longitudinal studies of stress-nicotine interactions in rodent models. We developed and validated a urinalysis protocol for measuring corticosterone levels and examined the relationship between urinary corticosterone and nicotinic acetylcholine receptor (nAChR) expression following chronic nicotine treatment. Male Sprague-Dawley rats (n=48) were housed in metabolic cages and received either saline or nicotine (0.4 mg/kg, s.c.) twice daily for 14 days. Urine samples were collected every 48 hours, and corticosterone concentrations were determined using enzyme immunoassay. Following treatment, brain tissues were processed for α4β2 and α7 nAChR binding using [³H]-epibatidine and [¹²⁵I]-α-bungarotoxin autoradiography, respectively. Urinary corticosterone showed significant positive correlation with plasma levels (r=0.82, p<0.001) and exhibited circadian variation consistent with the hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal axis rhythm. Chronic nicotine treatment elevated mean urinary corticosterone by 34% compared to controls (p<0.01). Nicotine-treated animals displayed upregulated α4β2 nAChR binding in cortical and hippocampal regions (25-40% increase, p<0.05) but unchanged α7 nAChR binding. Importantly, individual differences in urinary corticosterone levels significantly predicted the magnitude of α4β2 upregulation (r=0.71, p<0.01). These findings demonstrate that urinalysis provides a reliable, non-invasive method for monitoring corticosterone in rats and reveals a novel relationship between stress hormone levels and nicotinic receptor plasticity that may inform addiction vulnerability mechanisms.

title: Link between temperament traits, brain neurochemistry and response to SSRI: insights from animal model of social behavior.
abstract: Dominant-submissive relationships depend upon functionality of the neural circuits involving monoaminergic neurotransmission. Behavioral profiles of selectively bred dominant (Dom) and submissive (Sub) mice have been proposed to mimic hyperthymic- or depressive-like temperaments observed in patients with affective disorders. These mice differentially respond to psychotropic agents and stressful stimuli, however, the mechanisms underlying these differences remain unclear. To address these mechanisms, we analyzed the brain monoamine content and responses to paroxetine (PXT) in Dom and Sub mice. The behavioral effects of PXT (3 mg/kg, single injection) were assessed with the Elevated Plus Maze (EPM) and Forced Swim Test (FST). Monoamine tissue content was analyzed by HPLC-ECD. Compared to Dom, Sub mice had decreased levels of serotonin (5-HT) in the brainstem (BS), reduced levels of norepinephrine (NE) in the prefrontal cortex (PFC), hippocampus (HPC), and striatum (STR) and elevated levels of dopamine (DA) in PFC, HPC, STR and BS. In EPM, PXT administration increased locomotion and exploration in Dom mice, with no effect in Sub mice. In FST, PXT disrupted immobility in Dom mice only. The PXT-produced differences in regional monoamine content were strain-dependent and consistent with the behavioral alterations. Chronic PXT treatment, in vivo monoamine assays and sex-dependent analysis were out of the scope of this study and will be performed in the future in order to provide an in-depth evaluation of the neurochemical mechanisms underlying temperament-dependent responses to SSRIs. Our findings suggest neurochemical mechanisms that underlie temperament-based response to antidepressant treatment.

title: Sampling glutamate and GABA with microdialysis: suggestions on how to get the dialysis membrane closer to the synapse.
abstract: Microdialysis is currently optimized to sample the extrasynaptic pool. As such, the technique has facilitated discovery of ischemia-induced excitotoxic glutamate overflow (Benveniste H, Drejer J, Schousboe A, Diemer NH, 1987, Regional cerebral glucose phosphorylation and blood flow after insertion of a microdialysis fiber through the dorsal hippocampus in the rat. J. Neurochem., 49, 729-734) and adenosinergic sleep drive (Porkka-Heiskanen T, Strecker RE, Thakkar M, Bjorkum AA, Greene RW, McCarley RW, 1997, Adenosine: a mediator of the sleep-inducing effects of prolonged wakefulness. Science, 276 (5316), 1265-1268); and is proving essential for clinical monitoring of glutamate and cellular metabolites in stroke and head trauma (Sarrafzadeh AS, Sakowitz OW, Kiening KL, Benndorf G, Lanksch WR, Unterberg AW. Bedside microdialysis: a tool to monitor cerebral metabolism in subarachnoid hemorrhage patients? Crit. Care Med. 2002, 30 (5): 1062-1070). Study of the origin of extrasynaptic glutamate sampled with microdialysis has advanced understanding of extrasynaptic signal processing (Baker DA, Xi ZX, Shen H, Swanson CJ, Kalivas PW. The origin and neuronal function of in vivo nonsynaptic glutamate. J. Neurosci. 2002, 22 (20): 9134-9141; Baker DA, McFarland K, Lake RW, Shen H, Tang XC, Toda S, Kalivas PW, 2003, Neuroadaptations in cystine-glutamate exchange underlie cocaine relapse. Nat. Neurosci., 6, 743-749) in the CNS. Microdialysis studies furthermore demonstrate that synaptic pools of some neurotransmitters spill into the extrasynaptic space. For this reason, microdialysis has provided a window into the synaptic pool that has significantly advanced understanding of neurotransmitter control of behavior (Tanda G, Pontieri FE, Di Chiara G, 1997, Cannabinoid and heroin activation of mesolimbic dopamine transmission by a common mu1 opioid receptor mechanism. Science, 276, 2048-2050). Nonetheless, ability to sample synaptic pools of neurotransmitters is limited. Here we summarize evidence that microdialysis often fails to sample synaptic pools of neurotransmitters, such as glutamate and GABA because of rapid clearance and limited diffusion of these neurotransmitters from the synapse. Moreover, we consider means to move the dialysis membrane closer to the synapse to facilitate sampling of the synaptic pool of these neurotransmitters by minimizing tissue trauma, decreasing probe size and increasing temporal resolution.

title: Tobacco psychopharmacology and public health policy: it takes a community.
abstract: Public health policies targeting tobacco use have achieved significant population-level reductions in smoking prevalence, yet individual neurobiological mechanisms underlying nicotine dependence remain critical barriers to cessation success. This review synthesizes current psychopharmacological evidence with community-based intervention strategies to examine how multilevel approaches may optimize tobacco control outcomes. We conducted a comprehensive analysis of randomized controlled trials published between 2005-2010 examining nicotine replacement therapies, bupropion, and varenicline in community settings, alongside population-based studies evaluating policy interventions including taxation, smoke-free laws, and media campaigns. Neurobiological studies demonstrate that chronic nicotine exposure produces persistent alterations in dopaminergic and cholinergic signaling pathways, particularly within mesocorticolimbic reward circuits, contributing to sustained craving and relapse vulnerability. Pharmacotherapies targeting these systems show 1.5-3.0 fold increases in quit rates compared to placebo in clinical trials. However, real-world effectiveness varies substantially across populations, with lower success rates observed in socioeconomically disadvantaged communities. Community-wide interventions combining pharmacotherapy access with environmental policy changes demonstrate enhanced outcomes, with population quit rates increasing 15-25% beyond individual treatment approaches alone. Smoke-free policies amplify pharmacotherapy effectiveness by reducing environmental cue exposure and social reinforcement of smoking behavior. These findings suggest that optimal tobacco control requires integrated strategies addressing both neurobiological dependence mechanisms through evidence-based pharmacotherapies and environmental factors through comprehensive community-level policies. Future research should prioritize implementation science approaches examining how to effectively deploy combined psychopharmacological and policy interventions across diverse populations.

title: A neurochemical basis for an epigenetic vision of psychiatric disorders (1994-2009).
abstract: In 1996, Dr. Costa was invited by Prof. Boris Astrachan, Chairman of the Department of Psychiatry at the University of Illinois at Chicago, to direct the research of the "Psychiatric Institute, Department of Psychiatry, School of Medicine, at the University of Illinois at Chicago." He was asked to develop a seminal research program on psychiatric disorders. Viewed in retrospect, Dr. Costa met and surpassed the challenge, as was usual for him. To elucidate the molecular mechanisms whereby nurture (epigenetic factors) and nature (genetic factors) interact to cause major psychiatric disorders was at the center of Dr. Costa's mission for the last 15 years of his research at the Psychiatric Institute. The challenge for Dr. Costa and his colleagues (Auta, Caruncho, Davis, Grayson, Guidotti, Impagnatiello, Kiedrowski, Larson, Manev, Pappas, Pesold, Pinna, Sharma, Smalheiser, Sugaya, Tueting, Veldic [1-111]) had always been to find new ways to prevent and treat psychiatric disorders with pharmacological agents that failed to have major unwanted side effects. In this list, we have quoted the first authors of the papers pertaining to the field of research highlighted in the title. As you know, Dr. Costa was an eclectic scientist and in his 15 years of studies at UIC, he touched many other aspects of neuroscience research that are not discussed in this overview.

title: A simple method for quantitative calcium imaging in unperturbed developing neurons.
abstract: Calcium imaging has been widely used to address questions of neuronal function and development. To gain deeper insights into the actions of calcium as a second messenger, but also to measure synaptic function, it is necessary to quantify the level of calcium at rest and during calcium transients. While quantification of calcium levels is straightforward when using ratiometric calcium indicators, these dyes have several draw-backs due to their short wavelength excitation spectra, such as light scattering and cytotoxicity. In contrast, many single-wavelength indicators exhibit superior photostability, low phototoxicity, extended dynamic ranges and very high signal to noise ratios. However, quantifying calcium levels in unperturbed neurons has not been performed with these indicators. Here, we explore a new approach for determining the calcium concentration at rest as well as calcium rises during evoked and spontaneous neuronal activity in unperturbed developing neurons using a single-wavelength calcium indicator. We show that measuring the maximal fluorescence at the end of an imaging experiment allows determining calcium levels with high resolution. Specifically, we assessed the limits of calcium measurements with a CCD camera in small neuronal processes and found that even in small diameter dendrites and spines the intracellular calcium concentration and its changes can be estimated accurately. This approach may not only allow mapping patterns of neuronal activity quantitatively with the resolution of single synapses and a few tens of milliseconds, but also facilitate investigating the role of calcium as a second messenger.

title: Female psychopharmacology matters! Towards a sex-specific psychopharmacology.
abstract: There is increasing recognition that women have a higher prevalence of certain psychiatric illnesses, and a differential treatment response and course of illness compared to men. Additionally, clinicians deal with a number of disorders like premenstrual syndrome, premenstrual dysphoric disorder, and postpartum depression, which affect women specifically and for which treatment and biological pathways are still unclear. In this article we highlight recent research which suggests that different biological mechanisms may underlie sex differences in responsiveness to stress. Sex differences are evident at the receptor level; where the corticotropin-releasing factor receptor shows differential coupling to adaptor proteins in males and females. The neuropeptide oxytocin also shows sex-specific effects in a range of social behaviors. It may act as a biomarker in post-traumatic stress disorder where sex differences are evident. Studies in women using hormonal contraception show that some of these oxytocin-mediated effects are likely influenced by sex hormones. In female rats rapid changes in circulating progesterone levels are associated with exaggerated behavioral responses to mild stress and blunted responses to benzodiazepines that could be prevented by acute treatment with low-dose fluoxetine. Perceived barriers in research on women have hindered progress. The development of a sex-specific psychopharmacology as a basis for translating this type of research into clinical practice is vital to improve treatment outcomes for women.

title: PET in psychopharmacology.
abstract: Positron emission tomography (PET) has emerged as a powerful tool for investigating the neurobiological mechanisms underlying psychiatric disorders and monitoring the effects of psychotropic medications in living human brain. This review examines the current applications of PET imaging in psychopharmacology research and clinical practice. We analyzed 127 published PET studies conducted between 1995 and 2001 that examined the effects of antipsychotics, antidepressants, anxiolytics, and mood stabilizers on brain function and neurotransmitter systems. The studies employed various radioligands including [18F]fluorodeoxyglucose for metabolic imaging, [11C]raclopride for dopamine D2 receptor occupancy, [11C]WAY-100635 for serotonin 5-HT1A receptors, and [18F]MPPF for 5-HT1A binding. Results demonstrate that PET can reliably measure drug occupancy at specific receptor sites, with therapeutic efficacy of antipsychotics correlating with 65-80% D2 receptor occupancy, while occupancy above 80% predicts extrapyramidal side effects. Antidepressant studies revealed altered glucose metabolism in limbic and prefrontal regions following successful treatment, with metabolic changes preceding clinical improvement by 2-4 weeks. PET imaging also identified differential patterns of neurotransmitter system engagement between drug classes and predicted treatment response in individual patients. These findings establish PET as an invaluable method for dose optimization, drug development, and understanding the neurobiological basis of psychotropic drug action, offering significant potential for advancing personalized psychiatric medicine.

title: Psychopharmacology in autism.
abstract: Autism is a neurobiological disorder. The core clinical features of autism include impairment in social interaction, impairments in verbal and nonverbal communication, and restricted, repetitive, and stereotyped patterns of behavior, interests, and activities. Autism often has coexisting neuropsychiatric disorders, including seizure disorders, attention deficit hyperactivity disorder, affective disorders, anxiety disorder, obsessive-compulsive disorder, and Tourette disorder. No etiology-based treatment modality has been developed to cure individuals with autism. However, comprehensive intervention, including parental counseling, behavior modification, special education in a highly structured environment, sensory integration training, speech therapy, social skill training, and medication, has demonstrated significant treatment effects in many individuals with autism. Findings from preliminary studies of major neurotransmitters and other neurochemical agents strongly suggest that neurochemical factors play a major role in autism. The findings also provide the rationale for psychopharmacotherapy in individuals with autism. This article reviews studies of neurochemical systems and related psychopharmacological research in autism and related neuropsychiatric disorders. Clinical indications for pharmacotherapy are described, and uses of various medications are suggested. This article also discusses new avenues of investigation that may lead to the development of more effective medication treatments in persons with autism.

title: Comparative analysis of inhibitory effects of caged ligands for the NMDA receptor.
abstract: Caged ligands have emerged as powerful tools for studying fast synaptic transmission, yet their potential inhibitory effects on N-methyl-D-aspartate (NMDA) receptors remain poorly characterized. We systematically compared the inhibitory properties of six commonly used caged compounds - caged glutamate (CNB-glutamate), caged glycine (CNB-glycine), caged GABA (CNB-GABA), caged ATP (caged-ATP), caged calcium (NP-EGTA), and caged cyclic AMP (caged-cAMP) - on recombinant NMDA receptors expressed in Xenopus oocytes and native receptors in acute hippocampal slices. Electrophysiological recordings were performed using two-electrode voltage clamp in oocytes and whole-cell patch clamp in CA1 pyramidal neurons. NMDA-evoked currents were measured in the presence of varying concentrations (10-500 μM) of each caged compound before photolysis. CNB-glutamate and CNB-glycine exhibited the strongest inhibitory effects, reducing NMDA currents by 45±8% and 38±6% respectively at 100 μM concentrations (n=12-15 cells, p<0.001). Caged-ATP showed moderate inhibition (22±4%), while NP-EGTA, caged-cAMP, and CNB-GABA produced minimal effects (<10%). The inhibition was voltage-independent and non-competitive, suggesting binding to allosteric sites rather than the glutamate or glycine recognition sites. These inhibitory effects were completely reversible upon washout and were not observed after UV photolysis, confirming that intact caged compounds were responsible for the inhibition. Our findings demonstrate that caged ligands can significantly modulate NMDA receptor function independent of their photolytic products, with important implications for experimental design and data interpretation in studies employing flash photolysis techniques.

title: Heavy particle irradiation, neurochemistry and behavior: thresholds, dose-response curves and recovery of function.
abstract: Exposure to heavy particles can affect the functioning of the central nervous system (CNS), particularly the dopaminergic system. In turn, the radiation-induced disruption of dopaminergic function affects a variety of behaviors that are dependent upon the integrity of this system, including motor behavior (upper body strength), amphetamine (dopamine)-mediated taste aversion learning, and operant conditioning (fixed-ratio bar pressing). Although the relationships between heavy particle irradiation and the effects of exposure depend, to some extent, upon the specific behavioral or neurochemical endpoint under consideration, a review of the available research leads to the hypothesis that the endpoints mediated by the CNS have certain characteristics in common. These include: (1) a threshold, below which there is no apparent effect; (2) the lack of a dose-response relationship, or an extremely steep dose-response curve, depending on the particular endpoint; and (3) the absence of recovery of function, such that the heavy particle-induced behavioral and neural changes are present when tested up to one year following exposure. The current report reviews the data relevant to the degree to which these characteristics are common to neurochemical and behavioral endpoints that are mediated by the effects of exposure to heavy particles on CNS activity.
journal: Advances in space research : the official journal of the Committee on Space Research (COSPAR)

title: Recent Updates in Psychopharmacology for the Core and Associated Symptoms of Autism Spectrum Disorder.
abstract: Autism spectrum disorder (ASD) is a neurodevelopmental disorder characterized by core deficits in social communication and restricted, repetitive patterns of behavior. This article aims to review the recent literature pertaining to psychopharmacology for the core and associated symptoms of ASD including social impairment, repetitive behaviors, irritability, and language impairment. Recent medication trials targeting social impairment in ASD have focused on neuropeptides (oxytocin and vasopressin) and memantine. None of these three medications has demonstrated consistent benefit for social impairment in ASD; however, additional studies are underway. Two double-blind, placebo-controlled studies on selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors (SSRIs) provide evidence against the use of SSRIs for repetitive behaviors in youth with ASD. Preliminary studies have investigated cannabidiol (CBD) for irritability in ASD but further studies are needed to demonstrate safety and efficacy. Finally, three double-blind, placebo-controlled studies provide preliminary evidence for folinic acid for the treatment of verbal language deficits in children with ASD. The identification of safe and effective pharmacological treatments to ameliorate the core and associated symptoms of ASD has proven difficult.

title: A Focused Review on the Treatment of Pediatric Patients with Atypical Antipsychotics.
abstract: Atypical antipsychotics are increasingly prescribed to pediatric patients for various psychiatric conditions, yet comprehensive safety and efficacy data in this population remain limited. The unique developmental neurobiology of children and adolescents may confer differential treatment responses and adverse event profiles compared to adults. This focused review synthesizes current evidence regarding the clinical use of second-generation antipsychotics in pediatric populations aged 6-17 years. A systematic literature search was conducted using PubMed, EMBASE, and PsycINFO databases from 2008-2017, identifying randomized controlled trials, open-label studies, and case series examining aripiprazole, olanzapine, quetiapine, risperidone, paliperidone, and ziprasidone in pediatric patients. Primary outcomes included efficacy measures for FDA-approved indications (schizophrenia, bipolar disorder, autism spectrum disorders, and Tourette's disorder) and safety profiles including metabolic parameters, extrapyramidal symptoms, and prolactin elevation. Twenty-three studies encompassing 2,847 pediatric patients were included. Risperidone and aripiprazole demonstrated robust efficacy for irritability associated with autism spectrum disorders, while olanzapine and quetiapine showed comparable efficacy to adults for bipolar mania. Significant safety concerns emerged, including substantial weight gain (mean 4.2-8.7 kg over 6-12 weeks), metabolic dysregulation in 15-35% of patients, and hyperprolactinemia with risperidone and paliperidone. Extrapyramidal symptoms occurred less frequently than with typical antipsychotics but remained clinically significant. These findings support careful risk-benefit consideration when prescribing atypical antipsychotics to pediatric patients, emphasizing the need for regular metabolic monitoring and exploration of non-pharmacological interventions as first-line treatments when appropriate.
journal: Journal of child and adolescent psychopharmacology

title: Consensus statements on the clinical uses of pregabalin for Hong Kong.
abstract: To facilitate the understanding of pregabalin and optimize its clinical usage in Hong Kong, an expert panel (11 psychiatrists, one family physician and one anesthesiologist) experienced in treating anxiety and somatic symptoms was invited to establish a set of consensus statements based on several discussion areas. A non-systematic literature search for relevant articles was conducted. The panelists addressed the discussion areas by sharing their clinical experience and available literature in a couple of meetings. At the last meeting, consensus statements derived from the proceedings were discussed and finalized. A total of 11 statements were ultimately accepted by panel voting based on their practicability of recommendation in Hong Kong. These statements are aimed to act as a practical reference for local clinicians when they consider prescribing pregabalin in different clinical situations.
journal: Asia-Pacific psychiatry : official journal of the Pacific Rim College of Psychiatrists

title: Detection of cytochrome c oxidase activity and mitochondrial proteins in single cells.
abstract: Cytochrome c oxidase or mitochondrial respiratory chain complex IV is where over 90% of oxygen is consumed. The relationship between complex IV activity and mitochondrial proteins, which provides a guide to understanding the mechanisms in primary mitochondrial disorders, has been determined by histochemistry (activity) and immunohistochemistry in serial sections. In the central nervous system (CNS), mitochondrial activity and immunoreactivity have been determined in populations of cells in serial sections as capturing cells in more than one section is difficult. In this report we describe a method to determine complex IV activity in relation to mitochondrial proteins at a single cell level in the CNS. We performed complex IV histochemistry and immunohistochemistry consecutively in snap frozen sections. Although the product of complex IV histochemistry reduces the sensitivity of standard immunohistochemistry (secondary antibody and ABC method) the biotin-free Menapath polymer detection system (A. Menarini Diagnostics, Wokingham, UK) enables mitochondrial proteins to be detected following complex IV histochemistry. The co-occurring chromogens may then be separately visualised and analysed using multi-spectral imaging (Nuance system CRi, Woburn, MA). Our technique is applicable for exploring mitochondrial defects within single cells in a variety of CNS disorders and animal models of those diseases.

title: Advancing equity, diversity, and inclusion in the American College of Neuropsychopharmacology (ACNP): advances, challenges, and opportunities to accelerate progress.
abstract: The American College of Neuropsychopharmacology (ACNP) has historically faced challenges in achieving equitable representation across gender, racial, ethnic, and socioeconomic dimensions within its membership and leadership structure. To assess current diversity metrics and identify barriers to inclusion, we conducted a comprehensive analysis of ACNP membership demographics, leadership composition, and meeting participation patterns from 2015-2020. We surveyed 1,247 members regarding perceived barriers to advancement and collected demographic data on annual meeting speakers, award recipients, and council members. Additionally, we analyzed citation patterns and publication metrics across demographic groups to examine potential disparities in career advancement opportunities. Our analysis revealed significant underrepresentation of women (32% of membership), racial minorities (18% combined), and early-career investigators (23%) relative to the broader neuroscience workforce. Leadership positions showed even greater disparities, with women holding 28% of council positions and racial minorities comprising only 12% of committee chairs over the study period. Survey responses identified key barriers including limited mentorship opportunities, geographic constraints, financial barriers to meeting attendance, and perceived "old boys' network" dynamics. However, targeted initiatives implemented since 2018, including mentorship programs, travel awards, and structured networking events, demonstrated measurable improvements in diversity metrics. These findings highlight the need for sustained, multi-faceted approaches to advance equity in neuropsychopharmacology, including policy reforms, resource allocation, and cultural transformation to ensure the field benefits from diverse perspectives and talent.
journal: Neuropsychopharmacology : official publication of the American College of Neuropsychopharmacology

title: Rats can learn a temporal task in a single session.
abstract: Temporal processing is fundamental to adaptive behavior, yet the neural mechanisms underlying rapid acquisition of timing tasks remain poorly understood. Previous studies have shown that rodents can learn to time intervals over multiple training sessions, but whether rats can acquire temporal discrimination within a single session and the neurochemical basis of such rapid learning has not been systematically investigated. Here, we examined the ability of male Wistar rats (n=24) to learn a peak-interval timing task within a single 90-minute session using a novel fixed-interval 20-second schedule with unpredictable probe trials. Additionally, we investigated the role of dopaminergic signaling by administering either saline or haloperidol (0.1 mg/kg, i.p.) 30 minutes prior to training. Behavioral performance was assessed using peak time, peak rate, and coefficient of variation measures. Results demonstrated that control rats rapidly acquired temporal control, showing significant peak timing behavior within the first 30 minutes of training (p<0.001). Peak times averaged 19.8±2.1 seconds by session end, with coefficient of variation decreasing from 0.45 to 0.23 across the session. Haloperidol-treated animals showed significantly impaired acquisition, with delayed emergence of temporal control and higher variability throughout training (p<0.01). These findings reveal that rats possess remarkable capacity for rapid temporal learning within a single session and highlight the critical role of dopaminergic neurotransmission in the acquisition of timing behavior. This rapid learning paradigm provides a valuable tool for investigating the neurobiological mechanisms of interval timing.

title: Role of GIRK channels on the noradrenergic transmission in vivo: an electrophysiological and neurochemical study on GIRK2 mutant mice.
abstract: G protein-coupled inwardly rectifying potassium (GIRK) channels play a crucial role in neuronal excitability and neurotransmitter release regulation. While GIRK2 subunits are abundantly expressed in noradrenergic neurons, their specific contribution to norepinephrine transmission remains poorly understood. This study investigated the functional role of GIRK2 channels in noradrenergic neurotransmission using GIRK2 knockout mice and their wild-type littermates. In vivo electrophysiological recordings were performed in the locus coeruleus (LC) of anesthetized mice to assess noradrenergic neuronal firing patterns and responses to α2-adrenoceptor stimulation. Microdialysis experiments measured extracellular norepinephrine levels in the prefrontal cortex and hippocampus under basal conditions and following pharmacological challenges. Additionally, high-performance liquid chromatography quantified tissue norepinephrine content in multiple brain regions. GIRK2 knockout mice exhibited significantly increased spontaneous firing rates of LC neurons (2.8 ± 0.3 Hz) compared to wild-types (1.9 ± 0.2 Hz, p<0.01). The inhibitory response to clonidine, an α2-adrenoceptor agonist, was markedly attenuated in knockout animals (32% vs 68% firing rate reduction, p<0.001). Microdialysis revealed elevated basal norepinephrine levels in both prefrontal cortex and hippocampus of GIRK2-deficient mice. Tissue norepinephrine content was reduced by 25-40% across examined brain regions in knockouts, suggesting compensatory downregulation. These findings demonstrate that GIRK2 channels are essential modulators of noradrenergic neurotransmission, critically involved in maintaining physiological firing patterns and enabling proper α2-adrenoceptor-mediated feedback inhibition in norepinephrine neurons.

title: The psychopharmacology of 5-HT3 receptors.
abstract: The 5-HT3 receptor represents a novel class of serotonin receptors that differs fundamentally from previously characterized 5-HT1 and 5-HT2 subtypes in its mechanism of action. Unlike other serotonin receptors which are coupled to second messenger systems, the 5-HT3 receptor functions as a ligand-gated ion channel, allowing rapid depolarization upon serotonin binding. We examined the pharmacological properties and behavioral effects of 5-HT3 receptor activation and blockade using selective compounds in male Sprague-Dawley rats. The selective 5-HT3 agonist 2-methyl-5-HT (0.1-10 mg/kg, i.p.) produced dose-dependent increases in spontaneous locomotor activity and induced a characteristic behavioral syndrome including head weaving and flat body posture. These effects were completely blocked by pretreatment with the selective 5-HT3 antagonist ICS 205-930 (0.01-1 mg/kg, i.p.) but were unaffected by 5-HT1 or 5-HT2 receptor antagonists. Electrophysiological recordings from hippocampal slices revealed that 5-HT3 receptor activation produced rapid membrane depolarization with a reversal potential near 0 mV, consistent with a non-selective cation channel. Radioligand binding studies using [3H]ICS 205-930 demonstrated high-affinity, saturable binding sites in brain regions including area postrema, nucleus tractus solitarius, and hippocampus. These findings establish the 5-HT3 receptor as a pharmacologically distinct serotonin receptor subtype with unique electrophysiological properties. The development of selective 5-HT3 receptor ligands provides new tools for investigating serotonergic neurotransmission and may offer therapeutic opportunities for treating psychiatric and gastrointestinal disorders involving serotonergic dysfunction.

title: Assaying multiple biochemical variables from the same tissue sample.
abstract: Experiments often involve multiple analyses, such as assays of neurotransmitters and proteins, and this can require different initial sample preparations. Typically, this is accomplished by using different animals or different tissue samples from the same animal. Either approach renders comparisons between assays more variable and greatly increases the effort and/or cost. Using tissue collected from rat striatum and molecules of special relevance to studies of Parkinson's disease, we show that tissue sonication in water prior to aliquoting into the appropriate concentrated solutions (e.g. HClO(4) and lysis buffers) permits several types of measurements to be made from the same initial samples. Dopamine and its metabolite homovanillic acid, serotonin and its metabolite 5-hydroxyindoleacetic acid, tyrosine hydroxylase and its phosphorylation at Ser19 and Ser31, and the dopamine transporter were unaffected. However, phospho-Akt levels fell slightly and phospho-ERK1/2 tended to drop. We also present a simple technique to preserve phosphorylation state of proteins such as ERK1/2 by perfusing animals through the heart with a phosphatase inhibitor, NaF. Dopamine metabolite dihydroxyphenyl acetic acid (DOPAC) levels were raised with both techniques, however. The general principles reported here are likely to apply to other brain regions, facilitate multiple comparisons of variables, increase efficiency, and decrease costs.

title: Experimental neuropsychopharmacology, yesterday, today and tomorrow. A conversation with Michel Hamon.
abstract: Experimental neuropsychopharmacology has undergone remarkable transformation over the past five decades, evolving from rudimentary behavioral observations to sophisticated molecular investigations. This retrospective analysis examines the field's historical trajectory, current methodological advances, and future directions through comprehensive review of seminal contributions and emerging paradigms. We conducted a systematic evaluation of neuropsychopharmacological research milestones spanning 1970-2020, analyzing publications, methodological innovations, and translational outcomes. Key developments were assessed across three temporal phases: classical pharmacological approaches (1970-1990), molecular revolution period (1990-2010), and contemporary integrative era (2010-present). Historical analysis revealed fundamental shifts from receptor binding studies and behavioral pharmacology toward multi-omics approaches, optogenetics, and precision medicine strategies. The integration of neuroimaging, electrophysiology, and genetic tools has enabled unprecedented mechanistic insights into drug action and neural circuit function. Contemporary methodologies including CRISPR-Cas9 gene editing, single-cell transcriptomics, and computational modeling have revolutionized target identification and validation processes. Current challenges include translational gaps between preclinical models and clinical efficacy, personalized treatment approaches, and ethical considerations in neuropharmacological research. Future directions emphasize systems-level understanding of brain-drug interactions, artificial intelligence-driven drug discovery, and biomarker-guided therapeutic development. The field's evolution demonstrates successful transition from empirical observations to mechanistically-informed drug development, positioning experimental neuropsychopharmacology as a cornerstone of modern psychiatric and neurological therapeutics. Continued integration of technological advances with rigorous experimental design will be essential for addressing unmet clinical needs in brain disorders.

title: Neuropharmacology of Cevimeline and Muscarinic Drugs-Focus on Cognition and Neurodegeneration.
abstract: Muscarinic acetylcholine receptors play crucial roles in cognitive processes and are implicated in neurodegenerative diseases including Alzheimer's disease and Parkinson's disease. Cevimeline, a selective M1/M3 muscarinic receptor agonist clinically approved for Sjögren's syndrome, has emerged as a potential therapeutic candidate for cognitive enhancement and neuroprotection. This review synthesizes current understanding of cevimeline's neuropharmacological properties and compares its mechanisms with other muscarinic modulators in the context of neurodegeneration. We conducted a comprehensive literature analysis of preclinical and clinical studies examining cevimeline and related muscarinic drugs, focusing on their effects on synaptic plasticity, neuroinflammation, and cognitive performance. Preclinical studies demonstrate that cevimeline enhances long-term potentiation in hippocampal neurons and promotes acetylcholine release, while reducing amyloid-β accumulation and tau hyperphosphorylation in transgenic mouse models. Clinical trials in mild cognitive impairment patients showed modest improvements in memory and attention tasks following 12-week cevimeline treatment, with minimal anticholinergic side effects compared to non-selective muscarinic agonists. Mechanistic studies reveal that cevimeline activates PKC/MAPK signaling pathways and upregulates BDNF expression, contributing to synaptic strengthening and neuronal survival. Additionally, cevimeline exhibits anti-inflammatory properties by modulating microglial activation through M1 receptor-mediated pathways. These findings suggest that selective muscarinic receptor modulation, particularly through cevimeline, represents a promising therapeutic strategy for addressing cognitive decline and neurodegeneration. Further clinical investigations are warranted to establish optimal dosing regimens and long-term efficacy in neurodegenerative disorders.

title: Neuropharmacology of Synthetic Cathinones.
abstract: Synthetic cathinones, commonly known as "bath salts," are β-ketone analogues of amphetamines that have emerged as drugs of abuse with significant public health implications. These psychoactive substances exhibit complex pharmacological profiles through interactions with monoamine transporters, yet their precise mechanisms of action and neurotoxic potential remain incompletely characterized. This review synthesizes current knowledge regarding the neuropharmacological properties of prominent synthetic cathinones including mephedrone, methylenedioxypyrovalerone (MDPV), and α-pyrrolidinovalerophenone (α-PVP). We examined transporter selectivity profiles using in vitro uptake and release assays in HEK293 cells expressing human dopamine, norepinephrine, and serotonin transporters, alongside behavioral studies in rodent models of self-administration and locomotor sensitization. Synthetic cathinones demonstrated varied pharmacological profiles: mephedrone acted as a non-selective monoamine releaser similar to MDMA, while MDPV and α-PVP functioned primarily as potent dopamine and norepinephrine reuptake inhibitors with minimal serotonergic activity. Pyrrolidine-containing cathinones showed enhanced potency and abuse liability compared to their non-pyrrolidine counterparts. In vivo studies revealed robust locomotor stimulation and reinforcing properties, with some compounds producing hyperthermia and oxidative stress markers in brain tissue. Structure-activity relationships indicated that α-carbon substitution and pyrrolidine ring incorporation significantly influence transporter selectivity and pharmacological potency. These findings highlight the diverse neuropharmacological mechanisms underlying synthetic cathinone toxicity and provide crucial insights for developing targeted therapeutic interventions and informing regulatory policies regarding these emerging substances of abuse.

title: [Atypical antipsychotic drugs].
abstract: The introduction of atypical antipsychotic drugs has fundamentally altered the treatment landscape for schizophrenia and related psychotic disorders. Unlike conventional neuroleptics that primarily block dopamine D2 receptors, atypical agents demonstrate complex pharmacological profiles involving multiple neurotransmitter systems. We conducted a comprehensive review of the pharmacological mechanisms and clinical efficacy of atypical antipsychotics, focusing on clozapine, risperidone, olanzapine, and quetiapine. Clinical data from 24 controlled studies involving 3,847 patients with schizophrenia were analyzed, comparing efficacy and side effect profiles between atypical and conventional antipsychotics. Atypical agents demonstrated superior efficacy in treating negative symptoms, with 40-60% of patients showing significant improvement compared to 20-35% with haloperidol. Importantly, the incidence of extrapyramidal side effects was markedly reduced: tardive dyskinesia occurred in 0.5-5% of patients treated with atypical drugs versus 15-20% with conventional neuroleptics. The unique receptor binding profiles of atypical antipsychotics, particularly their high 5-HT2A/D2 receptor affinity ratios and interaction with cholinergic, histaminergic, and adrenergic systems, appear to underlie their improved tolerability. However, metabolic side effects including weight gain and glucose dysregulation emerged as new concerns, particularly with olanzapine and clozapine. These findings suggest that atypical antipsychotics represent a significant therapeutic advance, offering improved symptom control with reduced neurological complications, though requiring careful monitoring of metabolic parameters. Their introduction has established new standards for antipsychotic therapy in Dutch clinical practice.

title: [Psychopharmacological Studies in Mice].
abstract: Psychoactive compounds continue to pose significant challenges in understanding their mechanisms of action and potential therapeutic applications. This study investigated the behavioral and neurochemical effects of novel synthetic compounds XM-47 and XM-52 in male ICR mice to elucidate their psychopharmacological profiles. Mice (n=8-10 per group) received intraperitoneal injections of test compounds (0.1, 0.5, 1.0, 5.0 mg/kg) or vehicle control. Behavioral assessments included locomotor activity monitoring, elevated plus-maze testing, forced swim test, and rotarod performance over 120 minutes post-administration. Brain tissue samples were analyzed for monoamine neurotransmitter levels using high-performance liquid chromatography. XM-47 demonstrated dose-dependent increases in locomotor activity (peak at 1.0 mg/kg, 247% of control, p<0.01) and significant anxiolytic-like effects in the elevated plus-maze (increased open arm time, p<0.05). XM-52 exhibited primarily sedative properties with decreased locomotor activity at higher doses and impaired rotarod performance. Neurochemical analysis revealed XM-47 significantly elevated striatal dopamine levels (168% of control, p<0.01) and reduced serotonin turnover, while XM-52 increased GABA concentrations in the hippocampus and cortex. Neither compound showed antidepressant-like activity in the forced swim test. These findings suggest XM-47 acts primarily through dopaminergic mechanisms with stimulant and anxiolytic properties, whereas XM-52 demonstrates GABAergic activity consistent with its sedative profile. Further investigation into receptor binding specificity and therapeutic potential is warranted.
journal: Yakugaku zasshi : Journal of the Pharmaceutical Society of Japan

title: Synthesis and pharmacological evaluation of 4-phenoxy-1,2,3,4-tetrahydroisoquinolines and 4,5,6,6a-tetrahydrochromeno[2,3,4-de]isoquinolines.
abstract: The synthesis of novel 4-phenoxy-1,2,3,4-tetrahydroisoquinoline and 4,5,6,6a-tetrahydrochromeno[2,3,4-de]isoquinoline derivatives was undertaken to explore their potential as central nervous system active compounds. Tetrahydroisoquinoline scaffolds have demonstrated significant pharmacological activity at dopaminergic and adrenergic receptors, warranting investigation of structurally related analogues. A series of twenty-three compounds was synthesized via Pictet-Spengler cyclization reactions starting from appropriately substituted phenethylamines and aldehydes, followed by phenolic coupling reactions to generate the chromeno-fused derivatives. All compounds were characterized by 1H NMR, 13C NMR, and mass spectrometry. Pharmacological evaluation included radioligand binding assays at dopamine D1 and D2 receptors, α1- and α2-adrenergic receptors, and serotonin 5-HT1A and 5-HT2A receptors using rat brain membranes. Functional activity was assessed using cAMP accumulation assays and calcium mobilization studies. Several 4-phenoxy-tetrahydroisoquinoline derivatives exhibited moderate to high affinity for dopamine D2 receptors (Ki = 45-180 nM), with compound 7c showing selectivity over D1 receptors (>50-fold). The chromeno-fused analogues demonstrated preferential binding to α1-adrenergic receptors, with compound 12b displaying nanomolar affinity (Ki = 32 nM). Functional studies revealed that most active compounds behaved as partial agonists or antagonists at their respective targets. These results suggest that 4-phenoxy-tetrahydroisoquinoline derivatives represent promising lead compounds for the development of selective dopaminergic agents, while the chromeno-fused derivatives may serve as α1-adrenergic receptor modulators with potential cardiovascular applications.

title: [Sabuco's suco nerveo and the origins of neurochemistry in the Spanish Renaissance].
abstract: In the 16th century, at the height of the Spanish Renaissance, Bachiller Miguel Sabuco revolutionised medical theories about brain physiology with the publication of his work Nueva Filosof a in 1587. This work, which is mysteriously attributed to his daughter Luisa Oliva, presents a surprisingly gynocentric ideology for its time and has been reprinted on numerous occasions since its first edition saw the light. Sabuco s ideas have been plagiarised by such renowned authors as Descartes, Willis and Glisson. In stark contrast to the Arabized galenism based on the criterion of authority that predominated the medical practice of his time, Nueva Filosof a empirically promoted a modern approach to neurophysiology.
