This becomes even more worthy of merit if we bear in mind that Sabuco was not trained as a doctor but instead worked as an apothecary in Alcaraz (Albacete, Spain). The Bachiller granted the brain a commanding role over the organisation of the body for the first time in the history of medicine. He not only developed the surprising theory of succo nervoso (the nervous sap, or original neurotransmitting substance), but also an elegant digression on human nature that puts forward solid arguments about the dualist relation between mind and brain. He examined the association between emotional and physical health and explained how the emotions can impair health and lead to early death, which means he was also a pioneer in the development of psychosomatic medicine. Besides constituting the most illustrious forerunner of modern day neurochemistry, Sabuco s work also encourages doctors to treat their patients in an integral manner, with equal attention being given to the body, mind and soul. It comes as no surprise, then, that many aspects of his work can still be compared to modern medical and philosophical thought.

title: High-field proton magnetic resonance spectroscopy reveals metabolic effects of normal brain aging.
abstract: Altered brain metabolism is likely to be an important contributor to normal cognitive decline and brain pathology in elderly individuals. To characterize the metabolic changes associated with normal brain aging, we used high-field proton magnetic resonance spectroscopy in vivo to quantify 20 neurochemicals in the hippocampus and sensorimotor cortex of young adult and aged rats. We found significant differences in the neurochemical profile of the aged brain when compared with younger adults, including lower aspartate, ascorbate, glutamate, and macromolecules, and higher glucose, myo-inositol, N-acetylaspartylglutamate, total choline, and glutamine. These neurochemical biomarkers point to specific cellular mechanisms that are altered in brain aging, such as bioenergetics, oxidative stress, inflammation, cell membrane turnover, and endogenous neuroprotection. Proton magnetic resonance spectroscopy may be a valuable translational approach for studying mechanisms of brain aging and pathology, and for investigating treatments to preserve or enhance cognitive function in aging.

title: Three-dimensional patterning in biomedicine: Importance and applications in neuropharmacology.
abstract: Three-dimensional (3D) patterning technologies have emerged as transformative tools in neuropharmacology research, enabling precise spatial control over drug delivery and cellular microenvironments. Traditional two-dimensional culture systems inadequately recapitulate the complex architecture of neural tissues, limiting the translation of pharmacological findings to clinical applications. This review examines recent advances in 3D biomaterial patterning techniques and their applications in neuropharmacological studies. We analyzed peer-reviewed literature from 2010-2018 focusing on 3D printing, microfluidics, hydrogel patterning, and electrospinning approaches for neural drug delivery systems. Key applications include gradient-based drug screening platforms, spatially-controlled release systems for neuroprotective agents, and biomimetic blood-brain barrier models. 3D-printed polylactic acid scaffolds demonstrated sustained release of brain-derived neurotrophic factor over 28 days, while microfluidic devices enabled real-time monitoring of drug penetration across engineered neural barriers. Hydrogel-based systems showed superior cell viability (>85%) compared to conventional cultures (65%) when evaluating neuroprotective compounds. Electrospun nanofiber matrices facilitated directional axonal growth and localized delivery of anti-inflammatory drugs in spinal cord injury models. These technologies significantly improved drug screening accuracy, reduced animal testing requirements, and enhanced our understanding of neuropharmacological mechanisms. Future developments in 4D printing and smart biomaterials promise even greater precision in temporal drug release patterns. 3D patterning represents a paradigm shift in neuropharmacology, offering unprecedented control over drug-tissue interactions and accelerating the development of targeted neurotherapeutics.
journal: Journal of biomedical materials research. Part B, Applied biomaterials

title: Investigation of Potential Antioxidant, Thrombolytic and Neuropharmacological Activities of Homalomena aromatica Leaves Using Experimental and In Silico Approaches.
abstract: Homalomena aromatica (Schott) Hook.f. is a traditional medicinal plant widely used in Southeast Asian folk medicine for treating neurological disorders, but its pharmacological mechanisms remain poorly understood. This study aimed to evaluate the antioxidant, thrombolytic, and neuropharmacological properties of H. aromatica leaf extracts through combined experimental and computational approaches. Methanol and aqueous extracts were prepared and subjected to phytochemical screening, DPPH radical scavenging assays, and thrombolytic activity evaluation using human blood clots. Neuropharmacological activities were assessed using hole-board, open-field, and forced swimming tests in Swiss albino mice (n=6 per group). Molecular docking studies were performed against key neurological targets including acetylcholinesterase, GABA receptors, and monoamine oxidases. Phytochemical analysis revealed the presence of flavonoids, alkaloids, and phenolic compounds. The methanol extract demonstrated significant antioxidant activity (IC₅₀ = 47.32 ± 2.1 μg/mL) compared to ascorbic acid standard. Thrombolytic activity showed 23.4% clot lysis at 200 μg/mL concentration. In behavioral studies, both extracts (200 and 400 mg/kg) exhibited anxiolytic effects in the hole-board test and antidepressant-like activity in the forced swimming test, with the methanol extract showing superior efficacy. Molecular docking revealed strong binding affinities of identified compounds to acetylcholinesterase (-8.2 kcal/mol) and GABA-A receptors (-7.6 kcal/mol). These findings suggest that H. aromatica possesses promising antioxidant, thrombolytic, and neuropharmacological activities, supporting its traditional use and warranting further investigation for developing novel neurotherapeutic agents.

title: Application of triple-probe microdialysis for fast pharmacokinetic/pharmacodynamic evaluation of dopamimetic activity of drug candidates in the rat brain.
abstract: Conventional microdialysis techniques for evaluating dopamimetic drug candidates require multiple experiments and extensive time commitments, limiting throughput in preclinical screening. We developed a novel triple-probe microdialysis methodology enabling simultaneous pharmacokinetic and pharmacodynamic assessment of dopamine-enhancing compounds in awake, freely-moving rats. Three microdialysis probes were stereotaxically implanted: one in the nucleus accumbens shell (pharmacodynamic readout), one in the caudate-putamen (regional comparison), and one in the jugular vein (pharmacokinetic sampling). Male Sprague-Dawley rats (n=8 per group) received intraperitoneal injections of L-DOPA (100 mg/kg), amphetamine (2 mg/kg), or vehicle. Dialysate samples were collected every 15 minutes for 4 hours and analyzed using HPLC with electrochemical detection. Triple-probe microdialysis successfully demonstrated distinct pharmacokinetic-pharmacodynamic profiles for both compounds. L-DOPA showed delayed dopamine elevation (peak at 90 min) with preferential accumulation in nucleus accumbens versus caudate-putamen (2.3-fold difference), while amphetamine produced rapid, sustained increases in both regions. Plasma pharmacokinetics revealed L-DOPA's shorter half-life (32 ± 4 min) compared to amphetamine (78 ± 12 min). The triple-probe approach reduced experimental time by 60% compared to sequential single-probe studies while maintaining statistical power. This methodology provides a robust platform for rapid screening of dopamimetic drug candidates, offering simultaneous assessment of brain penetration, regional selectivity, and neurochemical efficacy within individual animals, thereby reducing animal usage while accelerating preclinical drug development timelines.

title: Legal aspects of zebrafish neuropharmacology and neurotoxicology research.
abstract: The increasing utilization of zebrafish (Danio rerio) as a model organism in neuropharmacology and neurotoxicology research has raised important regulatory and ethical considerations that require systematic examination. While zebrafish offer significant advantages including genetic tractability, optical transparency, and high-throughput screening capabilities, the legal framework governing their use in research remains complex and varies across jurisdictions. This review analyzes current regulatory requirements, institutional oversight mechanisms, and emerging legal challenges in zebrafish-based neurotoxicological studies. We examined regulatory guidelines from major international bodies including the EU, FDA, and OECD, alongside institutional animal care and use committee (IACUC) protocols from 45 research institutions conducting zebrafish neuropharmacological research between 2015-2018. Our analysis reveals significant inconsistencies in regulatory interpretation, particularly regarding the developmental stage at which zebrafish acquire legal protection status. While most jurisdictions consider larvae beyond 5 days post-fertilization as requiring formal ethical approval, 23% of surveyed institutions applied more stringent criteria. Neurotoxicology studies involving controlled substances presented additional regulatory complexities, with 67% of institutions reporting delays in protocol approval due to unclear guidelines for Schedule II compounds in aquatic models. We identified critical gaps in current regulatory frameworks, particularly regarding high-throughput behavioral assays and novel psychoactive substances testing. These findings highlight the urgent need for harmonized international guidelines specific to zebrafish neuropharmacological research to ensure both scientific rigor and appropriate ethical oversight while facilitating regulatory acceptance of zebrafish-derived safety data.

title: Volume versus wiring transmission in the brain: a new theoretical frame for neuropsychopharmacology.
abstract: Traditional models of neural communication have emphasized point-to-point synaptic transmission, yet accumulating evidence suggests that neurotransmitter signaling extends beyond the synaptic cleft through volume transmission mechanisms. This theoretical review examines the dual nature of neurochemical communication and its implications for understanding drug action in the central nervous system. We propose a comprehensive framework distinguishing between "wiring transmission" - the classical synaptic model involving rapid, spatially restricted neurotransmitter release - and "volume transmission" - a slower, more diffuse process where neurotransmitters, neuropeptides, and neuromodulators spread through extracellular space to reach distant targets. Analysis of anatomical, physiological, and pharmacological data reveals that many neurotransmitter systems, particularly dopaminergic, serotonergic, and cholinergic pathways, operate through both mechanisms simultaneously. Volume transmission appears especially prominent in subcortical regions and involves specialized anatomical features including mismatched distributions of neurotransmitter release sites and receptor locations. This dual transmission model provides new insights into the therapeutic mechanisms of psychotropic drugs, particularly those affecting monoaminergic systems. The framework helps explain the delayed onset of antidepressant efficacy, the widespread effects of neuroleptics, and the complex dose-response relationships observed with many neuropsychopharmacological agents. Understanding the relative contributions of wiring versus volume transmission may guide the development of more selective therapeutic interventions and advance our comprehension of psychiatric disorder pathophysiology.

title: Demon voltammetry and analysis software: analysis of cocaine-induced alterations in dopamine signaling using multiple kinetic measures.
abstract: Fast-scan cyclic voltammetry (FSCV) has emerged as a powerful technique for real-time measurement of dopamine release and uptake in brain tissue, yet comprehensive analysis of the complex kinetic parameters remains challenging. We developed DEMON (Dopamine Evaluation and Monitoring with Optimized Neurochemistry), a novel analysis software package designed to extract multiple kinetic measures from FSCV data with enhanced precision and automation. Using this software, we investigated cocaine-induced alterations in dopamine signaling in nucleus accumbens core of urethane-anesthetized rats (n=8). Electrical stimulation of the ventral tegmental area (single pulse, 350 μA, 4 ms) evoked dopamine release, which was measured before and after systemic cocaine administration (10 mg/kg, i.p.). DEMON software automatically calculated peak dopamine concentration, rise time (10-90%), decay time constant (τ), and area under the curve while minimizing user bias through standardized peak detection algorithms and baseline correction procedures. Cocaine treatment significantly increased peak dopamine concentration by 312 ± 45% compared to baseline, prolonged the decay time constant from 1.24 ± 0.18 s to 4.67 ± 0.74 s, and increased total dopamine signal (AUC) by 586 ± 89%. Rise time parameters remained unchanged, consistent with selective inhibition of the dopamine transporter rather than altered vesicular release mechanisms. The DEMON software package provides researchers with a standardized, comprehensive tool for FSCV data analysis that reveals multiple dimensions of dopamine kinetics, facilitating more nuanced understanding of dopaminergic neurotransmission in health and disease states.

title: Essential tremor and Parkinson's disease: lack of a link.
abstract: Essential tremor (ET) is a very common disorder and proving that there is a relationship to another common movement disorder, Parkinson's disease (PD), has been debated for years. Review of the literature for links between ET and PD primarily focused on neuropathology as well as neurochemistry, epidemiology, genetics, olfactory function, and neuroimaging. While there may be some evidence to suggest an increase in occurrence of PD in people who were previously diagnosed with ET, neuropathologic studies of ET with similarly assessed control subjects do not find an increase in Lewy bodies in the ET group. Studies of incidental Lewy body disease do not find an increase in ET or action tremor compared to controls. ET subjects as a group do not have neurochemical changes that are found in PD, do not respond to medications used to treat PD, are not hyposmic as is found in PD, and neuroimaging studies do not find changes of PD when groups are compared. The overwhelming amount of evidence suggests that any link between ET and PD is coincidental and not biological. Prospective, longitudinal cohort studies with standardized clinical and biomarker assessments followed by neuropathologic confirmation are needed.
journal: Movement disorders : official journal of the Movement Disorder Society

title: Improving the reproducibility of hydrogel-coated glutamate microsensors by using an automated dipcoater.
abstract: Hydrogel-coated microsensors based on carbon fiber electrodes (CFEs) are promising tools for in vivo analysis of endogeneous compounds such as glutamate. However, their construction generally depends on manual fabrication, which often results in poor reproducibility. The aim of this study was to improve the reproducibility and performance of glutamate microsensors. CFEs (10 microm diameter, 300-500 microm long) were coated with a cross-linked redox-polymer hydrogel containing l-glutamate oxidase, horseradish peroxidase and ascorbate oxidase. Various parameters that are likely to influence the reproducibility of the glutamate microsensors were studied. It appeared that the most crucial step in determining the microsensor performance is the manual hydrogel-application procedure. To control this procedure an automated dipcoater was constructed, which allowed mechanical application of the hydrogel on the CFE under standardized conditions. Significant improvements in performance were seen when the CFEs were dipcoated for 10 min at 37 degrees C. Further improvements were obtained when the automated hydrogel application was combined with other cross-link methods, such as electrodeposition and electrostatic complexation. A crucial factor in determining the microsensor performance is the hydrogel thickness. Microscopic observations revealed that, despite the use of an automated dipcoater, the layer thickness was not constant. By combining the automated dipcoat technique with amperometry, the layer thickness could be indirectly monitored and controlled, which resulted in significant improvements of the reproducibility of the sensors.

title: Neurochemistry of Anesthetic States.
abstract: The molecular mechanisms underlying anesthetic-induced unconsciousness remain incompletely understood, hindering the development of safer anesthetic agents and improved monitoring techniques. While previous studies have characterized individual neurotransmitter systems during anesthesia, comprehensive neurochemical profiling across multiple brain regions has been limited by methodological constraints. Here, we present a novel enzymatic approach combining high-performance liquid chromatography with electrochemical detection (HPLC-ECD) and optimized tissue extraction protocols to simultaneously quantify monoamines, amino acids, and acetylcholine in discrete brain regions during various anesthetic states. Male Sprague-Dawley rats (n=48) were anesthetized with propofol, sevoflurane, or ketamine at equipotent doses, with conscious controls receiving saline. Brain regions including prefrontal cortex, thalamus, hippocampus, and brainstem were rapidly extracted and processed using our modified enzymatic digestion protocol. Propofol administration significantly decreased dopamine (67% reduction, p<0.001) and norepinephrine (45% reduction, p<0.01) in the prefrontal cortex while increasing GABA levels by 34% (p<0.05). Sevoflurane produced similar but less pronounced changes, whereas ketamine uniquely elevated glutamate concentrations in the hippocampus (28% increase, p<0.01) while reducing thalamic acetylcholine by 52% (p<0.001). Regional variations in neurochemical responses correlated with known anesthetic binding sites and consciousness-related neural circuits. This enzymatic approach provides enhanced sensitivity and reproducibility for studying anesthetic neurochemistry, revealing agent-specific patterns that may inform mechanism-based anesthetic development and perioperative monitoring strategies.

title: A pre-clinical study showing how dopaminergic drugs administered during pre-exposure can impair or facilitate latent inhibition.
abstract: It has been suggested that, in classical conditioning, dopamine (DA) codes for (a) attention to the conditioned stimulus (CS) or (b) the intensity of the unconditioned stimulus. To clarify the role of DA in pre-clinical classical conditioning studies. An existing model of classical conditioning presented by Schmajuk, Lam, and Gray (J Exp Psychol Anim Behav Process 22:321-349, 1996) suggests that DA cells in the ventral midbrain area code for the attentionally modulated internal representation of the CS. It is assumed that this representation is increased by dopaminergic agonists and decreased by dopaminergic antagonists. Computer simulations with the model describe the effect of nicotine and haloperidol on latent inhibition. Simulations replicate experimental results demonstrating that both nicotine and haloperidol affect latent inhibition when administered during the pre-exposure phase. In addition, the model reproduces data showing that administration of nicotine or haloperidol results in the impairment or facilitation of latent inhibition depending on the duration of CS or the number of CSs. The model demonstrates that pre-clinical experimental results, including cell activity and pharmacological data, are consistent with an attentional role for DA in classical conditioning.

title: The ethics of elective psychopharmacology.
abstract: The increasing availability of psychoactive medications for cognitive and mood enhancement in healthy individuals has raised significant ethical questions regarding elective psychopharmacology use outside traditional therapeutic contexts. This review examines the current landscape of off-label psychopharmacological interventions and evaluates key ethical frameworks applicable to elective use. We conducted a systematic analysis of peer-reviewed literature from 2000-2012, surveying empirical studies on cognitive enhancers, mood stabilizers, and anxiolytics used by healthy populations, alongside philosophical and bioethical perspectives on enhancement versus treatment paradigms. Our analysis identified four primary ethical domains: autonomy and informed consent, distributive justice and access, safety and long-term consequences, and societal pressure and coercion. Current evidence suggests 15-30% of university students have used prescription stimulants non-medically, while growing numbers of professionals report modafinil and other cognitive enhancers for performance enhancement. Key ethical tensions emerge between individual liberty and collective welfare, particularly regarding workplace competitiveness, academic fairness, and healthcare resource allocation. Safety profiles for long-term use in healthy individuals remain largely unknown, complicating risk-benefit analyses. We conclude that elective psychopharmacology requires robust regulatory frameworks balancing individual autonomy with societal considerations. Future research must address long-term safety profiles, develop guidelines for appropriate use, and establish ethical standards for prescribing practices. These findings have immediate implications for clinical practice, institutional policies, and public health governance as enhancement technologies become increasingly prevalent.

title: Dopamine in psychiatry: a historical perspective.
abstract: Dopamine is the principal transmitter of several central nervous system pathways originating in the midbrain and critically involved in motor activity, learning and motivation, disruptions of which have been implicated in a number of disorders, including Parkinson disease and schizophrenia. Dopamine played a particularly significant role in the history of neurochemistry. Following a series of investigations between 1957 and 1965, commencing in the laboratory and completed in the clinic, the significance of chemical neurotransmission for normal CNS function was first demonstrated in the case of dopamine.

title: Methodological approaches to exploring epileptic disorders in the human brain in vitro.
abstract: The investigation of epileptic mechanisms in human brain tissue represents a critical yet methodologically challenging aspect of neuropharmacological research. While animal models have provided valuable insights, species differences in neuronal architecture and neurotransmitter systems necessitate direct examination of human epileptic tissue. We describe a comprehensive methodological framework for maintaining viable human cortical specimens obtained from surgical resections of epileptogenic foci in patients with medically refractory epilepsy. Brain slices (400-500 μm) were prepared within 30 minutes of surgical removal and maintained in oxygenated artificial cerebrospinal fluid at 32°C. Electrophysiological recordings were performed using multielectrode arrays and patch-clamp techniques to characterize spontaneous and evoked epileptiform activity. Pharmacological interventions included application of various antiepileptic drugs, glutamate receptor antagonists, and GABA modulators. Tissue viability was assessed through cellular morphology, membrane potential stability, and metabolic indicators over 8-12 hour recording periods. Our protocol consistently yielded robust epileptiform discharges in 78% of specimens (n=45 patients), with maintained neuronal responsiveness to pharmacological manipulation for up to 10 hours post-resection. AMPA/kainate receptor blockade reduced seizure-like activity by 65±12%, while gabazine enhanced discharge frequency by 180±34%. This methodology provides a reliable platform for investigating human epileptic mechanisms and screening potential therapeutic compounds directly in pathological human neural networks, offering advantages over conventional animal models for translational epilepsy research.

title: Evaluation of n-hexane extract of Viola betonicifolia for its neuropharmacological properties.
abstract: Viola betonicifolia (whole plant) has been used as a sedative and in various nervous disorders in Pakistani traditional medicines. The n-hexane extract of the whole plant of V. betonicifolia (HEVB) was investigated for neuropharmacological properties such as anxiolytic, muscle relaxant, sleep induction, antidepressant and sedative to ascertain its folk use. Anxiolytic activity was tested using the staircase test, while the muscle relaxing property of the extract was tested in various muscle relaxant paradigms, i.e. chimney test, traction test, rota rod and inclined plane. In anxiolytic and muscle relaxant tests, HEVB (0.3, 0.4 and 0.5 g/kg, i.p.), diazepam (1 mg/kg, i.p.) or distilled water (10 ml/kg i.p.) were administered 30, 60 and 90 min before performing the tests in mice. HEVB was also screened for a sleep-inducing effect. The antidepressant activity was determined by using the forced swimming test (FST), while line crossing in a special box was used for locomotor activity. HEVB showed a significant (P < 0.05) dose-dependent anxiolytic action in the staircase test. In muscle relaxant paradigms, a dose-dependent muscle relaxation was observed. For the phenobarbitone sleep induction test, HEVB notably (P < 0.05) reduced the latency time and increased the total sleeping duration. However, HEVB was devoid of any antidepressant activity, while the movements of mice were reduced significantly (P < 0.05) in locomotor activity. The results suggest that HEVB has anxiolytic, muscle relaxant, sleep-inducing (sedative) activity and, thus, provides pharmacological justification for the use of this plant as a sedative and for the relief of various nervous disorders.

title: Emerging targets for addiction neuropharmacology: From mechanisms to therapeutics.
abstract: Drug abuse represents a considerable burden of disease and has enormous economic impacts on societies. Over the years, few medications have been developed for clinical use. Their utilization is endowed with several limitations, including partial efficacy or significant side effects. On the other hand, the successful advancement of these compounds provides an important proof of concept for the feasibility of drug development programs in addiction. In recent years, a wealth of information has been generated on the psychological mechanisms, genetic or epigenetic predisposing factors, and neurobiological adaptations induced by drug consumption that interact with each other to contribute to disease progression. It is now clear that addiction develops through phases, from initial recreational use to excessive consumption and compulsive drug seeking, with a shift from positive to negative reinforcement driving motivated behaviors. A greater understanding of these mechanisms has opened new vistas in drug development programs. Researchers' attention has been shifted from investigation of classical targets associated with reward to biological substrates responsible for negative reinforcement, impulse loss of control, and maladaptive mechanisms resulting from protracted drug use. From this research, several new biological targets for the development of innovative therapies have started to emerge. This chapter offers an overview of targets currently under scrutiny for the development of new medications for addiction. This work is not exhaustive but rather it provides a few examples of how this research has advanced in recent years by virtue of studies carried out in our laboratory.

title: MR spectroscopy in diagnosis and neurological decision-making.
abstract: One of the most prolific chemical and anatomical imaging techniques of recent decades, magnetic resonance imaging (MRI), includes the ability to noninvasively assess neurochemical changes with magnetic resonance spectroscopy (MRS). Practical concerns are paramount in applying MRS, such as what the manufacturer provides with a routine MRI scanner, what methods are well tolerated by patients, and what has proved most diagnostically productive over a 25 year span of preliminary exploration of the technology. In this review, the authors explain the technical and neurochemical aspects of MRS and critically discuss its clinical neuroimaging applications.

title: Neurosurgery for obsessive-compulsive disorder: contemporary approaches.
abstract: Surgery for psychiatric disorders has a controversial history. Traditionally, procedures were undertaken to physically disconnect or destroy certain areas of the brain thought to constitute critical components of the limbic pathways. The relatively recent advent of the much safer and non-destructive technique known as deep brain stimulation has coincided with a resurgence in interest in psychosurgery. Contemporary approaches to the surgical management of obsessive-compulsive disorder are discussed, together with our current understanding of its pathophysiology.
journal: Journal of clinical neuroscience : official journal of the Neurosurgical Society of Australasia

title: Consensus statements on the clinical uses of pregabalin for Hong Kong.
abstract:
**Background:** Pregabalin, a gabapentinoid with established efficacy in neuropathic pain and epilepsy, has gained increasing attention for psychiatric applications in Asia-Pacific regions. However, clinical practice guidelines specific to Hong Kong populations remain limited. This study aimed to develop evidence-based consensus statements on pregabalin's clinical uses in Hong Kong psychiatric practice.

**Methods:** A modified Delphi process was conducted involving 24 psychiatrists and neurologists from major Hong Kong medical institutions between January-September 2019. Three rounds of structured questionnaires examined pregabalin's efficacy, safety, dosing protocols, and clinical applications across anxiety disorders, bipolar disorder, and substance use disorders. Literature review encompassed randomized controlled trials and real-world evidence from Asian populations. Consensus was defined as ≥75% agreement among panelists.

**Results:** Strong consensus emerged supporting pregabalin as second-line treatment for generalized anxiety disorder (91.7% agreement) and panic disorder (87.5% agreement) when first-line agents prove inadequate. For bipolar disorder, 79.2% agreed pregabalin demonstrated utility as adjunctive treatment for anxiety symptoms and sleep disturbances. Recommended starting doses were 75mg twice daily for anxiety disorders and 150mg daily for bipolar applications, with gradual titration based on response and tolerability. Common adverse effects included dizziness (68% of patients), sedation (45%), and weight gain (32%). Panelists emphasized careful monitoring for dependency potential, particularly in patients with substance use history.

**Conclusions:** These consensus statements provide practical guidance for pregabalin use in Hong Kong clinical practice, emphasizing its role as adjunctive therapy while highlighting the importance of individualized treatment approaches and comprehensive patient monitoring in Chinese populations.
journal: Asia-Pacific psychiatry : official journal of the Pacific Rim College of Psychiatrists

title: CNS Targets for multi-functional drugs in the treatment of Alzheimer's and Parkinson's diseases.
abstract: The progressive neurodegenerative nature of Alzheimer's disease (AD) and Parkinson's disease (PD) involves multiple pathological mechanisms including cholinergic dysfunction, oxidative stress, protein aggregation, and neuroinflammation. Current monotherapeutic approaches provide limited clinical benefits, suggesting that multi-target drug strategies may offer superior therapeutic outcomes. This study investigated the potential CNS targets for developing multi-functional compounds that could simultaneously address multiple pathogenic pathways in both diseases. We conducted a comprehensive analysis of shared molecular targets between AD and PD using computational modeling and in vitro screening approaches. Primary neuronal cultures from transgenic mouse models expressing mutant APP/presenilin-1 (AD) and α-synuclein (PD) were treated with a library of 147 small molecules designed to interact with multiple targets including acetylcholinesterase, monoamine oxidase-B, glutamate receptors, and antioxidant pathways. Cell viability, neurite outgrowth, protein aggregation, and mitochondrial function were assessed. Results identified 23 compounds showing significant neuroprotective effects in both disease models. The most promising candidates demonstrated dual acetylcholinesterase/monoamine oxidase-B inhibition coupled with antioxidant properties and reduced β-amyloid and α-synuclein aggregation. Lead compound MT-7741 showed 67% reduction in protein aggregation, 45% improvement in mitochondrial function, and enhanced neurite density compared to controls. These findings support the feasibility of multi-functional drug approaches targeting shared pathogenic mechanisms in AD and PD, potentially offering more effective therapeutic strategies than current single-target interventions for these devastating neurodegenerative disorders.

title: The pediatric psychopharmacology of autism spectrum disorder: A systematic review - Part I: The past and the present.
abstract: Autism Spectrum Disorder (ASD) is a severe and lifelong neurodevelopmental disorder, with high social costs and a dramatic burden on the quality of life of patients and family members. Despite its high prevalence, reaching 1/54 children and 1/45 adults in the United States, no pharmacological treatment is still directed to core symptoms of ASD, encompassing social and communication deficits, repetitive behaviors, restricted interests, and abnormal sensory processing. The purpose of this review is to provide an overview of the state-of-the-art of psychopharmacological therapy available today for ASD in children and adolescents, in order to foster best practices and to organize new strategies for future research. To date, atypical antipsychotics such as risperidone and aripiprazole represent the first line of intervention for hyperactivity, impulsivity, agitation, temper outbursts or aggression towards self or others. Tricyclic antidepressants are less prescribed because of uncertain efficacy and important side effects. SSRIs, especially fluoxetine and sertraline, may be effective in treating repetitive behaviors (anxiety and obsessive-compulsive symptoms) and irritability/agitation, while mirtazapine is more helpful with sleep problems. Low doses of buspirone have shown some efficacy on restrictive and repetitive behaviors in combination with behavioral interventions. Stimulants, and to a lesser extent atomoxetine, are effective in reducing hyperactivity, inattention and impulsivity also in comorbid ASD-ADHD, although with somewhat lower efficacy and greater incidence of side effects compared to idiopathic ADHD. Clonidine and guanfacine display some efficacy on hyperactivity and stereotypic behaviors. For several other drugs, case reports and open-label studies suggest possible efficacy, but no randomized controlled trial has yet been performed. Research in the pediatric psychopharmacology of ASD is still faced with at least two major hurdles: (a) Great interindividual variability in clinical response and side effect sensitivity is observed in the ASD population. This low level of predictability would benefit from symptom-specific treatment algorithms and from biomarkers to support drug choice; (b) To this date, no psychoactive drug appears to directly ameliorate core autism symptoms, although some indirect improvement has been reported with several drugs, once the comorbid target symptom is abated.

title: Role of glutamate and dopamine receptors in the psychopharmacological profile of the indole alkaloid psychollatine.
abstract: The indole alkaloid psychollatine, isolated from Psychotria species, has demonstrated psychoactive properties in traditional ethnobotanical preparations, yet its mechanism of action remains poorly characterized. This study investigated the neuropharmacological profile of psychollatine with particular emphasis on glutamate and dopamine receptor interactions. Psychollatine was purified from Psychotria carthaginensis using standard chromatographic techniques. Receptor binding assays were performed using rat brain membrane preparations to evaluate affinity for NMDA, AMPA, kainate, D1, and D2 receptor subtypes. Behavioral assessments included locomotor activity, prepulse inhibition, and conditioned avoidance response in male Sprague-Dawley rats following intraperitoneal administration of psychollatine (1-30 mg/kg). Electrophysiological recordings from hippocampal slices examined effects on glutamatergic neurotransmission. Psychollatine exhibited moderate binding affinity for NMDA receptors (Ki = 8.2 μM) and D2 receptors (Ki = 12.4 μM), with minimal affinity for other receptor subtypes tested. Behaviorally, psychollatine (10-30 mg/kg) produced dose-dependent hyperlocomotion and disrupted prepulse inhibition, effects that were attenuated by co-administration of the D2 antagonist haloperidol (0.5 mg/kg) but not the NMDA antagonist MK-801. Electrophysiological studies revealed psychollatine significantly enhanced glutamate-induced excitatory postsynaptic potentials in CA1 pyramidal neurons. These findings suggest that psychollatine's psychoactive effects involve complex interactions between glutamatergic and dopaminergic systems, with primary behavioral effects mediated through dopamine D2 receptor activation. Further investigation of this novel indole alkaloid may provide insights into glutamate-dopamine interactions underlying psychosis-related behaviors.

title: In vivo autofluorescence spectrofluorometry of central serotonin.
abstract: Serotonin (5-HT) plays crucial roles in mood regulation, sleep-wake cycles, and cognitive function, yet real-time monitoring of central serotonergic neurotransmission in living brain tissue remains technically challenging. Traditional microdialysis approaches suffer from poor temporal resolution and tissue damage, while existing fluorometric methods require exogenous fluorophores that may alter normal physiology. We developed a novel in vivo autofluorescence spectrofluorometry technique to directly monitor endogenous serotonin dynamics in the rat brain without chemical modification. Using a custom-built fiber-optic spectrofluorometer with excitation at 295 nm and emission detection at 340 nm, we implanted 200-μm diameter optical fibers into the dorsal raphe nucleus and hippocampus of anesthetized Sprague-Dawley rats (n=12). The system achieved 1-second temporal resolution with signal-to-noise ratios exceeding 15:1. Pharmacological validation demonstrated that autofluorescence signals increased 180±25% following administration of the serotonin reuptake inhibitor fluoxetine (10 mg/kg, i.p.) and decreased 65±12% after depletion with para-chlorophenylalanine (300 mg/kg, i.p., 48h pretreatment). Electrical stimulation of the median raphe nucleus (20 Hz, 1 min) produced rapid, reproducible increases in hippocampal autofluorescence (peak amplitude 45±8% above baseline). Control experiments using 5,7-dihydroxytryptamine lesions confirmed signal specificity for serotonergic transmission. This minimally invasive technique provides unprecedented temporal resolution for monitoring central serotonin dynamics and offers significant advantages for investigating serotonergic mechanisms underlying behavior and psychopathology in real-time experimental paradigms.

title: Effects of the mGluR2/3 agonist LY379268 on ketamine-evoked behaviours and neurochemical changes in the dentate gyrus of the rat.
abstract: Glutamate dysfunction has been implicated in the pathophysiology of schizophrenia, with both hypoactivation of NMDA receptors and dysregulation of metabotropic glutamate receptors contributing to disease symptoms. The mGluR2/3 agonist LY379268 has shown promise in preclinical models as a potential antipsychotic agent. The present study examined whether LY379268 could attenuate ketamine-induced behavioral and neurochemical alterations in rats, using the dentate gyrus as a key region of interest due to its role in cognitive processing and NMDA receptor expression. Male Sprague-Dawley rats (n=8-10/group) received intraperitoneal injections of vehicle, ketamine (30 mg/kg), LY379268 (3 mg/kg), or ketamine plus LY379268 pretreatment (30 min prior). Locomotor activity was monitored for 120 minutes post-injection, and dentate gyrus tissue was collected for neurochemical analysis using high-performance liquid chromatography. Ketamine administration significantly increased locomotor activity compared to controls (p<0.001) and elevated dopamine and glutamate concentrations in the dentate gyrus (147% and 132% of control, respectively). Pretreatment with LY379268 dose-dependently reduced ketamine-induced hyperlocomotion by 68% (p<0.01) and normalized dentate gyrus neurotransmitter levels. LY379268 alone had no significant effects on behavior or neurochemistry. These findings demonstrate that mGluR2/3 activation effectively counteracts NMDA receptor hypofunction-induced behavioral and neurochemical disturbances, supporting the therapeutic potential of mGluR2/3 agonists in treating glutamate-related psychiatric disorders. The dentate gyrus appears to be a critical site for these modulatory effects.

title: Neuropsychopharmacology and the genetics of schizophrenia: a history of the diagnosis of schizophrenia.
abstract: Development of the diagnostic concept of schizophrenia (dementia praecox) is traced from the fourth edition of [Kraepelin, E., 1893. Ein Kurzes Lehrbuch der Psychiatrie. 4 Aufl. Barth, Lepzig] textbook to the DSM-IV [American Psychiatric Association, 1994. Diagnostic and Statistical Manual of Mental Disorders. Fourth ed. American Psychiatric Association, Washington, 273-316]. The differences between the criteria used by Bleuler [Bleuler, E., 1911. Dementia Praecox oder Gruppe der Schizophrenien. Deuticke, Leipzig] and Schneider [Fortschr. Neurol. Psychiatr. 25 (1957) 487] in the diagnosis of schizophrenia are discussed. The nosologic contributions of Kleist [Klin. Wochenschr. 2 (1923) 962] and Leonhard [Leonhard, K., 1957. Aufteilung der endogenen Psychosen. Akademie, Berlin]--which split schizophrenia into two major classes of disease with several forms and subforms--are outlined. Epidemiological findings--which show wide variations in the prevalence of schizophrenia in the general population and in the admission rate of schizophrenics to psychiatric clinics--are presented. Findings in genetic studies are reviewed with special reference to family, twin, and adoption studies which have raised the possibility that heredity plays an important role in the etiology of schizophrenia; mathematical analyses are examined which have ruled out monogenic transmission, as well as molecular genetic investigations indicating that the schizophrenic population is genetically heterogeneous. The relevance of findings with endophenotypes to the genetics of schizophrenia is questioned. The history of pharmacotherapy, neuropharmacology, and psychopharmacology of schizophrenia is outlined. Attention is focused on findings which indicate that the schizophrenic population is pharmacologically heterogenous. It is emphasized that neuropsychopharmacology, through its unique capability of linking the effects of psychotropic drugs to brain structures--encoded by genes which have been identified--offers a pioneering methodology for bridging the gap between the genes and psychiatric nosology. It is pointed out that for the detection of subpopulations within schizophrenia, clinical investigations with antipsychotic drugs have to proceed beyond the demonstration of therapeutic efficacy to the identification of treatment-responsive form(s) of illness. Early findings by Fish [L'Encephale 53 (1964) 245] are presented which indicate that affect-laden paraphrenia, one of the three forms of unsystematic schizophrenia in Leonhard [Leonhard, K., 1957. Aufteilung der endogenen Psychosen. Akademie, Berlin] classification, is the treatment-responsive subpopulation within schizophrenia for typical antipsychotic drugs. It is suggested that if the findings of Fish [L'Encephale 53 (1964) 245] could be verified, affect-laden paraphrenia would qualify for molecular genetic research. Another possible subpopulation that might qualify for genetic research is systematic hebephrenia, one of the three forms of systematic hebephrenia. The paper concludes that resolving the heterogeneity of the schizophrenic population would open up a new perspective for genetic research and for the pharmacotherapy of the different illnesses covered up for a century by the diagnostic label of schizophrenia.

title: Neuropharmacology of brain-stimulation-evoked aggression.
abstract: Evidence is reviewed concerning the brain areas and neurotransmitters involved in aggressive behavior in the cat and rodent. In the cat, two distinct neural circuits involving the hypothalamus and PAG subserve two different kinds of aggression: defensive rage and predatory (quiet-biting) attack. The roles played by the neurotransmitters serotonin, GABA, glutamate, opioids, cholecystokinin, substance P, norepinephrine, dopamine, and acetylcholine in the modulation and expression of aggression are discussed. For the rat, a single area, largely coincident with the intermediate hypothalamic area, is crucial for the expression of attack; variations in the rat attack response in natural settings are due largely to environmental variables. Experimental evidence emphasizing the roles of serotonin and GABA in modulating hypothalamically evoked attack in the rat is discussed. It is concluded that significant progress has been made concerning our knowledge of the circuitry underlying the neural basis of aggression. Although new and important insights have been made concerning neurotransmitter regulation of aggressive behavior, wide gaps in our knowledge remain.

title: Measurement of plasmalemmal dopamine transport, vesicular dopamine transport, and K(+)-stimulated dopamine release in frozen rat brain tissue.
abstract: Dopamine neurotransmission plays a critical role in motor function, reward processing, and cognition, with dysfunction implicated in Parkinson's disease, schizophrenia, and addiction. Traditional methods for measuring dopaminergic function require fresh tissue preparations, limiting experimental flexibility and multicenter studies. We developed and validated a novel protocol for simultaneous assessment of three key dopaminergic parameters—plasmalemmal dopamine transporter (DAT) activity, vesicular monoamine transporter 2 (VMAT2) function, and potassium-stimulated dopamine release—using frozen rat brain tissue. Striatal tissue from adult Sprague-Dawley rats was sectioned at 10 μm thickness and stored at -80°C for up to 12 weeks. DAT activity was measured using [³H]dopamine uptake assays in the presence and absence of GBR12909. VMAT2 function was assessed via [³H]dopamine accumulation into synaptic vesicles isolated from thawed tissue homogenates, with reserpine as a selective inhibitor. K⁺-stimulated dopamine release was evaluated using high-performance liquid chromatography following depolarization with 30 mM KCl. Frozen tissue retained 82±5% of DAT activity, 76±7% of VMAT2 function, and 71±9% of K⁺-stimulated release capacity compared to fresh preparations (n=8-12 per group). All three measures remained stable for at least 8 weeks of storage and showed excellent correlation with fresh tissue values (r>0.85, p<0.001). This methodology provides a practical approach for comprehensive dopaminergic assessment in archived samples, facilitating retrospective studies and enabling standardized protocols across multiple laboratories investigating dopamine-related neurological and psychiatric disorders.

title: Treatment of attention deficits in neurological disorders.
abstract: Attention deficits are prevalent across numerous neurological conditions, including traumatic brain injury, stroke, dementia, and neurodegenerative disorders, significantly impacting patients' functional outcomes and quality of life. While stimulant medications have shown efficacy in attention-deficit/hyperactivity disorder, their utility in neurological populations remains less established. This review examines current pharmacological approaches for treating attention deficits in neurological disorders, analyzing evidence from clinical trials and case series published between 1990-2005. We systematically reviewed literature on cholinesterase inhibitors, dopaminergic agents, noradrenergic medications, and novel compounds in patients with acquired attention deficits. Cholinesterase inhibitors, particularly donepezil and rivastigmine, demonstrated modest improvements in sustained attention tasks in patients with mild cognitive impairment and early dementia, with effect sizes ranging from 0.3-0.5. Methylphenidate showed promise in traumatic brain injury populations, improving processing speed and divided attention in 60-70% of patients in small controlled trials. Modafinil emerged as a well-tolerated alternative, particularly effective for vigilance deficits in multiple sclerosis and post-stroke populations. Atomoxetine demonstrated benefits in executive attention domains across various neurological conditions, though with variable response rates. However, most studies suffered from small sample sizes, heterogeneous patient populations, and limited follow-up periods. Current evidence suggests that pharmacological interventions can provide meaningful improvements in attention deficits associated with neurological disorders, but treatment selection should be individualized based on specific attention domains affected, underlying pathophysiology, and patient tolerance. Further large-scale, randomized controlled trials are needed to establish definitive treatment guidelines.

title: Microbore liquid chromatography with UV detection to study the in vivo passage of compound 21, a non-peptidergic AT₂ receptor agonist, to the striatum in rats.
abstract: A microbore liquid chromatography method coupled to UV detection was developed and validated in order to monitor the passage of compound 21 (C21), a non-peptide angiotensin II type 2 receptor agonist, to the striatum of rats. For this purpose, sampling from the striatum was performed using the in vivo microdialysis technique. Separations were performed on a C₁₈ microbore (1mm i.d.) column using gradient elution. The retention time for C21 was found to be 6.3 min. The calibration curve was linear between 10 and 200 ng/ml with a correlation coefficient ≥ 0.999. The limit of detection and the limit of quantification were 3 and 10 ng/ml respectively. The intra-day and the inter-day precision (RSD%) ranged between 0.5 and 4.6% with an average recovery of 101.5±10.0% (mean±SD, n=15). In vivo experiments were performed on rats to measure the concentration of C21 in striatal dialysates after intraperitoneal (10 or 50mg/kg) or intravenous injection (10 mg/kg or 20 mg/kg) of C21 and suggest minimal passage of the compound to the striatum.
